1 
 
Sources Sought/Request For Information (RFI) 
Education Platform Requirements 
1.  Purpose and Scope  
This Sources Sought/ Request for Information is issued solely for market research and 
acquisition planning purposes.  This notice does not constitute a Request for Quote (RFQ), 
Invitation for Bid (IFB), or Request for Proposal (RFP) and does not obligate the Government to 
issue a solicitation or make an award. 
Responses are voluntary. No reimbursement will be provided for costs associated with 
responding to this notice.  
The Department of War Education Activity (DoWEA) seeks information to better understand 
current industry capabilities, architectural approaches, data strategies, and maturity related to a 
single, cohesive enterprise education platform that supports DoWEA’s global Pre-K – 12 
mission. 
2.  Background and Objective 
DoWEA operates a globally distributed education environment supporting instructional delivery, 
student services, and enterprise operations. As part of long-term modernization and consolidation 
planning, DoWEA is conducting market research to assess platforms that can: 
• Unify instructional and enterprise systems on a shared or tightly integrated data 
foundation. 
• Reduce system fragmentation and manual data movement. 
• Enable secure, near-real-time reporting, analytics, and AI-enabled insights at global scale. 
• Support interoperability with external analytics platforms and DoWEA-managed data 
environments. 
3. Teaching, Learning & Student Systems 
3.1 Integrated Platform Approach 
DoWEA is interested in platforms that support tightly integrated, natively developed or fully 
supported modules operating on a shared identity and data framework. Vendors may describe 
point to point or loosely coupled integrations; however, such approaches should be clearly 
identified and explained.  
3.2 Student Information System (SIS) 

2 
 
DoWEA seeks information on Student Information Systems (SIS) that can serve as the primary 
system of record for managing student data across the full student lifecycle.  Areas of interest 
include, but are not limited to: 
• Enrollment, registration, scheduling, attendance, grading, gradebook management, 
transcripts, discipline/behavior tracking, and program participation. 
• Secure student records management with rolebased access control, audit logging, 
alignment with the Privacy Act of 1974 and applicable DoW privacy regulations, and 
configurable data retention rules. 
• Support for DoWEA specific calendars, grading policies, credits, and reporting 
requirements. 
• Standards based, documented APIs that support data import and export for students, 
enrollment, roster, attendance, grades, transcripts, behavior, and program status data, 
including near real time exchange where available. 
3.3 Learning Management System (LMS) – Optional Module with Mandatory 
Integration 
DoWEA seeks information on education platforms that either include a native LMS module or 
provide strong, production ready integration with commonly used third party LMS solutions (for 
example, Schoology and Google Classroom) but not limited to those solutions.   Areas of interest 
include the platform’s ability to support: 
• Seamlessly integrate with external LMS platforms using standards-based mechanisms 
(e.g., LTI 1.3/Advantage, OneRoster, and secure APIs). 
• Support automated provisioning and synchronization of:  
o Courses, sections, teachers, students, and roles. 
o Assignments and grading workflows. 
o Enrollment changes, schedule updates, and staff reassignments. 
• Enable bidirectional grade pass back so that instructional grading performed in the LMS 
flows reliably to the SIS gradebook and transcript processes. 
• Maintain consistent user identity, authentication (SSO), and role-based access across the 
SIS and integrated LMS environments. 
• Provide visibility for instructional activity and learning signals (e.g., assignment 
completion, engagement indicators) for reporting and analytics, regardless of whether 
instruction occurs in a native or third-party LMS. 
• Ensure mobile support and parity of experience where supported by the external LMS. 
If a native LMS module is offered, vendors may describe capabilities related to: 
• Course and content management, assignments, rubrics, discussions, collaboration tools, 
and mobile applications. 
• Standards-based grading aligned with SIS grade calculation and transcript workflows. 

3 
 
• Learning analytics focused on engagement, progress, pacing, mastery, and course 
completion. 
3.4 Assessment 
DoWEA seeks information on assessment capabilities that support formative, benchmark, and 
summative use cases.  Areas of interest include: 
• Centralized, reusable item banks with tagging, version control, and alignment to 
standards. 
• Accommodation management aligned to IEP/504 requirements and multilingual learner 
needs. 
• Secure test administration with support for offline or low-bandwidth operation in global 
environments. 
• Proctoring and academic integrity safeguards, as applicable. 
• Import/export APIs supporting assessment results, accommodations, and item metadata. 
• Integration with LMS platforms (native or third-party) for assignment-based and 
classroom-embedded assessments. 
3.5 Special Education and Student Support Programs 
DoWEA seeks information on platforms that support special education and student services 
workflows.  Areas of interest include support for: 
• IEP/504, Multilingual Learner Programs (MLP), Advanced Academic Programs (AAP), 
and MTSS/RTI frameworks. 
• Intervention planning, service delivery logging, progress monitoring, and compliance 
reporting. 
• Strict role-based privacy controls to safeguard sensitive student data. 
• Standards-based APIs supporting exchange of accommodations, service records, progress 
data, and compliance indicators. 
4. Reporting, Analytics, and Data Interoperability 
DoWEA is interested in understanding how platforms support reporting and analytics across 
instructional and enterprise domains.  Areas of interest include: 
• Real-time or near real-time reporting across SIS, Assessment, Special Programs, and 
LMS platforms (native or third-party). 
• Role-appropriate dashboards for teachers, school leaders, district/regional leadership, and 
headquarters personnel. 
• Reporting shall not be degraded or functionally limited using an external LMS. 

4 
 
Vendors should describe how reporting data is structured and made available for analytical and 
AI use cases.  
5. Enterprise Operations & Business Services 
DoWEA seeks information on enterprise education platforms that provide or integrate with 
operational and business services to support a global education environment.  The following 
areas represent capabilities of interest for market research purposes. 
5.1 Finance / ERP 
Areas of interest include: 
• General Ledger, Accounts Payable/Receivable, multi-fund accounting, grants 
management, budgeting and forecasting. 
• Procurement and eProcurement workflows. 
• Inventory management and school activity funds. 
• Auditability and reporting are suitable for federal oversight. 
5.2 Human Resources /Human Capital Management (HCM) 
Areas of interest include: 
• Manpower management, including position control, Staff Deployment (by school, 
program, or funding source), Trend and Forecasting (enrollment driven staffing models), 
and Scenario Planning and Forecasting. 
• Recruiting, onboarding, licensure and credential tracking, staff evaluations, and substitute 
management. 
• Alignment of staff roles and credentials with instructional and system access. 
• Integration with enterprise identity and access management systems. 
5.3 Facilities /Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS) 
Areas of interest include: 
• Work order management, preventive maintenance scheduling, asset lifecycle tracking, 
and space utilization. 
• Safety, security, and facilities related compliance workflows. 
5.4 Transportation 
Areas of interest include: 
• Route planning, ridership management, and parent notifications. 

5 
 
• Fleet management and vehicle maintenance tracking. 
5.5 IT Service Management (ITSM) & End-User Computing 
Areas of interest include: 
• Device, software, and asset management. 
• IT ticketing and service workflows. 
• Integration with Unified Endpoint Management (UEM/MDM) platforms. 
• Classroom device management and instructional visibility. 
6. Unified Data, Analytics & Interoperability 
6.1 Unified Data Architecture 
DoWEA seeks information on platform support unified or federated data approaches across 
instructional and enterprise domains.  Areas of interest include: 
• A shared or tightly federated data model across SIS, LMS, ERP, HR, facilities, 
transportation, and IT operations. 
• Near real-time data flows with minimal manual reconciliation. 
6.2 APIs & Interoperability Standards 
 Vendors may describe support for open APIs and industry standards, including: 
• Open REST APIs with pagination, filtering, and webhook/event support. 
• Support for current and future standards, including but not limited to:  
o IMS OneRoster 1.1+ 
o Ed-Fi Data Standard 4.0+ (native or certified adapter) 
o LTI 1.3/Advantage 
o QTI/APIP (as applicable) 
o SCORM (legacy) and xAPI 
o Identity standards: SAML 2.0, OIDC/OAuth 2.0, SCIM 2.0 
6.3 AI-Enabled Analytics & Business Intelligence 
DoWEA is interested in understanding how platforms support analytics and AI enabled insights 
across instructional and operational domains.  Areas of interest include: 
• Executive, instructional, and operational dashboards. 
• Early warning indicators for attendance, grades, behavior, and course completion with 
configurable thresholds. 

6 
 
• Machine learning explainability, including feature importance and counterfactual 
explanations. 
• Intervention tracking linked to analytic triggers. 
• Self-service analytics, including natural language queries where appropriate. 
• Export or integration with DoWEA managed data warehouse or lakehouse infrastructure. 
• Availability of Metadata catalogs with data lineage visualization. 
7. Data Quality & Master Data Management 
Areas of interest include: 
• Automated validation for completeness, accuracy, timeliness, and consistency. 
• Pre-configured quality checks for critical data (student demographics, attendance, grades, 
staff credentials). 
• Data quality dashboards, alerts, and trend analysis. 
• Support for DoWEA defined custom validation rules. 
• Master Data Management for students, staff, schools, and courses. 
• Data classification, PII tagging. and retention/minimization controls. 
8. Reporting Integration with External AI-Enabled Analytics Platforms (e.g., MS Fabric) 
DoWEA seeks information on how platform reporting and analytics capabilities interoperate 
seamlessly with external, AI enabled enterprise analytics platforms, including but not limited to 
MS Fabric, Power BI, and DoWEA managed data warehouse or lakehouse environments. The 
reporting architecture must support both native platform reporting and advanced external 
analytics without data duplication, vendor lock-in, or loss of data fidelity.  Vendors shall 
describe how their solutions enable secure, scalable, and transparent integration across 
instructional, operational, and enterprise analytics environments.  
9. Data Access & Export 
The platform shall provide secure, documented, and supported mechanisms to expose 
reporting data to external AI-enabled analytics platforms, including:  
o REST-based APIs. 
o Event- or change-based data feeds. 
o Bulk and incremental data export capabilities for historical and snapshot data. 
• Data access methods shall support near real-time and scheduled delivery patterns suitable 
for ingestion by AI-enabled analytics platforms. 
• Exported data shall be available in open, analytics-optimized formats, including but not 
limited to:  
o Parquet 
o Delta 

7 
 
o CSV 
o JSON 
• The solution shall not require proprietary or vendor-locked connectors to integrate with 
MS Fabric or comparable AI-enabled analytic platforms. 
10. Compatibility with AI-Enabled Analytics Architectures 
The reporting capability shall be compatible with modern analytics and AI architecture, 
including: 
• Lakehouse and warehouse ingestion models, enabling reporting data to land directly in 
DoWEA-managed analytic environments. 
• Semantic and analytical models that support downstream BI, AI, and machine learning 
workloads without reengineering. 
• Data pipelines, notebooks, and AI workflows, enabling DoWEA to apply advanced 
analytics, machine learning, and cross-domain insights beyond the platform’s native 
capabilities. 
Vendors shall clearly describe: 
• How reporting data is structured for analytical and AI consumption. 
• How data supports instructional, operational, and enterprise analytics use cases. 
• How platform data can be joined with non-platform data sources (e.g., finance, HR, 
logistics, cybersecurity, or operations data) in external analytics environments. 
11. Data Model Transparency & Metadata 
The vendor must provide full transparency of the reporting data model, including:  
o Tables, fields, relationships, and keys. 
o Business definitions for metrics, indicators, and calculated values. 
• A metadata catalog shall be available natively or exportable, supporting:  
o Schema discovery. 
o Data lineage from source systems through reporting outputs. 
o Field-level descriptions suitable for ingestion into enterprise metadata catalogs. 
• Any native semantic layers must be exportable, reproducible, or documentable for use in 
external AI-enabled analytic platforms. 
12. Security, Governance & Access Control 
Reporting interfaces shall comply with DoWEA security and data governance requirements, 
including:  

8 
 
o Role-based access controls aligned to SIS, HR, and enterprise roles. 
o Row-level and column-level security enforcement that is preserved or enforceable 
in external platforms. 
The solution shall support enterprise identity integration to enable:  
o Consistent authentication and authorization across the platform and external 
analytics environments. 
o Centralized logging and auditability. 
Data classification, PII tagging, retention, and minimization controls shall be preserved when 
reporting data is exposed to or consumed by AI-enabled analytic platforms. 
13. Incremental, Event-Driven & Near Real-Time Reporting 
The platform shall support incremental data extraction (e.g., change data capture or equivalent) 
to reduce latency and processing overhead.  Event driven updates via webhooks or event streams 
consumable by AI enabled platforms. 
• Examples include but are not limited to: 
o Enrollment changes,  
o Attendance updates,  
o Grade postings,  
o Intervention changes,  
o IEP status updates. 
Vendors shall identify which reporting domains support incremental delivery versus full refresh 
models. 
14. AI & Advan

GENERAL INFORMATION

PLACE OF PERFORMANCE

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

DESCRIPTION

Request for Information

Enterprise Service Desk Managed Contact Center Infrastructure 

TAC RFI Number: 36C10B26Q0368

1. Introduction 

The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) Enterprise Command Operations (ECO) is issuing a Request for Information (RFI) in support of a planned future solicitation for its Enterprise Service Desk Managed Contact Center Infrastructure (CCI) solution. ECO is seeking feedback and engagement from interested parties. 

See Section 3 for CCI Solution capabilities and anticipated future capabilities.

This RFI is issued for information and planning purposes only and does not constitute a solicitation nor does it restrict the Government as to the ultimate acquisition approach. In accordance with (IAW) Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 15.101(c), responses to this notice are not offers and cannot be accepted by the Government to form a binding contract. The purpose of this RFI is to obtain market information on capable sources of supply, industry practices, and input specific to the information provided. The Government is not responsible for any cost incurred by industry in furnishing this information. All costs associated with responding to this RFI will be solely at the interested party’s expense. Not responding to this RFI does not preclude participation in any future Request for Proposals, if any is issued. Any information submitted by respondents to this RFI is strictly voluntary. All submissions become Government property and will not be returned. Any proprietary/sensitive information must be clearly marked by the vendor. 

2. Disclaimer

The Government will not reimburse any costs incurred in responding to this market research request. Participation is voluntary, and non-response will not preclude participation in any future solicitation. The Government may use the information received to refine requirements, 

acquisition strategy, and solicitation structure

3. CCI Solution Capabilities

The current CCI solution utilizes the NICE CXOne platform. Additional features and enhancements leverage Medallia and fill integration with VA’s IT Service Management (ITSM) tool, currently ServiceNow.

3.1 Current Capabilities

The VA operates in a multi‑vendor environment in which contractors must work collaboratively, using non‑proprietary materials and coordinated processes to ensure seamless, consistent service delivery. The CCI Contractor must fully cooperate with all stakeholders, participate in required joint activities, align with VA governance, share deliverables, and support coordinated handoffs and continuous service improvements across vendors.

The CCI Contractor will provide and maintain a fully integrated up-to-date CCI solution that interoperates with VA systems, supports multi-channel contact handling, delivers intelligent routing, real-time reporting, and secure access for both onsite and remote agents. They are responsible for designing and updating call flows and automated messages, ensuring compatibility with VA firewalls and change-management processes, addressing security vulnerabilities, and staying current with OEM releases. The solution must include complete call management capabilities, service observation, required Site Reliability Engineering (SRE), and support for operational disruptions, while also supplying all associated documentation and reports.

The Contractor must deliver comprehensive performance analysis and reporting across the CCI solution, ITSM, and ESD vendor operations, using automated monitoring tools, defined metrics, surveys, and trend analyses to assess service quality, Service Level Agreement (SLA)adherence, and customer satisfaction. The Contractor must also maintain robust data management practices, provide dashboards and reporting interfaces, support quality assurance reviews, and ensure continuity through coordinated disaster recovery, contingency, and escalation plans aligned with VA standards.

The CCI Solution has the following core capabilities:

Intelligent Routing & Queuing

Intelligent, skills‑based routing across modalities (voice, chat, web)

Universal queue; dynamic prioritization; overflow and default routing

Multi‑vendor routing and support for Moves, Adds, Changes, and Deletes (MACD) on queues and routing configs

Real‑time, rules‑driven routing (skills, availability, SLA, language, presence)

Warm/cold transfers; conferencing; multi‑channel transfer capability

Ability to use Interactive Voice Response (IVR)‑captured data for routing; omni‑channel context passing

Simulation/testing tools for routing; emergency routing controls

Expansion of ports and routing resiliency

Interactive Voice Response (IVR)

Visual IVR and speech/Natural Language Understanding (NLU)‑driven automated interactions

Elastic IVR ports for surges; configurable greetings and announcements

Database integration for data retrieval, verification, and self‑service

Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency (DTMF)/speech capture; transcription and reporting in English & Spanish

Natural speech recognition (English & Spanish) with high accuracy

Survey capability; accessibility support (Teletypewriter (TTY)/ American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII))

Comprehensive reporting, real‑time caller activity visibility

Ability to update messages rapidly; integrate with backend databases

Caller authentication; self‑service transactions; multi‑language support

Quality Management

Real‑time monitoring of interactions, queues, agents

100% or selective voice/screen recording; remote playback

Quality Management forms, scorecards, evaluators; multiple scoring systems

Call search, tagging, flagging; trend analysis across channels

Speech analytics; archive retrieval; Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS)‑compliant encryption

Supervisor monitoring, targeted recording, and evaluation tools

Integration of metadata, attachments, transcripts (including Spanish translation)

Recording & Monitoring

Full multimedia recording (voice, chat) with metadata capture

Archive, playback, selective or random recording

FIPS‑compliant encryption; configurable business‑rule recording

Synchronization of audio + chat transcript; search across metadata

Export and movement of archived data; masking of sensitive information

Ability to pause/stop live recording; apply speech analytics

Storage Requirements

Capacity for 100% call recording + 20% screen capture

National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)‑compliant retention: 6 months online, 18‑month archive

Elastic storage; ability to move data between archive and online

Agent Features

Softphone/desk phone support with full call controls

Screen pops with customer/IVR data; toll‑free/local dialing

Real‑time visibility into blocked calls

Agent state management (ready/not ready, reason codes)

Local/global phone books; voicemail; customizable buttons

Free seating with preserved metrics

Remote & End‑User Training

Train‑the‑trainer model; comprehensive system training

Government‑approved curriculum; reusable training materials (videos, docs)

Virtual interactive training; semi‑annual updates

Data analysis to identify training needs

Supervisor Tools

Real‑time team/agent status; silent monitoring; barge‑in/intercept

Alerts on agent events; escalation handling; assistance tools

Real‑time skill assignment changes; reason code management

Custom dashboards; reporting across all channels

Broadcast messaging; whisper coaching; filtering and alerts

Extensive reporting, drill‑down, and permission‑based access

Agent & Supervisor Desktop Tools

Softphone with call control, agent state changes, reason codes

Performance statistics; multi‑line appearance

Manager views: team status, forced state changes, reassignments

Web Chat – Core Capabilities

Secure chat integrated with ITSM; multi‑session handling

Transcripts, history, attachments, pre‑defined responses

Routing by business rules/skills; supervisor monitoring/coaching

Surveys; chat bot escalation; KM integration

Queueing, after‑hours messaging, browser compatibility

Workforce Management (WFM)

Forecasting (long/short‑term), scheduling, and “what‑if” modeling

Real‑time and historical adherence monitoring

Multi‑site, multi‑skill planning; scheduling templates

Intra‑day management (reassign, overflow, call‑flow changes)

Reporting on adherence, staffing, agent activities

Virtual Call Queue

Automatic callback with estimated wait times and retries

Maintain queue place when caller opts for callback

Configurable triggers (queue depth, wait time, time of day)

Voicemail message capability; multi‑timezone support

Reporting: successful/failed callbacks, usage, wait‑time accuracy

Automated Call Survey

Post‑call and delayed call‑back surveys (DTMF/voice)

Recording of comments; scoring, weighting, configuration

Reporting (15‑minute segments to yearly); trend and statistics

Linking results to interactions and agents; export tools

Text (SMS)

SMS chat with live agent; attachments; automated replies

Routing by business rules, keywords, and phone number

Ability to shift from automated to live agent without losing context

Confirmation texts; integration with VA Knowledge Base

Computer Telephony Integration (CTI)

Screen pops with caller and history data

Integration with third‑party systems via standard APIs

Support for intelligent routing, call control, reporting, blending

Screen Capture

Capture agent activity across multimedia interactions

Secure, FIPS‑compliant capture, storage, search, playback, export, and archival of interactions with full metadata

Configurable business rules controlling when and how interactions and screens are recorded

Real‑time authorized monitoring of agent screens

Enhanced Features

Web Callback functionality with agent‑availability–based routing and full VA operating‑hours integration

AI‑driven features including AutoPilot (voice assistant), Agent Co‑Pilot, and Supervisor Co‑Pilot

Full integration with ServiceNow, Medallia, reporting tools, and major‑incident IVR workflows

Advanced authentication and security enhancements including one‑time passcodes (SMS/IVR) and voice biometrics

3.2 Future Capabilities

The CCI solution requires integration, testing, and implementation of VA approved Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), Robotic Process Automation (RPA), Speech/Sentiment Analytics or third-party applications into the CCI solution using industry standard APIs and repeatable, agile software and system engineering processes. In addition the CCI solution is anticipated to expand into the following future capabilities:

Conversational AI (Voice & Chat)	

Natural language voice bots and chatbots

Multilingual conversational AI

Intent recognition with contextual memory

Dynamic conversation flow generation

Voice AI with natural prosody and interruption handling

AI Intent-Based Routing	

Routes contacts based on detected intent, sentiment, customer value and/or agent expertise

Predictive Routing	

Optimizes for CSAT, FCR and AHT

Journey-Aware Routing	

Considers prior interactions, unresolved tickets, open incidents and channel switching

Proactive and Predictive Service	

Proactive outreach notifying customers of outages, proactive service alerts and AI-triggered outreach when behavior indicates trouble

Predictive issue resolution when detecting potential service failures and automatically opening tickets or contacting customers

Workforce Engagement Management	

AI forecasting of contact volumes

Automated scheduling

Skill-based workforce planning

Real-time adherence monitoring

Agent coaching recommendations

Hyper-Personalized Customer Experience	

Customer identity resolution

Interaction history

Behavioral signals

Real-time personalization

Intelligent Process Automation	

Auto ticket creation

Backend task automation

System integration workflows

Automated service fulfillment

Knowledge Intelligence	

AI-generated Knowledge Articles

Knowledge GAP detection

Auto-recommendation during calls (within IVR and for agents)

Knowledge effectiveness scoring

Automatic knowledge creation from resolved cases

Real-Time Operational Intelligence	

Real-time service dashboards

Anomaly detection

Predictive workload forecasting

Customer journey analytics

Root cause identification

Integrated Service Management	

Ticket creation from calls

IVR context passed into tickets

Automated updates between systems

Incident correlation

Service status awareness

Simulation-Based Agent Training	

AI-generated training scenarios

Simulated customer interactions

Automated feedback

Experience Orchestration 	

Customer journey orchestration

Cross-system workflow execution

Real-time decision engines

Adaptive service workflows

4.0 Submittal Instructions

All responsible sources may submit a capability statement in accordance with the information below. Interested parties shall provide a maximum of five-pages briefly addressing their recommended solution and approach. All responses should at minimum provide: 

Recommended approach to maintaining, enhancing, and/or replacing ECO’s current CCI solution and associated benefits/risks to this approach

Flexible cost-effective Firm-Fixed-Price licensing models for the CCI solution to support fluctuating helpdesk agents and other users

Strategic opportunities for future capabilities and enhancements

Recommended contract vehicle, 

In addition, all responses should list your company business size and status.

Responses are due no later than 10:00AM ET Monday, May 4, 2026  via email to Dan DiNocera, Contract Specialist, at Daniel.dinocera@va.gov; Andrea Caltabilota, Contract Specialist, at Andrea.Caltabilota@va.gov; and Evan Schlisserman, Contracting Officer, at Evan.Schlisserman@va.gov.  Please submit all responses via email using the subject line “ECO CCI Solution- Capability Statement” and provide a Point of Contact, if needed.

1 
PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
 
Office of the Chief Information Officer (OCIO) Cemetery Enterprise Application Support 
(CEAS) 
 
PART 1 
GENERAL INFORMATION 
 
1.  General:  This is a non-personnel service contract to provide Cemetery Enterprise Application 
Support (CEAS) to the Office of the Chief Information Officer (OCIO) for Army National 
Military Cemeteries (ANMC) and the Office of Army Cemeteries (OAC).  The OCIO CEAS is 
responsible for support for the following areas, project management, change management and 
governance, and Enterprise Architecture (EA) and OCIO strategic planning support.  This is not 
a software development contract. 
 
1.1  Description of Services/Introduction:  The Contractor shall provide all personnel, 
equipment, supplies, facilities, transportation, tools, materials, supervision, and other items and 
non-personnel services necessary to perform the tasks as defined in this PWS except, for those 
items specified as government furnished property and services.  Contractor shall perform to the 
standards defined in this contract. 
 
1.2  Background: The Office of Army Cemeteries (OAC) is defined by Public Law as the 
statutory authority to manage and oversee operations at Arlington National Cemetery, the United 
States Soldiers’ and Airmen’s Home National Cemetery.  The Department of the Army created a 
special staff Principal, the Executive Director for Army Cemeteries and a policy oversight 
directorate and compliance for all Army cemeteries, both post cemeteries and national 
cemeteries.  In accordance with this law, this PWS outlines the contractor responsibilities in 
providing program management support, defining and establishing business processes, 
developing, implementing and sustaining plans for operations and maintenance for ANMC/OAC 
business systems which contain Department of War Cemetery data.  Additionally, the contractor 
shall provide enterprise architecture support in accordance with the DOW Architecture 
Framework.  Based on the special significance and the attention that national and post cemeteries 
receive, strict adherences to these specifications are essential. 
 
1.3  Objectives: The objective of this effort is to provide program management support across the 
evolving Cemetery Management suite of cloud applications.  The basic services in this PWS 
shall enable the Project Management Office to manage the development and operations of IT 
based systems to conduct cemetery operations and oversee the Change Control Board (CCB) and 
Enterprise Architecture Governance Board (EAGB).  These services shall be leveraged to align 
IT investments with ANMC/OAC strategic goals and objectives outlined in the ANMC Goals 
and Objectives and OAC Strategic Plan. 
 
1.4  Scope: The Contractor shall provide specialized professional expertise and program 
management support to implement the goals of the Government and OCIO operations and 
management.  The Government intends to acquire contract support to all activities outlined in 
this Performance Work Statement in support of the Army’s current and future goals and 

 
 2 
objectives for program level planning, operations and maintenance, and migrations.  The scope 
and project shall contain tasks and milestones to allow for efficient planning and budgeting for 
IT solutions. Scope will include inter-agency coordination with external cemetery stakeholders to 
include but not limited to Depmant of War Mortuaty and Casulaity Affairs, American Battle 
Monuments Commission (ABMC) and National Cemetery Administration (NCA) Veterans 
Affairs (VA).  
 
1.5 Period of Performance:  The period of performance shall be a base twelve (12) months with  
three (3) twelve (12) month options from date of award.  Option periods may be exercised in 
accordance with FAR Clause 52.217-9 entitled, “Option to Extend the Term of the Contract”. 
The period of performance reads as follows: 
 
Base Year:   19 Novmeber 2026 – 18 November 2027    
Option Year I:  19 Novmeber 2027 – 18 November 2028   
Option Year II: 19 Novmeber 2028 – 18 November 2029 
Option Year III:  19 Novmeber 2029 – 18 November 2030   
 
1.6  General Information 
1.6.1  Quality Control: Quality control is the responsibility of the contractor.  The contractor is 
responsible for the delivery of quality services/supplies to the government (see FAR 52.246-1, 
Contractor Inspection Requirements). 
The contractor shall develop, implement and maintain an effective quality control system that 
includes a written quality control plan (QCP).  The QCP shall implement standardized 
procedure/methodology for monitoring and documenting contract performance to ensure all 
contract requirements are met.  The contractors’ QCP must contain a systematic approach to 
monitor operations to ensure acceptable services/products are provided to the government.   The 
QCP, as a minimum, shall address continuous process improvement; procedures for scheduling, 
conducting and documentation of inspection; discrepancy identification and correction; 
corrective action procedures to include procedures for addressing government discovered non-
conformances; procedures for root cause analysis to identify the root cause and root cause 
corrective action to prevent re-occurrence of discrepancies; procedures for trend analysis; and 
procedures for collecting and addressing customer feedback/complaints.  The contractor shall 
upon request provide to the government its quality control documentation The Contractor shall 
deliver the QCP within thirty (30) days after contract award.  QCP must address test plan 
development, release coordination with ANMC and its stakeholders, and code promotion 
documentation. After acceptance of the quality control plan the contractor shall receive the 
contracting officer’s acceptance in writing of any proposed change to its QC system.   
 
1.6.2  Quality Assurance:  The Government shall evaluate the contractor’s performance under 
this contract in accordance with the Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP).  This plan is 
primarily focused on what the Government must do to ensure that the contractor has performed 
in accordance with the performance standards.  It defines how the performance standards will be 
applied, the frequency of surveillance, and the minimum acceptable defect rate(s). 
 
 
 
 

 
 3 
1.6.3  Federal Government Holidays: ANMC and OAC observe the following Federal holidays: 
 
            New Year’s Day   1st day of January 
 Martin Luther King Jr. Day   3rd Monday of January 
 Presidents Day   3rd Monday of February 
 Memorial Day    Last Monday of May 
 Juneteenth Day   19th day of June 
 Independence Day    4th day of July 
 Labor Day    1st Monday of September 
 Columbus Day   2nd Monday of October 
 Veterans Day    11th day of November 
 Thanksgiving Day   4th Thursday of November 
 Christmas Day               25th day of December 
 
1.6.4  Hours of Operation: The Contractor is responsible for conducting business to best align 
with ANMC’s operating hours between 0600-1800 Eastern Standard Time, Monday through 
Friday, except federal holidays or when the Government facility is closed due to local or national 
emergencies, administrative closings, occupational safety or health hazards, or similar 
Government directed facility closings.  The Contractor shall maintain an adequate work force at 
all times for the uninterrupted performance of the tasks defined within this PWS when the 
Government facility is not closed for these reasons.  When hiring personnel, the Contractor shall 
keep in mind the stability and continuity of the workforce are essential. 
 
1.6.5  Place of Performance:  The primary place of performance shall be Arlington National 
Cemetery and Contractor facility. 
 
1.6.6  Type of Contract:  The Government anticipates award of a firm fixed price contract. 
 
1.6.7  Security Requirements: This is an Unclassified contract.  There are no requirements for 
Contractor personnel to have route access to classified Government information in the 
performance of this contract; however, Contractors with elevated privileges shall be required to 
have/maintain a SECRET clearance in order to access any environment(s) connecting or crossing 
Army or DOW Information systems.  If Contractor personnel are required to attend meetings, 
briefings or work on a full time basis on site at a secure Government facilities, a valid, current 
final SECRET eligibility and access must be granted and posted in the Joint Personnel 
Adjudication System (JPAS) to gain access. 
 
1.6.7.1  P hysical Security:  The contractor shall be responsible for safeguarding all government 
equipment, information and property provided for contractor use. At the close of each work period, 
government facilities, equipment, and materials shall be secured. 
1.6.7.2  Key, Key Code.  All references to keys include key cards or fobs (and also  implies any 
other government issued means of access to allow the contractor to enter any ANMC secured area 
or facility. AN MC shall issue key cards/codes to the Contractor (and subcontractor). The 
Contractor shall be responsible for keys and key codes provided by the Government.  Keys shall 
not be lost, misplaced, or duplicated by Contractor personnel, nor used by unauthorized Contractor 
personnel. The Contractor shall develop and implement procedures to ensure that keys issued to 

 
 4 
the Contractor by the Government are safeguarded. The Contractor developed procedures covering 
key control shall be included in the Quality Control Plan. Such procedures shall include turn-in of 
any issued keys by personnel who no longer require access to locked areas.  
The Contractor shall prohibit the use of Government issued keys/key cards by any persons other 
than the Contractor’s employees. The Contractor shall prohibit the opening of locked areas by 
Contractor employees to permit entrance of persons other than Contr actor employees engaged in 
the performance of assigned work in those areas, or personnel authorized entrance by the 
Contracting Officer. 
The Contractor shall report any occurrence of duplicated, misplaced, or lost keys to the Contracting 
Officer and COR within twenty-four (24) hours after discovery of occurrence and submit a written 
report to the Contracting Officer by close of business the  next workday. The written report shall 
provide complete details relating to duplication, misplacement, and/or loss. 
1.6.7.2.1. In the event keys, other than master keys, are lost or duplicated, the c ontractor shall, 
upon direction of the contracting officer, re-key or replace the affected lock or locks; however, the 
government, at its option, may replace the affected lock or locks or perform re-keying.  When the 
replacement of locks or re -keying is performed by the government, the total cost of re -keying or 
the replacement of the lock or locks shall be deducted from the monthly payment due the 
contractor.  In the event a master key is lost or duplicated, all locks and keys for that system shall 
be replaced by the government and the total cost deducted from the monthly payment due the 
contractor. 
 
1.6.7.2.2. The contractor shall prohibit the use of government issued keys/key cards by any 
persons other than the contractor’s employees.  The contractor shall prohibit the opening of 
locked areas by contractor employees to permit entrance of persons other than contractor 
employees engaged in the performance of assigned work in those areas, or personnel authorized 
entrance by the contracting officer. 
 
1.6.7.3 Lock Combinations  The contractor shall establish and implement methods of ensuring that 
all lock combinations are not revealed to unauthorized persons.  The c ontractor shall ensure that 
lock combinations are changed when personnel having access to the combinations no longer have 
a need to know such combinations.  These procedures shall be included in the Contractor’s Quality 
Control Plan. 
 
1.6.8 Federal Facility Access, General Protection, Security Policies and Procedures. Contractor 
personnel (including subcontractors’ employees) entering government properties shall abide by 
the applicable installation, facility and area commander’s access and local policies and 
procedures.  The contractor shall provide required information for background checks to meet 
installation access requirements.  Contractor workforce shall also comply with all personal 
identity verification requirements as directed by DOW, HQDA, and/or local policy.  In addition 
to the changes clause otherwise authorized by this contract, should the Force Protection 
Condition Level (FPCON) or Health Protection Condition Level (HPCON) at any individual 
facility or installation change, the government may require changes in contractor security matters 
or processes.  Contractor employees, including sub-contractors, shall immediately report 
suspicious activity to the ANMC G2/3 - Directorate of Security and Operations at 703-614-4303 

 
 5 
during business hours, and 703-588-2800 during after-hours. Emergencies should be reported 
immediately by calling 911. 
 
ANMC/OAC Contractor personnel will be issued vehicle passes (hangtag/placards) that must be 
displayed when entering the Cemetery (regardless of whether the vehicle is privately or company 
owned, or as designated by ANMC G2/3 - Directorate of Security and Operations. The 
contractor shall maintain a current list of all vehicles and individuals issued such passes. The 
contractor shall provide a list of these vehicles to include make, model, color and associated 
license plate information to the COR within five (5) days of contract award and within one (1) 
day of any changes to the list through contract period of performance.   Additionally, contractors 
shall park in designated parking locations, as directed by ANMC, and if required, access/key 
cards shall be issued to Key Personnel to enter secured parking areas (see Section 1.6.7.2 Key 
and Key Control). 
 
1.6.9 Visit Notification.  Contractors visiting ANC shall provide 24 hour notice for all site-visits, 
provide vehicle information, and proper contractor identification may be required. Contractor 
employees whose duties require on site work at ANMC or OAC will be issued a photo ID badge 
and must be presented or visible to ANMC/OAC staff. 
 
1.6.10   Key Personnel and Special Qualifications:  The Government considers the Senior Project 
Manager (PM), Senior Management Analyst, and Enterprise Architect as Key Personnel.  Key 
Personnel identified in the proposal shall be identified in the proposal including resumes.  
Projected replacement of Key Personnel shall be submitted to the COR and KO for approval 
prior to a replacement.  All Key Personnel shall have means of verbal and email communication 
with the COR, as the Government anticipates communication, at a minimum twice daily.  Key 
Personnel shall be able to communicate fluently in

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
                          SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
 
 1 
 
1.0 OVERVIEW 
 
1.1   INTRODUCTION AND BACKGROUND 
 
The Office of Safeguards (Safeguards) within Governmental Liaison, Disclosure and Safeguards 
(GLDS) function of Privacy, Governmental Liaison and Disclosure (PGLD) is responsible for 
ensuring that Federal Tax Information (FTI) provided outside of the IRS is secured and used 
appropriately. Safeguards assess approximately 300 federal, state and local agencies, as well as 
their authorized contractors receiving FTI under various sub-sections of Internal Revenue Code 
(IRC) §6103 and other IRC provisions. Agencies must implement appropriate safeguards to 
protect confidentiality and prevent unauthorized access or use of FTI. Safeguards works in 
partnership with the recipient agencies to identify and mitigate risks to FTI. The specific 
safeguard requirements are outlined in IRC §6103(p)(4) and detailed in Publication 1075, Tax 
Information Security Guidelines for Federal, State, and Local Agencies. 
 
Publication 1075 is primarily based upon National Institute of Standards & Technology (NIST) 
Special Publication (SP) 800-53, Assessing Security and Privacy Controls for Information 
Systems and Organizations for moderate risk systems, IRC §6103 statutory requirements and IRS 
policy and procedure. All automated information systems and networks which receive, process, 
store, transmit, or protect FTI must meet these information protection requirements. 
 
Current Contract Number 2032H5-23-F-00312  
Incumbent:  Sky Solutions, LLC. 
Approximate contract value:  $15,864,000.00 
 
2.0 OBJECTIVE  
 
The objective of this requirement is to increase security of FTI and to ensure consistent 
application of information security standards across all partner agency information systems. 
 
3.0   REQUIREMENT TASKS 
 
The mission of Safeguards is to promote taxpayer confidence in the integrity of the tax system by 
ensuring the confidentiality of IRS information provided to federal, state, and local agencies.  
Safeguards verifies compliance with IRC §6103(p)(4) safeguard requirements through the 
identification and mitigation of any risk of loss, breach, or misuse of FTI held by external 
government agencies. 
 
The Contractor shall provide cybersecurity and information technology (IT) expertise. The 
Contractor shall optimize its processes, to reduce costs and minimize risk to FTI in possession of 
agency partners, while continuing to meet all regulatory and agency documented standards and 
guidance; and obtain information technology expertise and ancillary support to enable Safeguards 
to meet on-going and new regulatory objectives having an impact on the Safeguards Program. 
 
The Contractor shall collaborate with IRS leadership, staff, and other contractors in support of 
agency computer security reviews. Support shall include the following: conducting preparatory 
agency outreach; conducting pre-review research and analysis to determine scope and potential 
areas of concern; executing automated and manual computer security compliance scans of partner 
agency systems during the review; preparing reports, documenting scan results and other 

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
                          SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
 
 2 
computer security findings; and analyzing and responding to various partner agency computer 
security information submissions. Other computer security review-related support includes 
stakeholder outreach, risk-based modeling for agency review selection, computer security review 
methodology updates, and general IT expertise. The Contractor shall provide ad hoc support for 
the assessment of other projects, pilots, and new legislation that include provisions falling within 
the purview of Safeguards. 
 
In accordance with Executive Order (EO) 14179, the contractor is permitted to use Artificial 
Intelligence (AI) to assist in this task.  
 
The Contractor shall adhere to IRS Publication 1075 (Tax Information Security Guidelines for 
Federal, State, and Local Agencies), Federal Security Modernization Act (FISMA), Privacy Act 
of 1974, Internal Revenue Code  §6103, NIST Risk Management Framework and IRS Safeguards 
Program requirements when utilizing artificial intelligence tools to enhance technical support 
capabilities assisting in the management and updates of Safeguards Computer Security 
Evaluation Matrices (SCSEM’s) and associated automated evaluation files.  
 
Contractors shall not process, store, or have access to FTI, PII, SSI, Classified or controlled 
unclassified information and any agency specific confidential data.  
 
The Contractor shall utilize only publicly available information from approved sources including 
IRS Publication 1075, NIST Special Publications and Standards, IRS Safeguard Program public 
website materials, Federal regulations and guidance documents and publicly available industry 
best practice standards to include Center for Internet Security (CIS) Benchmarks and Defense 
Information Systems Agency (DISA) Secure Technical Implementation Guides (STIG). All 
queries submitted must contain no FTI or PII; referenced only publicly available information, 
comply with informational handling classifications and be reviewed for compliance prior to 
submission. 
 
3.1   TASK 1- ORIENTATION BRIEFING  
 
The contractor shall facilitate an orientation briefing via a video conference call (Microsoft (MS) 
Teams, Zoom, etc.) with its representatives, ensuring all parties have a common understanding of 
the task order tasks and requirements. The contractor shall bring, at a minimum, its key personnel 
to this meeting. 
 
3.2   TASK 2, RAMP-UP KNOWLEDGE TRANSFER/TRANSITION-IN  
  
3.2.1 RAMP-UP KNOWLEDGE 
 
Ramp-Up Knowledge Transfer is defined as the contractor acquiring and understanding the 
necessary documentation, information, and processes related to any work in progress. The 
Government anticipates this task will be performed in conjunction with the Transition-In 
activities after the award of this task order.  
 
The contractor shall appoint a knowledge transfer manager serving as key personnel to oversee all 
areas of information that are accounted for and all knowledge of information is transferred from 
the current incumbent. This can be the Project Manager (PM) or other approved key personnel. 

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
                          SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
 
 3 
Prior to the transfer of knowledge, the contractor shall develop a final Knowledge Transfer Plan 
(KTP). The final KTP shall identify the contractor resources and roles involved in the transfer 
process, provide a list of risks and mitigation strategies for the transfer, and contain a detailed 
resource balanced project schedule developed with Microsoft Project or compatible equivalent. 
The project schedule shall identify tasks, dependencies, deliverables, and milestones. The project 
schedule shall be at a sufficient level of detail to track progress on a bi-weekly basis. 
 
 
The contractor shall meet with the IRS bi-weekly (twice a month) to discuss the progress of the 
knowledge transfer. The contractor shall show an updated MS Project Schedule or compatible 
equivalent and discuss any risks or concerns at this time. 
 
In addition to the Ramp-Up activities in the approved KTP, the contractor shall perform the 
following activities during the Ramp Up: 
 
• Assign personnel and submit security background packages within 10 calendar 
days to ensure background investigations are completed efficiently and gain 
access to IRS’s network.  
• Create a project schedule at a sufficient level of detail to track progress on a 
weekly basis. 
• Request access within 2 business days to tools and documentation (based on 
                          successful adjudication of background clearance of contractor personnel) 
• Review and understand all the technical and process documentation.  
• Review and understand the case management system. 
•           Knowledge Transfer/Transition-In Status Reports, for the duration of  
                   the transition-in period only, shall detail status of activities identified  
                   in the final KTP. 
 
Note: Completed background requests shall be sent to IRS COR/Alternate COR via encrypted 
electronic file. Any incomplete packages will be rejected for re-submission within 2 workdays. 
 
The contractor shall provide a KTP and a MS project or compatible equivalent schedule that is 
accepted by the Government and successfully completes knowledge transfer in accordance with 
the plan with a total transition-in project schedule not to exceed one hundred twenty (120) 
calendar days after Government acceptance of the KTP.  In addition, the Contractor shall provide 
complete and accurate status reports for the KTP. 
 
3.2.2 TRANSITION- IN  
 
The Transition-In task permits an effective transition from the incumbent Contractor to the new 
Contractor. The Government estimates this task will take approximately 120 calendar days to 
complete from date of award of the task order. Successful transition can be defined by its capacity 
to maintain full continuity of operations. To ensure a successful, low-risk transition, the 
contractor shall follow the approved Transition-In Plan and keep the government fully informed 
of status throughout the transition-in period. During the transition-in period, the incumbent is 
responsible for performance. This Task would be worked concurrently with Task 2, Ramp-Up 
Knowledge Transfer.  
 

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
                          SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
 
 4 
In addition to the transition activities in the approved KTP, the contractor shall ensure the 
following activities have been successfully completed within 75 days of the transition-in period to 
ensure timely commencement of services after the 120-Day Transition-In period: 
 
1. Obtain final access to tools and documentation 
2. Comprehend and successfully perform all the technical and process documentation. 
3. Comprehend and successfully apply the existing data and system security policies in 
place. 
 
The Transition-in Checklist provides a vehicle to verify completion of the necessary transition-in 
activities. The Transition-in Checklist shall be developed by the Contractor and shall include all 
necessary activities to ensure an effective transition from the incumbent Contractor to a new 
Contractor that were performed. The Transition-in Checklist shall be signed by both, the 
Contractor and the COR after completion of the task. 
 
3.3   TASK 3, TASK MANAGEMENT  
 
3.3.1 General Requirements 
The Contractor shall provide personnel, including an equivalent contractor Subject Matter Expert 
(SME), to perform the requirements set forth herein. 
3.3.2 Monthly Status Report (MSR) Call 
The Contractor shall facilitate a Monthly Status Report (MSR) Call that includes presentation 
materials, such as a slide deck and supporting documentation, outlining expenditures, billings, 
progress, status, and any problems or issues encountered during task performance. The MSR shall 
include financial projections and an overview of support provided during the previous invoice 
period, including a list of all deliverables accepted and those in progress. All MSR materials and 
activities are subject to review and acceptance by the Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) 
and the Technical Point of Contact (TPOC). 
3.3.3 Task Order Program Management Plan (PMP) 
The Contractor shall develop, implement, and maintain a Task Order Program Management Plan 
(PMP), subject to Government review and approval, manage, track, and evaluate performance 
throughout the period of performance. The PMP shall include control policies and procedures 
consistent with standard industry practices for project administration, execution, and tracking. 
The PMP may consist of multiple components, including, a project plan and performance status 
dashboards. The PMP shall include a consolidated roll-up of performance data for all active 
subtasks and shall be structured to support reuse for MSR Calls and other reporting requirements. 
The Contractor shall maintain the PMP in accordance with agreed-upon project management best 
practices. The PMP is subject to review and acceptance by the COR and TPOC. 
 
3.3.4 Administrative Touchpoint 
The Contractor shall attend and facilitate a weekly Administrative Touchpoint meeting, not to 
exceed one (1) hour in duration, as scheduled by the COR. The purpose of this meeting shall be to 
address onboarding activities, Government Furnished Equipment (GFE), and other administrative 

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
                          SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
 
 5 
matters. The Contractor shall prepare, update, and present a performance status dashboard that 
identifies completed deliverables, timeliness of performance, and any deliverables returned due to 
deficient quality. These meetings and associated materials are subject to review by the COR and 
TPOC. 
 
3.3.5 Annual Planning Meeting 
The Contractor shall schedule, coordinate, and facilitate an Annual Planning Meeting with the 
COR, and the TPOC to discuss planned activities for the upcoming option year. The Contractor 
shall prepare and submit meeting materials to the COR for review and approval in advance of the 
meeting.  
 
3.4   TASK 4, COMPUTER SECURITY REVIEWS  
 
The Contractor shall work with external partner agency stakeholders to execute system 
configuration checks, interview system users (e.g., administrators), and to examine system 
artifacts (including policies and procedures) against management, operational and technical 
requirements, Center for Internet Security (CIS )Benchmarks, NIST requirements, and current 
IRS standards (e.g., IRS Publication 1075, IRC §6103). The scope of the assessment (“review”) 
will include all hardware and software associated with the processing, storing, or exchange of FTI 
including mainframes, operating system, telecom devices, applications, multi-function devices, 
firewalls, servers, cloud infrastructure, and databases. The contractor shall use the Safeguards 
Computer Security Evaluation Matrices (SCSEMs) and automated evaluation files as a guide to 
perform Computer Security Reviews on local, state or federal agencies, and their authorized 
contractors, that receive FTI under IRC §6103.  
Completion of computer security reviews resulted in the Safeguard review report (SRR) being 
issued to the external partner agency. The SRR consists of Sections A through H. Sections A 
through G are completed by the IRS staff and Section H dealing with information technology is 
completed by the contractor.  The SRR serves to identify an agency’s compliance with their

1 
 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
 
 
Request for CollaboraƟon (RFC) 
 
2028 Dress Rehearsal and 2030 Census 
Decennial as a Service (D-X) 
April 14, 2026 
 
 
(REVISED – APRIL 21, 2026*) 
 
This revised RFC removes all Help Desk related scope and terminology. References to incidents, 
Ɵckets, case management, HR or Safety, and other ITSM concepts have been eliminated to align 
its D-X requirement with its intended logisƟcs and XaaS-domain focus. These removals are 
reﬂected in the updated document. 
 
*All changes are made using Track Changes. 
 
 
 
 
Version 2.0 
 

 
2 
 
Purpose of This Follow-up Request for CollaboraƟon 
The U.S. Census Bureau (USCB) is issuing this Request for CollaboraƟon (RFC) to follow on the 
insights gained from the iniƟal Decennial-as-a-Service (D-X) Request for InformaƟon (RFI)  SAM 
noƟce #13PDRD25Q00000007 on 8/14/25, to gather addiƟonal industry input on areas not fully 
addressed in the original RFI responses. As we conƟnue reﬁning our acquisiƟon strategy, the 
USCB remains commiƩed to transparency, open communicaƟon, and meaningful collaboraƟon 
with industry partners. Accordingly, this RFC is designed to: 
 Address capability areas not fully addressed in the original RFI 
 Validate assumpƟons and operaƟonal models held by the government 
 Encourage industry to share best pracƟces, risks, and integraƟon strategies 
 Strengthen alignment between government expectaƟons and commercial capabiliƟes 
 Support a more informed, evidence-based acquisiƟon approach 
The detailed scope and operaƟonal descripƟons provided in the iniƟal D-X RFI conƟnue to serve as the baseline for 
this RFC. Please consider that informaƟon when assessing capability gaps or dependencies. 
The Census Bureau values industry experƟse and seeks to ensure that the D-X soluƟon is shaped 
through open dialogue, shared understanding, and collaboraƟve problem-solving. 
This RFC seeks industry partners capable of delivering a single fully integrated, naƟonwide 
equipment and endpoint lifecycle management ecosystem, operaƟng under a single-integrator 
model, to support logisƟcs, equipment handling, cellular services, site deployments, and 
operaƟonal support funcƟons necessary for the 2028 Dress Rehearsal and 2030 Decennial 
Census operaƟonal readiness across all states and U.S. territories. 
BACKGROUND  
The objecƟve of the D-X program is to deliver a complete, as-a-service soluƟon for centralized 
management of standard equipment and mobile devices that ensures cybersecurity 
compliance, operaƟonal eﬃciency, cost transparency, and end-to-end lifecycle accountability, 
while meeƟng all schedule milestones for the 2028 Dress Rehearsal and 2030 Decennial Census, 
including site deployment and teardown Ɵmelines, mobile device deployment cycles, ATO 
approvals, and system go-live dates.  The D-X soluƟon must be a turnkey, FedRAMP-authorized, 
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5-compliant, ATO-authorized, as-a-service operaƟonal ecosystem, 
coordinated under a single-integrator model, and including but not limited to the following 
capabiliƟes: 
 
 Devices, equipment and asset management services 

 
3 
 
o End-to-end management of all standard equipment and devices provided as-a-
service, including but not limited to mobile devices, peripherals, networking gear, 
printers/MFPs, and paper data capture equipment 
o Mobile device provisioning, conﬁguraƟon, and deployment 
o Equipment maintenance, repair, replacement and compliant disposiƟon 
o Inventory accuracy, chain-of-custody tracking, and real-Ɵme asset visibility 
 LogisƟcs, Warehousing, and Deployment OperaƟons 
o Warehousing (secure and nonsecure), kiƫ ng, staging, and order fulﬁllment for 
mobile devices and other equipment, with support for ﬂexible delivery opƟons 
such as informed delivery to individual residences, delivery rerouƟng, and a 
range of alternaƟve delivery methods proposed by industry to meet large-scale, 
distributed Census operaƟonal needs. 
o Shipping, returns, and reverse-logisƟcs workﬂows that incorporate ﬂexible 
delivery and return capabiliƟes, including informed delivery noƟﬁcaƟons, 
delivery rerouƟng, scheduled pickup windows, carrier-agnosƟc drop-oﬀ opƟons, 
and other alternaƟve delivery/return methods appropriate for naƟonwide and 
U.S. territory based ﬁeld operaƟons, supporƟng deliveries to and from ﬁeld 
oﬃces, regional census oﬃces, satellite operaƟonal sites, and individual 
employees’ residences across all states and U.S. territories. 
o Support for site deployments across all states and U.S. territories, including 
remote and island locaƟons, including installaƟon of vendor supplied equipment, 
peripherals, and other site-based infrastructure required to establish fully 
operaƟonal ﬁeld oﬃces. 
 Mobility, Uniﬁed Endpoint Management (UEM), and Cellular Services 
o UEM for device conﬁguraƟon, compliance, patching, and security baselines, and 
cross-plaƞorm governance to ensure that all mobile devices, regardless of 
operaƟng system or management plaƞorm, follow the same security baselines, 
conﬁguraƟon standards, compliance requirements, and lifecycle controls. 
 Mobile Device Management (MDM) for device enrollment, policy 
enforcement, compliance monitoring, and lifecycle controls 
o CoordinaƟon of mobile device provisioning throughout deployment cycles, 
including device setup, and readiness checks to ensure assets are fully 
operaƟonal. 
o Cellular service acƟvaƟon, eSIM management, coverage opƟmizaƟon, and surge 
capacity 
o IntegraƟon of mobility, idenƟty, and security baselines across all devices 
 
 Security, Compliance, and ConƟnuous Monitoring 

 
4 
 
o FedRAMP-authorized, NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5-compliant hosƟng and security 
controls, with conƟnuous monitoring and reporƟng aligned to federal 
requirements 
o ConƟnuous monitoring, vulnerability management, and incident response 
coordinaƟon 
o IntegraƟon of mobility, idenƟty, and endpoint security baselines across all 
devices and plaƞorms 
o Incident detecƟon, escalaƟon, and Tier 3 security response coordinated with 
Census security operaƟons 
o Secure data handling, media saniƟzaƟon, and compliant disposiƟon processes 
 OperaƟonal Support, Maintenance, and ITSM IntegraƟon 
o Tier 3 technical support and engineering-level issue resoluƟon integrated with 
the Government’s ITSM plaƞorm 
o Incident, request, and change management workﬂows aligned to Census 
processes and cross-vendor escalaƟon paths 
o Performance monitoring, SLA tracking, and escalaƟon governance across all XaaS 
service areas 
 ReporƟng, AnalyƟcs, and AutomaƟon 
o Real-Ɵme dashboards for inventory, logisƟcs, deployments, cellular usage, and 
support performance 
o PredicƟve analyƟcs for demand planning, device health, and operaƟonal 
readiness 
o AutomaƟon to streamline provisioning, updates, and service workﬂows 
 Program, Vendor, and Contract Management 
o Centralized coordinaƟon under a single-integrator model 
o Governance model that oversees and coordinates all Everything-as-a-Service 
providers and government systems 
o Risk management, performance oversight, and conƟnuous improvement 
Transparency and CollaboraƟon 
 This noƟce is part of our broader eﬀort to build a collaboraƟve, transparent, and 
industry-informed acquisiƟon environment for D-X. The Census Bureau may work to 
support a fair, informed, and well-aligned acquisiƟon process through the following: 
Publishing relevant operaƟonal plans, schedules, milestones, and mission constraints, as 
available, to help industry tailor responses to the realiƟes of Census ﬁeld operaƟons on 
the 2030 Decennial Census Website (hƩps://www.census.gov/programs-
surveys/decennial-census/decade/2030/2030-census-main.html). 

 
5 
 
 Engaging in two-way, iteraƟve communicaƟon, including follow-up quesƟons, reverse 
vendor days, and other collaboraƟve forums, to explore ideas, clarify concepts, and 
reﬁne approaches with industry when possible. 
 InviƟng industry feedback on feasibility, risks, and alternaƟves, and ensuring vendors 
have equal access to the informaƟon needed to develop well-informed responses. 
AddiƟonal InformaƟon for Industry 
The following areas were not fully addressed in the original RFI and require input to support 
planning, integraƟon, and acquisiƟon strategy development. 
For the purposes of this RFC, “Everything-as-a-Service (XaaS)” refers to a fully managed, subscripƟon-based service 
model in which vendors deliver the tools, technology, processes, people, and outcomes required to support Census 
operaƟons. This includes OEM-agnosƟc soluƟons, integrated service delivery, predictable pricing, and end-to-end 
lifecycle management across mulƟple service domains. The Census Bureau uses XaaS to describe a uniﬁed 
ecosystem of managed services that collecƟvely supports the 2028 Dress Rehearsal and 2030 Census. 
Managed Service Integrator (MSI) Model 
The USCB is considering a single-integrator model in which one Managed Service Integrator 
(MSI) provides end-to-end orchestraƟon across all D-X service domains. This MSI would 
coordinate mulƟple XaaS providers and ensure uniﬁed governance, interoperability, and 
accountability across logisƟcs, mobility, devices, warehousing, asset management, cellular 
services, site deployments, and support operaƟons. The USCB is seeking industry insight into 
MSI models, governance structures, and best pracƟces for managing a large, mulƟ-vendor 
ecosystem under a single-integrator construct. 
AssumpƟons: 
 Demonstrated experience coordinaƟng many diﬀerent operaƟonal areas, such as 
logisƟcs, devices, mobility, cellular services, site deployments, and support, so they 
funcƟon as one integrated service. 
 Proven tools and processes to keep all systems and vendors aligned, sharing accurate 
data, and working together smoothly across the enƟre operaƟon. 
 Can manage and support Decennial Census operaƟons across all states and U.S. 
territories, including remote and island locaƟons, making sure equipment, deployments, 
logisƟcs, cellular services, and support all work together smoothly at naƟonwide scale. 
 Takes responsibility for delivering consistent, end-to-end service outcomes across all 
operaƟonal areas, not just managing individual vendors 

 
6 
 
Command Center AssumpƟons 
The D-X vision includes a Command Center that provides a real-Ɵme operaƟonal cross-domain 
layer that provides conƟnuous visibility, coordinated response, and cross-domain decision 
support across the D-X ecosystem. It uniﬁes data from logisƟcs, asset management, endpoint 
management, cellular services, and help desk other XaaS-domain workﬂows into a single 
operaƟonal view, enabling leadership and operaƟons teams to monitor service health, track 
incidents and requests across departments track cross-domain condiƟons and workﬂow acƟvity, 
and take acƟon without switching between systems. 
AssumpƟons: 
 Provides a uniﬁed operaƟonal picture that enables real-Ɵme monitoring of service 
health, incident trendsservice-tower condiƟons, and cross-domain workﬂow status 
across all D-X service areas XaaS domains. 
 Enables proacƟve operaƟonsmanagement, including endpoint health monitoring, 
vulnerability tracking, and ﬁeld service coordinaƟon, by leveraging integrated data and 
automated alerƟng across all Ɵers XaaS towers. 
 Supports coordinated incident response and case rouƟng acrossresponse and workﬂow 
rouƟng across IT, HR, Safety, LogisƟcs, and ﬁeld operaƟonsother D-X service domains, 
ensuring that leadership and operaƟonal teams can track progress and take acƟon from 
a single environment. 
 Operates on a common data foundaƟon that may support AI-driven automaƟon, 
allowing the system to autonomously resolve requests, execute mulƟ-step workﬂows, 
and complete end-to-end acƟons within established policies. 
Data Interoperability and IntegraƟon 
D-X will rely on a combinaƟon of mulƟple commercial and government systems working 
together seamlessly. To achieve this, the USCB must understand how vendors approach data 
integraƟon, API frameworks, synchronizaƟon, and real-Ɵme visibility across disparate plaƞorms, 
including those operated by federal partners. We are seeking industry insight into best 
pracƟces, integraƟon paƩerns, and potenƟal challenges associated with building a uniﬁed 
operaƟonal ecosystem that spans both commercial and government environments. 
AssumpƟons: 
 Integrates commercial and government systems into a uniﬁed operaƟonal ecosystem 
that supports seamless data exchange and coordinated service delivery. 
 Applies federal and commercial best pracƟces to ensure data quality, synchronizaƟon, 
and real-Ɵme visibility across diverse plaƞorms and partner environments. 
 Provides end-to-end integraƟon across logisƟcs, UEM, cellular, and asset management 
systems to enable consistent, mission-ready operaƟons. 

 
7 
 
OEM-AgnosƟc Device and Equipment As-a-Service Models 
The USCB is evaluaƟng OEM-agnosƟc as-a-service models for mobile devices (smartphones, 
tablets, laptops), oﬃce equipment (printers, mulƟfuncƟon devices), paper data-capture 
equipment (scanners, leƩer openers, guilloƟnes), and oﬃce networking equipment (access 
points, servers, and related infrastructure). These models may include brand-speciﬁc or 
equivalent conﬁguraƟons where required. The USCB seeks industry input on how such 
as-a-service oﬀerings are typically structured commercially, including provisioning approaches, 
service levels, lifecycle services, refresh strategies, and performance management. The USCB 
also requests informaƟon on risks, dependencies, and constraints that should be considered 
when implemenƟng an enterprise-scale, mulƟ-vendor as-a-service model. 
AssumpƟons: 
 Bundles diverse equipment categories, including mobile devices, oﬃce and paper data 
capture equipment, scanners, and networking hardware, into a uniﬁed, scalable, 
OEM-agnosƟc program that delivers provisioning, kiƫ ng, and lifecycle services. 
 Predictable pricing, refresh cycles, and service-level commitments that are in alignment 
with other large-scale as-a-service programs, including break/ﬁx services, rapid 
replacement SLAs, inventory pooling, buﬀer stock strategies, and secure end-of-life 
disposiƟon. 
 Supports enterprise-scale device volumes, including approximately 360,000 
smartphones, 20,000 tablets, and 20,000 laptops, with the ability to surge or de-surge 
inventory and services based on operaƟonal tempo and ﬁeld deployment needs. 
 Provide cellular service for all mobile devices naƟonwide across all states and U.S. 
territories by leveraging the best-performing local carriers in each area, supported by 
performance monitoring, availability metrics, and rapid carrier remediaƟon pathways. 
 Support carrier ﬂexibility thro

Item 2. Enter title as it appears on data acquisition document cited in Item 
4. 
Item 3. Enter subtitle of data item for further definition of data item

Item 4. Enter Data Item Description (DID) number, military specification 
number, or military standard number listed in DoD 5010.12-L (AMSDL), or 
one-time DID number, that defines data content and format requirements. 
Item 5.  Enter reference to tasking in contract that generates requirement 
for the data item (e.g., Statement of Work paragraph number). 
Item 6.  Enter technical office responsible for ensuring adequacy of the 
data item.

by the Government. 
Item 8.  Specify requirement for approval of a draft before preparation of 
the final data item.

Item 11. Specify as-of date of data item, when applicable.

applicable. 
Item 14. Enter addressees and number of draft/final copies to be delivered 
to each addressee. Explain reproducible copies in Item 16. 
Item 15.  Enter total number of draft/final copies to be delivered. 
Item 16.  Use for additional/clarifying information for Items 1 through 15. 
Examples are: Tailoring of documents cited in Item 4; Clarification of 
submittal dates in Items 12 and 13; Explanation of reproducible copies in 
Item 14.; Desired medium for delivery of the data item. 
FOR THE CONTRACTOR 
Item 17. Specify appropriate price group from one of the following 
groups of effort in developing estimated prices for each data item 
listed on the DD Form 1423. 
a. Group I. Definition - Data which is not otherwise essential 
to the contractor's performance of the primary contracted effort 
(production, development, testing, and administration) but which is 
required by DD Form 1423. 
Estimated Price - Costs to be included under Group I are 
those applicable to preparing and assembling the data item in 
conformance with Government requirements, and the 
administration and other expenses related to reproducing and 
delivering such data items to the Government. 
b. Group II. Definition - Data which is essential to the 
performance of the primary contracted effort but the contractor is 
required to perform additional work to conform to Government 
requirements with regard to depth of content, format, frequency of 
submittal, preparation, control, or quality of the data item. 
Estimated Price - Costs to be included under Group II are 
those incurred over and above the cost of the essential data item 
without conforming to Government requirements, and the 
administrative and other expenses related to reproducing and 
delivering such data item to the Government. 
c. Group III. Definition - Data which the contractor must 
develop for his internal use in performance of the primary 
contracted effort and does not require any substantial change to 
conform to Government requirements with regard to depth of 
content, format, frequency of submittal, preparation, control, and 
quality of the data item. 
Estimated Price - Costs to be included under Group III are 
the administrative and other expenses related to reproducing and 
delivering such data item to the Government. 
d. Group IV. Definition - Data which is developed by the 
contractor as part of his normal operating procedures and his effort 
in supplying these data to the Government is minimal. 
Estimated Price - Group IV items should normally be shown 
on the DD Form 1423 at no cost. 
Item 18.  For each data item, enter an amount equal to that portion 
of the total price which is estimated to be attributable to the 
production or development for the Government of that item of data. 
These estimated data prices shall be developed only from those 
costs which will be incurred as a direct result of the requirement to 
supply the data, over and above those costs which would 
otherwise be incurred in performance of the contract if no data 
were required. The estimated data prices shall not include any 
amount for rights in data. The Government's right to use the data 
shall be governed by the pertinent provisions of the contract.
