Full Text Clauses
7.6.4-01 - Inspection and Acceptance - July 2008
(a) All goods and services shall be subject to inspection and test by the FDIC Oversight Manager, to the extent
practicable, at all times and places during the term of the award.  All inspections by the FDIC shall be made in such
a manner as not to unduly delay the work.
 
(b) The FDIC shall have fifteen (15) business days from the date of Contractor''s delivery to determine if such goods
and services are in compliance with the requirements of the contract.  If any services performed or goods delivered
hereunder are not in conformity with the requirements of this Award, the FDIC shall have the right to require
Contractor to reperform the services or redeliver the goods in conformity with the requirements of the Award, at no
additional increase in total contract amount.  When the services to be performed are of such a nature that the defect
cannot be corrected by reperformance of the services, the FDIC shall have the right to (1) require Contractor
immediately to take all necessary steps to ensure future performance of the services in conformity with the
requirements of the contract; and (2) reduce the contract price to reflect the reduced value of the services
performed.  In the event Contractor fails promptly to reperform the services or redeliver the goods, or to take
necessary steps to ensure future performance of the services or delivery of the goods in conformity with the
requirements of the Award, the FDIC shall have the right to either (1) by contract or otherwise, have the services
performed or the goods delivered in conformity with the contract requirements and charge to Contractor any cost
occasioned to the FDIC that is directly related to the performance of such services or the delivery of such goods; or
(2) terminate this Award for default as provided in 7.6.6-02, Termination for Default.
 
(c) Contractor shall provide and maintain an inspection system acceptable to the FDIC covering the goods or
services to be delivered or performed hereunder.  Records of all inspection work by Contractor shall be kept
complete and available to the FDIC during the term of this Award and for such longer period as may be specified
elsewhere in this Award.

Full Text Clauses
7.3.2-41 - FDIC Personnel - July 2008
(a)  FDIC Oversight Manager.  The Oversight Manager is the person designated in writing by the Contracting
Officer to represent the FDIC for the purpose of monitoring technical performance and accepting goods or services.
The Oversight Manager is not authorized to issue any instructions or directions which effect any substantive change
in this contract, including, but not limited to, an increase or decrease in the price of this contract, or a change in the
delivery date(s) or Period of Performance.  Specific areas of delegated authority are more particularly defined in the
Oversight Manager Appointment Memorandum. 
 
The Oversight Manager is Duane Bouie. He can be reached at dbouie@fdic.gov.
 
 
(b)  FDIC Contracting Officer.  The Contracting Officer is the person with FDIC-delegated authority to enter into,
modify, administer, and terminate contracts and orders.
 
The Contracting Officer is Diamond Toles. She can be reached at ditoles@fdic.gov.
 
7.5.13-01 - Method of Payment - Electronic Fund Transfer (EFT) - March 2014
(a) Payment methods.  Payments by the FDIC may be made by check or electronic funds transfer (EFT), or by a
third party in lieu of payment directly from the FDIC, at the option of the FDIC. If the FDIC makes payment by EFT,
the FDIC may, at its option, also forward the associated payment information by electronic transfer.  Any third party
payments will be made by the FDIC''s commercial purchase card issuer.  In the event Contractor certifies in writing
to the payment office that Contractor does not have an account with a financial institution or an authorized payment
agent, the FDIC would make payments by other than EFT.
 
(b) Contractor Payment Requests.  If the FDIC elects for third party payments to be made, Contractor shall make
payment requests through a charge to the FDIC purchase card with the third party, at the time and for the amount
due in accordance with the terms of this contract.  Contractor and the third party shall agree that payments due
under this contract shall be made upon submittal of payment requests to the third party in accordance with the
terms and conditions of an agreement between Contractor, the Contractor''s financial agent (if any), and the third
party and its agents (if any).  No payment shall be due the Contractor until such agreement is made.   Payments
made or due by the third party are not subject to the Prompt Payment Act or any implementation thereof in this
contract.  Documentation of each charge against the FDIC''s purchase card shall be provided to the Contracting
Officer upon request.

Clauses:

Performance Work Statement

A. Introduction:  

The US Geological Survey (USGS), Denver Argon Geochronology Lab (DAGL), Denver, CO has a requirement for the automated operation of mass spectrometers and peripheral hardware (e.g. valves, lasers). The DAGL aims to achieve this by developing new, customized automation software modules built using a commercially available programming environment designed to enable construction of applications with graphical user interfaces for hardware automation, such as LabVIEW or an equivalent environment. This effort requires support for developing the overall application architecture, programming the custom automation software, and training laboratory staff in its construction, use, and maintenance.

B. Background: 

	

The US Geological Survey’s (USGS) Denver Argon Geochronology Lab (DAGL), located at the Geology, Geophysics, and Geochemistry Science Center (GGGSC), produces 40Ar/39Ar geochronology data for multiple USGS Programs, including the Mineral Resources Program and National Cooperative Geologic Mapping Program, among others. These data benefit a variety of stakeholders, from federal and state government agencies, to industry and academia.

The DAGL historically used a Macintosh-based, closed-source software application called Mass Spec for operating analytical instruments and associated peripheral hardware. Changes in USGS information technology (IT) regulations forced the DAGL to move away from Macintosh systems in the lab in 2022, and the developer of Mass Spec is nearing retirement and has indicated support will terminate. In response, the DAGL purchased a new open-source software application called Pychron that was being embraced by the 40Ar/39Ar community at that time. Although Pychron has allowed for intermittent operation of the lab, it has proven to not be sufficient for our needs. The Pychron developer is not regularly available for required bug fixes (which are abundant, especially following regular mandatory IT patching), and while the code is open-source, we have found the documentation is not sufficient for us to contribute meaningfully to code maintenance, and the lack of transparency of calculations has introduced significant issues for data management and quality.

The pivot to develop a customized LabVIEW (or equivalent)-based software application has the benefit of direct involvement by laboratory staff, enabling ongoing updates and improvements to be made after they are fully trained in the application. We aim to utilize an environment that represents a major software development environment and framework that is successfully deployed as customized applications in similar USGS labs (e.g. Denver Noble Gas lab, Moffett Argon Lab). We intend to build on the shoulders of these existing customized LabVIEW (or equivalent) applications to create new software specific to our analytical systems, but we do not currently have the expertise to do this.

C. Definitions:

40Ar/39Ar geochronology – an analytical method that involves the measurement of isotopes of argon in a material to determine a geologic age.

LabVIEW– a graphical programming environment and software framework provided for purchase by National Instruments that allows users to create custom applications (including graphical user interfaces) for hardware automation/control, and data collection.

D. Scope:

The USGS DAGL requires a complete LabVIEW (or equivalent)-based, custom software solution for acquiring data on the Thermo Scientific Argus VI mass spectrometers located within the DAGL. While developing the software, the Contractor shall actively involve laboratory personnel in the design and implementation of the new application(s) and simultaneously train them in the programming environment with a focus on components that will aid them in the long-term operation and maintenance of the software application(s). In addition to communicating with and operating the Argus mass spectrometers, the software application(s) will communicate with and/or control a variety of hardware peripherals, including Photon Machines lasers (and motorized sample stages), pressure gauges, ion pumps, Agilent DAQs, and thermocouples. The software application(s) will appropriately store analytical data and instrument metadata in files or a database, as needed by lab personnel. The software will integrate instrument/hardware communications and analytical data storage with required sample metadata for 40Ar/39Ar geochronology into an appropriate graphical user interface. 

E.  Technical Requirements.

The Contractor shall provide the following:

Basic Training. Train lab personnel in basic programming environment concepts and design patterns, with a focus on software components relevant to instrument control and data acquisition and storage.

Create modules in programming environment for communications with and control of:

Thermo Scientific Argus VI Mass Spectrometers

Pressure gauges

Ion pumps

Agilent DAQs for control of valves and readback of thermocouples

Photon Machines CO2 and UV laser systems

Create graphical user interface for sample metadata entry

Create module for data acquisition, to include:

Graphical user interface for building and executing run sequences 

Live instrument feedback (e.g., visuals of valve states and plots of data as they are acquired)

Create module for data storage to files and/or database

Create complete solution (including graphical user interface) for integrating the above modules

Lab personnel will be proficient in programming environment such that they can maintain and modify the resulting software application(s).

Modules will be accepted when they are able to control each piece of hardware.

User interface sufficiently captures sample metadata and stores in appropriate locations.

Data can be accurately and reliably acquired in an automated run sequence.

Data are accurately and reliably stored in appropriate file formats and/or database.

The complete solution is user-friendly and accessible to lab personnel. Source code will be version controlled and stored in one or more USGS DAGL GitLab repositories.

E. Performance standards:

If the Contractor fails to perform any task to the required technical standards, quality standards or time standards, the Government will notify the Contractor of the failure. The Contractor shall re-do or re-perform all substandard activities at no added cost to the Government.

September 2021v1  Page 1 of 2 
Department of Interior 
Presolicitation Notice 
 
Title: US Geological Survey (USGS) Earthquake 
Science Center (ESC) Science and Technical 
Support Services (ESATS) 
Announcement Number: 140G0326R0003 
Bureau: Geological Survey Response Date: Not Applicable 
I. DISCLAIMER 
 
Pre-Solicitation Notice – Science and Technical Support Services for the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS), 
Earthquake Science Center (ESC).  
 
The solicitation package—including the SF -1449, Statement of Work, and all required attachments—will 
be posted on SAM.gov | Contract Opportunities on or about May 6, 2026. 
 
This notice is issued solely for informational and planning purposes and does not constitute a solicitation.  
No responses are required at this time. 
 
 
II. PURPOSE 
 
The Department of the Interior (DOI), Office of the Secretary (OS), U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) 
Acquisition Team  intends to issue a Request for Proposal (RFP) for Solicitation No. 140G0326R0003. This 
acquisition will be solicited as a total Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business (SDVOSB) 
set-aside.  
 
The Government anticipates awarding a Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP), single-award, five-year Indefinite 
Delivery/Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract, with a planned start date of August 1, 2026.  
 
 NAICS / PSC Codes 
• NAICS: 541512 (size standard $34M) 
• PSC: DA01 
 
III. REQUIREMENTS 
 
The USGS requires mission-critical technical support with expertise in: 
• Geophysical monitoring network operations 
• Integrated scientific software and IT infrastructure 
• Ground-motion monitoring networks 
• Product generation, data delivery, and 24/7/365 system availability  
 
Supported programs include the Earthquake Notification System (ENS), Northern California Seismic 
Network (NCSN), Pacific Northwest Seismic Network (PNSN), Southern California Seismic Network 
(SCSN), and the USGS ShakeAlert® Earthquake Early Warning (EEW) Project.  
 
 


September 2021v1  Page 2 of 2 
The contractor will provide: 
• Customized scientific software/application development 
• Database development and administration 
• Systems administration 
• Seismic network engineering 
• DevOps engineering 
• Support for sensor networks, field telemetry, data-processing centers, and alert-distribution systems 
operated jointly by USGS and university partners across multiple regions 
 
IV. Government Point of Contact 
 
Contracting Officer: 
Charlan Fejarang 
Department of the Interior, Office of the Secretary 
Geological Survey Acquisition Team (GSAT) 
Email: charlan_fejarang@ios.doi.gov 
Ph: 916-278-9329

search_text:
Request for Information (RFI) for Enterprise Governance, Data Tagging, and Records Management Solution for Microsoft 365

1. GENERAL INFORMATION

Notice Type: Request for Information (RFI) / Sources Sought

RFI Number: RFI-ARMY-DTSPO-2026-0420

Issuing Office: Army Contracting Command – Aberdeen Proving Ground, Huachuca Division, Bravo Branch

NAICS Code: 541512 - Computer Systems Design Services

Date Issued: 20 April 2026

Response Due Date: 28 April 2026, 4:00 PM Arizona Time

DISCLAIMER: This Request for Information (RFI) is issued solely for information and planning purposes. It does not constitute a Request for Proposal (RFP) or a promise to issue an RFP in the future. This RFI does not commit the Government to contract for any supply or service whatsoever. The Government will not pay for any information or administrative costs incurred in response to this RFI. All information received in response to this RFI that is marked “Proprietary” will be handled accordingly.

2. BACKGROUND

The U.S. Army operates one of the largest and most complex Microsoft 365 (M365) environments in the world, presenting significant challenges for governance, security, and compliance. The Government requires a technical solution to enforce policy, automate administrative and records management tasks, provide hierarchical visibility and control, and empower a delegated administration model across Major Commands (MACOMs) and subordinate organizations. The solution must seamlessly integrate with and enhance the native capabilities of the M365 platform while scaling to meet the Army's performance demands.

3. SCOPE OF REQUIREMENT

The Government is seeking a mature, scalable Commercial-Off-The-Shelf (COTS) solution to meet requirements across several key functional areas, including Enterprise Governance, Data Sensitivity and Security (Data Tagging), Automated Records Management, SharePoint & Teams Management, and Storage Optimization. The solution must be a configurable, vendor-supported platform, not a custom software development effort.

4. INFORMATION REQUESTED

Interested parties are requested to provide a response that addresses the following sections.

PART I: CORPORATE INFORMATION

1.1 Company Name & Address:

1.2 Point of Contact: (Name, Title, Email, Phone Number)

1.3 CAGE Code & Unique Entity Identifier (UEI):

1.4 Business Size & Socio-Economic Status: (e.g., Large Business, Small Business, 8(a), WOSB, SDVOSB, etc.)

1.5 Federal Contract Experience: Briefly describe your experience providing IT solutions to DoD or other Federal agencies. Include relevant contract vehicles you currently hold (e.g., GSA MAS, SEWP, ITES-SW2).

1.6 Subcontracting: Does your approach for this requirement require subcontracting?

PART II: SOLUTION OVERVIEW

2.1 Solution Name & Description: Provide the name of your potential proposed COTS solution and a brief, high-level overview of its architecture (e.g., Software as a Service (SaaS), on-premises virtual appliance, hybrid).

2.2 Federal Security Compliance: Describe your potential solution's current security posture. Is it FedRAMP authorized? At what level (Low, Moderate, High)? Does the solution operate in Azure Government Cloud at DoD Impact Level 5 (IL5)?

2.3 Maturity: How long has the potentially proposed solution been commercially available?

PART III: CAPABILITY & TECHNICAL QUESTIONS

Please provide a direct response to each of the following questions regarding your potentially proposed solution.

How do you integrate with other capabilities? Particularly MS Purview and SharePoint Online.

What Admin permissions or API permissions required for the software to function?

How are admin permissions managed within the software?

How are logs handled?

How are reports and alerts managed? Are they customizable?

What administrative roles or service accounts from your solution require permissions within the Army’s Microsoft 365 (A365) tenant?

Do any components require tenant-wide administrative permissions (e.g., Global Admin, SharePoint Admin, Compliance Admin)? If so, how are these used?

Does the product use Azure Entra ID app registrations, and what Application Programming Interface (API) permissions are required?

Does the solution require any vendor access to Army M365 data or your solution’s supporting infrastructure during normal operations or troubleshooting?

Describe which functions of your solution run inside the Army M365 tenant versus within your hosted environment.

Does any processing leverage vendor-managed Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning (AI/ML) models, and can AI/ML analytics be performed without exporting sensitive content or metadata?

What is the expected volume of API calls per day, and does the solution require elevated throttling limits?

Does your solution support Common Access Card/Personal Identity Verification (CAC/PIV), conditional access, and Entra ID-based identity?

What access (if any) do your personnel or systems require to Army M365 data or to your solution’s supporting infrastructure (e.g., SaaS back end, support tools, logs), including during troubleshooting, and what authentication/authorization controls govern that access?

Can the solution operate with zero vendor access after deployment, and can all vendor access be disabled or audited by Army Network Enterprise Technology Command (NETCOM)?

Identify whether any Army data (including metadata, logs, labels, or content) is copied, stored, or processed outside the M365 boundary. Include any dependencies on vendor-managed infrastructure, hosting outside Department of Defense (DoD) IL5, or use of non-U.S. regions or subcontractors.

If your solution includes SaaS or vendor-hosted components, identify the compliance level of those environments (e.g., FedRAMP High, DoD IL5) and status of any relevant authorizations (e.g., DISA Provisional Authorization for IL5). 

How does your solution enforce Army-aligned delegated administrative scoping, and are all administrative actions fully audit-logged with the ability to export logs to Army Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) tools in near real time?

Where are audit logs, telemetry, and analytics data stored, and can all such data remain entirely within the Army’s M365 tenant? If any logs reside in vendor systems, identify what data elements are stored, for how long, and in what regions or environments.

How does the solution ensure compliance with National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) retention rules when performing:

Redundant, Obsolete, and Trivial (ROT) cleanup

lifecycle actions

metadata remediation

bulk operations

Does any processing leverage vendor-managed AI/ML models outside the Army environment?

Does your backup capability require extraction of data outside Microsoft’s boundary, and (if so) are backups stored in environments certified at IL5?

Can data restores be performed without violating retention or legal hold constraints?

Provide a complete list of all administrative actions your system can take on Army content or configuration, including:

Where the logic runs (tenant / vendor / hybrid)

Required permissions

How actions are validated and logged

Whether actions can be restricted by MACOM or command

What guardrails exist to prevent accidental or malicious bulk changes?

5. SUBMISSION INSTRUCTIONS

Format: Responses shall be submitted in PDF format. 

Submission: All responses must be submitted electronically to the points of contact below no later than the Response Due Date.

Name: Contracting Officer, Walter L. Carbajal 

Email: walter.l.carbajal.civ@army.mil 

Subject Line: "Response to RFI RFI-ARMY-DTSPO-2026-0420

Questions: Any questions regarding this RFI shall be submitted via email to the same points of contact no later than 23 April 2026, 4:00 PM Arizona Time.

Description of Services/Introduction:  This is a non-personal service(s) contract to 
provide capabilities for Sensitivity Labeling, Records Management and Workspace

1.2  
Background: The Army produces and hosts a large volume of work product across a 
variety of workspaces, including OneDrive, Teams and Sharepoint. This volume of data and 
number of workspaces presents administrative challenges in the areas of data protection, data 
management and workspace management. Additionally, the Army has to comply with Federal

Administration (NARA). The Army therefore requires capabilities which enable the Army to 
manage data and workspaces, protecting sensitive information, enabling operations and 
complying with Federal Records Management obligations.

252.232-7006 Wide Area WorkFlow Payment Instructions. (Jan 2023)
WIDE AREA WORKFLOW PAYMENT INSTRUCTIONS (JAN 2023) 
 (a) Deﬁnitions. As used in this clause- 
 "Department of Defense Activity Address Code (DoDAAC)" is a six position code that uniquely identiﬁes a unit, activity, or organization. 
 "Document type" means the type of payment request or receiving report available for creation in Wide Area WorkFlow (WAWF). 
 "Local processing ofﬁce (LPO)" is the ofﬁce responsible for payment certiﬁcation when payment certiﬁcation is done external to the entitlement system. 
 "Payment request" and "receiving report" are deﬁned in the clause at 252.232-7003, Electronic Submission of Payment Requests and Receiving Reports. 
 (b) Electronic invoicing. The WAWF system provides the method to electronically process vendor payment requests and receiving reports, as authorized by Defense 
Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS) 252.232-7003, Electronic Submission of Payment Requests and Receiving Reports. 
 (c) WAWF access. To access WAWF, the Contractor shall- 
 (1) Have a designated electronic business point of contact in the System for Award Management at https://www.sam.gov; and 
 (2) Be registered to use WAWF at https://wawf.eb.mil/ following the step-by-step procedures for self-registration available at this web site. 
 (d) WAWF training. The Contractor should follow the training instructions of the WAWF Web-Based Training Course and use the Practice Training Site before submitting 
payment requests through WAWF. Both can be accessed by selecting the "Web Based Training" link on the WAWF home page at https://wawf.eb.mil/ 
 (e) WAWF methods of document submission. Document submissions may be via web entry, Electronic Data Interchange, or File Transfer Protocol. 
 (f) WAWF payment instructions. The Contractor shall use the following information when submitting payment requests and receiving reports in WAWF for this contract or 
task or delivery order: 
 (1) Document type. The Contractor shall submit payment requests using the following document type(s): 
 (i) For cost-type line items, including labor-hour or time-and-materials, submit a cost voucher. 
 (ii) For ﬁxed price line items- 
 (A) That require shipment of a deliverable, submit the invoice and receiving report speciﬁed by the Contracting Ofﬁcer. 
____ 
(Contracting Ofﬁcer: Insert applicable invoice and receiving report document type(s) for ﬁxed price line items that require shipment of a deliverable.) 
 (B) For services that do not require shipment of a deliverable, submit either the Invoice 2in1, which meets the requirements for the invoice and receiving report, or the 
applicable invoice and receiving report, as speciﬁed by the Contracting Ofﬁcer. 
FA701426R0008
Page 8 of 36

____ 
 (Contracting Ofﬁcer: Insert either "Invoice 2in1" or the applicable invoice and receiving report document type(s) for ﬁxed price line items for services.) 
 (iii) For customary progress payments based on costs incurred, submit a progress payment request. 
 (iv) For performance based payments, submit a performance based payment request. 
 (v) For commercial ﬁnancing, submit a commercial ﬁnancing request. 
 (2) ) Fast Pay requests are only permitted when Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 52.213-1 is included in the contract. 
 [Note: The Contractor may use a WAWF "combo" document type to create some combinations of invoice and receiving report in one step.] 
 (3) Document routing. The Contractor shall use the information in the Routing Data Table below only to ﬁll in applicable ﬁelds in WAWF when creating payment requests 
and receiving reports in the system. 
 Routing Data Table*

(**Contracting Ofﬁcer: If the contract provides for progress payments or performance-based payments, insert the DoDAAC for the contract administration ofﬁce assigned 
the functions under FAR 42.302(a)(13).) 
 (4) Payment request. The Contractor shall ensure a payment request includes documentation appropriate to the type of payment request in accordance with the payment 
clause, contract ﬁnancing clause, or Federal Acquisition Regulation 52.216-7, Allowable Cost and Payment, as applicable. 
 (5) Receiving report. The Contractor shall ensure a receiving report meets the requirements of DFARS Appendix F.

Aeronautical Mobile Application Request for Information - Questions for Respondents

General

Discuss your company’s experience with creating Department of War (DOW) ISO images of DVDs.

What exposure has your company had with military aviation operations (e.g. mission planning, DAFIF data processing, Terminal procedure creation or evaluation, airfield surveys, airfield data collection.  etc.)?

What is your company’s experience working with data management and quality tools that would enable data monitoring, data auditing, and data quality assurance?

What is your company’s experience developing and managing Application Program Interface (APIs) and/or Software Development Kits (SDKs)?

Has your company had contracts requiring it to work based on the Aeronautical Information Regulation and Control (AIRAC) cycle?

Describe your company history developing software plug-ins to the Android Tactical Assault Kit (ATAK).

What level of experience does your company have with Aviation software development?

What is your experience working with Electronic Flight Bags (EFB)/ Electronic Knee Boards (EKB)?

What is your experience with 1801 flight plans and filing?

What is your experience with creating applications that do not require an internet connection to have the data updated?

What is your company's demonstrated experience in managing Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud environments specifically for DOW applications? Please provide examples of past projects, including their scope and scale.

Could you describe your experience with managing applications that are utilized by foreign partners?

Did you set up access controls based on data sharing agreements?

How would you approach the management and dissemination of aeronautical (aero) data through a cloud environment, ensuring it is securely accessible to both DOW and authorized non-DOW sources?

Is your company ISO 9001 Certified?

If yes, what ISO Certifications does your company possess?

If not, how long would it take for you to obtain this certification?

Is your company AS9100D certified?

When did you get your AS9100D certification?

Have you ever failed an AS9100D audit?  

If so, what were the findings? 

Were the identified discrepancies corrected? 

Have you been re-audited, and what were the results?

Security

Describe your cyber security processing implementation working with NGA software security processes.

If your company employs non-US citizens, would you be able to isolate them from any work on this contract?

If your company currently doesn’t have classified clearances, are you willing to make the required investments to achieve the required clearances?

Technical

Describe your level of experience working with Aeronautical Publications and Charts as noted below

Was this experience to display aeronautical data and information for use in a mobile application or something else?

What is your experience working with Airport Diagrams, Instrument Approach Procedures, Enroute Charts, and Vertical Obstructions?

What is your experience geo-referencing charts?

What is your experience working with the NGA/DOW/FAA Table Formatted Aeronautical Data Set (TFADS-O) dataset?

What is your experience working with NAVPLAN charts?

What is your experience working with CADRG and/or ECRG?

What is your experience working with FAA Sectionals?

What is your experience working with commercial imagery from MAXAR, formally known as Digital Globe?

What is your experience working with Air Force Weather products and data? Was this work done with their new cloud-based system?

What is your experience developing APIs to access this data?

What is your experience obtaining a U.S. Air Force –1 weather briefing?

What is your experience working with Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast (ADS-B) services and/or devices?

What is your experience working with NGA/DOW/FAA Flight Information Publications and Flight Information Products (FLIP)?

What is your experience working with NGA/DOW/FAA Aeronautical Planning documents?

What is your experience working with Digital Aeronautical Flight Information File (DAFIF) versions and formats?

What is your experience with NGA’s Electronic – Instrument Procedure Library (E-IPL)?

What is your experience with Mobile Device Management (MDM) software?

Do you have experience developing software that enables data deployment to clients through mobile devices over a locally hosted Wi-Fi network?

What is your experience working with Air Mobility Command (AMC) GIANT Reports?

Do you have experience creating and converting maps into an MB Tile format?

What is your experience displaying documents in the .pdf format?

What is your experience displaying kml and/or kmz files on a moving map? 

What approach would you take to provide data to a user in a Denied Degraded Intermittent/Low-Bandwidth (DDIL) environment?

What is your experience with developing mobile applications (native and/or hybrid) for the following mobile platforms:  

iPad (iOS) 

Android

MacOS

Microsoft Windows

Google and Apple stores for application deployment

Configuration Management

Describe your lean/agile experience with evidence of demonstrated proficiency in agile software engineering including CI/CD pipeline methodology.

Describe your methodology for managing multiple customers requiring same or different features in the software baseline.

Describe your proficiency in the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) and agile product management (managing evolving requirements, Backlog grooming, Release Planning, Product Owner prioritization of the software build, etc.). 

Provide a recommendation for transitioning to Open Source modeled on the Code.mil framework for managing the iOS and Android mobile applications and explain your answer.

PAGE |  1
Approved for Public Release, 22-195
THE NGA SOFTWARE WAY
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
SOFTWARE METRICS
To deliver on its current and future mission, NGA needs to deliver useful software faster and more consistently. 
To measure progress toward this intent, NGA will track three key metrics for each software product: availability, lead time for 
changes, and deployment frequency. Additionally, each software product is expected to have its own product-specific metrics 
to track how well the product is working for its users. Together, these metrics balance speed and reliability and describe a product’s 
impact and value.
To consistently track these metrics and realize the above intent, this document provides a set of elements NGA and contractor teams 
must meet while building, iterating on, and operating software products. These elements ensure each software team has the people, 
processes, and tools in place to effectively operate at NGA. 
The metrics and elements in this document, collectively referred to as “The NGA Software Way,” serve as an implementation guide 
to supplement NGA’s Technology Strategy and provide additional specific guidance for teams building and operating software. 
BEFORE WRITING 
CODE WHEN BEGINNING  
AND THROUGHOUT 
DEVELOPMENT
BY THE FIRST DELIVERABLE
 THAT PROVIDES VALUE

2. Deeply Understand User

Value to Users
4. Use Version Control
5. Track Work Consistently
6. Automate Testing
7. Document and Share APIs
8. Provide Support and 
Feedback Mechanisms
9. Automate Deployments
10. Automate Monitoring
11. Automate Alerting and 
Incident Response
12. Use Data to Drive 
Decisions
13. Iterate and Improve 
Continually
THE NGA SOFTWARE WAY ELEMENTS

CHANGES

PAGE |  2
Approved for Public Release, 22-195
A NOTE FROM THE CTO 
To deliver on its current and future mission, NGA needs to deliver useful software faster 
and more consistently. To do this, the NGA Technology Strategy describes changes in 
approach, tools, and behavior that will result in faster iterations, more flexible products, 
and better-satisfied users and customers. The NGA Software Way is an implementation 
guide to supplement NGA’s Technology Strategy and provide additional specific 
guidance for teams building and operating software.
Our intent is to get to a place where NGA software products are designed and built 
with users, can be automatically updated in production daily, and use product-specific 
metrics to make decisions. We believe in small, empowered, cross-functional teams that 
deeply understand their users and constantly work directly with those users to deliver on 
their mission.
Implementing The NGA Software Way won’t happen all at once; we must start with 
iteratively tracking and improving these metrics and meeting each element below. As 
we do this together, and with an understanding of this intent, we will successfully deliver 
software products with the speed, accuracy, and precision NGA’s mission demands.   
SOFTWARE METRICS
The three initial metrics all programs must track for each software product delivered to users are availability, lead time for changes, 
and deployment frequency. It is important to note that NGA is not unique in using these metrics. These are three of the primary 
metrics DevOps Research and Assessment (DORA) has studied over the past six years. These metrics use data from over 31,000 
professionals worldwide as well as rigorous statistical methods to study the most effective ways to develop and deploy technology. 
More information about the DORA metrics and study can be found in this DORA report: https://services.google.com/fh/files/misc/
state-of-devops-2019.pdf.
These metrics provide visibility into how NGA balances speed (deployment frequency and lead time for changes) and stability 
(availability) during product development and operations. These two efforts, speed and stability, were once believed to be negatively 
correlated — increasing one would decrease the other. DORA research has conclusively shown that speed and stability are 
outcomes that enable each other and high-performing teams do better in both of these areas. This is what NGA is aiming for and 
moving toward. 
For software teams using NGA’s enterprise-managed version control and Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) 
pipeline, deployment frequency and lead time for changes will be automatically tracked within those services’ dashboards. To 
enable that automation, the recommended tagging method can be found in the appendix. For teams not using NGA’s enterprise-
managed version control and CI/CD pipeline, lead time for changes and deployment frequency must be independently calculated and 
transparently shared. All teams are expected to regularly share their metrics with teammates, stakeholders, users, and leadership. 
Eventually, teams will programmatically share these metrics to a central, NGA-managed location. 
AVAILABILITY
Availability is about ensuring a product or service is accessible to users when they need it. At a 
macro level, availability represents technology teams and organizations meeting user expectations 
about the software they are deploying and operating. Any time a user is not able to access a 
software product, that product is unavailable. Availability may be impacted by actions a product 
team itself takes or through issues with upstream or downstream dependencies. There is no 
distinction in availability to users based on what caused the issue; whether planned or unplanned 
and no matter where the issue originated, the impact of a product being unavailable when users 
need it is the same.
Alex Loehr
NGA Chief Technology Officer

PAGE |  3
Approved for Public Release, 22-195
Higher availability is better, as it means the product is accessible when users need and expect it. 
Tracking availability automatically enables teams to know when something goes wrong, giving the 
opportunity to investigate issues more rapidly and proactively. Although teams may have different 
availability requirements depending on mission and user needs, teams are generally expected to 
maintain at least 99.9% availability in production, which translates to about one business day of 
downtime per year.
LEAD TIME FOR CHANGES
Lead time for changes tracks how long it takes to deliver a feature for mission use once it has 
been developed. Lead time for changes is calculated as the median time between when code is 
committed to when it is deployed to a production environment, where it can be used during an 
actual mission (not in a test environment). That figure is measured across all commits within a 
given time period. Measuring this enables teams to understand where bottlenecks are in getting 
features to production and prioritize where to optimize. 
Shorter lead times are better because they enable faster feedback on what is built and allow 
teams to course correct more rapidly. Additionally, faster lead times allow faster fixes with higher 
confidence when there are issues, which directly impacts availability as well. DORA research 
shows high-performing teams have lead times for changes of less than one week. 
DEPLOYMENT FREQUENCY 
Deployment frequency is how often a product is deployed to production. A product team will 
do many additional deployments to testing, development, staging, and other non-production 
environments. The deployment frequency metric is specifically related to deployments to 
production, however, where users actually use the product to complete the mission or function. 
To the maximum extent possible, deployments are expected to be automated (see Automated 
Deployments on page 9), so increasing deployment frequency does not linearly increase workload 
on the team. 
Deployment frequency is a proxy for batch size. Reducing batch size has consistently been shown 
to accelerate feedback, improve efficiency, reduce risk and overhead, increase team motivation 
and urgency, and reduce costs (for more details, see Accelerate, page 16, by Nicole Forsgren, Jez 
Humble, and Gene Kim). DORA research shows high-performing teams deploy at least once per 
week. 
                    +
PRODUCT-SPECIFIC METRICS
In addition to the above three metrics that will be consistently tracked across all software programs, 
each software product is expected to have its own key performance indicators (KPIs) and metrics to 
track how well the product is working for its users. While the first three metrics are about balancing 
speed and stability, these product-specific metrics are about product priorities and value. Software 
products contribute to NGA’s mission in different ways, so their success must be uniquely measured, 
and each team must define its own product-specific metrics. 
Because these product-specific metrics will not be consistent across NGA, they cannot be defined at 
the organizational level. However, each product will include: (1) performance metrics that describe if 
the product is doing what it’s supposed to; (2) business metrics that convey how well the

# Questions Answers w/ SOO Para. Reference
1
Point of clarification for 1.5.1.2 - will installations without local IT support require the vendor provide 
any additional vulnerability scanning and management capabilities? We noted the previous response 
that the request is to provide the critical oversight, analysis, remediation guidance and hands-on 
technical support to manage the vulnerability lifecycle, not on operating the testing tools themselves.
No additional vulnerability scanning and management capabilities are required.  
While tool provision is out of scope (SOO 1.5.2), the contractor is responsible for 
the technical support for ongoing vulnerability management, including monitoring, 
lifecycle management and remediation guidance, and hands-on support as 
required by installations, both remote and onsite as needed (SOO 1.5.1.2, 
4.1.3.1.1).
2
Is AFSVC open to a fully vendor-managed model for remote security operations (SOC, SIEM)?
No. The SOO outlines a collaborative model to empower local staff, not a fully 
vendor-managed operation. Your role is to augment, not replace, local personnel 
(SOO 1.3.1, 2.2.3).
3
To what degree is in-person / hands-on support expected, and for what geographic areas? Would a 
proposed solution for quarterly travel be acceptable, or is the vendor expected to provide permanent 
local staff to support installations?
On-site and remote assistance is required (SOO 3.1). Expect hands-on support at 
~20-30 of 104 locations (SOO 1.4, 1.5.1.2). Travel is as-needed for physical audits 
and audit preparedness; permanent local staff are not required (SOO 6.4.2).
4
What level of security clearance is expected for vendor personnel? Will all vendor staff engaged in 
supporting AFSVC require a Tier 1? Will any vendor staff require a Tier 3?
Tier 1 is sufficient, most of the work is unclassified (SOO 6.6.1). Refer to SOO 
6.7.2.2, Contractor personnel shall not be assigned to perform duties within IAT-II 
or IAT-III, and or requiring “Limited Privileged Access” such as system or database 
administration rights, to any NAFI application or network.
5
Will vendor interact with any CUI / does the proposed solution require a CMMC certification?
Yes, the vendor will handle CUI and require training per SOO 6.6.3. Refer to SOO 
3.2 Contractor Qualifications.
6
Are all assets centrally managed (e.g., centralized patching tools, endpoint management, 
configuration management)?If not, identify unmanaged or partially managed assets.
The objective is to achieve a "proactive, centrally-governed security posture" (SOO 
1.3.1), which indicates assets are not currently all centrally managed. The SOO 
notes that "critical gaps in security tools and on-site technical support prevent a 
proactive, centrally managed defense" (SOO 1.2.1). 
7 Are legacy or end of life systems present? If yes, list known unsupported software or systems and 
any business constraints.
Any in-scope legacy or end of life systems will be upgraded by August 2026.  Many 
are scoped out using P2PE terminals.
8
Are externally facing assets included in scope?(Web servers, VPNs, firewalls, cloud services, etc.)
External assets are out of scope of this SOO, penetration testing is being 
conducted, per SOO 1.5.2.  The SOO requires expertise with firewalls, VPNs (SOO 
3.2.1.4), and cloud platforms like AWS, Azure, and GCP (SOO 3.2.1.9). 
9
Is there any preference for fee arrangements - per endpoint, hourly, fixed fee, etc.
The contract will be fixed fee, based on deliverables (SOO 3.1). See Contract 
Admin Data, Paragraph 4.
10 Will any cloud workloads be in-scope? Do you utilize any cloud service providers (e.g., AWS, Azure, 
GCP) for any part of your payment processing environment?
Yes, some Aloha Cloud is in CDE scope. Required expertise in AWS, Azure, and 
GCP, and the directive to prioritize cloud-based tools, confirm cloud workloads are 
in scope (SOO 3.2.1.9, 4.1.1.3).
11
 Many installations have successfully achieved PCI compliance using validated P2PE solutions, 
hosted payment pages, or centralized enterprise payment gateways. Has AFSVC evaluated whether 
standardizing one or more of these proven, lower-scope approaches—where 
appropriate—could enhance consistency across installations while reducing lifecycle costs and 
simplifying compliance sustainment, as an alternative to deploying and maintaining separate 
tool suites at each location?
Standardization is underway, as AFSVC has moved from a decentralized to 
centralized approach. An enterprise Tool suite solution, where applicable, is being 
requested in this SOO (4.1.1).
12 The SOO currently directs the contractor to recommend and implement a “complete enterprise tool 
suite” (SIEM, DLP, MFA, etc.) and to prioritize “modern, automated, and cloud-based tools.”  As a full-
service QSA with experience delivering thousands of services to similar environments outlined in both 
the original and current SOO we find that the scope in the first SOO to more cost effective and timely. 
Would AFSVC consider modifying the requirement to include, as part of Phase 1, a QSA-
performed assessment that delivers a validated scope-reduction roadmap and presents 
multiple compliant implementation approaches—including both tool-enabled and scope-
optimized models—along with a side-by-side 3–5 year total cost of ownership analysis to 
support AFSVC decision making?
The SOO requires the contractor to conduct a "Gap Analysis" and deliver a 
"Solution Implementation Strategy" for a "complete enterprise tool suite" (SOO 
4.1.1.1, 4.1.1.4). The contractor's role is to provide technical support, not to 
perform a QSA function, although collaboration with a QSA vendor is expected 
(SOO 4.1.3.2.2).
13
The SOO emphasizes on-site technical support, 24/7 monitoring, ongoing tool management, and 
sustainment across up to 30 installations. Has AFSVC conducted, or would it consider conducting, 
a comparative analysis of total lifecycle cost—including personnel, licensing, maintenance, 
training, and option years—across alternative compliant approaches, such as tool-intensive 
implementations versus scope reduction and process simplification, which for many federal 
and DoD organizations has historically reduced compliance effort by 70–90%, to ensure long-
term operational sustainability and resource alignment?
The SOO establishes the requirement to deploy and manage a standardized tool 
suite (SOO 1.5.1). A comparative cost analysis of alternative compliance 
approaches is not a specified deliverable within the SOO. The contractor is 
expected to provide a detailed cost analysis for the recommended tools as part of 
the implementation strategy (SOO 4.1.1.4.2). 
A P2PE based scope reduction strategy is in place, while certain components 
remain within full PCI scope.  Supporting data and documentation will be provided 
upon contract award.  
14
The SOO states that the AFSVC intends to award all phases to a single vendor but that Phases 2 and 4 
are at the AFSVC’s discretion based on Phase 1 results. Would AFSVC consider adding language to 
allow the Phase 1 contractor to recommend and potentially execute a QSA-led scope-reduction 
strategy, as well as evaluate multiple compliant execution paths, so the AFSVC can select the 
approach that best aligns with cost, risk tolerance, and sustainment objectives—potentially 
eliminating the need for Phases 2 and 4—prior to proceeding to subsequent phases?
The Government is currently engaged with a QSA firm; however, the expectation 
is that you will collabrate to develop and execute a forward-looking strategy. 
15
DoD cybersecurity guidance (e.g., Zero Trust principles, Cloud Security Playbook elements, and 
integration with existing DISN/DoDIN frameworks) emphasizes risk-based, least-privilege controls 
over broad tooling deployment. How does the SOO's focus on implementing and managing a full 
suite of security tools (SIEM, DLP, MFA across CDEs) align with these mandates, and would the 
AFSVC be open to requiring Phase 1 recommendations to demonstrate compatibility with DoD-
wide security architectures to avoid redundant or conflicting implementations?
Refer to SOO  1.2 Background and 1.3 Objective.  As part of the Phase 1: Gap 
Analysis and Remediation (SOO 4.1.1), AFSVC is requesting a strategy and 
recommendation for the required  security tools and services to comply with PCI 
DSS (SOO 4.1.1.3 and 4.1.1.4).  The solution must align with DoD standards, 
including NIST SP 800-53, CISA directives, and the DoD RMF (SOO 6.9.2) while 
closing PCI control gaps and avoiding redundancy. For further clarification, the DAF 
NAFIs process payment card transactions over locally managed commercial 
netwoks rather than the NIPRNet.
16
PCI DSS best practices for sustainment emphasize repeatable, low-effort processes over complex 
tooling. Has the AFSVC evaluated whether the proposed enterprise tool suite—including 
licensing, configuration, monitoring, and update requirements—aligns with sustainable 
compliance models observed in other federal environments, and would AFSVC consider 
incorporating long-term sustainment factors, such as operational complexity, maintenance 
demands, and staffing dependencies, into Phase 1 deliverables to support stable and durable 
compliance outcomes?”
A PCI DSS compliant recommendation is part of  SOO 4.1.1, Phase 1: Gap Analysis 
and Remediation
17
Should the Contractor plan for steady-state operations only, or include surge support for assessment
cycles, remediation efforts, or incident response events?
The contractor must plan for both. The SOO requires "Ongoing Technical Support" 
(SOO 4.1.3), including "24/7 Support Services" (SOO 4.1.3.1.3), "Tier 2/3 
Sustainment Support" for escalations (SOO 2.2.5), incident response assistance 
(SOO 4.1.3.4.2.4), and "PCI DSS Assessment Preparation Support" (SOO 4.1.3.2).
18 Are clearance requirements (e.g., Public Trust, Secret) required at contract start, and will the 
Government sponsor clearances if needed?
All clearances will be sponsored by the Govt once the Contract is awarded. See 
SOO Para 6.7. To ensure all Personnel needing background checks meet the 
requirements.
19 What are the expected incident response roles and responsibilities for the Contractor (e.g., triage, 
escalation, remediation support), and how will these integrate with Government teams?
The contractor is responsible for "hands-on technical support for 
ongoing...incident response" (SOO 1.5.1.2) and must have experience with 
"containment, eradication, and recovery" (SOO 3.2.1.3). Suspected incidents must 
             20 Are there minimum staffing levels, labor categories, or experience thresholds that the Government 
will use to determine technical acceptability?
AFSVC is seeking industry to provide this answer.
21 Is a Facility Clearance (FCL) required at time of proposal or award, and if so, will the Government 
sponsor the prime or subcontractor for FCL?
All clearances will be sponsored by the Govt once the Contract is awarded. See 
SOO Para 6.7. To ensure all Personnel needing background checks meet the 
requirements.
22 Through which channels do you accept payments (e.g., E-commerce, Card-Present/Retail, or Mail 
Order/Telephone Order)?
CDE scoping and inventory documentation will be provided upon contract award. 
Refer to SOO 4.1. 
23 Approximately how many credit/debit card transactions do you process annually across all 
channels?
Over 13 million annual payment card transactions (SOO 1.4).
24 Are you operating as a Merchant (taking payments for your own goods/services), a Service Provider 
(processing on behalf of others), or both?
AFSVC is a Merchant (SOO 1.1.1).
25 Does your organization store, process, or transmit Primary Account Numbers (PAN) in any electronic 
format?
Yes. The purpose of this SOO is to ensure full security of the CDE (SOO 1.1.1, 
1.3.1).
26
Do you store any Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD) after authorization (e.g., CVV, CID, or PIN data)?
No. Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD) is not stored.
27 For E-commerce, do you use a third-party redirect (like a hosted payment page), an iFrame, or do you 
capture the data directly on your own servers?
Hosted payment pages are used for individual activity ecommerce solutions; an 
enterprise eCommerce solution is in development. 
28 Is your Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) currently segmented from the rest of your corporate

The CDE is segmented.
29

Yes.

Physical POS are in use and in the process of full PCI-validation.
31 If you take orders over the phone (MOTO), are calls recorded, and if so, is card data being captured in 
those recordings?
Calls are not recorded in the case of phone orders.
32 Can you provide a list of all third parties that could impact the security of your cardholder data (e.g., 
web hosting, data centers, managed firewall providers)?
Third party details can be delivered upon contract award.
33 Do you have an up-to-date network diagram and data flow diagram that specifically identifies all 
cardholder data flows?
We do have an up-to-date standardized network diagram.
34 Is your IT or security infrastructure managed in-house, or is it outsourced to a Managed Service 
Provider (MSP)?
The SOO implies a mixed environment. This contract is required due to a lack of 
specialized on-site IT support at many locations (SOO 2.0).
35 What was your most recent PCI DSS validation level (e.g., SAQ A, SAQ D, or Level 1 ROC), and when 
was it last completed?
AFSVC NAFI is a Level 1 Merchant and is currently undergoing a SAQ-D assessment 
with a QSA firm. Specific assessment results are considered sensitive and are not 
disclosed publicly.
36
What kind of service you are looking for? A GAP, Advisory, or a final assessment?
See SOO.

UNCLASSIFIED 
1 
 
UNCLASSIFIED  
Enclosure (1) 
 
 PROFESSIONAL, TECHNICAL AND ANALYTICAL SUPPORT  
TO NAVY ELECTRONIC WARFARE 
 
 
1.0 INTRODUCTION AND GENERAL INFORMATION: 
 
1.1 INTRODUCTION 
This Performance Work Statement (PWS) supports Naval Information Warfighting Development 
Center (NIWDC) warfighting capabilities with specific focus towards Electronic Warfare Data 
(EWD)production (using the Navy Information Operations Database (NIODB)) currently operated 
on an Amazon Web Service (AWS) Impact Level (IL6) cloud hosting environment.  NIODB is a 
Navy approved source of parametric, and order of battle data used in the development of Electronic 
Warfare (EW) System Libraries.  Aiding the warfighter with a single source of authoritative EW 
data, it improves threat and own force identification, enables Anti-Ship Missile Defense, enhances 
interoperability, and improves mission planning through improved threat data fidelity.  OPNAV 
Instruction 3430.23D provides policy for United States Fleet Forces Command ( USFFC) and 
Naval Information Forces ( NAVIFOR) execution of the Electronic Warfare Reprogrammable 
Systems Support Program.  NIWDC EWD is NAVIFOR designated EW Functional Data Manager 
and is the primary organization for this support. 
 
1.2 TYPE OF CONTRACT 
The Government intends to award an Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity contract with Firm -
Fixed Price provisions for services. Individual task orders will specify positions. 
 
1.3 ORDERING PERIOD 
The resultant Indefinite Delivery, Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract will consist of the following 
five year base ordering period and will contain FAR 52.217-8 – Option to Extend Services to allow 
for a six- month extension of the ordering period if circ umstances necessitate the exercise of this 
option. The ordering periods are as follows: 
 
 
Base Ordering Period:                                        28 March 2027 – 27 March 2032 
FAR 52.217-8 Option Ordering Period:            28 March 2032 – 27 September 2032 
 
 
1.4 PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE 
Base Year:   28 March 2027 - 27 March 2028 
 Ordering Year I:  28 March 2028 - 27 March 2029 
 Ordering Year II:  28 March 2029 - 27 March 2030 
 Ordering Year III: 28 March 2030 - 27 March 2031 
 Ordering Year IV: 28 March 2031 - 27 March 2032 
 FAR 52.217-8:  28 March 2032 - 27 September 2032 
 
NOTE: The performance period of individual task orders will be established at the time of 
task order issuance. 
 
 

UNCLASSIFIED 
2 
 
UNCLASSIFIED  
Enclosure (1) 
 
1.5 PLACE OF PERFORMANCE/WORKING HOURS 
Performance will occur at the following locations: 
• Naval Information Warfare Training Group Norfolk, 1247 West C Street, Building U-
132, Naval Station, Norfolk, VA. 23511 
• Vendor company accredited facility/corporate office upon COR approval 
• Working hours are Monday – Friday, 7:30 am – 4:00 pm except Federal Holidays.  The 
expectation is an 8-hour day. 
 
1.6 OFFICE SPACE 
The Government will provide office space, desks, chairs, and common office supplies.  In 
addition to office space, each Contractor employee shall be provided with access to government 
computers and telephones, but not cellular phones, for official use only.  Under no circumstances 
will Contractor-provided personal computers, or any personal electronic devices (e.g., cell 
phones, tablets, Fitbits), be connected to any Government IT system, including but not limited to 
the Navy/Marine Corps Intranet (NMCI).  Contractor personnel shall comply with all DOD and 
Navy IT regulations, policies and procedures including those concerning internet usage and 
cybersecurity.  
 
For contract personnel working at the vendor accredited facility/corporate office, the vendor will 
furnish material/workspace as necessary to perform assigned tasks. 
 
1.7 HOLIDAYS: 
The contractor is not required to provide services on these days: 
 
New Year’s Day      Labor Day  
Martin Luther King Jr.’s Birthday    Columbus Day  
President’s Day      Veteran’s Day  
Memorial Day      Thanksgiving Day  
Juneteenth Day      Christmas Day  
Independence Day 
 
2.0 SCOPE:    
 
This PWS encompasses support for NIWDC Missions, Functions, and Tasks (MF&T) related to 
the mission areas of Navy Electronic Warfare Functional Data Management and Navy 
Information Operations Database Support.  This PWS provides services and support that 
includes both short-term and annual support contracts in some or all of the following EW tasks.

  3.1.1 Subject Matter Expert II Functional Responsibility: 
 Technical Signals Intelligence (SIGINT) & Electronic Warfare Data and System Requirements 
analysis,   technical guidance for data management/interoperability, database design and 
development, data standardization and maintenance, data migration, content quality assurance 
support of the Navy Information Operations Database (NIODB), and development of technical 
reports.   
 
This support shall include: 
 
• Conduct detailed analysis of complex communication & non-communication waveforms 
to determine pulse repetition intervals (PRI), pulse duration, and scanning characteristics. 
• Liaison with Navy and DoD Electronic Warfare system engineers to document, interpret, 
and analyze current and fielded EW system/community data requirements. 
• Research, identify, and validate authoritative information sources to support documented 
and validated training system/community requirements. 
• Provide Subject Matter Expertise in Technical SIGINT data analysis (i.e. Electronic 
Warfare Integrated Reprogramming (EWIR), Combined Emitter Database (CED)) and 
National Intelligence Product (ONI, NASIC, NGIC, MSIC, NGA, DIA, NSA) analysis in 
support of Electronic Warfare (EW) systems. 
 
  3.1.2 Subject Matter Expert II Qualification Required:   
The following knowledge, skills and abilities should be considered the minimum requirements      
for this position: 
 
• 10 years demonstrated experience in the field of Technical Electronic Intelligence 
(TECHELINT) data analysis and authoritative national data sources to include EWIR, 
and CED  
• Demonstrated experience in the field of Electronic Warfare systems, RADAR threat 
parameter analysis, threat/friendly order of battle (OOB) analysis, Specific Emitter 
Identification (SEI), and authoritative national data sources to include ONI, NASIC, 
NGIC, MSIC, NGA, DIA, and NSA. 
• Demonstrated experience in the use of data query and visualization tools to include CA 
ERwin Data Modeling Software, MS Excel, and Oracle SQL Developer. 
• Comprehensive knowledge of data rationalization and standardization efforts to 
document and cross-correlate EW system data requirements in addition to interpreting 
highly technical Database Description Documents (DBDD) and System Requirements 
Specifications (SRS).

• Ability to effectively communicate orally and in writing to produce finished products, 
liaise with current and potential customers, prepare and present briefing documents, 
charts, graphs, and presentations. 
 
3.1.3 Subject Matter Expert I Functional Responsibility: 
 Technical Signals Intelligence (SIGINT) & Electronic Warfare Data and System Requirements 
analysis, technical guidance for data management/interoperability, database design and 
development, data standardization and maintenance, data migration, content quality assurance 
support of the Navy Information Operations Database (NIODB), and development of technical 
reports.   
 
This support shall include: 
• Liaison with Software Developers to produce, assess, and validate unique EW data 
products developed from NIODB in support of Navy and DoD EW systems/community 
partners. 
• Research, development, recommendation, and documentation of Quality Assurance 
processes, data analysis of derivative database product development, and QA verification 
tasks related to NIODB. 
• Create detailed technical papers recommending and describing QA processes that will 
ensure development of intuitive, precise, user friendly, easily integrated, web enabled QA 
tools. 
 
3.1.4 Subject Matter Expert I Qualification Required:   
The following knowledge, skills and abilities should be considered the minimum requirements      
for this position: 
 
• Demonstrated experience in the field of Electronic Warfare systems, RADAR threat 
parameter analysis, threat/friendly order of battle (OOB) analysis, Specific Emitter 
Identification (SEI), and authoritative national data sources to include ONI, NASIC, 
NGIC, MSIC, NGA, DIA, and NSA. 
• Demonstrated experience in the use of data query and visualization tools to include CA 
ERwin Data Modeling Software, MS Excel, and Oracle SQL Developer. 
• Demonstrated experience in Own Force Data (OFD) platform/parametric analysis to 
support production of validated data in the NIODB and derivative products. 
• Demonstrated experience in North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) 
platform/parametric analysis to support production of releasable validated data into the 
Joint Electronic Warfare Centers NATO database, SCORPIO. 
• Ability to effectively communicate orally and in writing to produce finished products, 
liaise with current and potential customers, prepare and present briefing documents, 
charts, graphs, and presentations 
 
 
 
3.2 Electronic Warfare Reprogrammable Systems Library Analysis Support:   
 

UNCLASSIFIED

  Estimated FTE:

  3.2.1 Subject Matter Expert II Functional Responsibility:   
The Contractor shall provide engineering and technical support, assistance, and training for EW 
Reprogrammable Libraries, also known as “Threat Databases” in support of AN/SLQ-32 Anti-
Ship Missile Defense (ASMD) , training databases and AN/BLQ-10B subsurface Electronic 
Surveillance (ES) threat libraries. Services include: Interpreting intelligence information, 
performing multi-int analysis, assigning threat levels, knowledge of geo-area filtering, 
performing parametric mode combining, implementing system white paper rules, 
writing/modifying technical documents, and researching ELINT/Order of Battle (OOB) platform 
threat data from national agency (i.e. ONI/NASIC/NGIC/MIDB/DIA) sources. Perform analysis 
using the Navy Information Operations Database (NIODB), (CED), and Electronic Warfare 
Integrated Reprogramming Database (EWIRDB). The library build process is continuous 
throughout the year. 
 
This support shall include: 
 
• Research, identify, and validate authoritative information sources to support  production 
of sensor parameter data in NIODB and derivative products.   
• Conduct detailed analysis of complex non-communication waveforms to determine pulse 
repetition intervals (PRI), pulse duration, and scanning characteristics to support 
development of SLQ-32(V) Tactical Trainer libraries.

analysis and authoritative national data sources to include NGES, EWIR, and CED.

parameter analysis, threat/friendly order of battle (OOB) analysis, Specific Emitter 
Identification (SEI), and authoritative national data sources to include ONI, NASIC, 
NGIC, MSIC, NGA, DIA, and NSA. 
• Demonstrated experience with the SLQ-32(V) and NCTE/LVC is highly desired but not 
required. 
• Demonstrated operational EW experience with the Navy’s tactical training libraries to 
include proven experience with tactical training simulation systems     
• Demonstrated experience in the use of data query and visualization tools such as MS 
Excel.

CONTINUATION PAGE

36C26226Q0506 Question and Answers

1) Can you provide photographs of the installation locations?

ANSWER: We can mark the locations on the plan drawing. 

2) Can you describe the types of walls the screens will be mounted to? I.E. CMU, drywall with metal studs, drywall with wood studs, brick?

ANSWER: Mostly dry walls and some locations with tiled walls. 

3) Will the screens be mounted in Landscape or Portrait format?

ANSWER:  The VA San Diego Healthcare System will rely on the contractors' expertise to assess and provide professional recommendations to determine the optimal orientation. As noted in the Statement of Work (SOW), the design must comply with the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) requirements. 

4) You mention a range for screen size of 47"-65". Will you require different sized screens for different locations? Should screen size be standardized for all 16 installation locations?

ANSWER: The VA San Diego Healthcare System will rely on the contractors' expertise to assess the space and provide professional recommendation to help the facility make an informed decision. 65” are most likely going near the main entrances and 47” for other locations.

5) Is there any branding, color pallet, or logo placement that should be incorporated into the design of the screens? 

ANSWER: Yes. Similar navy blue color and logo as seen on the main VA website: https://www.va.gov/ shall be used as a starting point for design. 

The contractor shall have design discussion sessions with VASD for interface design. VASD will be relying on the contractor's design expertise. 

6) Will power and data connections be available at each installation location?

ANSWER: Power will be VASD’s responsibility. Cabling and network set up will be the contractor’s responsibility. 

7) Will the installer be able to utilize the dumpster, compactors, or recycling facilities at each installation location? Or will they be responsible for hauling away all packaging material? 

ANSWER: No. Contractors are responsible for their housekeeping. Note that at the main La Jolla Medical Center contractors shall only use the service elevators. 

8) Can the screens be received at each facility prior to installation? Or can they be received at a central location? Or will the installer be responsible for bringing the screens to each installation location? 

ANSWER: The contractor is responsible for safely storing their materials and tools. No materials, screens, or tools shall be left or stored on-site unsupervised at any time. VASDHS is not responsible for the storage, damage, or loss of any items left at VASDHS's medical center or clinics.

9) Can you clarify the expected time frame for issuing a PO and the desired installation date for the 16 screens? 

ANSWER: The PO will be issued once the contract has an award.  Dates for the installation will be June 30-July 31.

ANSWER: No. Contractors are responsible for their housekeeping. Note that at the main La Jolla Medical Center contractors shall only use the service elevators. 

8) Can the screens be received at each facility prior to installation? Or can they be received at a central location? Or will the installer be responsible for bringing the screens to each installation location? 

ANSWER: The contractor is responsible for safely storing their materials and tools. No materials, screens, or tools shall be left or stored on-site unsupervised at any time. VASDHS is not responsible for the storage, damage, or loss of any items left at VASDHS's medical center or clinics.

9) Can you clarify the expected time frame for issuing a PO and the desired installation date for the 16 screens? 

ANSWER: The PO will be issued once the contract has an award.  Installation shall begin before June 30th. 

Will floor plans, CAD drawings, or other layout documentation be provided for these locations?

ANSWER: VASD can provide archived floor plan drawings (CAD/PDF). Locations are still being finalized by end users. Generally expected areas include main entrances and lobbies. The VASD will also rely on contractors' expertise to assess the space and provide professional recommendations on this matter.  

Question 6 of the initial RFI response notes that power will be the responsibility of VASD, while cabling and network setup fall to the contractor.

Will both power and data be available at the installation locations? 

Or will the contractor be responsible for extending power and data to each location? 

ANSWER: Network cabling set up will be the contractor’s responsibility. Power will be VASD’s responsibility. 

Are there any active construction projects underway at these facilities? If so, who is serving as the General Contractor?

ANSWER: There are multiple active construction projects under different contractors. Pathfinding screen locations are not affected by these constructions. 

Pg 39 of the SOW requires all items to be delivered by 5-01-2026, however, per RFI response #9 the work is to be performed between 6-30-2026 and 7-31-2026. Further RFI response #8 states that the contractor is responsible for all tools and materials at all times. What is to be delivered on 5-01-2026? 

ANSWER: The PO will be issued once the contract has an award.  Installation shall begin before June 30th.

SECTION L - INSTRUCTIONS, CONDITIONS, AND NOTICES TO QUOTERS 
L.1. OVERVIEW  
The USCB is issuing this competitive Request for Quotation (RFQ) under the General Services 
Administration (GSA) Multiple Award Schedule (MAS) for its CenTAM Acquisition 
Framework requirements. Through this effort, USCB intends to establish four (4) blanket 
purchase agreements (BPAs) under the MAS to acquire application development, 
modernization, data engineering, geospatial, and related technical services in support of its 
mission.  
All four BPAs under this RFQ are being competed as total small business set-asides.  
Only small businesses holding the applicable GSA MAS IT Category SIN 54151S are 
eligible to submit quotes.  
The Government will accept only quotes submitted by small businesses under GSA MAS SIN 
54151S. Any quote submitted by a Quoter that is not a small business will not be accepted and, 
therefore, not considered for evaluation or award.  
For the purposes of this RFQ “quoter” means a small business concern. Contractors that submit a 
proposal will be referred to as quoters.  
The Government intends to execute and award the initial orders for the single award BPAs based 
on initial quotes. Therefore, quoters are highly encouraged to propose their best technical and 
pricing quotes in their initial submissions. 
L.2. AUTHORITY (GSAR 538.71 – FSS ORDERING PROCEDURES) 
This RFQ is issued under GSAR Subpart 538.71 – Federal Supply Schedule (FSS) Ordering 
Procedures, as implemented via GSA’s Revolutionary FAR Overhaul (RFO) class deviation. 
Ordering will be conducted using RFQs and evaluation of quotations under FSS procedures. 
Unless otherwise prescribed, FAR Parts 5, 6, 13, 14, 15, 16, and 19 do not apply to these 
ordering procedures.  
These procedures constitute “competitive procedures” under 41 U.S.C §152, including 152(4) 
for procurements conducted under the Small Business Act. They enable the Government to 
conduct best-value evaluations consistent with GSAR 538.71 - FSS ordering procedures.  
L.3. COMMUNICATING INNOVATIVE TECHNIQUES WITH INDUSTRY 
This solicitation encompasses several innovative procurement techniques (see GSAR 538.7102-2 
for more coverage of streamlined processes and innovative procurement techniques). To increase 
transparency of them with industry, they are listed below. The Periodic Table of Acquisition 
innovations (PTAI), https://acquisitiongateway.gsa.gov/periodic-table, includes a full tile sharing 
the description, problems solved, benefits of use, small business benefit, and real sample 
documents (including training aids) for each:  
• Communicating Innovative Techniques with Industry (CITI)  

 
2 
 
• Confidence Ratings  
• Down-Selects 
• Fusion Procurements  
• Highest Technically Rated Vendors  
• On-the-Spot Consensus Evaluation  
• Streamlined Documentation  
L.4. CENTAM BPA STRUCTURE 
The CenTAM Acquisition Framework will consist of four BPAs to support the full spectrum of 
USCB application modernization, data engineering, analytics, geospatial, and related IT services 
needs. The Enterprise BPA is intended to support large-scale, more complex efforts across all 
USCB programs requiring broad technical depth and integration. The Systems and Program 
Support (SPS) BPA is intended to support smaller-scale, narrowly scoped efforts across all 
USCB programs. The Decennial BPA is dedicated to supporting the Decennial Program only, 
while the Geospatial BPA supports Geospatial initiatives across USCB.
