3 
 
The Government also intends to award initial orders with the single-award BPAs: 
• Decennial Order 01 (DTAM)  with the Decennial BPA  
• Geospatial Order 01 (GeoTAM)  with the Geospatial BPA  
Orders under any BPA may be issued as Firm-Fixed Price (FFP),Time and Materials, (T&M) 
Labor Hour (LH), or any other authorized contract type.  
L.5. QUOTER ELIGIBILITY AND TEAM PARTICIPATION LIMITATIONS 
L.5.1 QUOTER ELIGIBILITY  
To be eligible to submit a quote under this RFQ, Quoters must hold the applicable MAS SIN for 
the BPA(s). 
Participation as a Joint Venture (JV) or SBA-approved Mentor-Protégé JV (MPJV) is 
permitted, subject to the following conditions: 
• The JV or MPJV entity itself must hold the required MAS SIN at the time of the 
quotation submission.  
MAS Certified Teaming Arrangements (CTAs) are not permitted under this RFQ.  
L.5.2 TEAM PARTICIPATION LIMITATIONS 
A Quoter may participate in this acquisition either as a prime contractor or a subcontractor, 
but only on one team.  
Participation on multiple teams, whether across BPAs or within a single BPA, is prohibited.  
Enterprise, Decennial, and Geospatial BPAs: 
• A Quoter may submit a quote for one or more of the following BPAs:  Enterprise, 
Decennial, or Geospatial BPAs. 
SPS BPA: 
• Submitting a quote for Enterprise, Decennial, and/ or Geospatial BPAs  precludes 
participation in the  SPS BPA in any capacity  (Prime or subcontractor).  
• Quoters submitting a quote for the SPS BPA may participate only in the SPS BPA and 
may not participate (as a Prime or subcontractor) in Enterprise, Decennial, or 
Geospatial BPAs. 
L.5.3 PERMITTED PARTICIPATION PATHS 
Each Quoter must choose one of the following participation paths: 
• Enterprise BPA only; or 
• Decennial BPA only; or 
• Geospatial BPA only; or 

 
4 
 
• Any combination of Enterprise + Decennial + Geospatial; or 
• SPS BPA only 
Participation (Prime or subcontractor) in the SPS BPA mutually excludes participation (Prime 
or subcontractor) in the Enterprise, Decennial, or Geospatial BPAs.  
L.5.4 RESPONSIBILITY FOR TEAM COMPLIANCE 
It is the Quoter’s responsibility to ensure that all proposed subcontractors fully understand and 
comply with the solicitation’s BPA quoting restrictions. If any proposed subcontractor is found 
to be in violation of these restrictions, either at submission or at any point during evaluation: 
• The non-compliant subcontractor will be removed from the Quoter’s team ; and 
• If the Quoter relied upon that subcontractor for Demonstrated Prior Experience or 
Key Personnel, those portions of the submission will also be removed from 
consideration.  
Removal of such required information may result in the quote being deemed incomplete and 
non-compliant, and therefore ineligible for award for the affected BPA(s). 
L.6. GENERAL  
Quoters are instructed to read the entire RFQ document, including all attachments, prior to 
preparing your quote. Omission of any information from the quotation submission requirements 
may result in the quote being deemed “Noncompliant” and disqualified from further evaluation.  
To aid in the evaluations, quotes shall be clearly and concisely written, neatly prepared, properly 
indexed, and logically assembled. Quoters are asked to bear in mind that all material submitted 
should be directly pertinent to the solicitation requirements. Extraneous narratives, elaborate 
brochures, marketing materials, and so forth, shall not be submitted. All pages of each part shall 
be appropriately numbered, identified with the name of the Quoter, the date, and the solicitation 
number to the extent practicable.  
Quoter’s submissions shall address all requirements identified in Section L and the evaluation 
factors set forth in Section M of the solicitation. 
In responding to this RFQ, it is the Quoter’s responsibility to provide current, complete, and 
accurate information in their quotes. If in reviewing the quotes, the Government identifies or 
otherwise learns that the provided information is not accurate or misrepresents the Quoter’s 
status or capabilities, that information may be used by the contracting officer to determine the 
Quoter not being eligible for award.  
Quotes must be received on or before the due dates and times specified in the RFQ, and any 
subsequent amendments. Only information received on or before the due date and time shall be 
considered for award. Any submission, modification, or revision received after the time specified 
for receipt of quotes is “late” and will not be considered unless it is received before award is 
made AND the Contracting Officer determines that accepting the late quote would not unduly 
delay the acquisition. Facsimile quotes and hard copy quotes via portal mail will not be accepted.  
Quotes shall be submitted via email to Matthew Gore at matthew.s.gore@census.gov and Bryan 

 
5 
 
Shearer at bryan.allan.shearer@census.gov and the submission email shall be entitled “Quoter 
Name – (BPA(s))”)) 
The quotes shall be valid for no less than 210 calendar days from the quote due date. The Quoter 
shall make a clear statement that the quote is valid for the specified number of calendar dates in 
the Business & Price Volumes of the quote. The Government reserves the right to request 
additional information after receipt of the Quoter’s response to the solicitation.  
This RFQ does not commit the Government to pay any cost for the preparation and submission 
of a quote.  
Penalty for False Statements: Quoters are advised that 18 U.S.C. 1001 prohibits the knowing 
and willful submissions of false, fictitious, or fraudulent statements, representations, or 
certifications to the Government. Any such submission may result in criminal penalties, 
administrative remedies, elimination from this competition, or other actions as permitted by law. 
L.6.1 COMMUNICATIONS  
Prior to releasing the Draft RFQ, the Government will issue drafts of Sections C, J, L, and M. 
The intent will be to receive questions and comments solely during the pre-solicitation period to 
identify discrepancies and clarify requirements for the final solicitation. 
The Government does not intend to conduct a formal questions & answers (Q&A) period after 
the final RFQ is issued. However, the Government reserves the right to open an additional round 
of questions if deemed necessary and in the Government’s best interest.  
All correspondence in conjunction with this solicitation must be directed to the Government 
Contracting Officer and Contract Specialist identified below: 
Contracting Officer: Matthew Gore 
E-mail address: matthew.s.gore@census.gov 
Contracting Specialist: Bryan Shearer 
E-mail address: bryan.allan.shearer@census.gov 
L.6.2 ANTICIPATED SOLICITATION SCHEDULE 
The dates included in the table below are intended to be helpful with planning purposes and 
reflect the Government’s anticipated solicitation schedule. It is the Government’s intent to 
adhere to this schedule. However, these dates are subject to change. Any changes will be clearly 
communicated to the Quoters.  

 
6 
 
Table 2. Anticipated Solicitation Milestone Schedule 
Activity Anticipated Date 
Final RFQ Release May 2026 
Phase1 Submission Due  May 2026 
Advisory Down-Select Notifications, Schedule of Oral Briefings June 2026 
Phase 2 Submission Due June 2026 
Oral Briefings June – July 2026 
CenTAM BPA Award Notifications July 2026 
Decennial BPA Order 01 (DTAM) Award Notification July 2026 
Geospatial BPA Order 01 (GeoTAM) Award Notification July 2026 
L.6.3 QUOTATION PREPARATION INSTRUCTIONS 
The instructions below establish the required format and content so quotes can be 
evaluated equitably and efficiently. 
Two-Phase Process  
This solicitation uses a two-phase approach to streamline submissions for each of the 4 BPAs: 
• Phase 1 – Demonstrated Prior Experience  (Written) 
o Mandatory. Quoters must submit a Phase 1 response for each BPA they intend 
to pursue. 
• Advisory Notifications: after Phase 1, the Government will advise, per BPA, whether 
the quoter should proceed to Phase 2.  
o Each BPA is evaluated separately and independently  and will receive its own 
individual confidence rating  for each Phase 1 Factor. 
o A Quoter may be advised to proceed for one BPA (e.g., Decennial) but not for 
another (e.g., Enterprise).  
• Phase 2 – Technical & Management Oral Briefing (Oral & Written); Key 
Personnel (Decennial BPA & Geospatial BPA only) (Written); and Price (Written) 
NOTE (Decennial and Geospatial dependency on Enterprise): Decennial and Geospatial 
BPA work relies extensively on core Enterprise capabilities; therefore, Enterprise topics (Phase 
1 Demonstrated Prior Experience Examples #1–4 and Phase 2 Technical & Management Oral 
Briefing Topics #1-3) as well as prior experience are required inputs for Decennial and 
Geospatial Quoters. 
NOTE (SPS independence): The SPS BPA is independent of the Enterprise, Decennial, and 
Geospatial BPAs. SPS quoters respond only to SPS-specific requirements. 
Quotation Requirements 
Table 3 below identifies all required submission files for each Phase, including the applicable 

 
7 
 
format and page or time limits. 
For efficiency, Quoters may submit a single, combined file for certain quote requirements when 
submitting quotes for multiple BPAs. However, some quote requirements must be submitted as 
separate, stand-alone files for each BPA. The table below identifies, for each requirement,

QUESTION NO. QUESTION REFERENCE ANSWER

UNITED STATES CAPITOL POLICE 
OFFICE OF ACQUISITION MANAGEMENT 
 
 
 
 
April 10, 2026
 
 
 
Subject: Request for Quotes (RFQ) for Comprehensive Wellness Application for the 
United States Capitol Police 
 
 
To Whom It May Concern, 
 
The United States Capitol Police (USCP), Office of Liebengood Center for Wellness 
(LCW) has a requirement for a comprehensive wellness application. You are hereby 
requested to submit a technical quote for a comprehensive wellness application in 
accordance with the enclosed Statement of Work. USCP intends to procure services 
under a Firm Fixed-Price contract type. 
 
 
Factors for Award 
 
Award will be made in accordance with the procedures of FAR Part 13 – Simplified 
Acquisition Procedures. The basis for award will be best value. In so doing, the 
Government may award to other than the lowest priced quote or other than the highest 
technically rated quote. This acquisition will be conducted using a phased approach. Phase I 
will consist of potential proposers submitting a document that addresses how it will address 
all elements under Factor 1. The next phase, a Pre-Award Demonstration will be scheduled at 
the conclusion of initial evaluation of quoters from Phase I. This demonstration will be 
considered Phase II of the RFQ. The purpose of the demonstration is to allow up to three (3) 
of the highest rated quoters to showcase the Wellness Application and present the 
functionality of the final product to USCP stakeholders.  All three Parts of the quote (listed 
below) will be considered when making the determination of the highest rated Quoters. 
Quoters that are selected to present for Phase II shall be required to prepare a virtual 
presentation (MS Teams) that shall not exceed one hour. Evaluation criteria for the 
Demonstration will be provided at the conclusion of Phase I evaluation.  
 
Phase I. The following technical evaluation factors are in order of importance:  
 
Factor 1 – Technical Capability and Understanding of the Requirements 
  
For Factor 1, the contractor shall demonstrate comprehensive understanding of the tasks 
outlined in Section C3-C7 of the SOW to include the following sub factors: 
  
1.1. Approach to accomplish scope elements in C3-C7 of the SOW 


1.2. Expertise accomplishing the elements in C3-C7 of the SOW 
1.3. Strategy for accomplishing the elements in C3-C7 of the SOW 
 
Factor 2 – Past Performance 
 
Past Performance: The Related Corporate Experience section shall briefly describe 
the experience of the vendor performing work similar to this one from the past three 
(3) years. Provide a demonstration of the contractor's knowledge and understanding 
of the SOW and stated requirements in general. Short abstracts of related work shall 
include the name, current affiliation and phone number of the sponsor's project officer 
(or equivalent), and dates the work was performed. Additionally, quoters shall provide 
at least three (3) references that have received services from the contractor, which are 
comparable to those described in this solicitation. Projects must have been completed 
within the last two (2) years or shall be currently in progress. 
 
The vendors past performance will be evaluated under existing and prior contracts for 
similar products and services over the last three years. The USCP will focus on 
information that demonstrates quality of performance relative to the complexity of the 
procurement under consideration. Please submit no more than three (3) Past 
Performance references for similar efforts as described in the SOW. For each contract 
or task order provided as a reference, the vendor shall send the Past Performance 
Questionnaire (Attachment 2) to current clients for whom the firm has provided the 
same or similar services. Those clients providing past performance references shall 
submit an electronic version of the 
COMPLETED-FILLED OUT forms. Vendors 
shall include a list of clients who received the Past Performance Questionnaire, 
including the point-of-contact name, phone number, and email address. The past 
performance references must be for work performed in the capacity of a prime 
Contractor.
 
 
If a vendor has no past performance history in the requisite Contract amount, the 
vendor may submit information on past performance at lower dollar levels, providing 
the vendor adheres to the above limitations as to the number of chronologically 
consecutive Contracts. 
 
Vendors shall provide the contracts - regardless of dollar value and subject matter 
that were terminated for default or convenience by the Government within the past 
three (3) years. The vendor shall provide a brief explanation of the circumstances in 
each case. 
 
Evaluation rating for Factor 1: 
 
  
ADJECTIVAL RATING DESCRIPTION 
  
Fully Comply Excellent in all respects; offers one or more 

significant advantages not offset by 
disadvantages; very good probability of 
success with overall low degree of risk in 
meeting the Government’s requirements 
Comply High quality in most respects; offers one or 
more advantages not offset by 
disadvantages; good probability of success 
with overall low to moderate degree of risk 
in meeting the Governments requirements 
Does not Comply The quote contains major errors, omissions, 
or deficiencies, or an unacceptable high 
degree of risk in meeting the Government’s 
requirements; and these conditions cannot 
be corrected without a major rewrite of the 
quote 
Evaluation rating for Factor 2: 
Substantial 
Confidence  
Based on the Quoter’s recent (5 years) and relevant (similar in 
magnitude and scope of this effort) performance record, the 
Government has a high expectation that the Quoter will successfully 
perform.   
Satisfactory 
Confidence  
Based on the Quoter’s recent (5 years) and relevant (similar in 
magnitude and scope of this effort) performance record, the 
Government has a reasonable expectation that the Quoter   
 Will successfully perform. 
Limited 
Confidence  
Based on the Quoter’s recent (5 years) and relevant (similar in 
magnitude and scope of this effort) performance record, the 
Government has a limited expectation that the Quoter will 
successfully perform.   
No 
Confidence  
Based on the Quoter’s recent (5 years) and relevant (similar in 
magnitude and scope of this effort) performance record, the 
Government has no expectation that the Quoter will successfully 
perform.   
Unknown 
Confidence 
(Neutral)  
The Quoter does not have recent (5 years) and relevant (similar in 
magnitude and scope of this effort) performance; or the Quoter’s 
performance record is so sparse; a meaningful confidence rating 
cannot be reasonably assigned.   
Factor 3 - Pricing Table 

Pricing shall be in accordance with the CLINs identified in Section B to include the anticipated 
descriptions or categories needed under this Contract. However, the Contractor is encouraged to 
identify and state all elements needed for the proposed solution. 
 
Quote Submission Information 
 
Cover Letter: The cover letter shall include the quotation number, contractor name, 
address and phone number, a statement specifying the extent of agreement with all terms 
and conditions, and agreement to furnish any or all items upon which prices are offered at 
the price set opposite each item in Section B of the RFQ. The letter shall include the 
name, title, phone and facsimile numbers of persons authorized to negotiate on the 
contractor's behalf in connection with this RFQ, and the name, title and signature of the 
person authorized to sign the quotation. The letter must reference the quotation number 
and acknowledge that it transmits an offer in response to the quotation. It shall state the 
following items: Duns Number, Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN), and that the 
quotation is valid for sixty days from date of submission. The name of the official 
authorized to bind the Contractor's organization shall be clearly identified and if an 
amendment(s) is issued, the Contractor must acknowledge acceptance of the amendment.
 
 
Technical Capability and Understanding of the Requirement: The Technical portion of 
your quote (Part I) to include Technical Approach shall not exceed 10 pages excluding the 
biographies. If this portion of the quote exceeds the maximum page limitation, only the 
first 10 pages will be evaluated and the rest discarded. 
 
Past Performance (Part II): See Factor 2 above for Past Performance submission 
requirements.
 
 
(Part III) Price: completed pricing table as specified in Section B. There is no page limit 
for the pricing quote (Section B of the RFQ). 
 
Notice: The Technical Price portion of your quote must be submitted in separate 
documents. All documents associated with the written technical quote must not include 
any price information. 
 
All questions are due by Tuesday, April 14, 2026 (EDT) at noon via email: in accordance 
with Attachment 4 Questions and Answers Template. 
 
For your quotation to be considered responsive, it must be submitted no later than 2:00 
P.M., Eastern Daylight Savings Time on April 21, 2026, via email to 
Jennell.joyner@uscp.gov.
 
 
This RFQ contains proposed terms and conditions which are to be included in any 
resulting contract. Any exceptions or deviations to these terms and conditions must be 
clearly indicated in the Contractor's quote. Cover letters shall indicate whether any 
exceptions or deviations are contained in the quote, the RFQ or specification requirements 
to which exception or deviation is taken, the page number(s) of the quote affected, and the 

price impact if any, and reason for the exception/deviation unless the rationale is reflected 
in the quote itself. 
 
In the event that your company declines to submit a quotation, please communicate this 
fact to the Contract Specialist as soon as possible. 
 
Please be advised that the USCP is under no obligation to pay for any costs incurred in the 
preparation of this quotation or to procure any of the supplies or services identified herein.  
 
 
The Government reserves the right to award without discussions. 
 
 
If you need additional information, please contact Jennell Joyner at Jennell. joyner@uscp.gov. 
 
 
 
Sincerely, 
 
 
 
 
Michael Iszard 
Contracting Officer 
 
Enclosed: 
 
I) Statement of Work 
2) Request for Quotation - QSU USCPQ202600001 
3) Past Performance Questionnaire 
4) Questions and Answers Template 
5) Vendor Enrollment Form

SECTION C 
Statement of Work 
Comprehensive Wellness Application for the United States Capitol Police 
C.1 Purpose 
The purpose of this requirement is to acquire a comprehensive digital wellness application 
that supports the physical, mental, emotional, spiritual, and occupational well-being of the 
United States Capitol Police workforce, including officers, civilian employees, supervisors, 
retirees, and family members. The Contractor shall provide a secure, confidential, user-
friendly mobile and web platform that delivers 24/7 access to wellness resources, 
professional support connections, peer support tools, educational materials, analytics, and 
crisis response pathways that are tailored to the needs of law enforcement personnel. 
C.2 Background 
The United States Capitol Police established the Howard C. Liebengood Center for Wellness 
to expand accessibility to wellness resources for its personnel and their families. The 
Department requires a modern, law-enforcement-focused wellness application that 
consolidates resources, enhances early support for employees, preserves confidentiality, 
and strengthens resilience across the workforce. The application shall incorporate best 
practices in law enforcement wellness, employee assistance, and behavioral health support 
technologies. 
C.3 Scope 
The Contractor shall provide a commercial or commercial-derivative wellness application 
solution, including licensing, configuration, implemen

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
1 
1.0 OVERVIEW 
1
.1   INTRODUCTION AND BACKGROUND 
T
he Office of Safeguards (Safeguards) within Governmental Liaison, Disclosure and Safeguards 
(GLDS) function of Privacy, Governmental Liaison and Disclosure (PGLD) is responsible for 
ensuring that Federal Tax Information (FTI) provided outside of the IRS is secured and used 
appropriately. Safeguards assess approximately 300 federal, state and local agencies, as well as 
their authorized contractors receiving FTI under various sub-sections of Internal Revenue Code 
(IRC) §6103 and other IRC provisions. Agencies must implement appropriate safeguards to 
protect confidentiality and prevent unauthorized access or use of FTI. Safeguards works in 
partnership with the recipient agencies to identify and mitigate risks to FTI. The specific 
safeguard requirements are outlined in IRC §6103(p)(4) and detailed in Publication 1075, Tax 
Information Security Guidelines for Federal, State, and Local Agencies. 
P
ublication 1075 is primarily based upon National Institute of Standards & Technology (NIST) 
Special Publication (SP) 800-53, Assessing Security and Privacy Controls for Information 
Systems and Organizations for moderate risk systems, IRC §6103 statutory requirements and IRS 
policy and procedure. All automated information systems and networks which receive, process, 
store, transmit, or protect FTI must meet these information protection requirements. 
2.0
 OBJECTIVE 
Th
e objective of this requirement is to increase security of FTI and to ensure consistent 
application of information security standards across all partner agency information systems. 
3
.0   REQUIREMENT TASKS 
T
he mission of Safeguards is to promote taxpayer confidence in the integrity of the tax system by 
ensuring the confidentiality of IRS information provided to federal, state, and local agencies.  
Safeguards verifies compliance with IRC §6103(p)(4) safeguard requirements through the 
identification and mitigation of any risk of loss, breach, or misuse of FTI held by external 
government agencies. 
T
he Contractor shall provide cybersecurity and information technology (IT) expertise. The 
Contractor shall optimize its processes, to reduce costs and minimize risk to FTI in possession of 
agency partners, while continuing to meet all regulatory and agency documented standards and 
guidance; and obtain information technology expertise and ancillary support to enable Safeguards 
to meet on-going and new regulatory objectives having an impact on the Safeguards Program. 
T
he Contractor shall collaborate with IRS leadership, staff, and other contractors in support of 
agency computer security reviews. Support shall include the following: conducting preparatory 
agency outreach; conducting pre-review research and analysis to determine scope and potential 
areas of concern; executing automated and manual computer security compliance scans of partner 
agency systems during the review; preparing reports, documenting scan results and other 
computer security findings; and analyzing and responding to various partner agency computer 
security information submissions. Other computer security review-related support includes 
stakeholder outreach, risk-based modeling for agency review selection, computer security review 
methodology updates, and general IT expertise. The Contractor shall provide ad hoc support for 
Draft

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
                          SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
 
 2 
the assessment of other projects, pilots, and new legislation that include provisions falling within 
the purview of Safeguards. 
 
In accordance with Executive Order (EO) 14179, the contractor is permitted to use Artificial 
Intelligence (AI) to assist in this task.  
 
The Contractor shall adhere to IRS Publication 1075 (Tax Information Security Guidelines for 
Federal, State, and Local Agencies), Federal Security Modernization Act (FISMA), Privacy Act 
of 1974, Internal Revenue Code  §6103, NIST Risk Management Framework and IRS Safeguards 
Program requirements when utilizing artificial intelligence tools to enhance technical support 
capabilities assisting in the management and updates of Safeguards Computer Security 
Evaluation Matrices (SCSEM’s) and associated automated evaluation files.  
 
Contractors shall not process, store, or have access to FTI, PII, SSI, Classified or controlled 
unclassified information and any agency specific confidential data.  
 
The Contractor shall utilize only publicly available information from approved sources including 
IRS Publication 1075, NIST Special Publications and Standards, IRS Safeguard Program public 
website materials, Federal regulations and guidance documents and publicly available industry 
best practice standards to include Center for Internet Security (CIS) Benchmarks and Defense 
Information Systems Agency (DISA) Secure Technical Implementation Guides (STIG). All 
queries submitted must contain no FTI or PII; referenced only publicly available information, 
comply with informational handling classifications and be reviewed for compliance prior to 
submission. 
 
3.1   TASK 1- ORIENTATION BRIEFING  
 
The contractor shall facilitate an orien tation briefing via a video conference call (Microsoft (MS) 
Teams, Zoom, etc.) with its representatives, ensuring all parties have a common understanding of 
the task order tasks and requirements. The contractor shall bring, at a minimum, its key personnel 
to this meeting. 
 
3.2   TASK
 2, RAMP-UP KNOWLEDGE TRANSFER/TRANSITION-IN  
  
3.2.1 RAMP-UP KNOWLEDGE 
 
Ramp-Up Knowledge Transfer is defined as the contractor acquiring and understanding the 
necessary documentation, information, and processes related to any work in progress. The 
Government anticipates this task will be performed in conjunction with the Transition-In 
activities after the award of this task order.  
 
The contractor shall appoint a knowledge transfer manager serving as key personnel to oversee all 
areas of information that are accounted for and all knowledge of information is transferred from 
the current incumbent. This can be the Project Manager (PM) or other approved key personnel. 
Prior to the transfer of knowledge, the contractor shall develop a final Knowledge Transfer Plan 
(KTP). The final KTP shall identify the contractor resources and roles involved in the transfer 
process, provide a list of risks and mitigation strategies for the transfer, and contain a detailed 
resource balanced project schedule developed with Microsoft Project or compatible equivalent. 
Draft

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
                          SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
 
 3 
The project schedule shall identify tasks, dependencies, deliverables, and milestones. The project 
schedule shall be at a sufficient level of detail to track progress on a bi-weekly basis. 
 
 
The contractor shall meet with the IRS bi-weekly (twice a month) to discuss the progress of the 
knowledge transfer. The contractor shall show an updated MS Project Schedule or compatible 
equivalent and discuss any risks or concerns at this time. 
 
In addition to the Ramp-Up activities in the approved KTP, the contractor shall perform the 
following activities during the Ramp Up: 
 
•
 Assign personnel and submit security background packages within 10 calendar 
days to ensure background investigations are completed efficiently and gain 
access to IRS’s network.  
• Create a project schedule at a sufficient level of detail to track progress on a 
weekly basis. 
•
 Request access within 2 business days to tools and documentation (based on 
                          successful adjudication of background clearance of contractor personnel) 
• Review and understand all the technical and process documentation.  
• Review and understand the case management system. 
•           Knowledge Transfer/Transition-In Status Reports, for the duration of  
                   the transition-in period only, shall detail status of activities identified  
                   in the final KTP. 
 
Note: Completed background requests shall be sent to IRS COR/Alternate COR via encrypted 
electronic file. Any incomplete packages will be rejected for re-submission within 2 workdays.
 
 
The contractor shall provide a KTP and a MS project or compatible equivalent schedule that is 
accepted by the Government and successfully completes knowledge transfer in accordance with 
the plan with a total transition-in project schedule not to exceed one hundred twenty (120) 
calendar days after Government acceptance of the KTP.  In addition, the Contractor shall provide 
complete and accurate status reports for the KTP. 
 
3.2.2 TRANSITION- IN  
 
The Transition-In task permits an effective transition from the incumbent Contractor to the new 
Contractor. The Government estimates this task will take approximately 120 calendar days to 
complete from date of award of the task order. Successful transition can be defined by its capacity 
to maintain full continuity of operations. To ensure a successful, low-risk transition, the 
contractor shall follow the approved Transition-In Plan and keep the government fully informed 
of status throughout the transition-in period. During the transition-in period, the incumbent is 
responsible for performance. This Task would be worked concurrently with Task 2, Ramp-Up 
Knowledge Transfer.  
 
In addition to the transition activities in the approved KTP, the contractor shall ensure the 
following activities have been successfully completed within 75 days of the transition-in period to 
ensure timely commencement of services after the 120-Day Transition-In period: 
 
1. Obtain final access to tools and documentation 
2. Comprehend and successfully perform all the technical and process documentation. 
Draft

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
4 
3. Comprehend and successfully apply the existing data and system security policies in
place.
The Tran
sition-in Checklist provides a vehicle to verify completion of the necessary transition-in 
activities. The Transition-in Checklist shall be developed by the Contractor and shall include all 
necessary activities to ensure an effective transition from the incumbent Contractor to a new 
Contractor that were performed. The Transition-in Checklist shall be signed by both, the 
Contractor and the COR after completion of the task. 
3.3   TA
SK 3, TASK MANAGEMENT 
3.3.1 Gene
ral Requirements 
The Contractor shall provide personnel, including an equivalent contractor Subject Matter Expert 
(SME), to perform the requirements set forth herein. 
3.3.2 Monthly Status Report (MSR) Call 
The Contractor shall facilitate a Monthly Status Report (MSR) Call that includes presentation 
materials, such as a slide deck and supporting documentation, outlining expenditures, billings, 
progress, status, and any problems or issues encountered during task performance. The MSR shall 
include financial projections and an overview of support provided during the previous invoice 
period, including a list of all deliverables accepted and those in progress. All MSR materials and 
activities are subject to review and acceptance by the Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) 
and the Technical Point of Contact (TPOC). 
3.3.3 Task Order Program Management Plan (PMP) 
The Contractor shall develop, implement, and maintain a Task Order Program Management Plan 
(PMP), subject to Government review and approval, manage, track, and evaluate performance 
throughout the period of performance. The PMP shall include control policies and procedures 
consistent with standard industry practices for project administration, execution, and tracking. 
The PMP may consist of multiple components, including, a project plan and performance status 
dashboards. The PMP shall include a consolidated roll-up of performance data for all active 
subtasks and shall be structured to support reuse for MSR Calls and other reporting requirements. 
The Contractor shall maintain the PMP in accordance with agreed-upon project management best 
practices. The PMP is subject to review and acceptance by the COR and TPOC. 
3.3.4 Adminis
trative Touchpoint 
The Contractor shall attend and facilitate a weekly Administrative Touchpoint meeting, not to 
exceed one (1) hour in duration, as scheduled by the COR. The purpose of this meeting shall be to 
address onboarding activities, Government Furnished Equipment (GFE), and other administrative 
matters. The Contractor shall prepare, update, and present a performance status dashboard that 
identifies completed deliverables, timeliness of performance, and any deliverables returned due to 
deficient quality. These meetings and associated materials are subject to review by the COR and 
TPOC. 
Draft

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
SAFEGUARDS IT COMPUTER SECURITY SUPPORT 
5 
3.3.5 Annual Planning Meeting 
The Contractor shall schedule, coordinate, and facilitate an Annual Planning Meeting with the 
COR, and the TPOC to discuss planned activities for the upcoming option year. The Contractor 
shall prepare and submit meeting materials to the COR for review and approval in advance of the 
meeting.  
3.4   
TASK 4, COMPUTER SECURITY REVIEWS 
T
he Contractor shall work with external partner agency stakeholders to execute system 
configuration checks, interview system users (e.g., administrators), and to examine system 
artifacts (including policies and procedures) against management, operational and technical 
requirements, Center for Internet Security (CIS )Benchmarks, NIST requirements, and current 
IRS standards (e.g., IRS Publication 1075, IRC §6103). The scope of the assessment (“review”) 
will include all hardware and software associated with the processing, storing, or exchange of FTI 
including mainframes, operating system, telecom devices, applications, multi-function devices, 
firewalls, servers, cloud infrastructure, and databases. The contractor shall use the Safeguards 
Computer Security Evaluation Matrices (SCSEMs) and automated evaluation files as a guide to 
perform Computer Security Reviews on local, state or federal agencies, and their authorized 
contractors, that receive FTI under IRC §6103.  
Completion of computer security reviews resulted in the Safeguard review report (SRR) being 
issued to the external partner agency. The SRR consists of Sections A through H. Sections A 
through G are completed by the IRS staff and Section H dealing with information technology is 
completed by the contractor.  The SRR serves to identify an agency’s compliance with their 
statutory obligations under IRC 6103(p)(4) and provide guidance to the agency to improve their 
security posture and meet the safeguarding standards described in Publication 1075. Please refer 
to the Computer Security Safeguards Review Report Standard Opera

search_text:
Performance Work Statement

DSpace Institutional Repository Support Services

Dudley Knox Library

Naval Postgraduate School

Background/Introduction:

The Naval Postgraduate School (NPS), located in Monterey, California, supports the U.S. Navy, other military branches, and foreign military services with postgraduate education for their personnel. Additionally, NPS provides significant research capabilities to the Department of Defense, Department of Homeland Security, and others. Dudley Knox Library (DKL) is the academic library at NPS and provides research scholarly content relevant to NPS instructional and research needs. Some of this content is housed and delivered via Calhoun, a centralized repository for the NPS scholarly record and archival materials.

The Naval Postgraduate School's Dudley Knox Library requires a Registered Duraspace Service Provider to provide software upgrades, customizations and maintenance for its DSpace-based institutional repository, Calhoun. (https://calhoun.nps.edu). Calhoun is built on DSpace open-source software loaded on internal NPS servers. Calhoun runs DSpace v.7.6 with customizations and integration with 3rd party services including handle server and backup of content into Internet Archive and AWS cloud storage. DKL has a second server that mirrors Calhoun for the purpose of testing batch ingests before they enter the Calhoun repository. Calhoun uses extended Dublin Core metadata, contains over 71,000 records; ingests at least 2,500 records per year; and has a history of 99.7% annual up time.

Scope:

The scope of this contract includes DSpace upgrades, configuration and dependency management, customizations, delivered and maintained modules, and technical support and monitoring.

Tasks:

3.1	Upgrades

The contractor shall support the ongoing lifecycle of the NPS DSpace-based institutional repository (Calhoun), ensuring that the platform remains secure, supported, and aligned with community best practices to include:

Planning and execution of scheduled platform upgrades

Coordination of upgrade activities across test and production environments

Validation of repository functionality following upgrades

Documentation and administrative handoff

Upgrade readiness assessments, including dependency analysis and evaluation of impacts to existing customizations and reusable platform features prior to committing to a target release.

Upgrade to the latest stable DSpace 9 Long-Term Support (LTS) release in 2027

A subsequent major upgrade in or around 2029, version determination to be based on platform stability, community adoption, and NPS requirements.

During periods between major platform upgrades, the contractor shall support platform stability, configuration updates, dependency maintenance, and incremental improvements, as well as activities that reduce future upgrade risk and support evolving NPS priorities.

3.2     Configuration and Dependency Management

Ongoing configuration management to ensure compatibility with supported versions of underlying software components to include:

Application configuration updates

Dependency upgrades (e.g., Java, Tomcat, PostgreSQL, Solr)

Security patching and configuration hardening.

3.3	Customizations

This task covers customizations and enhancements developed specifically for NPS workflows, policies, and repository use cases.

3.3.1   	Repository Architecture and Data Modeling

Support for the evolution and maintenance of the repository’s organizational structure and metadata model to include:

Maintenance and refinement of DSpace entity-based modeling

Ongoing support for repository reorganization strategies

Metadata schema adjustments and governance support.

	3.3.2   Identity and Branding

Support for NPS-specific user experience and access requirements to include:

Maintenance of NPS branding and theming

Integration with NPS authentication and single sign-on (SSO) systems

	3.3.3   Ingest, Submission, and Workflow Enhancements

Support for NPS-defined ingest, submission, and content management processes to include:

Maintenance of batch ingest workflows (e.g., CSV + ZIP)

Support for custom submission workflows and input forms

Support for curation tasks and automated processing

Workflow improvements driven by NPS operational need

3.3.4    Selective Exports

Search-based metadata and citation export functionality implemented as an NPS-specific customization to include:

Search-driven export workflows

Support for multiple output formats

3.3.5    Advanced Replication Task Suite (RTS)

Support for backup, replication, and restore of DSpace items implemented as an NPS-specific customization to include:

Versioned replication of items and bitstreams

Restore and recovery capabilities.

3.3.6   NPS-Specific Enhancement Workpackages

The following workpackages represent discrete, independently exercisable NPS-specific enhancements that have been implemented historically and may require ongoing maintenance, refinement, testing, or adaptation during the renewal period. Each workpackage may be scheduled, prioritized, deferred, or exercised independently by NPS during the contract period.

3.4	Modules

Modules are reusable platform components delivered and maintained across supported DSpace versions. Modules represent discrete, reusable software components delivered and maintained as part of the NPS DSpace platform.

3.4.1	Content and Usage Analysis (CUA) Module
	

Provides insight into repository usage and engagement to include:

Collection and aggregation of usage metrics

Item-level and repository-level analytics

Reporting capabilities aligned with DSpace data models

Ongoing compatibility with supported DSpace versions

3.5	Operational Support

3.5.1	Technical Support Services

The contractor shall provide ongoing operational support for the NPS DSpace platform to include:

Incident response and troubleshooting

Configuration and administrative assistance

Support for upgrades, change requests, and feature analysis

3.5.2	Monitoring and Reporting

Support for proactive system monitoring and transparency to include:

Automated monitoring of platform health

Incident detection and response

Periodic reporting on system performance and support activity.

3.5.3	Execution Support

The contractor shall provide project management, testing, coordination, and documentation support necessary to successfully execute the scope of work defined in this agreement to include:

Day-to-day coordination and scheduling of work activities across upgrade, customization, and support efforts

Support for testing and validation activities associated with upgrades and enhancement workpackages

Preparation and maintenance of technical and administrative documentation related to delivered work

4.0	Deliverables:

5.0	Performance Requirements Summary:

If performance falls below the AQL defined above, the Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) shall document the instance(s), coordinate with the Contracting Officer and advise the Contractor.  The Contractor will be requested to review the documentation and provide a written response on how performance will be corrected in the future.  Re-performance of any work for failure to perform in accordance with the specified AQL or task requirement shall be completed at the Contractor’s own expense and at no additional cost to the Government.

Minimum Technical Requirements:

Registered DSpace Service Provider

Expertise with management and support of DSpace v.7.6 (or higher) in a library setting

Expertise with Linux Enterprise Systems

Expertise with Handle.net

Web and Java server application expertise, i.e. NGINX, Tomcat, Apache

7.0	Period of Performance:

  

BASE: 9/15/26 – 9/14/27

OY1: 9/15/27 – 9/14/28

OY2: 9/15/28 – 9/14/29

OY3: 9/15/29 – 9/14/30

OY4: 9/15/30 – 9/14/31

8.0     Place of Performance:

All work shall be conducted remotely from the contractor’s place of business.

Government Furnished Property:

Not applicable.

Work Week and Hours of Operation:

The Contractor shall provide services during normal working hours excluding federal holidays. Normal working hours are 0700-1700 Pacific Time, Monday through Friday, unless requirements dictate otherwise. Exceptions can be permitted by the TPOC upon request and at the TPOC’s discretion.

Following is a list of holidays observed by the Government.   

Name of Holiday			Time of Observance

New Year’s Day		 	1 January

Martin Luther King Jr. Day		Third Monday in January

President's Day			Third Monday in February

Memorial Day			Last Monday in May

Juneteenth				19 June

Independence Day 			4 July

Labor Day				First Monday in September 

Columbus Day			Second Monday in October

Veteran's Day			11 November

Thanksgiving Day			Fourth Thursday in November

Christmas Day			25 December

If any of the above holidays occur on a Saturday or a Sunday, then such holiday shall be observed by the Contractor in accordance with the practice as observed by the assigned Government employees at the using activity

11.0   Transition Activities: 

It is essential to the Government that services required under this PWS are performed without interruption. At the conclusion of any performance period, including option periods or extensions, the services provided under this PWS may be awarded to another contractor. The contractor in place shall be required to assist in the transition activities. 

Security Requirements:

Must have a favorably adjudicated T3 security investigation.

    

This requirement is unclassified. Contractor Key Personnel must be U.S. Citizens. Contractors performing on this contract are required to familiarize themselves with, and participate in, the Naval Postgraduate School’s OPSEC program. Must be familiar with and comply with NAVPGSCOLINST 3432.1B, the NPS Critical Information List, DoDI 5205.2E and their applicable references. The contractor will be required to complete OPSEC and intelligence training within 30 days of beginning the work or provide proof of OPSEC and counterintelligence training completed within the previous 12 months. The contractor may not publicly release any information about developmental work or curriculum at NPS without prior written approval from the Preliminary Investigator (PI).

Identification of Contractor Employees:

In accordance with DFAR 211.106, there shall be a clear distinction between Government employees and service contractor employees. Service contractor employees shall identify themselves as contractor personnel by introducing themselves or being introduced as contractor personnel. Contractor personnel will be required to obtain and wear badges or other visible identification for meetings with Government personnel to provide a clear distinction between service contractor employees and Government personnel. In addition, contractor personnel shall appropriately identify themselves as contractor employees in telephone conversations and in formal and informal written correspondence. They must also ensure that all documents or reports produced by contractors are suitably marked as contractor products or that contractor participation is appropriately disclosed. 

Non-Personal Services Statement:

Contractor employees performing services under this order will be controlled, directed, and supervised at all times by management personnel of the contractor. Contractor management will insure that employees properly comply with the performance work standards outlined in the PWS. Contractor employees will perform their duties independent of, and without the supervision of, any Government official or other Defense Contractor. The tasks, duties, and responsibilities set forth in the task order may not be interpreted or implemented in any manner that results in any contractor employee creating or modifying Federal policy, obligating the appropriated funds of the United States Government, overseeing the work of Federal employees, or otherwise violating the prohibitions set forth in Parts 7.5 and 37.1 of the Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR).  The Government will control access to the facility and will perform the inspection and acceptance of the completed work.

SUPTXT 204-9400 (4-22) CONTRACTOR UNCLASSIFIED ACCESS TO FEDERALLY CONTROLLED  
FACILITIES, SENSITIVE INFORMATION, INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY SYSTEMS OR PROTECTED HEALTH INFORMATION

The following shall be inserted in full text in all solicitations and contracts (including commercial acquisitions) which require contractor unclassified access to federally controlled facilities, sensitive information, Information Technology (IT) systems or protected health information. For commercial acquisitions, this text shall be incorporated into either the PWS or PWS.

The security text does not apply in cases where the contractor/vendor does not have access to Navy Marine Corps Intranet (NMCI) computers, is not issued a Common Access Card (CAC) and is involved in training or other short term duties of less than 30 days’ duration that allow for the use of a visitor request. In these cases, the government employee must submit a Visitor Access Request (VAR) to the main gate or applicable processing entity for your facility and assume responsibility to escort those without CAC Credentials.

This local text does not apply to non-United States (U.S.) Nationals (foreign nationals) who are contactor employees performing work overseas. The local text is applicable to the U.S. Nationals living in the U.S. or overseas who are performing work on a Navy contract. 

The investigation of a non-U.S. national at a foreign location must be consistent with a National Agency Check with Written Inquiries (NACI), to the extent possible and include a fingerprint check against the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) criminal history database, an FBI investigations file (name check) search and a name check against the terrorist screening database. Also, the above cited reference notes that Foreign Nationals may not be granted CAC credentials until completion of their investigation and not in the interim.

 Per Department of Defense Memorandum (DoDM) 5200.2, Department of Defense (DoD) components must initiate and ensure completion of a background investigation before applying the credentialing standards to a non-U.S. national at a foreign location. The background investigation must be favorably adjudicated before a CAC can be issued to a non-U.S. national at a foreign location. The type of background investigation may vary based on standing reciprocity treaties concerning identity assurance and information exchanges that exist between the U.S. and its allies or agency agreements with the host country.

CONTRACTOR UNCLASSIFIED ACCESS TO FEDERALLY CONTROLLED FACILITIES, SENSITIVE INFORMATION, IT SYSTEMS OR PROTECTED HEALTH INFORMATION

search_text:
Aeronautical Mobile Application Request for Information - Questions for Respondents

General

Discuss your company’s experience with creating Department of War (DOW) ISO images of DVDs.

What exposure has your company had with military aviation operations (e.g. mission planning, DAFIF data processing, Terminal procedure creation or evaluation, airfield surveys, airfield data collection.  etc.)?

What is your company’s experience working with data management and quality tools that would enable data monitoring, data auditing, and data quality assurance?

What is your company’s experience developing and managing Application Program Interface (APIs) and/or Software Development Kits (SDKs)?

Has your company had contracts requiring it to work based on the Aeronautical Information Regulation and Control (AIRAC) cycle?

Describe your company history developing software plug-ins to the Android Tactical Assault Kit (ATAK).

What level of experience does your company have with Aviation software development?

What is your experience working with Electronic Flight Bags (EFB)/ Electronic Knee Boards (EKB)?

What is your experience with 1801 flight plans and filing?

What is your experience with creating applications that do not require an internet connection to have the data updated?

What is your company's demonstrated experience in managing Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud environments specifically for DOW applications? Please provide examples of past projects, including their scope and scale.

Could you describe your experience with managing applications that are utilized by foreign partners?

Did you set up access controls based on data sharing agreements?

How would you approach the management and dissemination of aeronautical (aero) data through a cloud environment, ensuring it is securely accessible to both DOW and authorized non-DOW sources?

Is your company ISO 9001 Certified?

If yes, what ISO Certifications does your company possess?

If not, how long would it take for you to obtain this certification?

Is your company AS9100D certified?

When did you get your AS9100D certification?

Have you ever failed an AS9100D audit?  

If so, what were the findings? 

Were the identified discrepancies corrected? 

Have you been re-audited, and what were the results?

Security

Describe your cyber security processing implementation working with NGA software security processes.

If your company employs non-US citizens, would you be able to isolate them from any work on this contract?

If your company currently doesn’t have classified clearances, are you willing to make the required investments to achieve the required clearances?

Technical

Describe your level of experience working with Aeronautical Publications and Charts as noted below

Was this experience to display aeronautical data and information for use in a mobile application or something else?

What is your experience working with Airport Diagrams, Instrument Approach Procedures, Enroute Charts, and Vertical Obstructions?

What is your experience geo-referencing charts?

What is your experience working with the NGA/DOW/FAA Table Formatted Aeronautical Data Set (TFADS-O) dataset?

What is your experience working with NAVPLAN charts?

What is your experience working with CADRG and/or ECRG?

What is your experience working with FAA Sectionals?

What is your experience working with commercial imagery from MAXAR, formally known as Digital Globe?

What is your experience working with Air Force Weather products and data? Was this work done with their new cloud-based system?

What is your experience developing APIs to access this data?

What is your experience obtaining a U.S. Air Force –1 weather briefing?

What is your experience working with Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast (ADS-B) services and/or devices?

What is your experience working with NGA/DOW/FAA Flight Information Publications and Flight Information Products (FLIP)?

What is your experience working with NGA/DOW/FAA Aeronautical Planning documents?

What is your experience working with Digital Aeronautical Flight Information File (DAFIF) versions and formats?

What is your experience with NGA’s Electronic – Instrument Procedure Library (E-IPL)?

What is your experience with Mobile Device Management (MDM) software?

Do you have experience developing software that enables data deployment to clients through mobile devices over a locally hosted Wi-Fi network?

What is your experience working with Air Mobility Command (AMC) GIANT Reports?

Do you have experience creating and converting maps into an MB Tile format?

What is your experience displaying documents in the .pdf format?

What is your experience displaying kml and/or kmz files on a moving map? 

What approach would you take to provide data to a user in a Denied Degraded Intermittent/Low-Bandwidth (DDIL) environment?

What is your experience with developing mobile applications (native and/or hybrid) for the following mobile platforms:  

iPad (iOS) 

Android

MacOS

Microsoft Windows

Google and Apple stores for application deployment

Configuration Management

Describe your lean/agile experience with evidence of demonstrated proficiency in agile software engineering including CI/CD pipeline methodology.

Describe your methodology for managing multiple customers requiring same or different features in the software baseline.

Describe your proficiency in the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) and agile product management (managing evolving requirements, Backlog grooming, Release Planning, Product Owner prioritization of the software build, etc.). 

Provide a recommendation for transitioning to Open Source modeled on the Code.mil framework for managing the iOS and Android mobile applications and explain your answer.

PAGE |  1
Approved for Public Release, 22-195
THE NGA SOFTWARE WAY
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
SOFTWARE METRICS
To deliver on its current and future mission, NGA needs to deliver useful software faster and more consistently. 
To measure progress toward this intent, NGA will track three key metrics for each software product: availability, lead time for 
changes, and deployment frequency. Additionally, each software product is expected to have its own product-specific metrics 
to track how well the product is working for its users. Together, these metrics balance speed and reliability and describe a product’s 
impact and value.
To consistently track these metrics and realize the above intent, this document provides a set of elements NGA and contractor teams 
must meet while building, iterating on, and operating software products. These elements ensure each software team has the people, 
processes, and tools in place to effectively operate at NGA. 
The metrics and elements in this document, collectively referred to as “The NGA Software Way,” serve as an implementation guide 
to supplement NGA’s Technology Strategy and provide additional specific guidance for teams building and operating software. 
BEFORE WRITING 
CODE WHEN BEGINNING  
AND THROUGHOUT 
DEVELOPMENT
BY THE FIRST DELIVERABLE
 THAT PROVIDES VALUE 
TO A USER

AVAILABILITY LEAD TIME FOR

PAGE |  2
Approved for Public Release, 22-195
A NOTE FROM THE CTO 
To deliver on its current and future mission, NGA needs to deliver useful software faster 
and more consistently. To do this, the NGA Technology Strategy describes changes in 
approach, tools, and behavior that will result in faster iterations, more flexible products, 
and better-satisfied users and customers. The NGA Software Way is an implementation 
guide to supplement NGA’s Technology Strategy and provide additional specific 
guidance for teams building and operating software.
Our intent is to get to a place where NGA software products are designed and built 
with users, can be automatically updated in production daily, and use product-specific 
metrics to make decisions. We believe in small, empowered, cross-functional teams that 
deeply understand their users and constantly work directly with those users to deliver on 
their mission.
Implementing The NGA Software Way won’t happen all at once; we must start with 
iteratively tracking and improving these metrics and meeting each element below. As 
we do this together, and with an understanding of this intent, we will successfully deliver 
software products with the speed, accuracy, and precision NGA’s mission demands.   
SOFTWARE METRICS
The three initial metrics all programs must track for each software product delivered to users are availability, lead time for changes, 
and deployment frequency. It is important to note that NGA is not unique in using these metrics. These are three of the primary 
metrics DevOps Research and Assessment (DORA) has studied over the past six years. These metrics use data from over 31,000 
professionals worldwide as well as rigorous statistical methods to study the most effective ways to develop and deploy technology. 
More information about the DORA metrics and study can be found in this DORA report: https://services.google.com/fh/files/misc/
state-of-devops-2019.pdf.
These metrics provide visibility into how NGA balances speed (deployment frequency and lead time for changes) and stability 
(availability) during product development and operations. These two efforts, speed and stability, were once believed to be negatively 
correlated — increasing one would decrease the other. DORA research has conclusively shown that speed and stability are 
outcomes that enable each other and high-performing teams do better in both of these areas. This is what NGA is aiming for and 
moving toward. 
For software teams using NGA’s enterprise-managed version control and Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) 
pipeline, deployment frequency and lead time for changes will be automatically tracked within those services’ dashboards. To 
enable that automation, the recommended tagging method can be found in the appendix. For teams not using NGA’s enterprise-
managed version control and CI/CD pipeline, lead time for changes and deployment frequency must be independently calculated and 
transparently shared. All teams are expected to regularly share their metrics with teammates, stakeholders, users, and leadership. 
Eventually, teams will programmatically share these metrics to a central, NGA-managed location. 
AVAILABILITY
Availability is about ensuring a product or service is accessible to users when they need it. At a 
macro level, availability represents technology teams and organizations meeting user expectations 
about the software they are deploying and operating. Any time a user is not able to access a 
software product, that product is unavailable. Availability may be impacted by actions a product 
team itself takes or through issues with upstream or downstream dependencies. There is no 
distinction in availability to users based on what caused the issue; whether planned or unplanned 
and no matter where the issue originated, the impact of a product being unavailable when users 
need it is the same.
Alex Loehr
NGA Chief Technology Officer

PAGE |  3
Approved for Public Release, 22-195
Higher availability is better, as it means the product is accessible when users need and expect it. 
Tracking availability automatically enables teams to know when something goes wrong, giving the 
opportunity to investigate issues more rapidly and proactively. Although teams may have different 
availability requirements depending on mission and user needs, teams are generally expected to 
maintain at least 99.9% availability in production, which translates to about one business day of 
downtime per year.
LEAD TIME FOR CHANGES
Lead time for changes tracks how long it takes to deliver a feature for mission use once it has 
been developed. Lead time for changes is calculated as the median time between when code is 
committed to when it is deployed to a production environment, where it can be used during an 
actual mission (not in a test environment). That figure is measured across all commits within a 
given time period. Measuring this enables teams to understand where bottlenecks are in getting 
features to production and prioritize where to optimize. 
Shorter lead times are better because they enable faster feedback on what is built and allow 
teams to course correct more rapidly. Additionally, faster lead times allow faster fixes with higher 
confidence when there are issues, which directly impacts availability as well. DORA research 
shows high-performing teams have lead times for changes of less than one week. 
DEPLOYMENT FREQUENCY 
Deployment frequency is how often a product is deployed to production. A product team will 
do many additional deployments to testing, development, staging, and other non-production 
environments. The deployment frequency metric is specifically related to deployments to 
production, however, where users actually use the product to complete the mission or function. 
To the maximum extent possible, deployments are expected to be automated (see Automated 
Deployments on page 9), so increasing deployment frequency does not linearly increase workload 
on the team. 
Deployment frequency is a proxy for batch size. Reducing batch size has consistently been shown 
to accelerate feedback, improve efficiency, reduce risk and overhead, increase team motivation 
and urgency, and reduce costs (for more details, see Accelerate, page 16, by Nicole Forsgren, Jez 
Humble, and Gene Kim). DORA research shows high-performing teams deploy at least once per 
week. 
                    +
PRODUCT-SPECIFIC METRICS
In addition to the above three metrics that will be consistently tracked across all software programs, 
each software product is expected to have its own key performance indicators (KPIs) and metrics to 
track how well the product is working for its users. While the first three metrics are about balancing 
speed and stability, these product-specific metrics are about product priorities and value. Software 
products contribute to NGA’s mission in different ways, so their success must be uniquely measured, 
and each team must define its own product-specific metrics. 
Because these product-specific metrics will not be consistent across NGA, they cannot be defined at 
the organizational level. However, each product will include: (1) performance metrics that describe if 
the product is doing what it’s supposed to; (2) business metrics that convey how well the

2. NATURE/DESCRIPTION OF ACTION(S): MOD 01: This is J&A Modification 01 
(MOD 01) to the originally approved J&A (JA25-010), with bold text representing the 
updated language from the initial J&A. 
 
The nature of this action is to procure one, 6-month firm-fixed-price base period and 
three, 2-month option periods to extend the period of performance (PoP) against the 
existing DISA J6 C4E Platform Services task order (TO) HC102819F0687.  The PoP for 
the current TO ends December 12, 2024.  This action will extend the PoP by up to 12 
months, from December 13, 2024 - December 12, 2025 (if all options are exercised).  
This extension will ensure continued support during the protest period of the follow-on 
Platform Services effort as well as provide additional time, if necessary, based on the 
protest proceedings and associated resolution.  The structure of the extension provides for 
an off-ramp opportunity whereby options will not be exercised unless required to provide 
continuity of services in the absence of the follow-on effort being in place.  The Platform 
Services effort actively supports the Pentagon’s network systems. The base period will be 
funded by fiscal year (FY) 2025 operations and maintenance funding. 
 
MOD 01: J&A MOD 01 includes two increases: 1) an increase of  to 
accommodate the previously awarded bridge amount ( )  that exceeded the 
original J&A estimated value ( ) and 2) an increase in the J&A estimated 
value by $  to support a four-month extension of the current task order period 
of performance (PoP).

The current bridge task order PoP expires on December 12, 2025. The original bridge 
was established to provide additional time to complete the competitive follow-on award.  
Award of the follow-on effort is now estimated for January 2026 due to the extended 
time required to complete corrective actions in response to the post award protest 
received following initial award.  This modification to the J&A provides a four-month 
extension (December 13, 2025 – April 12, 2026). The four-month PoP extension aligns 
with the current award projection and transition time requirements. The extension will be 
funded by FY 2026 operations and maintenance funding.  
 
MOD 02: This is J&A Modification 02 (MOD 02) to the originally approved J&A 
(JA25-010) and Modification 01, with bold text representing the updated MOD 02 
language. 
 




Platform Services Bridge JA25-010 MOD 02

4.  IDENTIFICATION OF THE EXCEPTION TO FAIR OPPORTUNITY:   
 
(a) Exception to Fair Opportunity. This modification must be issued on a sole-source basis in 
the interest of economy and efficiency.   
 
(b) Justification. 
(1) Minimum Government requirements.  DISA J6 C4E and its stakeholders depend 
on PaaS, IaaS, VDaaS, storage, backup and recovery, cyber compliance, 
mainframe, specific mission and enterprise application support, configuration 
management, 24x7 monitoring and organizational messaging system support in 
the Pentagon and at alternate location sites within the NCR. A lack of contractor 
support to carry out these functions will negatively impact DISA J6 C4E network 
users and may result in unavailability of the network or introduce vulnerabilities 
to the network, increasing the risk of cybersecurity attacks.  
(2) Proposed sole source contractor.  Due to the bid protest filed by Open San 
Consulting (dba OSC Edge) and GCyber LLC, SAIC is the only responsible 
source at this time that can fulfill this requirement without risk of a substantial 
break in Platform services support to the DISA J6 C4E.   
(3) A sole source extension modification is necessary due to post-award protests filed 
in response to the award of the follow-on BPA, HC102825A0001.  
 
   
5.  COST/PRICE FAIR AND REASONABLE DETERMINATION: During source selection of 
solicitation number 832267288, a cost/price evaluation was conducted in accordance with FAR 
15.404.  The contract prices were determined to be reasonable for the base and all option periods.  
The proposed cost/prices for the extension will be compared to the current TO prices as well as 
the Independent Government Cost Estimate and the proposed prices for the follow-on effort to 
determine fair and reasonableness.    
 
6.  MARKET RESEARCH: This is an extension to the existing TO to ensure continued support 
until the post-award protest and transition period of the follow-on contract, HC102825A0001, 
are complete.  Market research was conducted for the follow-on contract, which was completed 
within the last 12 months.  The market research identified potential sources which enabled the 
follow-on contract to be conducted as a competitive small business set-aside.  
 
7.  ANY OTHER SUPPORTING FACTS: Not Applicable 
 
8.  ACTIONS THE AGENCY MAY TAKE TO REMOVE BARRIERS THAT LED TO THE 
RESTRICTED CONSIDERATION:  
 
(a) Current Contract: HHSN316201200012W/HC102819F0687  
 
(b) Status: Expires December 12, 2024.  Competitive small business set-aside source 
selection was completed for the follow-on. Award announced October 21, 2024.  Stop-
work order was issued on October 31, 2024 due to a bid protest. 

Platform Services Bridge JA25-010 MOD 02 
CUI 
 
Page 5 of 6 Pages

MOD 01: With award of the initial bridge, the current task order is now set to expire 
April 12, 2026.  
 
MOD 02: The task order will now expire June 12, 2026. Additionally, a one-
month option period may be exercised to extend performance through July 12, 
2026 (as needed). 
 
9.  REFERENCE TO THE APPROVED ACQUISITION PLAN (AP): On April 1, 2019, 
Platform Services was originally awarded to Unisys Corporation.  On June 14, 2019, the 
Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Program Support Center notified affected 
Department of Defense (DoD) components that their Program Support Center was terminating its 
assisted acquisition program for non-HHS agencies effective immediately, because the HHS was 
unable to certify to its compliance with DoD procurement requirements in accordance with 
Section 801 of the FY 2008 National Defense Authorization Act. As such, the DoD was required 
to assume procurement and administration responsibilities for any affected contracts to maintain 
continuity of mission essential service.  On July 3, 2019, the requirement was transferred to 
DISA/DITCO for administration and a continued contract (TO HC102819F0687) was awarded 
the same day and subsequently administered by DITCO-Scott.  HHS would not provide DISA 
any documents (e.g., AP) from their contract file as the file was embargoed by HHS General 
Counsel.  Given the very limited time to award a new order to the incumbent contractor as a 
continued contract, DITCO-Scott did not prepare an AP.  Further, in accordance with DISA 
Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DARS) 5807.103(S-91)(ii), an AP is not required for this 
bridge contract action.
