search_text:
# Questions Answers w/ SOO Para. Reference
1
Point of clarification for 1.5.1.2 - will installations without local IT support require the vendor provide 
any additional vulnerability scanning and management capabilities? We noted the previous response 
that the request is to provide the critical oversight, analysis, remediation guidance and hands-on 
technical support to manage the vulnerability lifecycle, not on operating the testing tools themselves.
No additional vulnerability scanning and management capabilities are required.  
While tool provision is out of scope (SOO 1.5.2), the contractor is responsible for 
the technical support for ongoing vulnerability management, including monitoring, 
lifecycle management and remediation guidance, and hands-on support as 
required by installations, both remote and onsite as needed (SOO 1.5.1.2, 
4.1.3.1.1).
2
Is AFSVC open to a fully vendor-managed model for remote security operations (SOC, SIEM)?
No. The SOO outlines a collaborative model to empower local staff, not a fully 
vendor-managed operation. Your role is to augment, not replace, local personnel 
(SOO 1.3.1, 2.2.3).
3
To what degree is in-person / hands-on support expected, and for what geographic areas? Would a 
proposed solution for quarterly travel be acceptable, or is the vendor expected to provide permanent 
local staff to support installations?
On-site and remote assistance is required (SOO 3.1). Expect hands-on support at 
~20-30 of 104 locations (SOO 1.4, 1.5.1.2). Travel is as-needed for physical audits 
and audit preparedness; permanent local staff are not required (SOO 6.4.2).
4
What level of security clearance is expected for vendor personnel? Will all vendor staff engaged in 
supporting AFSVC require a Tier 1? Will any vendor staff require a Tier 3?
Tier 1 is sufficient, most of the work is unclassified (SOO 6.6.1). Refer to SOO 
6.7.2.2, Contractor personnel shall not be assigned to perform duties within IAT-II 
or IAT-III, and or requiring “Limited Privileged Access” such as system or database 
administration rights, to any NAFI application or network.
5
Will vendor interact with any CUI / does the proposed solution require a CMMC certification?
Yes, the vendor will handle CUI and require training per SOO 6.6.3. Refer to SOO 
3.2 Contractor Qualifications.
6
Are all assets centrally managed (e.g., centralized patching tools, endpoint management, 
configuration management)?If not, identify unmanaged or partially managed assets.
The objective is to achieve a "proactive, centrally-governed security posture" (SOO 
1.3.1), which indicates assets are not currently all centrally managed. The SOO 
notes that "critical gaps in security tools and on-site technical support prevent a 
proactive, centrally managed defense" (SOO 1.2.1). 
7 Are legacy or end of life systems present? If yes, list known unsupported software or systems and 
any business constraints.
Any in-scope legacy or end of life systems will be upgraded by August 2026.  Many 
are scoped out using P2PE terminals.
8
Are externally facing assets included in scope?(Web servers, VPNs, firewalls, cloud services, etc.)
External assets are out of scope of this SOO, penetration testing is being 
conducted, per SOO 1.5.2.  The SOO requires expertise with firewalls, VPNs (SOO 
3.2.1.4), and cloud platforms like AWS, Azure, and GCP (SOO 3.2.1.9). 
9
Is there any preference for fee arrangements - per endpoint, hourly, fixed fee, etc.
The contract will be fixed fee, based on deliverables (SOO 3.1). See Contract 
Admin Data, Paragraph 4.
10 Will any cloud workloads be in-scope? Do you utilize any cloud service providers (e.g., AWS, Azure, 
GCP) for any part of your payment processing environment?
Yes, some Aloha Cloud is in CDE scope. Required expertise in AWS, Azure, and 
GCP, and the directive to prioritize cloud-based tools, confirm cloud workloads are 
in scope (SOO 3.2.1.9, 4.1.1.3).
11
 Many installations have successfully achieved PCI compliance using validated P2PE solutions, 
hosted payment pages, or centralized enterprise payment gateways. Has AFSVC evaluated whether 
standardizing one or more of these proven, lower-scope approaches—where 
appropriate—could enhance consistency across installations while reducing lifecycle costs and 
simplifying compliance sustainment, as an alternative to deploying and maintaining separate 
tool suites at each location?
Standardization is underway, as AFSVC has moved from a decentralized to 
centralized approach. An enterprise Tool suite solution, where applicable, is being 
requested in this SOO (4.1.1).
12 The SOO currently directs the contractor to recommend and implement a “complete enterprise tool 
suite” (SIEM, DLP, MFA, etc.) and to prioritize “modern, automated, and cloud-based tools.”  As a full-
service QSA with experience delivering thousands of services to similar environments outlined in both 
the original and current SOO we find that the scope in the first SOO to more cost effective and timely. 
Would AFSVC consider modifying the requirement to include, as part of Phase 1, a QSA-
performed assessment that delivers a validated scope-reduction roadmap and presents 
multiple compliant implementation approaches—including both tool-enabled and scope-
optimized models—along with a side-by-side 3–5 year total cost of ownership analysis to 
support AFSVC decision making?
The SOO requires the contractor to conduct a "Gap Analysis" and deliver a 
"Solution Implementation Strategy" for a "complete enterprise tool suite" (SOO 
4.1.1.1, 4.1.1.4). The contractor's role is to provide technical support, not to 
perform a QSA function, although collaboration with a QSA vendor is expected 
(SOO 4.1.3.2.2).
13
The SOO emphasizes on-site technical support, 24/7 monitoring, ongoing tool management, and 
sustainment across up to 30 installations. Has AFSVC conducted, or would it consider conducting, 
a comparative analysis of total lifecycle cost—including personnel, licensing, maintenance, 
training, and option years—across alternative compliant approaches, such as tool-intensive 
implementations versus scope reduction and process simplification, which for many federal 
and DoD organizations has historically reduced compliance effort by 70–90%, to ensure long-
term operational sustainability and resource alignment?
The SOO establishes the requirement to deploy and manage a standardized tool 
suite (SOO 1.5.1). A comparative cost analysis of alternative compliance 
approaches is not a specified deliverable within the SOO. The contractor is 
expected to provide a detailed cost analysis for the recommended tools as part of 
the implementation strategy (SOO 4.1.1.4.2). 
A P2PE based scope reduction strategy is in place, while certain components 
remain within full PCI scope.  Supporting data and documentation will be provided 
upon contract award.  
14
The SOO states that the AFSVC intends to award all phases to a single vendor but that Phases 2 and 4 
are at the AFSVC’s discretion based on Phase 1 results. Would AFSVC consider adding language to 
allow the Phase 1 contractor to recommend and potentially execute a QSA-led scope-reduction 
strategy, as well as evaluate multiple compliant execution paths, so the AFSVC can select the 
approach that best aligns with cost, risk tolerance, and sustainment objectives—potentially 
eliminating the need for Phases 2 and 4—prior to proceeding to subsequent phases?
The Government is currently engaged with a QSA firm; however, the expectation 
is that you will collabrate to develop and execute a forward-looking strategy. 
15
DoD cybersecurity guidance (e.g., Zero Trust principles, Cloud Security Playbook elements, and 
integration with existing DISN/DoDIN frameworks) emphasizes risk-based, least-privilege controls 
over broad tooling deployment. How does the SOO's focus on implementing and managing a full 
suite of security tools (SIEM, DLP, MFA across CDEs) align with these mandates, and would the 
AFSVC be open to requiring Phase 1 recommendations to demonstrate compatibility with DoD-
wide security architectures to avoid redundant or conflicting implementations?
Refer to SOO  1.2 Background and 1.3 Objective.  As part of the Phase 1: Gap 
Analysis and Remediation (SOO 4.1.1), AFSVC is requesting a strategy and 
recommendation for the required  security tools and services to comply with PCI 
DSS (SOO 4.1.1.3 and 4.1.1.4).  The solution must align with DoD standards, 
including NIST SP 800-53, CISA directives, and the DoD RMF (SOO 6.9.2) while 
closing PCI control gaps and avoiding redundancy. For further clarification, the DAF 
NAFIs process payment card transactions over locally managed commercial 
netwoks rather than the NIPRNet.
16
PCI DSS best practices for sustainment emphasize repeatable, low-effort processes over complex 
tooling. Has the AFSVC evaluated whether the proposed enterprise tool suite—including 
licensing, configuration, monitoring, and update requirements—aligns with sustainable 
compliance models observed in other federal environments, and would AFSVC consider 
incorporating long-term sustainment factors, such as operational complexity, maintenance 
demands, and staffing dependencies, into Phase 1 deliverables to support stable and durable 
compliance outcomes?”
A PCI DSS compliant recommendation is part of  SOO 4.1.1, Phase 1: Gap Analysis 
and Remediation
17
Should the Contractor plan for steady-state operations only, or include surge support for assessment
cycles, remediation efforts, or incident response events?
The contractor must plan for both. The SOO requires "Ongoing Technical Support" 
(SOO 4.1.3), including "24/7 Support Services" (SOO 4.1.3.1.3), "Tier 2/3 
Sustainment Support" for escalations (SOO 2.2.5), incident response assistance 
(SOO 4.1.3.4.2.4), and "PCI DSS Assessment Preparation Support" (SOO 4.1.3.2).
18 Are clearance requirements (e.g., Public Trust, Secret) required at contract start, and will the 
Government sponsor clearances if needed?
All clearances will be sponsored by the Govt once the Contract is awarded. See 
SOO Para 6.7. To ensure all Personnel needing background checks meet the 
requirements.
19 What are the expected incident response roles and responsibilities for the Contractor (e.g., triage, 
escalation, remediation support), and how will these integrate with Government teams?
The contractor is responsible for "hands-on technical support for 
ongoing...incident response" (SOO 1.5.1.2) and must have experience with 
"containment, eradication, and recovery" (SOO 3.2.1.3). Suspected incidents must 
             20 Are there minimum staffing levels, labor categories, or experience thresholds that the Government 
will use to determine technical acceptability?
AFSVC is seeking industry to provide this answer.
21 Is a Facility Clearance (FCL) required at time of proposal or award, and if so, will the Government 
sponsor the prime or subcontractor for FCL?
All clearances will be sponsored by the Govt once the Contract is awarded. See 
SOO Para 6.7. To ensure all Personnel needing background checks meet the 
requirements.
22 Through which channels do you accept payments (e.g., E-commerce, Card-Present/Retail, or Mail 
Order/Telephone Order)?
CDE scoping and inventory documentation will be provided upon contract award. 
Refer to SOO 4.1. 
23 Approximately how many credit/debit card transactions do you process annually across all 
channels?
Over 13 million annual payment card transactions (SOO 1.4).
24 Are you operating as a Merchant (taking payments for your own goods/services), a Service Provider 
(processing on behalf of others), or both?
AFSVC is a Merchant (SOO 1.1.1).
25 Does your organization store, process, or transmit Primary Account Numbers (PAN) in any electronic 
format?
Yes. The purpose of this SOO is to ensure full security of the CDE (SOO 1.1.1, 
1.3.1).
26
Do you store any Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD) after authorization (e.g., CVV, CID, or PIN data)?
No. Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD) is not stored.
27 For E-commerce, do you use a third-party redirect (like a hosted payment page), an iFrame, or do you 
capture the data directly on your own servers?
Hosted payment pages are used for individual activity ecommerce solutions; an 
enterprise eCommerce solution is in development. 
28 Is your Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) currently segmented from the rest of your corporate 
network, or is the entire network in scope?
The CDE is segmented.
29
Do any third-party vendors or partners have remote access to your internal network or the CDE?
Yes.
30 Do you use physical Point of Sale (POS) terminals or hardware security modules (HSM)? If so, are they 
PCI-validated?
Physical POS are in use and in the process of full PCI-validation.
31 If you take orders over the phone (MOTO), are calls recorded, and if so, is card data being captured in 
those recordings?
Calls are not recorded in the case of phone orders.
32 Can you provide a list of all third parties that could impact the security of your cardholder data (e.g., 
web hosting, data centers, managed firewall providers)?
Third party details can be delivered upon contract award.
33 Do you have an up-to-date network diagram and data flow diagram that specifically identifies all 
cardholder data flows?
We do have an up-to-date standardized network diagram.
34 Is your IT or security infrastructure managed in-house, or is it outsourced to a Managed Service 
Provider (MSP)?
The SOO implies a mixed environment. This contract is required due to a lack of

UNCLASSIFIED
FAR 8.4 Limited-Sources Justification exceeding SAT
 
FAR 8.4 Limited-Sources Justification exceeding SAT, v6.3, 02/05/2026 2 | P a g e  
UNCLASSIFIED 
4. IDENTIFICATION OF THE AUTHORITY AND CIRCUMSTANCE THAT JUSTIFIES LIMITING THE SOURCE. 
FAR 8.405-6(a)(1)(i)(C) -- In the interest of economy and efficiency, the new work is a logical follow-on to 
an original Federal Supply Schedule order that was not previously issued under limited-sources 
procedures. 
5. DESCRIPTION OF THE SUPPLIES OR SERVICES REQUIRED TO MEET THE AGENCY S NEEDS. 
AWP is a General Services Administration (GSA) Federal Supply Schedule (FSS) order, awarded 
competitively on 30 September 2020. The task order was awarded as a Firm Fixed Price contract with a 
base year, four option years through 29 September 2025. The current order is extended by 6 months 
utilizing 52.217-8 Option to Extend Services, which expires on 29 March 2026.  Contractor shall assume 
Applications Service Provider (ASP) responsibilities for the Agile AWP products. The Contractor shall 
provide Operations & Maintenance (O&M) and software/website development services to support and 
sustain the AWP products (as referenced in Table 1.1) across multiple security domains. Operations shall 
include support activities and execution of tasks that enable AWP II to achieve performance and service 
levels in an efficient and cost-effective manner. The Contractor shall prepare and deliver software, 
source code, and documentation (per CDRL A019: Software Product Specification) for each baseline 
update or release. The Software Product Specification (SPS) shall include executable software, source 
files, and software support information/documentation. The Government Program Management Office 
(PMO) anticipates the need for software development to address emerging requirements but cannot 
specify details at this time. 
 
The Discovery Catalog has been incorporated into the Globe baseline, and will still require updates to 
content, useability and UI/UX enhancements.  The Contractor will provide content management support 
for Discovery Catalog that includes review of new content requests and maintenance of existing 
content.  On the Secret and TS domains Globe Lite has been incorporated into the Globe baseline and 
has replaced mGlobe as 
search. 
 
This justification is a result of addressing critical tasking required to meet national security requirements 
in support of the war fighter and meet ongoing mandatory security requirements. The maintenance 
activities found under these services include corrective, adaptive and perfective. Under corrective 
maintenance, this will identify, isolate, and rectify a fault or problem to restore a system or application 
to an operation state. In addition, this will provide further development support to fix identified defects, 
which falls under corrective maintenance. The adaptive maintenance provides modifications to keep a 
system or application usable in a changed or changing environment. The perfective maintenance 
delivers a modification to improve system or application performance or maintainability. Under adaptive 
and perfective maintenance, these funds will also be used for crisis support (such as Israel-Gaza and 
Ukraine-Russia).  
 
 
  
 



UNCLASSIFIED
FAR 8.4 Limited-Sources Justification exceeding SAT
 
FAR 8.4 Limited-Sources Justification exceeding SAT, v6.3, 02/05/2026 4 | P a g e  
UNCLASSIFIED 
We are proceeding with an extension of the current contract to maintain essential services while 
simultaneously conducting a comprehensive assessment of evolving requirements. This extension 
period will provide the necessary time to coordinate with other offices and thoroughly define and align 
future contractual and technical specifications with NGA strategic objectives. 
As program requirements evolve, and the availability of competitive offerings change, program options 
for future purchases will be examined. The requirements office will continue to strive to promote 
competition to the maximum extent possible. This will be the opportunity for other providers to 
participate in market research and potential competitive acquisitions. 
Prior to the conclusion of this extension, NGA plans to gain a better understanding of industries current 
, holding an industry day, 
and schedule one-on-one discussions with vendors in order to conduct solid market research. 
10. APPROVING OFFICIAL DETERMINATION OF THE CIRCUMSTANCE THAT APPLIES UNDER FAR 8.405-
6(a)(1).  
I, acting as the Procuring Activity Competition Advocate, have determined that limiting sources to fewer 
than those required by FAR 8.405-2, pursuant to the authority of 41 U.S.C. 152(3), is justified based on 
the following circumstance: FAR 8.405-6(a)(1)(i)(C) -- In the interest of economy and efficiency, the new 
work is a logical follow-on to an original Federal Supply Schedule order that was not previously issued 
under limited-sources procedures.

1 October 2019 
Administrative Update 1, 13 May 2020 
 
CIO-T 
 
SUBJECT: NGA Web Property Standards and Guidelines 
 
References: See Enclosure 1.  
 
1. PURPOSE. This Instruction: 
 
a. Establishes policy, assigns responsibilities, and designates authorities for NGA 
web property standards and guidelines on enterprise domains in accordance with (IAW) 
Reference (d). 
 
b. Rescinds NGA Instruction (NI) 8955.5, “NGA Instruction for Preparing NGA Web 
Pages,” 23 May 2009. 
 
2. APPLICABILITY. This Instruction applies to all NGA civilian employees, military 
service members, other Government agency personnel assigned to NGA, and 
contractors, hereinafter referred to as personnel. 
 
3. DEFINITIONS. See Glossary. 
 
4. POLICY. It is NGA policy to: 
 
a. Establish web property standards and guidelines to manage compliance, style, 
and content for NGA web properties on enterprise domains. 
 
b. Disseminate information electronically on multiple security domains IAW relevant 
US Government laws and policies, DoD policies, IC policies, and NGA policies.   
 
c. Establish uniform style and mandatory content elements for NGA web properties 
on enterprise domains IAW Reference (d). 
 
5. RESPONSIBILITIES. See Enclosure 2. 
  

UNCLASSIFIED 
 
NGAI 8955.6 
2 
 
UNCLASSIFIED 
6. EFFECTIVE DATE. This Instruction is effective on the date of signature. 
 
 
 
 
Susan Shuback 
Associate Chief Information Officer

ENCLOSURE 2 
 
RESPONSIBILITIES 
 
1. Chief Information Officer (CIO). 
 
a. Establishes and oversees web standards for enterprise domains including 
administration, security, and sustainment of web infrastructure, services, tools, and 
Domain Name Service (DNS) directory development IAW Reference (l). 
 
b. Designates the Director, CIO and Information Technology Services (D/CIO-T), as 
the authority to manage the enterprise domain web property framework. 
 
2. D/CIO-T. 
 
a. Manages web properties residing on the enterprise domains. 
 
b. Assigns Director, Future Experience Office (D/TF), to develop NGA web property 
standards and guidelines for enterprise domains. 
 
3. D/TF. 
 
a. Establishes, assigns, and maintains DNS guidelines and standards. 
 
b. Maintains NGA web style guidance to ensure a consistent user experience across 
enterprise domains. 
 
c. Reviews, assumes control of, and removes NGA web properties that are not 
compliant with the standards and guidelines of this Instruction. 
 
d. Promotes usage of best practices to mitigate privacy risks and effectively protect 
personally identifiable information (PII) collected, stored, or transmitted on NGA web 
properties. (Reference (m)) 
 
e. Maintains the NGA Web Guidance and Resources website (hereinafter referred to 
as the Online Resources), located at https://www.intranet.nga.ic.gov/web/. 
 
4. Director, Office of Corporate Communications. 
 
a. Manages the review of all NGA products and information that are publicly 
released (where applicable), all external marketing (e.g., social media, correspondence 
with the press, and promotional products), and all writing for public release for 
adherence to NGA messaging standards on public-facing web properties available over 
the World Wide Web IAW Reference (n). 
 
b. Ensures that information released to the public is unclassified IAW Reference (n). 

UNCLASSIFIED 
 
NGAI 8955.6 
7 
 
UNCLASSIFIED 
 
c. Coordinates and interacts with media and external sources regarding NGA web 
properties IAW Reference (n). 
 
5. Senior Component Official for Privacy collaborates with the D/CIO-T Chief 
Information Officer to ensure that protections and disclaimers are in place for web 
properties that collect, store, or transmit PII IAW Reference (m). 
 
6. Information Developers and Designers. 
 
a. Comply with web standards and guidance provided in the Online Resources when 
developing, modifying, and maintaining NGA web properties on enterprise domains. 
 
b. Comply with all header and footer guidance provided at the Online Resources, 
Content Delivery Network for all NGA web properties. 
 
c. Format and publish NGA web properties that enable all users to retrieve and use 
information, including Section 508 compliance, IAW References (d), (l), and (q). 
  

UNCLASSIFIED 
 
NGAI 8955.6 
8 
 
UNCLASSIFIED 
ENCLOSURE 3 
 
NGA WEB PROPERTIES GUIDANCE 
 
1. Workflow processes for requesting and developing NGA web properties are provided 
in the Web Governance Concept of Operations (CONOPS), the relevant Standard 
Operating Procedures (SOPs), and this Instruction.  
 
2. The CONOPS, SOPs, and links to style guides, design elements, and best practices 
are provided in the Online Resources. 
 
3. Web Design Style Guidance. 
 
a. NGA web properties meet and maintain high standards of effectiveness and 
usability to provide quality information that is readily discoverable and accessible to 
authorized users. (References (d) and (q)) 
 
b. The UXD Web Style Guide is a resource to help developers and software 
engineers overcome common design problems when creating a new web property. A 
core function of the Online Resources is to provide assistance in the creation of a more 
consistent “look and feel” across NGA web properties. The Online Resources UXD Web 
Style Guide is located on the following networks: 
 
NGANet/Joint Worldwide Intelligence 
Communications System (JWICS) 
https://www.intranet.nga.ic.gov/web/ 
http://design.nga.ic.gov 
Secret Internet Protocol Router 
Network (SIPRNet) https://design.nga.smil.mil 
Sensitive But Unclassified (SBU) https://designsystem.digital.gov/ 
http://design.nga.mil 
 
c. The efficient, effective, and consistent use of NGA web properties and web 
services standards promotes a more citizen-centered Government and user-friendly 
experience. 
 
4. Writing in Plain Language. 
 
a. Plain language and communication have many benefits. Avoiding vagueness and 
unnecessary complexity makes it easier for users to understand information and use 
services. Plain writing assists the user in complying with applicable requirements to 
better understand the NGA web property. (References (b) and (j)) 
 

UNCLASSIFIED 
 
NGAI 8955.6 
9 
 
UNCLASSIFIED 
b. NGA web properties on enterprise domains must conform to the plain writing 
memorandums and referenced materials identified in the content guidance provided in 
the Online Resources. 
 
5. Copyright Protections. 
 
a. Copyright is a form of legal protection grounded in the US Constitution and 
granted by law for original works created in any medium. 
 
b. NGA web properties on enterprise domains must comply with the US Copyright 
laws and referenced materials IAW Reference (f). Copyright guidance is provided in the 
Online Resources. 
 
6. Accessibility Guidance. 
 
a. Accessibility is one of the most important aspects of web development. 
Accessibility allows the greatest number of users to view content more completely. 
Users have a better experience if accessibility is built into web properties from the 
beginning. 
 
b. US Code and Public Laws require Federal Agencies to ensure electronic and 
information technology (EIT) is accessible to people with disabilities. Section 508 of the 
Workforce Investment Act of 1998 applies to all Federal agencies when developing, 
procuring, maintaining, or using EIT. (References (a) and (q)) 
 
c. NGA web properties on enterprise domains comply with Section 508 
requirements. Accessibility guidance and tools are located on networks identified in 
paragraph 3.b. of this Enclosure. 
 
d. For overall questions regarding Section 508 compliance, contact the Section 508 
coordinator at NGASection508Coordinator@coe.ic.gov. (References (a) and (q)) 
 
7. Analytics, Performance Metrics, and Feedback Guidance. 
 
a. Web analytics are the collection, analysis, and communication of web data for 
purposes of understanding and optimizing web usage. Web analytics measure web 
traffic and assess the effectiveness of a web property. The resulting analysis helps the 
organization determine the value of its content or an application, and provides insight to 
improve its offerings and the user experience. 
 
b. NGA web properties on enterprise domains should have a web analytics 
collection capability. (Reference (d)) Web analytic solutions are provided in the Online 
Resources. 
 
8. Security Guidance. 
 

UNCLASSIFIED 
 
NGAI 8955.6 
10 
 
UNCLASSIFIED 
a. NGA web properties provide Government information and services to support the 
performance of NGA’s mission. Security and risk management are essential functions 
incorporated into the lifecycle of NGA web properties, including classification markings, 
public key infrastructure (PKI) certificates, and hypertext transfer protocol secure 
(HTTPS) IAW References (c), (k) and (p). 
 
b. Intelligence sources, methods, and activities are safeguarded IAW Reference (i). 
Additional information for classification marking requirements is provided in the Online 
Resources. 
 
c. Websites, services, and online resources available over the public internet that 
are maintained and operated in whole or in part by NGA are provided through HTTPS. 
(References (c) and (k)) Additional information for HTTPS implementation is provided in 
the Online Resources. 
 
9. Search and Discovery Guidance. 
 
a. Search functions are universal and an expected website feature commonly used 
to find information. Search engine optimization (SEO) is critical to reaching users who 
primarily rely on commercial search engines. 
 
b. NGA web properties on enterprise domains must provide search capabilities IAW 
References (d) and (h). D/TF provides search solutions, how to incorporate a search 
capability into an NGA web property, and how to maximize discovery of web assets via 
SEO and NGA search tools. Additional information is provided in the Online Resources. 
 
 
c. NGA web properties on enterprise domains are published to a registry to assist 
search and discovery of content and services. Search and Discovery Guides are 
available in the Online Resources. 
 
d. NGA web properties on enterprise domains contain metadata tags to assist 
search and discovery. Search and Discovery Guides are located on networks identified 
in paragraph 3.b. within this Enclosure provided in the Online Resources. 
 
10. NGA Dissemination on Domain Networks Guidance. 
 
a. IC computer domain networks disseminate intelligence information. NGA has 
multiple IC domain networks based on classification. Web properties are stored on 
servers that are connected to the domain networks and are reachable using DNS. 
When the DNS is translated into an Internet Protocol (IP) address, an information 
system (IS) can pinpoint the location of another IS/server where the web property is 
stored. 
 
b. NGA web properties on all enterprise domains must register for a DNS Uniform

This notice pertains to Request for Information (RFI)/Sources Sought Notice 36C10B26Q0078 that was posted on Sam.Gov for Network Engineering, Design, Implementation and Infrastructure Support (NEDIIS) Re-Compete. This requirement will be solicited and released under Fair Opportunity on GSA Alliant 3.  Estimated release date is anticipated May 2026.

GENERAL INFORMATION

PLACE OF PERFORMANCE

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

DESCRIPTION

This is an RFI/Source Sought for Network Engineering, Design, Implementation and Infrastructure Support (NEDIIS) ReCompete.  Please see the attached for more information. 

Request for Information 

Network Engineering, Design, Implementation and Infrastructure Support (NEDIIS)

VA-26-00019737

Introduction

This Request for Information (RFI) is for planning purposes only and shall not be considered an Invitation for Bid, Request for Task Execution Plan, Request for Quotation or a Request for Proposal.  Additionally, there is no obligation on the part of the Government to acquire any products or services described in this RFI.  Your response to this RFI will be treated only as information for the Government to consider.  You will not be entitled to payment for direct or indirect costs that you incur in responding to this RFI.  This request does not constitute a solicitation for proposals or the authority to enter into negotiations to award a contract.  No funds have been authorized, appropriated or received for this effort.  Interested parties are responsible for adequately marking proprietary, restricted or competition sensitive information contained in their response.  The Government does not intend to pay for the information submitted in response to this RFI.

This action may be competed under the Transformation Twenty One Total Technology Next Generation multiple award, indefinite delivery, indefinite quantity contract using fair opportunity procedures in accordance with Federal Acquisition Regulation 16.505.  

The North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) for this requirement is 541512 with a size standard of $34 million.  

2. Submittal Information:

All responsible sources may submit a response in accordance with the below information.  

There is a page limitation for this RFI of 25 pages.  The Government will not review any other information or attachments included, that are in excess of the 25 page limit. NO MARKETING MATERIALS ARE ALLOWED AS PART OF THIS RFI.  Generic capability statements will not be accepted or reviewed.  Your response must address capabilities specific to the services required in the attached PWS and must include the following:

Interested Vendors shall at a minimum, provide the following information in the initial paragraph of the submission:

Name of Company

Address

Point of Contact

Phone Number

Fax Number

Email address

Company Business Size and Status

For VOSB and SDVOSBs, proof of verification in SBA VetCert.	

NAICS code(s)

Socioeconomic data

Data Universal Numbering System (DUNS) Number

Existing Contractual Vehicles (GWAC, FSS, or MAC)

Provide a summary of your capability to meet the requirements contained within the draft PWS, as well as the following areas:

Provide at least one prime example of staffing a Federal contract with at least 150 FTEs that span network engineering, cybersecurity, software engineering, systems administration, and business process support disciplines that are distributed between at least 3 geographic locations. Specific examples or references provided must include the agency, point of contact, dollar value, and contract number. 

Provide at least one prime example of experience auditing and providing telecom expense-oriented support for a similar customer (with customer point of contact information). Specific examples or references provided must include the agency, point of contact, dollar value, and contract number. 

Provide at least one prime example demonstrating ability to serve as proxy between the Government and Telecom providers including CenturyLink, AT&T, Verizon, and T-Mobile. Proxy activities include managing circuit billing and providing training on telecom billing systems, to name a few. Specific examples or references provided must include the agency, point of contact, dollar value, and contract number. 

Provide expertise with non-traditional telecom cost-savings models. Specific examples or references provided must include the agency, point of contact, dollar value, and contract number.

Corporate experience or expertise in performing these services and specific examples or references.  Specific examples or references provided must include the agency, point of contact, dollar value, and contract number.

Your company’s intent and ability to meet the set aside requirement in accordance with VAAR 852.219-73 (JAN 2023) (DEVIATION) VA Notice of Total Set-Aside For Certified SDVOSBs and 13 CFR §125.6, which states the contractor will not pay more than 50% of the amount paid by the government to the prime for contract performance to firms that are not certified SDVOSBs listed in the SBA certification database (excluding direct costs to the extent they are not the principal purpose of the acquisition and the SDVOSB/VOSB does not provide the service, such as airline travel, cloud computing services, or mass media purchases). When a contract includes both services and supplies, the 50 percent limitation shall apply only to the service portion of the contract. 

Your response shall include information as to available personnel and financial resources; full names of proposed team members and the PWS requirements planned to be subcontracted to them, which must include the prime planned percentage or the names of the potential team members that may be used to fulfill the set aside requirement. 

Has the draft PWS provided sufficient detail to describe the technical requirements that encompass the software development and production operations support services to be performed under this effort. 

______ YES _______ NO (if No, answer question f)

If “NO”, please provide your technical comments/recommendations on elements of the draft PWS that may contribute to a more accurate proposal submission and efficient, cost effective effort.

 

Responses are due no later than 1:00pm EST, December 01, 2025 via email to Diana Sesta, Contract Specialist at Diana.Sesta@va.gov and Susan Banasiak, Contracting Officer at Susan.Banasiak@va.gov.  Please note “Network Engineering, Design, Implementation and Infrastructure Support (NEDIIS)” in the subject line of your response.  Mark your response as “Proprietary Information” if the information is considered business sensitive.  The email file size shall not exceed 5 MB.

BACKGROUND

The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) is the second-largest federal department, serving approximately 19 million Veterans in over 1,700 facilities and sites worldwide, making it the largest integrated health care system in the United States. This impressive service delivery is made possible through the coordinated efforts of three major line organizations: the Veterans Health Administration (VHA), the Veterans Benefits Administration (VBA), and the National Cemetery Administration (NCA). These efforts are orchestrated at the VA Central Office (VACO) located in Washington, D.C.  

OIT delivers available, adaptable, secure, and cost-effective technology services to the VA and acts as a steward for all VA's Information Technology (IT) assets and resources. Currently, 60% of the 16,000 plus OIT employees and contractors are Veterans. 

The Connectivity and Collaboration Services (CCS) team delivers world class Telephony, Network, and Collaboration Services in an environment that encourages transparency, innovation, flexibility, fiscal responsibility, and accountability with 100% focus on Veteran outcomes.​

CCS serves at the forefront of VA’s digital transformation, enabling enterprise collaboration and connecting Veterans to VA through secure and resilient voice, video, data, and messaging services. 

CCS is divided into three pillars.

Core Network Services (CNS) - This pillar fuses the development, engineering, and operation of VA network services into a single organization, driving efficiency and enabling a unified strategy for our network infrastructure.​​​​​​​

Collaboration Services (CS) - CS provides enterprise level support focused on providing dynamic, flexible and efficient collaboration amongst VA staff and Veteran outreach. CS nationally supports the Microsoft 365 suite of products that VA currently employs for enterprise messaging and collaboration. These products include Exchange, Teams, OneDrive and others.

Unified Communications (UC) - UC continues to modernize, enhance, and operate world class enterprise Unified Communications services and solutions to connect Veterans, their families and VA professionals to the resources and services they need.

Direct Contractor support for this Task Order (TO) will reside within CCS Division under the Office of Information and Technology.  Mainly under CNS with optional tasks under the CS, UC, and ETS Business Office pillars.

Under CCS, The CNS team is a vital team supporting the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) mission by providing robust and reliable network infrastructure that connects VA facilities, Clouds, Internet, contractors, guests, and Veterans to critical VA resources and services. CNS enables seamless access to essential applications, data, and services across all VA divisions, ensuring that VHA,(VBA, and NCA can deliver top-tier healthcare, benefits, and cemetery services to Veterans across the nation. In doing so, CNS directly impacts the quality and timeliness of the VA's service delivery by maintaining the infrastructure that powers every networked interaction within VA facilities.

CNS is responsible for overseeing the telecommunications and networking technologies that serve as the backbone for VA operations. This includes provisioning the high-speed, secure, and resilient connections that allow VA personnel to collaborate effectively, enable contractors to access necessary resources, support guests’ informational needs, and allow Veterans direct access to their own health and benefits information. By providing stable network connectivity, CNS plays a crucial role in critical system availability to the VA workforce, whether they are in medical centers, regional offices, veteran centers, or remote clinics.

CNS Priorities

Communications - internally within network operations, engineering, and provisioning teams

Site Availability - Uptime for sites, redundancy, resiliency, minimizing downtime, and data supporting this.

Compliance - baseline configuration compliance, code standards, time to deploy, tracking Known Exploited Vulnerabilities/Zero Days.

CNS operates a very large enterprise network for our customers. As of (FY26, CNS is responsible for the following:

Daily Remote Access Users: ~246,000 Unique Users

Sites: ~2,200

Circuits: ~2,600

Routers: ~5,000

Firewalls: ~4,000

LAN Switches: ~45,000

Wi-Fi Access Points: ~200,000

The VA Enterprise Network will consist of products and services which are increasingly automated, digitally assisted and integrated with security across all service domains. 

Network Operations, Implementations

1 Section 5.a.i, 
Factor 1 
The FAA's requirements may allow other companies who may have less 
experience than us to qualify. For example, we have completed such 
projects for $250K that could be used for reference.  Another company 
may have an IDIQ with a ceiling of $10M per year and completed a 
reference project for only $50K and still qualify. Effectively the other 
company would be accepted with a $50K project and our company 
would be excluded for its $250K project. 
The FAA's evaluation criteria and process for Factor 1 
were carefully selected and will not be changed except 
as noted in the updated Announcement. All corporate 
experience examples must be recent and relevant per 
the requirements in Section 5.a.i. Also, Footnote 3 
defines how Offerors must calculate the Annual Dollar

Factor 1

(e.g., a minimum validated score of 30,000 points) rather than only

threshold would have their Factor 2 Concept Papers evaluated; those 
that do not would be eliminated. 
No. Factor 1 will not be restructured to pass/fail. 
3 Section 5.a.i,

ATLAS effort, subject to reasonable limits that maintain prime

The Offeror itself will have privity of contract with the

performance. Therefore, it is essential for the FAA to 
assess the Offeror’s experience to ensure the Offeror 
can perform this highly complex project successfully. 
However, the FAA has updated the Announcement 
based on feedback received. Offerors may now utilize

the FAA will make the Phase 1 down-select decision.  
5  Given the SOO’s emphasis on achieving Continuous Authorization to 
Operate (cATO) by embedding security controls natively into the 
development and operational fabric and the Phase 1 requirement to 
demonstrate ‘tools and approach, ’ is FAA seeking: (1) primarily a systems 
integrator to build and operate a modernization factory, (2) a software 
platform that agencies will use as part of ATLAS, or (3) a combined 
solution where the SI brings or integrates such a platform? 
The FAA anticipates a combined solution in which the 
Offeror demonstrates both the capability to operate a 
modernization factory and integrate or provide enabling 
platform that supports DevSecOps. Offerors should 
describe their proposed operating and tooling 
approach.

Factor 1

delivery practices matured in the commercial sector, accelerates access 
to emerging technologies and industry best practices, and reduces 
performance risk through demonstrated success across diverse yet 
mission relevant environments. 
The Announcement has been updated to permit

select Decision

GENERAL INFORMATION

PLACE OF PERFORMANCE

DESCRIPTION

Subject: Sources Sought, Remote Automated Laboratory (RALS) System Software and Support for the VA San Francisco Healthcare System (VASFHCS) located at 4150 Clement Street, San Francisco, CA 94121.

The purpose of this sources sought is to conduct market research to support the procurement of Remote Automated Laboratory (RALS) System Software and Support for the VA San Francisco Healthcare System. This notice serves to survey the market in an attempt to ascertain whether or not sources are capable of providing the requested supplies. This notice allows potential vendors to submit a non-binding statement of interest and documentation demonstrating their capability. The sources sought effort is intended to assess industry capabilities and develop/support the intended direction of acquisition planning. Network Contracting Office (NCO) 21 is requesting responses from qualified business concerns; the qualifying NAICS code for this effort is  541512 Computer Systems Design Services.

This sources sought is to gain knowledge of potential qualified sources and their size classifications (Service Disabled/Veteran Owned Small Business (SDVOSB/VOSB), Hub zone, 8(a), small, small disadvantaged, woman owned small business, or large business) relative to NAICS  541512 Computer Systems Design Services. Responses to this notice, will be used by the Government to make appropriate acquisition decisions.  After review of the responses to this notice, a solicitation announcement may be published. Responses to this notice are not considered adequate responses to any corresponding solicitation announcement.  All interested offerors will have to respond to the solicitation announcement in addition to responding to this notice. 

Background: The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), VASFHCS has a requirement for Remote Automated Laboratory (RALS) System Software and Support.  Refer to the attached documents for project requirements:

Statement of Work

Price List

INSTRUCTIONS:

Submit a brief description, two-three (2-3) pages (including cover letter) that demonstrates how your company can provide the requested supplies that are required.  Include past experience in providing this system to the VA, other Government (Federal or State) agency, or for a private facility.  Please specify your availability and your address. PLEASE INDICATE WHETHER YOU HOLD A GSA FEDERAL-SUPPLY SCHEDULE CONTRACT THAT COVERS THESE SUPPLIES AND/OR SERVICES AND, IF SO, THE CONTRACT NUMBER.  Provide BUSINESS SIZE AND SOCIO-ECONOMIC STATUS; (a) Indicate whether your business is large or small (b) If small, indicate if your firm qualifies as a small, emerging business, or small disadvantages business (c) If disadvantages, specify under which disadvantaged group and if your firm is certified under Section 8(a) of the Small Business Act (d) Indicate if your firm is a certified Service-Disabled Veteran Owned Small Business (SDVOSB) or Veteran Owned Small Business (VOSB) (g) Include the UEI number of your firm. (h) State whether your firm is registered with the System for Award Management (SAM) at http://www.sam.gov and/or the SBA’s Dynamic Small Business Search (DSBS) at SBA DSBS.  If not, please NOTE that any future solicitation could only be awarded to a contractor who is registered in SAM and to receive award based on VOSB or SDVOSB status you must be registered in the SBA Registry (i) provide current commercial pricing and any applicable discounts. (j) link to the respondent’s General Services Administration (GSA) schedule, or attached file of same, if applicable (k) citation of any current or past customers (i.e., within the past two (2) years) to include contract number, point of contact telephone number or e-mail address; and (l) other material relevant to establishing core competencies of the firm. 

Response is due by 10:00 AM Mountain Time, Thursday, April 23, 2026.  Please submit e-mail responses to Mrs. Tawnya Krommenhoek, Contract Specialist, at email address: tawnya.krommenhoek@va.gov.   Your response should include a STATEMENT OF CAPABLILITY, GSA SCHEDULE INFORMATION, UEI#, BUSINESS SIZE, AND SOCIO-ECONOMIC STATUS information as explained above.  Please place “Attention: Remote Automated Laboratory (RALS) System Software and Support” in the subject line of your email.  This notice is to assist the VA in determining sources only, questions will not be received at this time.   


NOTES: Issuance of this notice does not constitute any obligation whatsoever on the part of the Government to procure these services, or to issue a solicitation, nor to notify respondents of the results of this notice. No solicitation documents exists at this time; however, in the event the acquisition strategy demonstrates that GSA is a viable option for procuring a solution, the RFQ shall be posted to GSA eBuy where only those firms who currently hold a GSA Schedule with the applicable SIN will be able to respond. The Department of Veterans Affairs is neither seeking quotes nor accepting unsolicited quotes, and responses to this notice cannot be accepted as offers. Any information the vendor considers proprietary should be clearly marked as such. The U.S. Government will not pay for any information or administrative costs incurred in response to this sources sought notice. 

See attached document: 36C26126Q0619_Price List.

search_text:
Performance Work Statement 
AFLCMC Engineering Directorate (EN-EZ) 
Collateral Conference Room 162 
 
1.0 Background 
The U.S. Government requires a technology upgrade for the conference room located at 
conference room 162 located in Building 28, Area B, WPAFB, OH The purpose is to integrate 
a modern Uniﬁed Communications (UC) capability using Microsoft Teams into a Collateral 
Secret SIPRNET environment. The room contains existing VTC equipment that is outdated 
and scheduled for decommissioning but will remain in place temporarily. The new solution 
must be integrated with certain remaining components while replacing those that are 
incompatible. 
2.0 Scope 
The contractor shall provide and install a complete, turn-key solution to enable MS Teams 
functionality within the designated conference room. The work includes providing and 
installing a new camera, microphone system, and control system processor, and 
integrating these components with the existing displays and control interface. The ﬁnal 
system must be capable of dual operation, allowing users to select either the new MS 
Teams capability or the legacy VTC system until it is decommissioned. The solution must 
be engineered to function independently after the legacy system is removed without 
requiring rework. 
3.0 Speciﬁc Tasks 
The contractor shall: 
• 3.1 Install New Camera System: Provide and install one (1) 4K-capable web camera. 
The camera shall be mounted on the front wall, centered between the two existing 
wall displays. 
• 3.2 Install New Microphone System: Provide and install a microphone solution 
optimized for MS Teams. The preferred solution is a single tabletop device capable 
of capturing audio from all participants in the room. The device must feature a 
readily accessible physical mute/unmute control. Ceiling-mounted microphone 
solutions are not desired. 
• 3.3 Integrate Audio: Conﬁgure the audio output from the new MS Teams system to 
be routed through the two (2) existing 75-inch wall-mounted displays. 

• 3.4 Replace Control System Processor: Replace the outdated, end-of-life Crestron 
rack-mounted controller with a modern processor unit that is capable of supporting 
both the new MS Teams integration and the legacy VTC system. 
• 3.5 Reprogram Control Interface: Reprogram the existing Crestron touch panel to 
provide a single, intuitive user interface. The control panel must allow users to easily 
select and operate one of the following modes: 
• Microsoft Teams Session 
• Legacy GVS VTC System 
• PC-based Presentation (local display) 
• 3.6 System Integration and Dual-Use Functionality: Integrate the new MS Teams 
equipment with the legacy VTC system to allow for seamless switching between the 
two. The technical solution must be designed so that the future removal of the 
legacy VTC hardware does not disable or negatively impact the functionality of the 
newly installed MS Teams solution. 
4.0 Security and Compliance Requirements 
All equipment, software, and conﬁgurations provided under this contract must comply with 
all applicable security requirements for operation on SECRET networks. This includes, but 
is not limited to: 
• 4.1 DISA STIGs: The system and its components must be conﬁgured in compliance 
with all relevant Defense Information Systems Agency (DISA) Security Technical 
Implementation Guides (STIGs). 
• 4.2 IA Accreditation: All hardware and software components must be listed on the 
DoD Information Network (DoDIN) Approved Products List (APL) and meet the 
requirements for use in a secure environment. The solution must be capable of 
achieving Information Assurance (IA) accreditation. 
  
See BOM for more details.

GFE (Government Furnished Equipment)
Item ID Description Intended Use / Status Quantity
GFE-01 75-inch Wall-Mounted Displays To be integrated. Audio from the new MS Teams solution shall be routed to these displays. 2
GFE-02 Rack-Mounted Amplifier/Mixer To be integrated. To be used for audio routing to the wall displays. 1
GFE-03 Equipment Rack/Cabinet To be used. For placement of the new rack-mounted control processor. 1
GFE-04 Crestron Touch Control Panel To be integrated. The existing control panel in the room, which requires reprogramming. 1
GFE-05 Polycom VTC Codec Legacy System. The core component of the GVS VTC system. Must be integrated for dual-use control. 1
GFE-06 Polycom Analog Camera Legacy System. The camera for the GVS VTC system. Not to be used by the new MS Teams solution. 1
GFE-07 Polycom Analog Ceiling Microphones Legacy System. The microphones for the GVS VTC system. Not to be used by the new MS Teams solution. 1 Set
GFE-08 Sound Bar Legacy System. The primary audio output for the GVS VTC system. 1
GFE-09 Existing Cabling Infrastructure Legacy System. All cables and adapters associated with the current GVS VTC system. 1 Lot
BOM (Bill of Materials)
Item Description Quantity
1. Control System Processor A modern, rack-mounted Crestron processor unit to replace the existing end-of-life controller. Must support integration of both the new MS Teams solution and the legacy GVS VTC system. 1
2. 4K Web Camera A 4K-capable web camera suitable for MS Teams use in a conference room setting. 1
3. Camera Mount A suitable wall mount for the 4K web camera. 1
4. Microphone System A tabletop microphone solution capable of providing full audio coverage for the conference room. Must feature a physical mute/unmute control. 1
5. Ancillary Equipment & Cabling All necessary cables, adapters, connectors, and other minor components required to deliver a complete and functional technical solution. As Required
6. Professional Services: Installation All labor required for the professional installation, integration, and testing of the equipment listed above. 1 Lot
7. Professional Services: Programming All labor required to reprogram the existing Crestron touch panel to control both the new MS Teams system and the legacy GVS VTC system, as specified in the PWS. 1 Lot

Justification and Approval 
For Other than Full and Open Competition 
Plan ID Number:  2026-LGO-0004 
 
 
  
Form Updated 4/14/2023 
Justification & Approval  
For Other than Full and Open Competition 
 
As a legislative branch agency, the Library is subject to the competition and advertising requirements of 41 U.S.C. § 6101. Like the Competition in 
Contracting Act and the FAR, 41 U.S.C. § 6101 does not require competition when a contracting officer certifies that only one source or brand-name 
item is available, when dictated by public exigency, or when otherwise authorized by law.  
 
This document sets forth the justification and approval for award of a contract or order by means providing for other than full and open competition 
per 41 U.S.C. § 6101, LCR 7-210, and, as applicable, FAR 6.302, FAR 13.106-1, FAR 8.405, and FAR 16.505. Contracting without competition 
shall not be justified on the basis of a lack of advance planning or concerns related to availability of funds.  
 
This form shall be used to support instances where competition will be limited to a single source, a limited number of sources, including where a 
brand name product is required. 
 
Part 1 – Identification 
 
1. Contracting Agency and Activity:  Library of Congress, Library Collections and Services Group (LCSG), 
Financial Management Directorate (FMD)        
 
2. Nature of Action Being Approved  
 Contract (or modification) 
 LoC or Leg Branch Order (or modification) 
 GSA Schedule or GWAC: Identify Schedule/Contract  
 Other: Identify        
Plan ID Number: 2026-LGO-0004 
Contractor (or Brand Name): Springshare 
Estimated Value (Base and all options):  
Period of Performance:  7/1/2026-6/30/2031 
 
3. Description of Supplies or Services Required to Meet Library Needs  
Describe the supplies or services that are required to meet the Library’s minimum needs and the delivery schedule required to meet those needs. Do 
not focus on a description of the program.  
 
The Library requires the following supplies or services:  
 
The Library requires an integrated public service platform that provides tools designed for the unique, not-for-profit public services of 
libraries. These services are distinct from for-profit operations and, consequently, do not align to generic commercial software 
designed for maximizing customer sales. The integrated tools must support and integrate the core public services work of the Library’s 
reference services.  The services that need to be integrated include: 
 
Service Description 
Receive and Respond 
to Library Reference 
Requests 
Requirements 
 A live chat platform with unlimited concurrent operators, unlimited fallback widgets, and 
multi-operator internal chat. 
 Ability to create customized question forms;  
 A searchable public facing 24/7 accessible knowledge base of FAQs;  
 Unlimited FAQ Groups - each with own homepage, customization controls, widgets, 
 A Social Media Management tool to integrate any number of Twitter accounts, Facebook 
Pages, and Pinterest accounts 
 Forms and user interfaces for both client-facing and administrative features and functions 
comply with the Revised Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, as amended (29 
U.S.C. 794d) and in accordance with the Contract Clauses for SaaS/PaaS. 
 reference transaction data reports 
 Hosting environment to include cloud servers, support, maintenance, and free updates 
whenever they are available.  
 Technical support response time within 24 hours of being contacted by a Library 
designated official or the COR 
Create, Edit, and 
Publish Online 
Library Guides and 
Bibliographies 
Requirements 
 Ability to create unlimited topical research guides, bibliographies, and other resources to 
make them available to staff and researchers both on/off site. 


Justification and Approval 
For Other than Full and Open Competition 
Plan ID Number:  2026-LGO-0004 
 
 
Form Updated 12/10/2025 
 Ability to customize the layout of individual guides, while adhering to a set of defined 
templates set by an authorized administrator, group of administrators, or Web Services. 
 Ability to make the published resources available to patrons externally to the Library as 
well as internally 
 Ability to create guides collaboratively with other content authors across the Library 
 Ability to customize the look and feel of research guides and bibliographies for identity 
and branding, as well as to create custom (friendly) URLs. 
 Ability to create research guides and bibliographies that are responsive to different 
devices (e.g., desktops, tablets, smartphones) and browsers (e.g., IE, Chrome, FireFox). 
 Ability for automated link checking to identify broken links in published research guides 
and bibliographies. 
 Ability to stop access to research guides that are no longer relevant, and to download 
such guides for archiving 
 Ability for researchers and staff to print selected guides, or parts of a particular guide. 
Technical support response within 2 hours of being contacted by the Library.  
Scheduling Platform 
for Library staff in 
public facing roles  
Requirements for the scheduling platform:  
 Allows 200+ account holders to organize multiple schedules in one place 
 Ability to add shift notes and assignments  
 Optimized for mobile devices to allow working away from desk 
 
Built in email functionality 
Schedule Library 
Reference 
Appointments and 
Space. 
 
Requirements: 
 Enables users, or Library staff on behalf of users, to book library spaces online or with 
mobile phones. 
 Enables authorized Library staff to set up rooms, spaces, and customize rules and 
availability 
 The module enables full check-in and checkout of equipment. 
 
Manage and Record 
Library Reference 
Transactional Data 
Requirements 
 An integrated Reference Analytics component with up to 5 data-sets allowing the 
Library to enhance systems statistics and to record all reference transactions 
 Collaborative recording and analysis of library statistics and data points.  
 Ability to store all library public service data in one platform and use cross-dataset 
analytics. 
 A tool that provides a graphical interface to create survey and forms for enhancing 
engagement with staff and patrons that does not require advanced coding knowledge  
 
 
The contractor also shall provide continuity of technical support and maintenance for the staff and service units in the Library that rely 
on these tools for researcher and patron engagement.   The scope of work in the SOW covers the work listed above in the Springshare 
ongoing services for "Ask-a-Librarian" and the daily chat services, as well as the included services LibGuides, LibStaffer staff work 
scheduling tool; LibInsight analytics tool; LibCal resource scheduling tool; and the LibWizard tool. 
 
 
 
Part 2 – Program Office Justification & Approval 
 
4. Authority for Contracting Without Competition Over $350,000 
Up to and including $350,000 
For this requirement up to $350,000, using simplified acquisition procedures under FAR part 13, the basis for contracting without providing 
for full and open competition is:  
 Only one responsible source is reasonably available to meet Library requirements. 41 U.S.C. § 6101(b); FAR 13.106-1.  
  Product or service is reasonably available from only one source.  
 Source controls copyrights, patents or other exclusive licensing arrangements.  
  Urgent circumstances; only one source can reasonably deliver by required date. Option years are not permitted.  
 Source is expressly authorized or required by statute: Identify the authority  
 
  Library’s needs can only be satisfied by a brand-name product which is essential to the Government’s requirement. FAR 6.302-1(c)

For Other than Full and Open Competition 
Plan ID Number:  2026-LGO-0004 
 
 
Form Updated 12/10/2025 
PART 3 – CONTRACTING OFFICER DETERMINATIONS & CERTIFICATION 
 
8. Efforts to Solicit from As Many Potential Sources As Practicable  
Unless a justification is based on FAR 6.302-2, Unusual and compelling urgency, contracting officers shall post justifications for other than full and 
open competition, including brand name justifications, to the Contract Opportunities website on sam.gov (or eBuy for Brand Name Justifications 
under FAR Part 8) prior to requesting a proposal or otherwise entering into negotiations.  
 
 Advance notice will be published in sam.gov for 15 days before a contract is awarded. FAR 5.203(a). 
 Advance or concurrent notice will be published in sam.gov for a reasonable time for commercial items FAR 5.203(a) and 12.204(b).  
 
We made the following efforts to interest potential sources in this award: A detailed market research was performed by the subject 
matter expert and determined that no sources exist that can compete with Springshare at this time. Posting the J&A for 
the required 15 days will notify the market. In the event that respondents are found, their product will be evaluated at that 
time. 
 
9. Listing of Sources That Expressed Interest in Writing 
 
The following sources expressed interest in this award: N/A  
 
 
10. Additional Information 
 
The following additional information is relevant to this justification and approval: N/A 
 
 
11. Contracting Officer Determination that Price Is Fair and Reasonable  
As explained in detail below, I have determined that the prices for this contracting action are fair and reasonable based on comparison of the 
prices to the following (see FAR 15.404-1(b)(2)):  
  
  Historical prices paid by the Government       Published competitive price lists  
  Library’s Independent Government Estimate       Prices obtained through market research 
  Other:                Prices obtained through competitive proposals 
 
The following analysis (based on attached pricing information) supports my determination: Fair and reasonableness will be documented in 
the Contract Review Memorandum (CRB). 
 
12. Contracting Officer Determination of Responsibility  
   Checked System for Award Management (or will check in the case of competitive proposals for a brand name product)  
    
 
13. Contracting Officer Determination of Best Value  
I have determined that this action represents the best value to the Government because: Best Value will be documented in the CRB. 
 
 
14.  Contracting Officer Certification 
I certify that, to the best of my knowledge and belief, this justification is accurate and complete. For those actions up to $900,000, I approve 
contracting without competition for this requirement. 
 
 
__Cristina A Vega___

4 
 
Department of Homeland Security Customs & Border Protection (CBP) Statement of Work 
(SOW) for Illegal Transshipment Pilot 
1.0 BACKGROUND  
 
U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) plays a critical role in the enforcement of trade 
regulations and the collection of customs revenue, positioning itself as the nation’s second 
largest revenue collection agency. In Fiscal Year 2025, CBP successfully collected $216.7 
billion in duties, taxes, and fees, continuing a legacy of robust enforcement that has led to the 
collection of 99.5% of all customs tariff revenue owed to the U.S. since 1992.  
 
Despite this high collection rate, unscrupulous importers continue to engage in deceptive 
practices to evade antidumping and countervailing duties, tariffs, and other trade regulations, 
harming compliant business and eroding the revenue base. Chief among these deceptive 
practices are illegal transshipment schemes, or the act of routing of goods through a third country 
to obscure their true country of origin and avoid higher tariffs.  
 
To combat illegal transshipment, CBP has intensified its enforcement efforts by leveraging 
advanced technology, data analytics, and partnerships with other federal agencies 
and industry partners. These measures aim to detect and prevent illegal transshipment and other 
illicit schemes, ensuring compliance with trade laws and protecting U.S. economic interests.  
 
Under the Illegal Transshipment Pilot, CBP will explore whether Artificial Intelligence (AI) 
technology solutions offered by the private sector can help uncover illegal transshipment 
schemes at scale and with a high degree of accuracy. This technology demonstration presents 
opportunities for the private sector and CBP to identify enforcement efficiencies and maximize 
revenue collection.   
 
 
2.0 SCOPE   
CBP will assess the value of Artificial Intelligence (AI) driven commercial solutions that identify 
imports which pose a high risk for illegal transshipment. The development and implementation of 
these private sector solutions will enable CBP to explore how cutting -edge technologies may be 
leveraged to inform enforcement actions that prevent high- risk imports from entering the U.S. 
and/or lead to the identification of additional duties owed. 
 
Vendors will deliver insights about illegal transshipment risk via  referral packages  to CBP. 
Referral packages shall articulate specific risk factors that vendors believe are indicative of illegal 
transshipment. Referral packages must also identify or be easily traced to imports that are in transit 
and have not yet arrived in the U.S . To support this requirement, vendors may choose to receive 
the below manifest information for imports originating from countries that CBP identifies as being 
higher risk for illegal transshipment. CBP will make manifest information available to vendors no 
later than 72 hours before imports are expected to arrive in the U.S.   
 
• Port of Unlading 
• Port of Lading 
• Arrival Date 
• Bill Number 

5 
 
• HTS Code 
• Shipper 
• Consignee 
• Cargo Description 
• Declared Country of Origin 
 
Vendors will be given an opportunity to prove out the scalability and accuracy of their illegal 
transshipment solutions over the course of a no-cost, 180-day base period of performance. 
Scalability will be measured by the number of actionable referral packages vendors submit to 
CBP. Accuracy will be measured by the percentage of referral packages that lead to the 
prevention of high-risk imports from entering the U.S. and/or the identification of additional 
duties owed. CBP will have full discretion over which referral packages to investigate based on 
confidence levels, potential revenue impact, ongoing or previous enforcement actions, and 
resource availability. Only referral packages that CBP chooses to investigate and act upon will 
be factored into its assessment of accuracy.  
 
Vendors who achieve the performance milestones defined in Section 6.0 may have their option 
periods exercised by CBP.  
 
The solutions delivered under the Illegal Transshipment Pilot as outlined in this SOW must drive 
enhancements and efficiencies around targeting for illegal transshipment, maximizing the 
collection of revenue owed to the U.S., and allocation of CBP resources towards areas of highest 
revenue risk. The desired end state for this SOW is to identify AI-driven solutions that 
demonstrate the ability to accurately detect illegal transshipment at a rate of 90 percent or 
higher—all without exposure to law enforcement sensitive algorithms , CBP’s targeting models, 
and other sensitive data sets.  
 
 
3.0 PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE   
The contract base period of performance is intended to be 180 days and may contain up to two 
options.  
 
 
4.0 TYPE OF CONTRACT 
This award will establish a Non-FAR Contract through the DHS Commercial Solution Opening 
Pilot Program (CSOP). 
 
5.0  POINTS OF CONTACT 
Contracting Officer: Peter Giambone 
Email: PETER.A.GIAMBONE@CBP.DHS.GOV  
  Contract Specialist: Aristide Horugayve 
   Email: aristide.horugavye@cbp.dhs.gov  
6.0 DELIVERABLES AND SUPPORT  
 
Based Period Deliverables 
Vendors shall submit illegal transshipment referral packages to CBP via email in accordance 
with the below performance milestone schedule. Referral packages must be actionable, meaning 

6 
 
they:  
• Must identify and trace to imports that are in transit and have not yet arrived in the U.S.; 
and,  
• Must contain substantive supporting evidence (minimum of five pages) with which to 
make high-confidence decisions about illegal transshipment risk.  
During the base period of performance, vendors will be asked to progressively demonstrate the 
scalability and accuracy of their solutions in 60-day increments. CBP will provide regularly 
scheduled feedback on whether referral packages:  
• Meet the above criteria for being considered actionable; and,  
• Lead to the prevention of high-risk imports being released into the U.S. and/or the 
identification of additional duties owed.  
CBP may at any time issue a stop work order to vendors who are unable to meet or exceed the 
scalability and accuracy thresholds for each 60-day increment.

Vendors who achieve the performance milestones defined above during the base period may be

If the first option period is exercised and the vendor successfully achieves the performance

7.0      COMPLIANCE REQUIREMENTS  
 
7.1 Information Technology Security Compliance Requirements 
The contractor shall adhere to all DHS and CBP IT security policies and the basic requirements, 
security authorization, encryption compliance, and pass security review. 
 
7.1.1 DHS Security Policy Compliance Requirements  
All hardware, software, and services provided under this CSOP contract must be compliant with 
DHS 4300A, the DHS Management Directive 140-01, and Information Security Program and 
CBP Information Systems Security Policies and Procedures Directive, HB 1400-05D.  
7.1.2 Basic Requirements 
The contractor shall adhere to all DHS and CBP IT security policies listed in the applicable 
documents section, including the guidelines and policies stated in the DHS Sensitive Systems 
Policy Directive 4300A or any subsequent, replacement or revised publication. This policy 
mandates DHS organizational elements, including contractors, follow guidelines outlined in the 
DHS MD 4300A, DHS Sensitive Systems Directive, Information Technology Security Program, 
Version 13, with attachments or any subsequent, replacement or revised publication. 
DHS Directive 4300A, Basic Requirements outlines the management, operational and 
technical baseline security requirements (BLSR) for DHS Components to ensure 
confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and non-repudiation of sensitive 
information systems. The 4300A Directive provides greater detail of the BLSRs, including 
the roles and responsibilities associated with each. 
CBP will provide personnel with the appropriate background investigation, clearance levels to 
support the security certification/accreditation processes under this Agreement in accordance 
with DHS MD 4300A, DHS Sensitive Systems Policy and Directive. During all systems 
development life cycle (SDLC) phases of CBP systems, CBP personnel will develop 
documentation and provide any required information for all levels of classification in support 
of the certification/accreditation process. In addition, all security certification/accreditation will 
be performed using the DHS certification/accreditation process, methodology and tools. 
systems development life cycle (SDLC) phases of CBP systems, CBP personnel will develop 
documentation and provide any required information for all levels of classification in support 
of the certification/accreditation process. In addition, all security certification/accreditation will 
be performed using the DHS certification/accreditation process, methodology and tools. 
Legacy air gapped non-integrated systems shall: 
a) Wi-Fi shall not be used and be disabled. 
b) All operating systems must use the requisite DHS Security Technical Implementation 
Guides (STIGs) for hardening configurations. 
c) All vendor contractors shall not remove image data from systems nor store said data 

8 
 
on contractor networks. 
d) Ground-truthing or tuning of systems will be performed only on CBP systems and not 
on contractor networks unless specifically authorized. 
e) Integrated networked systems shall follow the above requirements and additionally: 
o If systems are using WIFI the design must be explicitly reviewed and approved 
by the CBP EIOD organization and Security and Technology Policy (STP). 
f) Other wireless connectivity may be approved by EIOD 
g) Systems must be patched and security updates maintained according to DHS patching 
standards (within 30 days usually) CBP Information Systems Security Policies and 
Procedures, Information Security Continuous Monitoring guidelines. 
h) Username/Password authentication is not allowed. 
 
7.1.3  Security Authorization 
a) A Security Authorization of any infrastructure directly in support of the DHS 
information system shall be performed by the contractor as a general support 
system (GSS) prior to DHS occupancy to characterize the network, identify 
threats, identify vulnerabilities, analyze existing and planned security controls, 
determine likelihood of threat, analyze impact, determine risk, recommend 
controls, perform remediation on identified deficiencies, and document the results. 
The Security Authorization shall be performed in accordance with the DHS 
Security Policy and the controls provided by the hosting provider shall be equal to 
or stronger than the Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) 199, 
Standards for Security Categorization of Federal Information and Information 
Systems, Section 3, security categorization of the DHS information system.
 
b) At the beginning of the contract, and annually thereafter, the contractor shall 
provide the results of an independent assessment and verification of security 
controls in the contractor’s format. The independent assessment and verification 
shall apply the same standards tha
