ATTACHMENT A

Red Hat Professional Services

Ansible Automation Platform Integration

for: 

Abacus Visual Playbook Builder and Automation Orchestrator

Department of Veterans Affairs

Veterans Health Administration (VHA)

Veterans Integrated Service Network 23 (VISN 23)

Healthcare Technology Management / Biomedical Engineering

Document Purpose

This document serves as Attachment A to the Performance Work Statement (PWS) for the Abacus Visual Playbook Builder and Automation Orchestrator acquisition. It defines the scope and requirements for Red Hat Professional Services engagement.

This document covers the interconnected technical areas requiring Red Hat expertise:

VM Provisioning and VLAN Management — API-driven infrastructure automation across multiple Nutanix sites

Windows Certificate Authentication — Secure, scalable authentication for managing Windows hosts

Certificate Management Workflows — Git-based certificate lifecycle management with four (4) distinct workflows

Presigned URL Workflow — S3 asset retrieval for playbook execution without direct AWS credentials

Real-Time Job Output Streaming — WebSocket integration for live job status in external UI

AAP Infrastructure and Self-Management — Operational best practices for running AAP

Abacus: The Orchestration Application

Abacus is the external application that orchestrates all user interaction and workflow coordination. Understanding Abacus's role is essential context for the AAP integration design.

Abacus Responsibilities

User interface for all VM provisioning and configuration requests

Determines which AAP job templates to invoke based on user actions and site context

Specifies which playbooks to execute (via Git commit reference)

Manages S3 asset references and coordinates presigned URL workflow

Handles user authentication and authorization before calling AAP APIs

Displays real-time job execution output streamed from AAP

Retrieves workstation onboarding script from Git

AAP's Role

AAP serves as the automation backend — it does not provide user-facing interfaces for end users. All user interaction happens in Abacus; Abacus calls AAP APIs; AAP runs playbooks against target infrastructure.

VM Provisioning and VLAN Management

This section describes the requirements for automating Windows VM provisioning and network management across multiple Nutanix sites.

Multi-Site Architecture

The Government has eight (8) geographically distributed sites, each with its own Nutanix Prism Element cluster. A single AAP installation serves all sites with strict security boundaries between them.

Multi-Site Security Model

The primary security requirement is strict site isolation. Users at Site A shall not have access to, view, or modify resources at Site B — even through misconfiguration.

Implementation approach:

One (1) AAP Organization per site (e.g., Minneapolis, Omaha, Fargo)

Organization-scoped Nutanix credentials (each credential can only access its site's cluster)

AD group to AAP Team mapping for RBAC

Site-specific VLAN configuration files in Git (vars/sites/{site}_vlans.yml)

Physical Sites:

Fort Meade, Fargo, Sioux Falls, Saint Cloud, Minneapolis, Omaha, Iowa City, Central Iowa

Logical Site:

VISN23 (administrative; no dedicated cluster; staff can work across all physical sites)

Each physical site has its own Prism Element cluster and AD security groups. VISN23 staff are granted cross-site access via AD group membership.

VLAN/Network Configuration Support

VLAN and network configuration management requirements are specified in Category LV [LV1-LV22]. The implementation mechanism (Abacus-native functionality, AAP workflows, dedicated service, or equivalent) is at Contractor discretion.

Red Hat Scope for VLAN-Related Work:

Red Hat's involvement in VLAN management is limited to technical guidance and integration support if the Contractor selects an AAP-based implementation approach:

API-Driven VM Provisioning

VM creation is triggered by Abacus via AAP's REST API — not through AAP's self-service portal. Abacus specifies the site, and the appropriate job template is invoked.

API Design:

One (1) Job Template per site (eight (8) total): minneapolis-vm-create, omaha-vm-create, etc.

Abacus determines which endpoint to call based on user's site

Site identifier implicit in endpoint (not passed as parameter)

VM Creation Parameters:

VM Creation Workflow Steps

The VM creation workflow shall execute the following steps:

Load VLAN configuration from Git for the target site

Perform "subnet math" to determine which VLAN the IP address belongs to

Validate IP is within usable range (not gateway, broadcast, etc.)

Check for IP conflicts (query existing VMs in Nutanix)

Create VM in Nutanix using specified template

Configure disks and CD-ROMs

Configure Windows hostname and network settings (mechanism at Contractor discretion per [L25])

Power on VM

Onboard VM for Windows authentication (see Section 3.6)

Add host to AAP inventory based on site, project, and group

Return result to Abacus

Automated VM Onboarding

After a VM is created and powered on, it must be onboarded into the Windows certificate authentication system before it can be managed by Ansible. This onboarding is an extension of the VM creation workflow.

Workflow continuation after VM power-on:

Wait for VM to complete initial Windows setup (sysprep completion)

Execute onboarding configuration (either via sysprep or initial connection)

Validate WinRM connectivity using certificate authentication

Add host to AAP inventory with correct Organization, Inventory, environment group, and component group memberships

Tag host with appropriate metadata (creation date, template used, etc.)

DRY Architecture Requirement

All eight (8) sites shall use identical playbook code — a single Git repository with no per-site playbook copies. Site-specific differences shall be handled via:

Organization-scoped credentials

Job Template extra variables (site_name, nutanix_cluster)

Site-specific variable files in Git (vars/sites/{site}_vlans.yml)

Benefit: Update playbook logic once; all eight (8) sites automatically receive the update.

VM Template Registry (Admin-Only)

Infrastructure administrators need the ability to register new Windows VM templates so they become available for VM provisioning. This shall be restricted to admin AD groups only.

Windows Certificate Authentication

This section describes the certificate-based authentication design for managing Windows hosts with Ansible.

Problem Statement

The Government needs to manage a mixed environment of Windows machines where some are domain-joined and others exist in workgroups. Several critical constraints eliminate traditional authentication methods.

Why Traditional Methods Do Not Work:

Solution: Two-Certificate Architecture

The solution is a certificate-based authentication system where Ansible uses WinRM over HTTPS with client certificate authentication. This approach uses two (2) separate certificates with distinct purposes.

Certificate 1: Windows Host Server Certificate

Purpose: Encrypts the WinRM communication channel (like HTTPS for a website)

Location: Each Windows host generates its own self-signed certificate

Private key: Never leaves the Windows host; never shared with Ansible

Validation: Ansible ignores validation (ansible_winrm_server_cert_validation: ignore)

Certificate 2: Ansible Client Certificate

Purpose: Proves Ansible's identity; replaces username/password authentication

Location: Single CA-signed certificate from Venafi on Ansible controller

Private key: Stored on Ansible controller only

Public key: Distributed to all Windows hosts (Trusted People store)

Mapping: Windows hosts map this certificate to local ansible_admin user

Git Repository for Platform Configuration

All certificate materials, root CAs, and generated scripts are stored in a dedicated Git repository. This design provides several benefits:

No TLS trust dependency: Git access via SSH requires no TLS certificate validation

Version history: All changes are tracked with full audit trail

Single source of truth: One authoritative location for all certificate-related materials

Resilience: If S3 or other infrastructure becomes untrusted due to CA rotation, Git remains accessible

Certificate Management Workflows Overview

Certificate management is divided into four (4) distinct workflows, each with a specific purpose and scope:

Initial Host Onboarding Workflow

When adding a new Windows host to Ansible management, an administrator performs an initial RDP session to execute a PowerShell setup script. This is the "moment of physical presence" to establish trust relationships.

The onboarding script performs these steps:

Generate self-signed certificate and configure WinRM HTTPS listener on port 5986

Import the root CA certificate that signed the Ansible controller certificate into Trusted Root store

Read Ansible controller certificate thumbprint

Create local ansible_admin user account with strong random password (generated locally, never transmitted)

Create certificate mapping: "when this certificate is presented, authenticate as ansible_admin"

After onboarding, Ansible can connect using certificate authentication without passwords transmitted over the network.

Workflow 1: Certificate Preparation and Staging

This workflow extracts certificate materials from a Venafi export, stages them in Git, and generates the onboarding script. It is a "safe" operation that does not touch any Windows hosts.

Trigger:

Admin uploads venafi-export.pfx (or ZIP containing PFX) to controller-certificate/ folder in Git, then runs the job template with the decryption password.

Steps:

Pull latest from Git repository

Detect file format (PFX directly, or ZIP containing PEM files, etc.)

Decrypt/extract using provided password: Private key → Store in AAP credential storage; Public certificate → Write to controller-certificate/public-cert.pem; Thumbprint → Extract and write to controller-certificate/thumbprint.txt; Signing root CA → If not already in root-ca/ folder, add it

Render onboarding-workstation.ps1 from Jinja2 template, embedding the root CA, thumbprint, public certificate, and logic for local user creation and certificate mapping

Write rendered script to generated/onboarding-workstation.ps1

Commit and push all changes to Git

Job completes successfully

Workflow 2: Certificate Rotation to Existing Hosts

This workflow pushes the new certificate to all managed Windows hosts. It requires careful design for reliability and rollback safety.

Pre-Conditions:

Workflow 1 has completed — all materials staged in Git

The old certificate is still valid (running 45-60 days before expiration)

AAP currently authenticates to Windows hosts using the old certificate

Both old and new credentials are available to AAP

Safety Mechanism: Scheduled Task Rollback

Before changing anything on a host:

Create a Windows scheduled task that will restore the old certificate mapping

Task triggers either on system reboot OR after a timeout (e.g., 10-15 minutes)

Then make the change:

Apply new certificate mapping (new thumbprint → ansible_admin)

Validate immediately:

Attempt to connect using the NEW certificate

Based on result:

Success: Delete the scheduled task (disarm the rollback), mark host as rotated

Failure: Leave scheduled task in place, mark host as failed — the host will auto-recover when the task fires or on next reboot

Workflow 3: Root CA Distribution to Windows Hosts

This workflow pushes all root CA certificates from the Git repository to all managed Windows hosts. It is idempotent — Windows handles "already present" gracefully.

Purpose:

Ensure Windows hosts trust all internal root CAs. This is needed for presigned URL downloads from Nutanix Objects S3 endpoint and any other internal HTTPS services Windows hosts may need to access.

Trigger:

Admin runs job template after adding new CA to root-ca/ folder in Git.

Steps:

Pull latest from Git repository

Read all .crt files from root-ca/ folder

For each managed Windows host: Push all CA certificates to Trusted Root Certification Authorities store (Windows handles idempotency natively)

Track per-host status: success, failed, unreachable

Persist state so re-runs can target only incomplete hosts

Report summary

Workflow 4: Execution Environment CA Trust Update

This workflow updates the AAP Execution Environment's CA trust store without requiring a container image rebuild.

Purpose:

Ensure the Execution Environment trusts all internal root CAs. This is required for amazon.aws.aws_s3 collection connecting to Nutanix Objects (presigned URL generation) and any other collections/modules that connect to internal TLS endpoints.

Desired Outcome:

Pull latest from Git repository

Read all .crt files from root-ca/ folder

Transform to format required by EE (Red Hat to recommend appropriate format)

Update the EE's CA trust source via the recommended mechanism

Result: Collections use system CA trust, which now includes new CAs — without rebuilding the EE image

Administrator Certificate Renewal Workflow

The annual certificate renewal should be as simple as possible for the administrator.

Desired admin experience:

Admin requests new certificate from Venafi portal, downloads the export (typically a ZIP containing an encrypted PFX)

Admin uploads the raw Venafi export (unmodified) to the Git repository (controller-certificate/ folder) via the Git web interface

Admin triggers Workflow 1 through AAP

Admin provides the PFX decryption password when prompted (this password shall be treated as sensitive data and not persisted after the workflow completes)

Admin reviews the generated onboarding script and staged materials

Admin triggers Workflow 2 to rotate existing hosts

If a new root CA was added, admin triggers Workflow 3 and Workflow 4 (or these may be automatically chained)

Key requirement:

The administrator should not need access to Linux command-line tools, OpenSSL, or manual file manipulation. The goal is that an administrator can complete annual certificate renewal by performing two (2) primary actions: uploading a file and providing a password.

CA Certificate Updates

When a new internal root CA is issued (for example, when the certificate authority used to sign the Nutanix Objects TLS certificate rotates), the administrator commits the new CA certificate file to root-ca/ in the Git repository.

Required propagation:

Workflow 3: Push new CA to all existing managed Windows workstations

Workflow 4: Update Execution Environment CA trust via ConfigMap/Secret

Workflow 1 (if re-run): Regenerate onboarding script (though new hosts only need the controller certificate's signing CA)

Operational Visibility and Recovery

All certificate management workflows involve changes across hundreds of hosts or critical infrastructure. Red Hat shall design these workflows with operational visibility as a fir

Synopsis/Notice of Intent to Sole Source 
 
The F-35 Joint Program Office (JPO) of Arlington, V A, intends to solicit, negotiate, and award a 
sole source contract under the authority of Revolutionary FAR Overhaul (RFO) Federal 
Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 6.103-1, “Only One Responsible Source And No Other Supplies 
or Services Will Satisfy Agency Requirements”, to Lockheed Martin Aeronautics (LM Aero). 
The proposed North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code for this acquisition 
is 541512, Computer Systems Design Services, with a small business size standard of $34.0 
million. One or more of the items under this acquisition is subject to Free Trade Agreements. 
 
All responsible sources may submit a capability statement in response to this notice of intent to 
sole source. The F-35 JPO will consider all capability statements received within fifteen (15) 
days after the date of publication of this pre-solicitation notice. 
 
Potential offerors must have a Top-Secret security clearance to be eligible for award. If the 
Government chooses to release a solicitation for the requirements described herein, the 
Government will provide a DD-254 form. 
 
The F-35 JPO has a requirement to support the Foreign Military Sales (FMS) customers by (1) 
establishing connectivity between a Mission Data (MD) development site at Eglin AFB, FL, and 
a MD Validation & Verification (V&V) site at Lockheed Martin Aeronautics in Fort Worth, TX, 
and (2) providing the required hardware and software for said development and test. This effort 
is referred to XRL Integration and supports the creation of FMS Mission Data Files (MDF). The 
contractor shall be responsible for procuring, delivering, and installing the necessary hardware, 
software, and network infrastructure that supports MDF efforts and for interconnectivity.   
 
LM Aero is the sole designer, developer, and integrator of the F-35 aircraft, and the only entity 
with the experience, proprietary software, and technical expertise needed to equip the 
reprogramming laboratory and integrate it with a complimentary reprogramming facility. As a 
result, LM Aero is the only known qualified source able to fulfill the requirements specified 
herein. 
 
Points of contact are Jonathan Chen, Contract Specialist, at Jonathan.Chen@jsf.mil, and Colin 
McCarthy, Contracting Officer, at Colin.McCarthy@jsf.mil.

Version 11-30-2016-1 
FAR Part 11—Describing Agency Needs 
Page 1 of 4 
 
 
STATEMENT OF WORK (SERVICES) 
(SOW) 
 
GENERAL INFORMATION 
Title of Project: 
Support for XNAT instance for FMRIF. 
 
Statement of Need and Purpose: 
The Functional MRI Facility (FMRIF) in the National Inst itute of Mental Health (NIMH) is seeking support 
for its data and workflow management platform, XNAT, which currently aggregates data from multiple  MRI 
scanners in the NMR Center (where FMRIF’s and other  IC’s scanners are located), provides access to 
these data to the users of these scanners, and also serves as platforms for managing, sharing, and 
processing these data. 
 
Background Information and Objective: 
Since its inception, FMRIF has operated a series of web-based platforms that serve to both aggregate 
image data from the MRI scanners ow ned and operated by the FMRIF, and to share these data out to its 
users, so they could securely retrieve their data using a web browser, or other web-based tools.  The first 
such service used by FMRIF was a commercial PACS system from Intelerad.  However, as both the number 
of scans conducted on FMRIF scanners rose, along with the volume of Functional MRI data generated, 
FMRIF encountered significant performance issues that could not be resolved by the vendor of that system.  
FMRIF then investigated multiple options to replace the Intelerad system, exploring both commercial and 
open source software (including, but not limited to, dcm4chee, XNAT, COINS, and  NiDB).  In all cases, 
there were either significant deficiencies, and / or la ck of flexibility, that prec luded use of any of these 
packages by FMRIF.  Ultimately , a custom database and web application was created in-house, by a 
FMRIF-employed programmer, and that platform served FMRIF for almost a decade.  However, in 2020, 
because of a loss of staff and expertise with FMRIF’s in-house data management systems, FMRIF sought 
a commercial option, with support, to replace its in-house data management solution.  That contract 
(75N95020P00651) was awarded to Radiologics to help FMRIF deploy, migrate data to, and support a 
single instance of XNAT.  This was successfully rolled out to FMRIF’s and other Facilities’ users, and now 
FMRIF seeks an agreement to continue supporting it s XNAT instance for its data warehousing, 
management, and processing needs. 
 
Period of Performance: 
Support for 1 year is required, starting on ASAP in FY 26, with 4 additional opti on years of support also 
being requested. 
 
SCOPE OF WORK 
General Requirements: 
Independently and not as an agent of the Government, the Contractor shall furnish all the necessary 
services, qualified personnel, material, equipment, and facilities, not otherwise provided by the Government 
as needed to perform the Statement of Work below: 
 

Version 11-30-2016-1 
FAR Part 11—Describing Agency Needs 
Page 2 of 4 
 
Specific Requirements: 
 
Support of FMRIF’s XNAT platform includes: 
• Updating XNAT software on supported instances to provide new capabilities, and address any 
emergent performance and security issues. 
• Updating integration with institutional authentic ation system (LDAP and Active Directory) to 
facilitate user authentication and system access. 
• Maintaining and updating interfaces with multiple MR scanner vendors to allow automatic capture 
and aggregation both raw (k-space) and DICOM data generated by these vendors’ platforms. 
• Supporting transport of acquire d and computed DICOM data between (i.e. send to and receive 
from) the platform and commercial PACS systems, and MR scanners 
• Continue to provide training and training materi als for FMRIF staff and users to administer and use 
the system. 
• Continue to develop and provide tools for versi on tracking and maintaining provenance of data and 
analysis pipelines. 
• Support scaling of compute and storage needs as the number of scanners, aggregated studies, 
and data processing compute loads increase.  
• Develop and support interfaces for visualizi ng and analyzing data, including, but not limited to, 
derivation of basic quality assurance metrics. 
• Continue supporting tools and / or interfaces for metadata search and management.  
• Supporting and developing tools to leverage OCI-compliant containers for data processing, 
including, but not limited to, containers providing tools such as AFNI, FreeSurfer, and FSL. 
• Support interfacing with large- scale batch processing system such as slurm and PBS on local HPC 
and commercial cloud compute resources. 
• Support exporting image and study data into BIDS  (Brain Imaging Data Structure) compliant data 
sets. 
• Support providing automated and customizable dat a anonymization / de-identification for exported 
datasets. 
• Support open REST-based APIs to all services  and data, and support for external language SDKs 
(e.g. MATLAB, Python, and R). 
• Provide mechanisms and interfaces for validat ion of data uploads into the system, and detection 
and logging of errors or omissions with such uploads. 
• Support command line integration access to API to facilitate scripting, data import and export, job 
scheduling and status, and building analysis pipelines. 
• Provide guaranteed response times to customer qu eries about issues, acknowledging of receipt of 
report of issues within 24 hours, with a 4 week window to resolving any issues raised. 
• Develop and provide options to consolidate dat a storted by XNAT on disk into fewer objects, 
allowing backup of XNAT to tape, and providing a more streamlined path for XNAT sites to migrate 
their instnaces to cloud services. 
• Keeping software deployed at, developed for, NIH open source, and available to the wider research 
community. 
• Provide at least 3 references wh o can attest to ability and experience supporting XNAT instances. 
 

Version 11-30-2016-1 
FAR Part 11—Describing Agency Needs 
Page 3 of 4 
 
LEVEL OF EFFORT: 
The contractor is expected to employ staff fa miliar with DICOM communication protocols, understand 
various computer network communication protocols and security standards, and also familiar with medical 
imaging data processing requirements.  Staff should be intimately familia r with the XNAT application, its 
operation, and should have extensive experience developing software and features that work both within, 
and interact with, the XNAT application. 
 
GOVERNMENT RESPONSIBILITIES: 
The government will supply all information necessary for contractor to complete their efforts. The 
government will also provide either physical or virtual computer systems on which the software platform will 
be deployed.  Under a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) between the contractor and government, the 
government will also provide remote access to vendor to system(s) on which the platform is deployed, to 
facilitate trouble-shooting, and performance and feature enhancements. 
 
DELIVERY OR DELIVERABLES: 
The contractor must deploy the scientific imaging data management system to a FMRIF-operated physical 
or virtual computers, and demonstrate how its operations meets and conforms to the above-listed “Specific 
Requirements.”  The contractor must also help ma intain the performance of the system, as well its 
adherence to all security policies and requirements. 
 
REPORTING REQUIREMENTS: 
The contractor should report general performance me trics of the platform (relative compute load, and 
storage usage).  The contractor should also report  when systems are access ed, and for what purposes 
was access made.  The contractor should also notify when system patches and updates become available, 
to enhance performance of the platform, as well as satisfy security and policy requirements. 
 
OTHER CONSIDERATIONS: 
Travel: 
No travel costs are anticipated to execute tis contract.  Any and all travel costs are expected to be covered 
by the costs of this contract. 
 
Key Personnel: 
The relevant key personnel have been specified under the “Level of Effort” section. 
 
Information System Security Plan: 
This statement of work requires the contractor to (1) develop, (2) have the ability to access, and (3) maintain 
a federal information system.  All work on this pla tform, and access to the machines on it will be under a 
Memorandum of Understanding betw een the contract and NIH’s Center  for Information Technology.  
Contractor will maintain systems to provide best perf ormance, and also maintain compliance with current 
NIH policies and requirements. 
 
Data Rights: 
Any data generated by this procurement or system will belong to the Government.  The Government will 
retain all rights over any generated data. 

Version 11-30-2016-1 
FAR Part 11—Describing Agency Needs 
Page 4 of 4 
 
 
Section 508—Electronic and Information Technology Standards: 
The contractor shall comply with Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act (29 U.S.C. § 794d), as amended by 
the Workforce Investment Act of 1998 (P.L. 105-220), August 7, 1998.  This system will not be accessible 
to the general public – it will only be accessible NIH employees with the relevant training. 
 
Publications and Publicity: 
This SOW is not expected to generate any publications. 
Confidentiality of Information: 
This SOW is not expected to utilize or generate any confidential information. 
 
ATTACHMENTS 
1. Independent Government’s Cost Estimate  
2. Evaluation / Selection Criteria

Page 1 of 4

search_text:
### Sheet: Experience
PBES RFI - Experience
Questions
What experience, if any, do you have with supporting the Department of Air Force (DAF) Planning, Programming, and Budgeting (PPB) processes--both MAJCOM and Air Staff?  Please provide examples.
Describe your experience and internal process for creating system training materials.  Based on your familiarity with PBES what are your recomendations to implement changes and improvements to the current training material.
Describe your level of experience and/or any certifications held with each the following: Enterprise Performance Management (EPM), Oracle Analytics Server (OAS), Oracle DB Server, Oracle Data Integrator (ODI), Oracle DRM, Oracle HTTP Server (OHS), Oracle Hyperion Planning, Essbase, Oracle Internet Directory, Oracle Weblogic Server, RHEL, Windows Server 2019 and newer.   If so, please describe how those experiences would be used to support PBES and it's mission to generate Program Objective Memorandum, Budget Estimate Submissin, Rapid Allocation Process, and President's Budget seamlessly. Describe your specific experience managing and configuring Oracle DRM or a similar Master Data Management (MDM) tool within a federal financial context. How do you ensure data governance and stewardship?
Describe your experience in developing, maintaining, and troubleshooting automated data interfaces between a primary system like PBES and external authoritative data sources. Please provide an example where you successfully automated an interface that was previously a manual process.
Please provide contract number, name of customer/organization supported, customer/organization POC including contact information for the questions listed above, if applicable.

### Sheet: Staffing
PBES RFI - Staffing
Questions
What certifications or qualifications would you recommend your staff hold in areas such as software development, cybersecurity, database/system administration, or project management? Do you currently have personnel with the recommended certifications and qualifications to work PBES should they receive authorization to do so?
PBES is hosted on the SIPR network; can you provide a capability support team that has the appropriate security clearances and IA (IAT II or higher) Workforce Certifications to sustain a classified system?
Do you currently have Functional Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) on staff? Functional SME’s must be experts with the POM (Program Ojective Memorandum) , BES (Budget Estimate Submission) and PB (President's Budget) processes from Major Command level to Office of Secretary of Defense.  This includes the Programming function with the different AF panels through the Appropriation Managers on the Budget side of the process. The SMEs must also be familiar with the entire PPB&E process and how it all ties together.  They must be able to integrate the 30-year Plan with the Programming and Budget FYDP (Five Year Defense Plan).
Please provide contract number, name of customer/organization supported, customer/organiztion POC including contact information for the questions listed above, if applicable.

### Sheet: Management
PBES RFI - Management
Questions
Can you describe the recommended team structure, including roles (e.g., system analysts, software engineers, database administrators)?  Can you describe how your team would collaborate with the functional customer (e.g. a Functional Management Office (FMO)) and the Program Management Office (PMO) to ensure seamless POM, BES, PB development?
How would you plan to allocate staff resources for a POM/PB project?  Can you provide details on staffing levels, including full-time equivalents (FTEs) and any subcontractors?  What contingency plans would you put in place to address unexpected staff shortages or turnover?
How would your company handle data validation for rounds (versions within the cycle that add rates, manpower, DAF decisions)  during budget cycles? What steps would you take to update financial or performance data? How would you manage changes and ensure consistency in the data?
Describe your management approach for supporting cybersecurity readiness and providing software ugrades to PBES while delivering new capabiilty and keeping PBES operational (minimum downtime).
How would you manage the project timeline, milestones, and deliverables?  What communication channels would you use to keep the Government informed of your progress and to address any issues?  How would you manage risk and how would you share those risks with the FMO and PMO?
How would you handle changes to requirements, scope, or priorities during the project? Outline your change management process.  What mechanisms would you use to communicate changes to the Government?
Describe your approach to quality assurance (QA) and testing. How would you verify the accuracy and reliability of the IT system the DAF uses to generate a POM and PB?  What testing methodologies (e.g., unit testing, integration testing) would your team employ?
This customer operates under very stringent timelines.  What project management tools and techniques do you use to ensure timely and within-budget delivery?
Regarding standing up/building development environments, what  critical milestones or phases would you include in a schedule or timeline? How would you account for potential delays or unforeseen challenges?  What resources (human and technical) would you allocate to meet the timeline? On average, what is the timeline to build a development environment?
The Government will provide a development environment (DevLab). Describe your experience working within a Government-Furnished Environment. How do you manage configurations, dependencies, and documentation when you do not control the underlying infrastructure?
Please provide contract number, name of customer/organization supported, customer/organiztion POC including contact information for the questions listed above, if applicable.

### Sheet: Processes
PBES RFI - Processes
Questions
Would you provide training to government personnel on using the IT system for POM and PB generation?  How would you transfer knowledge and documentation to ensure long-term sustainability?
Describe how you would align domain subject matter expertise with leading technologies to drive transformation for DAF financial planning/financial management information systems—specifically program and budgeting systems—while leveraging leading commercial software and best practices.
How would your company ensure accurate and reliable data within the annual PPB (POM, RAP, BES, PB) process?  What validation rules or procedures would you follow to verify data integrity?  Are there any specific tools or methodologies you would employ for data validation?  Are there opportunities to leverage BOTS or other automated technology for accelerating data validation exercises?
Provide examples of risks encountered in previous similar projects and how you managed them to ensure project success.
How would you deliver—using Agile, CI/CD, DevSecOps processes—new capabilities for approved requirements while addressing/reducing significant technical debt in a Cloud One environment?
How would you provide "over the shoulder" support to PBES end users in the Pentagon to include supporting with data loading, data validation, use of PBES, etc?
Describe the process you would use to support the PBES PMO/FMO with requirements development, requirements prioritization, product backlog refinement, program increment planning, sprint planning, daily scrum, sprint review, sprint retrospectives, leadership reviews, demonstrations, technical interchanges and engineering reviews.
Tell us about your PPBE experience and experience with systems that supports that process.  Expand how you would validate accuracy and support the system workflow processes and procedures.
What processes would you use in PBES to support Financial and Program Structures such as PE numbers, appropriate codes, program codes, operating agency codes, weapon system codes, program activity codes, and J-books?
Beyond general quality assurance, what is your specific approach to performance and load testing, particularly in preparation for peak usage during critical POM/BES/PB budget cycles? Describe the tools and metrics you would use to ensure the system remains stable and responsive under stress.
Please provide contract number, name of customer/organization supported, customer/organiztion POC including contact information for the questions listed above, if applicable.

REQUEST FOR INFORMATION (RFI)/ SOURCES SOUGHT (SS) 
Program and Budget Enterprise System (PBES) 
6 April 2026 
 
1. SYNOPSIS 
 
THIS IS A REQUEST FOR INFORMATION (RFI) ONLY. THERE IS NO SOLICITATION 
AVAILABLE AT THIS TIME. REQUESTS FOR A SOLICITATION WILL NOT RECEIVE A 
RESPONSE. 
 
The Air Force Materiel Command (AFMC), Air Force Life Cycle Management Center 
(AFLCMC) Business and Enterprise Systems Directorate (BES) Financial Systems Division 
(GBG) Contracting Division (GBK), Wright-Patterson Air Force Base (WPAFB), Ohio is 
conducting market research seeking vendor configuration and capabilities for support, operation, 
maintenance and enhancement of the Program and Budget Enterprise System (PBES). 
 
This RFI is for planning purposes only and shall not be considered as an invitation for bid, 
request for quotation, request for proposal (RFP), or as an obligation on the part of the 
Government to acquire any products or services. Your response to this RFI will be treated as 
information only. No entitlement to payment of direct or indirect costs or charges by the 
Government will arise as a result of contractor submission of responses to this announcement or 
the Government use of such information. This request does not constitute a solicitation for 
proposals or the authority to enter into negotiations to award a contract. No funds have been 
authorized, appropriated, or received for this effort. 
 
The information provided is considered market research and may be used by the Department Air 
Force (DAF) in developing its acquisition strategy, Statement of Objectives (SOO) or 
Performance Work Statement (PWS), and performance specifications. Interested parties are 
responsible for adequately marking proprietary or competition sensitive information contained in 
their response. The Government reserves the right to contact the submitting parties, on an as-
required basis, for further clarification on material provided. The Government reserves the right 
to contact additional customers to obtain information that will be used to evaluate the offeror’s 
interest. 
 
2. BACKGROUND AND DESCRIPTION 
 
PBES subsumed/replaced the DAF’s four legacy Planning, Programming, Budgeting and 
Execution (PPBE) budget formulation systems, ABIDES, RAPIDS, FSDM, and ETT, and fully 
deployed in December 2021. PBES provides capabilities for comprehensive, transparent, 
accurate, and timely financial information in support of program budget and resource allocation 
decisions. 
 
The Air Force Program Executive Office (AFPEO) Business Enterprise Systems (BES) 
Financial Systems Division (GBG) is responsible for performing and managing the acquisition, 
development, implementation, capability support, and life cycle management in support of 
PBES.  Electronic reporting for the planning, programming, and budgeting (PPB) process makes 

PBES a vital component of the Air Force community’s effort to provide timely and accurate 
budget information to make Congressional decisions. 
 
3. REQUIREMENTS OVERVIEW 
 
The Contractor shall provide the necessary experienced personnel, material, documentation, and 
support to provide software configuration, capability support, and user support for PBES and its 
users. As it pertains to software configuration, the contractor shall take an iterative approach to 
ensure feedback can be acted on quickly and that responsive changes can be made at each stage 
of a sprint or product cycle. 
 
The Contractor shall also support the PMO and the SAF/FMIB’s FMO’s (Functional 
Management Office) continuous improvement of the PBES solution throughout the PBES 
lifecycle. The Contractor shall support the PMO and FMO’s management of business capability, 
technical implementation, and configuration for PBES, including continued cybersecurity 
readiness and enduring support for, and required upgrades to, PBES. All design 
methodology/decisions for the environments/application shall be approved by the PMO and 
SAF/FMIB FMO prior to implementation. 
 
The Contractor shall utilize the Agile framework and associated processes documented by the 
PMO to perform development and support sustainment activities for PBES as an entire system, 
to include its various components and all underlying prerequisites. Activities and components 
include, but are not limited to: transition, planning, requirements definition, capability 
development, defect resolution, enhancement development, code refactoring, program increment 
planning and execution, integration and design, configuration management, testing (both 
automated and manual), demonstrations, cybersecurity, training, architecture management, 
database development, management and architecture, data validation, data modeling, data 
extract, transform and load development and processes, environment management, system 
operations support, problem reports and resolutions, standards compliance, data deliverables, 
intellectual property management and compliance, and government furnished property 
management. These activities require the Contractor to be involved in enterprise level strategy 
and planning for PBES, architectural and solution engineering and management, business 
systems compliance and management, agile team iteration planning and execution, and user 
interface and training. USAF owns and operates PBES, and as such is an integrated stakeholder 
and team member of all Contractor activities related to PBES. Likewise, the Contractor is an 
integrated team member of all PBES planning, development, testing, and operations and support. 
 
PBES requires customer service focused on the user experience and technical support focused on 
resolving a technical issue or problem in the fastest, most cost-effective way. The user 
experience is primarily supported by training, subject matter experts (SMEs) and Help Desk 
technicians located at the Pentagon in SAF/FMIB secure office space. 
 
The Contractor shall provide SME support. The SMEs should have extensive knowledge of the 
PPB process, PBES, and can provide operations support, development and sustainment support, 
as required by the Government. The SMEs shall be able to use the PPB process and translate the 
processes into the specific requirements used to develop and operate PBES to generate reports 

such as the Program Objective Memorandum (POM), Budget Estimate Submission (BES), 
President’s Budget (PB) on an annual basis in a high-operations tempo wi

REQUEST FOR INFORMATION (RFI) FOR CAD MODEL CONVERSION AND COMMENT 
ADJUDICATION SERVICES 
THIS IS A REQUEST FOR INFORMATION (RFI) ONLY . This RFI is issued solely for 
information and planning purposes – it does not constitute a Request for Proposal (RFP) or 
a promise to issue an RFP in the future. This request for information does not commit the 
Government to contract for any supply or service whatsoever. Further, the Government is 
not at this time seeking proposals and will not accept unsolicited proposals. Responders 
are advised that the U.S. Government will not pay for any information or administrative 
costs incurred in response to this RFI; all costs associated with responding to this RFI will 
be solely at the interested party’s expense. Not responding to this RFI does not preclude 
participation in any future RFP , if any is issued. 
 
1.0 Description & Background 
Naval Surface Warfare Center Panama City Division (NSWC PCD) is seeking information on 
industry capabilities to perform a large-scale Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model 
conversion and engineering comment adjudication project. 
We currently maintain a system model consisting of approximately 8,000 individual part 
and assembly files. These files were developed in a variety of CAD software packages. The 
primary goal is to consolidate all of these files into a single, native SolidWorks format within 
a compressed timeframe. Additionally, a significant number of engineering comments 
associated with these models need to be professionally adjudicated. 
This RFI will be used to identify potential sources with the technical expertise, capacity, 
and project management skills to meet these requirements. 
2.0 Scope of Requirements 
The overall goal of this effort is to enable a robust, model-based configuration management 
environment for the system model within a SolidWorks PDM (Product Data Management) 
system. To achieve this, all system models must be converted to a uniform, native 
SolidWorks format. 
The work is divided into a primary, time-sensitive task and secondary tasks with a more 
flexible timeline. Interested parties are encouraged to propose timelines and approaches 
that reflect this prioritization. 
 
2.1 Primary Task: High-Priority Model Conversion 

This is the main effort and is on an accelerated schedule. 
• Requirement: Convert approximately 8,000 CAD models from their source formats 
into native SolidWorks files. The primary goal is a rapid turnaround to deliver a 
complete, uniform set of model files. 
• Source Files: 
• 40% Siemens NX 
• 40% SolidWorks 
• 10% CREO (formerly Pro/ENGINEER) 
• 10% Other (Vendors should specify which "Other" formats they have 
experience converting). 
• Key Deliverables: 
• Converted models must be native SolidWorks parts (.sldprt) and assemblies 
(.sldasm) with full feature tree history and design intent preserved. 
• All assembly mate and part constraints must be fully functional. 
• The conversion process must ensure no loss of data, geometry, or metadata. 
• A final, verified, and organized data package of all native SolidWorks models. 
• Project Timeline: All work under this Primary Task must be completed 
within approximately two (2) months from the start of work. 
 
2.2 Secondary Tasks: Drawing & Comment Resolution 
These tasks are secondary to the model conversion and are not bound by the same 
aggressive timeline. Responders may propose completing these tasks concurrently with 
the primary task if feasible, or as a subsequent phase of work. 
2.2.1 Sub-Task: Drawing Update and Re-formatting 
• Requirement: Convert all engineering drawings associated with the models from 
Task 2.1 and update them to a new Government-provided standard. 
• Key Deliverables: 
• All drawings converted to native SolidWorks Drawing files (.slddrw). 

• Drawings must remain fully associated and correctly linked to their 
respective 3D models. 
• All drawings must be updated to incorporate a new Government-provided 
SolidWorks drawing template and adhere to all specified formatting 
guidance. 
2.2.2 Sub-Task: Implementation of Approved Engineering Changes 
• Requirement: Implement approximately 5,000 comments into the newly converted 
SolidWorks models and drawings. 
• Government-Furnished Information: A Comment Resolution Matrix (CRM) will be 
provided. This matrix details all comments and their corresponding Government-
approved corrective actions. All GFI will be CUI (Controlled Unclassified 
Information). 
• Key Deliverables: 
• Execution and implementation of the approved corrective actions directly 
into the native SolidWorks files (both models and drawings). 
• A final disposition report or an updated CRM, verifying the completion of 
each required change. 
• A version-controlled set of the final, modified SolidWorks files. 
3.0 Requested Information 
Interested parties are requested to provide a capability statement that addresses the 
following areas: 
• Corporate Information: 
• Company Name, Address, and Website. 
• Point of Contact (Name, Title, Email, and Phone Number). 
• CAGE Code and/or UEI Number. 
• Business Size and Socio-Economic Status (e.g., Small Business, 8(a), 
Woman-Owned, etc.). The applicable North American Industry Classification 
System (NAICS) code for this effort is anticipated to be 541330 (Engineering 
Services). 
• Technical Approach & Capabilities: 

• A detailed description of your proposed methodology for the CAD conversion 
task, including software tools, quality assurance checks, and processes to 
ensure model integrity. 
• A detailed description of your proposed process for the comment 
adjudication task, including how you would track, report, and resolve the 
comments. 
• An outline of your project management plan. This plan should specifically 
address the two-month timeline for the Primary Task and provide a separate, 
proposed timeline for the Secondary Tasks. Please include proposed 
milestones and your communication strategy. 
 
• Innovative Solutions & Artificial Intelligence (AI): NSWC PCD is interested in 
understanding and potentially leveraging innovative solutions to increase the 
efficiency and accuracy of this project. Responders are encouraged to 
describe any proposed use of Artificial Intelligence (AI), machine learning, or 
other advanced automation techniques in their approach to either the CAD 
conversion or the comment adjudication tasks. Please detail the expected 
benefits (e.g., reduced timeline, improved quality, cost savings) and any 
associated risks of using such technologies. 
• A description of your data handling and security protocols to protect 
sensitive information. 
• Past Performance & Experience: 
• Provide brief summaries of at least two (2) and no more than four (4) projects 
of similar size and scope completed within the last five years. Include the 
client, a description of the work, and the project outcome. 
• Cost Information: 
• Provide a Rough Order of Magnitude (ROM) cost estimate. This ROM should 
be broken down to clearly distinguish between the cost for the Primary Task 
(2.1) and the costs for each of the Secondary Tasks (2.2.1 and 2.2.2). 
4.0 Submission Instructions 
• Responses are requested no later than 1500 CST 29 April, 2026. 
• Submissions should be sent electronically in Portable Document Format (PDF) 
to: garrett.j.calhoun.civ@us.navy.mil. 

• The subject line of the email should read: "Response to RFI for CAD Conversion 
Services" . 
• Proprietary information, if any, MUST BE CLEARLY MARKED. All submissions 
become Government property and will not be returned. 
5.0 Questions 
• All questions regarding this RFI shall be submitted in writing by email 
to: garrett.j.calhoun.civ@us.navy.mil. Phone inquiries will not be accepted.

National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA)

QUALITY ASSURANCE SURVEILLANCE PLAN (QASP)

Foundation Sustainment Support, Software and Integration (FS3i)

Software COTS Procurement and Common Services Task Order

31July 2025

TABLE OF CONTENTS

QUALITY ASSURANCE SURVEILLANCE PLAN (QASP)

INTRODUCTION

This Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP) is pursuant to the requirements listed in the Foundation Sustainment Support, Software and Integration (FS3i) IDIQ Performance Work Statement (PWS) and the “Software COTS Procurement and Common Services” Task Order (TO) Performance Work Statement (PWS). This plan sets forth the procedures, guidelines and surveillance measure for the FS3i Government Program Management Office (GPMO) will use to ensure the required performance standards or service levels are achieved by the contractor.

Purpose

This QASP is a government-developed document used to determine if the contractor’s performance meets the performance standards contained in the contract. The QASP defines the roles and responsibilities of all members of the GPMO, identifies the performance objectives, defines the methodologies used to monitor and evaluate the contractor’s performance, describes quality assurance documentation requirements, and describes the analysis of quality assurance monitoring results.

The contractor is responsible for implementing and delivering performance that meets contract standards using its Quality Control Plan (QCP). The QASP provides the structure for the government’s surveillance of the contractor’s performance to assure that it meets contract standards.

The government has provided the contractor an informational copy of the QASP as an Attachment to the solicitation to support the contractor’s efforts in developing a QCP.

The QASP is part of the contract in that the CO and/or COR will report the results of the surveilled Acceptable Performance Levels (APLs) to the Award Fee Board for consideration in the Award Fee determination. 

Performance Management Approach

The PWS structures the acquisition around “what” service or quality level is required, as opposed to “how” the contractor should perform the work (i.e. results, not compliance). This QASP will define the performance management approach taken by the GPMO to monitor and manage the contractor’s performance to ensure the expected outcomes or performance objectives communicated in the PWS are achieved. Performance management rests on developing a capability to review and analyze information generated through performance assessment. The ability to make decisions based on the analysis of performance data is the cornerstone of performance management; this analysis yields information that indicates whether expected outcomes for the project are being achieved by the contractor.

Performance management represents a significant shift from the more traditional quality assurance (QA) concepts in several ways. Performance management focuses on assessing whether outcomes are being achieved and to what extent. This approach migrates away from scrutiny of compliance with the processes and practices used to achieve the outcome. A performance-based approach enables the contractor to play a large role in how the work is performed, as long as the proposed processes are within the stated constraints. The only exceptions to process reviews are those required by law (federal, state, and local) and compelling business situations, such as safety and health. A “results” focus provides the contractor flexibility to continuously improve and innovate over the course of the contract as long as the critical outcomes expected are being achieved and/or the desired performance levels are being met.

Performance Management Strategy

The contractor is responsible for the quality of all work performed. The contractor measures that quality through the contractor’s own quality control (QC) program. QC is work output, not workers, and therefore includes all work performed under this contract regardless of whether the work is performed by contractor employees or by subcontractors. The contractor’s QCP will set forth the staffing and procedures for self-inspecting the quality, timeliness, responsiveness, customer satisfaction, and other performance requirements in the PWS. The contractor will develop and implement a performance management system with processes to assess and report its performance to the designated government representative. This QASP enables the government to take advantage of the contractor’s QC program.

The government representative(s) will monitor performance and review performance reports furnished by the contractor to determine how the contractor is performing against communicated performance objectives. The government will make determination regarding incentives based on performance measurement metric data and notify the contractor of those decisions. The contractor will be responsible for making required changes in processes and practices to ensure performance is managed effectively.

ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES

Program Manager

The PM has the ultimate authority for the success or failure of the program and represents the Agency to the awarded vendor. The PM’s authorized representatives assist in administering the program contract. The PM is responsible for general/technical and financial administration of the program and to ensure proper government surveillance of the contractor’s performance. The PM is not empowered to make any contractual commitments or authorize any contractual changes on the government’s behalf. Any changes the contractor deems may affect contract price, terms, or conditions shall be referred to the CO for action. The PM and additional representatives will have the responsibility for completing QA monitoring forms used to document the inspection and evaluation of the contractor’s work performance. Government surveillance may occur under the inspection of services clause for any service relating to the contract.

Contracting Officer

The contracting officer (CO) is responsible for monitoring contract compliance, contract administration, and cost control and for resolving any differences between the observations documented by the Program Manager (PM) and/or Contracting Officer's Representative (COR), and the contractor. The CO will designate one full-time COR as the government authority for performance management. The number of additional representatives serving as technical inspectors/monitors depends on the complexity of the services measured, as well as the contractor’s performance, and must be identified and designated by the CO.

2.1 The Contracting Officer’s Representative

The Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) is designated in writing by the CO to act as his or her authorized representative to assist in administering a contract. COR limitations are contained in the written appointment letter. The COR is responsible for general/technical administration of the project and ensures proper government surveillance of the contractor’s performance. The COR is not empowered to make any

contractual commitments or to authorize any contractual changes on the government’s behalf. Any changes that the contractor deems may affect contract price, terms, or conditions shall be referred to the CO for action. The COR will have the responsibility for completing QA monitoring forms used to document the inspection and evaluation of the contractor’s work performance. Government surveillance may occur under the inspection of services clauses (Reference Part I, Section E of the contract) for any service relating to the contract.

METHODOLOGIES TO MONITOR PERFORMANCE

Surveillance Techniques

In an effort to minimize the performance management burden, simplified surveillance methods shall be used by the government to evaluate contractor performance when appropriate. These are the primary methods of surveillance:

100% Inspection – Each month, within 5 business days following contractor delivery, the COR will review the generated documentation to ensure compliance to quality performance

While it is the Government’s intent to conduct inspections 100% of the time, the volume of inspections may exceed the capacity of the GPMO. Should that happen the GPMO will conduct inspections in accordance with the priorities established by the Government Program Manager (GPM)

Periodic Inspection – The COR typically performs periodic inspections on a monthly basis

Random Sampling – At the CO/COR’s discretion, the government may review inspect documents generated by the contractor, without notice.

Validation – manual or automated testing demonstrates that the software baseline is behaving as expected by the users

Customer Feedback – described in 3.1.1

Customer Feedback

The contractor is expected to establish and maintain professional communication between its employees and customers. The primary objective of this communication is customer satisfaction. Customer Satisfaction is the most significant external indicator of the success and effectiveness of all services provided and can be measured through customer surveys.

Performance management drives the contractor to be customer focused through initially and internally addressing customer feedback and investigating the issues and/or complaints but the customer always has the option to communicate feedback/complaints to the CO and COR, as opposed to the contractor.

Customer feedback/complaints may also be obtained either from the results of formal customer satisfaction surveys or from random customer surveys.

QUALITY ASSURANCE DOCUMENTATION

The Performance Management Feedback Loop

The performance management feedback loop begins with the communication of expected outcomes. Performance standards are expressed in the PWS and assessed using the performance monitoring techniques shown in Attachment 1 – Performance Requirements Summary.

Monitoring Forms

The government’s QA surveillance, accomplished by the COR, will be reported using the monitoring form in Attachment 2 – Sample Quality Assurance Monitoring Form. This form, when completed, will document

the government’s assessment of the contractor’s performance under the contract to ensure that the required results are being achieved. The COR and CO will retain a copy of all completed QA surveillance forms.

ANALYSIS OF QUALITY ASSURANCE ASSESSMENT

Determining Performance

The Government will use the monitoring methods cited in Attachment 1 to determine whether the performance standards/service levels/AQLs have been met. If the contractor has not met the minimum requirements, it may be asked to develop a corrective action plan to show how and by what date it intends to bring performance up to the required levels.

Reporting

At the end of each month, the COR will prepare a written report for the CO and GPM, summarizing the overall results of the quality assurance surveillance of the contractor’s performance. This written report, will include the contractor’s submitted report and the completed quality assurance monitoring forms (Attachment 2 – Sample Quality Assurance Monitoring Form), will become part of the QA documentation. It will enable the government to evaluate whether the contractor is meeting the stated objectives and/or performance standards, including cost/technical/scheduling objectives. This report will be provided to the Award Fee board as input for final Award Fee determination and used for purposes of the contractor’s annual Performance Assessment Report (PAR). 

Reviews and Resolution

The CO or PM may require the contractor’s project manager, or a designated alternate, to meet with the CO and/or PM and other government personnel as deemed necessary to discuss performance evaluation. The PM will define a frequency of in-depth reviews with the contractor, including appropriate self-assessments by the contractor; however, if the need arises, the contractor will meet with the PM and/or COR as often as required or per the contractor’s request. The agenda of the reviews may include the following:

Monthly performance assessment data and trend analysis

Issues and concerns of both parties

Projected outlook for upcoming months and progress against expected trends, including a corrective action plan analysis

Recommendations for improved efficiency and/or effectiveness

Issues arising from the performance monitoring processes

The COR will coordinate and communicate with the contractor to resolve issues and concerns regarding marginal or unacceptable performance.

The COR, CO and GPM along with the contractor will jointly formulate tactical and long- term courses of action. Decisions regarding changes to metrics, thresholds, or service levels will be clearly documented. Changes to service levels, procedures, and metrics will be incorporated as a contract modification at the convenience of the CO.

ATTACHMENT 1 – PERFORMANCE REQUIREMENTS SUMMARY

The Performance Objectives and associated Acceptable Performance Levels (APLs) detailed in the table below are referenced from Appendix D – Performance, of the FS3i “Software COTS Procurement and Common Services” Task Order PWS.

SECTION B - SUPPLIES OR SERVICES AND PRICE/COSTS  
 
B.1 SCOPE  
 
The United States Senate (hereinafter referred to as the “Senate”) Office of the Sergeant at Arms 
(“SAA”) is responsible for the delivery of Senate Office web presences on the Senate’s public internet 
network, www.senate.gov.  In order to provide Senate Offices with services and software that meet their 
technical and functional requirements, the SAA will enter into contracts with web developers to design, 
develop, and/or maintain the Senate Offices’ public websites in part or in whole for United States 
Senator and Committee Offices (“Senate Office” or “Office”). The web developer(s) shall hereinafter 
be referred to as the “Contractor”.  
 
B.2 DESCRIPTION OF SERVICES  
 
The Contractor shall assist Senate Offices with the requested website services, as listed in Section B.7.1, 
for their public senate.gov websites.  Any products or services to be furnished under this Contract shall 
be ordered by issuance of Task Order(s) by the Contracting Officer (see Section G.1).  The Contractor 
will be provided with a base build that is configured with tools, features, content types, and plugins for 
use by Senate Office websites.  The Senate reserves the right to further define and/or change 
requirements through contract or task order modifications.  
 
Management rights to the site and its content, in whatever stage of completion, shall be the sole property 
of the Senate Office.  
 
B.3 CONTRACT TYPE  
 
The SAA anticipates award of hybrid Multiple Award Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity (“IDIQ”) 
contracts utilizing both Firm-Fixed-Price and Labor-Hour Task Orders.  Under  a Labor-Hour Task 
Order, the Contractor shall not exceed the existing funding and originally proposed labor hours on the 
Task Order without written approval from the Contracting Officer.  In addition, under a Labor-Hour 
Task Order, if the actual number of hours required to complete the task is lower than the number 
proposed, the Contractor shall invoice for the actual number of hours to complete the work.  All services 
shall be provided at fixed labor rates that will comprise either a Firm-Fixed-Price or Labor-Hour 
estimate per Task Order.   
 
The SAA may elect to award multiple IDIQ contracts for the same or similar services should it be 
determined it is in the best interest of the Senate.  In addition, the Senate reserves the right to resolicit 
and award additional IDIQ Contracts later, as the SAA determines necessary at its sole discretion.   
 
B.4 PRICING  
 
The schedule at Section B.7.1 shall provide the firm-fixed labor-hour and monthly rates for the services 
offered herein under this Contract.  All pricing shall be fully burdened with all administrative costs and 
other direct costs unless specifically otherwise identified or agreed upon by the SAA.  Travel costs are 
not expected under this Contract, however in the event that travel outside of the Metropolitan 
Washington Area is required, the Contractor shall provide a Firm-Fixed-Price for the required long-
distance travel and the SAA shall approve the travel by issuance of a written Task Order or Task Order 
modification prior to the Contractor incurring any such costs. 

Page 3 of 57  
  
The Contractor shall provide website design and development, website maintenance and end-user 
support, training, and content migration services on an hourly basis.  Alternately, Website maintenance 
and end-user support may also be provided monthly at a firm-fixed price (FFP).  All pricing submissions 
shall be rounded to the nearest whole dollar, i.e., $250.46 is $250.00, $250.73 is $251.00.  
 
The Contractor’s goal shall be to deliver all services within the task order value and period of 
performance, in accordance with industry best practices.   
 
B.5 TERM  
 
This Contract is effective for a one-year base period, which shall commence on or about January 1, 
2027.  The Contract will have five (5) one-year option periods.  The SAA may exercise option(s) in 
accordance with Section H.9, Exercise of Options.   
 
B.6 PRICE RE-DETERMINATION AND ADJUSTMENT  
 
The Contractor may offer a price reduction at any time effective upon the date offered by the Contractor 
and shall apply to all services performed on or after the effective date of price change.  Price increases 
shall be mutually agreed.  Any pricing changes shall be incorporated into the Contract through a 
modification hereto.   
 
B.7 PRICING SCHEDULE  
 
The Schedule at B.7.1 contains the rates for the Contractor, which the SAA shall use and issue either 
Firm Fixed Price or Labor-Hour Task Orders to the Contractor as a part of this Contract.   
 
In response to Senate-issued task order proposal requests, Contractors will submit price proposals 
reflecting the rates provided in the table below.

B.8 CONTRACT CEILING AND GUARANTEED MINIMUM   
 
The maximum ceiling of each IDIQ contract is $5,637,600.00.  There is no guaranteed minimum on 
this IDIQ Contract.  The Senate does not guarantee any work from this Multiple Award IDIQ Contract, 
as individual Task Orders will be competed between multiple awarded IDIQ Contract holders.  
 
SECTION C - TASK ORDERS/STATEMENT OF WORK  
 
C.1 INDEFINITE DELIVERY-INDEFINITE QUANTITY ("IDIQ")  
 
This is an indefinite delivery indefinite quantity ("IDIQ") Contract. 
 
C.1.1 Delivery or performance shall be made only as authorized by Task Orders issued in accordance 
with this Contract.  Senate Offices shall purchase services by issuing a written Task Order executed by 
the Contracting Officer.  
 
C.1.2 Any Task Order issued during the effective period of this Contract and not completed within that 
period shall be completed by the Contractor within the time specified in the Task Order, including any 
extension agreed to in writing by the SAA.  The Contract shall govern the parties' rights and obligations 
with respect to that Task Order to the same extent as if the Task Order was completed during the 
Contract term.   
 
C.1.3 The Contractor shall use SAA-provided resources solely and exclusively for providing and 
supporting websites in accordance with Task Orders issued under this Contract and must use Senate 
supplied equipment when connecting to Senate systems, networks, and VPNs. Any and all other 
applications, functionality, utilization, or data are strictly prohibited on, or from, interacting with SAA-
provided resources.  Unauthorized use of SAA-provided resources by the Contractor, its employees, or 
subcontractors may result in contract termination.  
 
C.2 ORDERING  
 
C.2.1 Any services to be furnished under this Contract shall be ordered by issuance of Task Order(s) by 
the Contracting Officer (see Section G.1).  Orders shall be addressed to the Contractor’s point of contact 
in Section A, Block 11A of the Contract.  
 
C.2.2 All delivery orders or Task Orders are subject to the terms and conditions of this Contract.  In the 
event of conflict between a delivery order or Task Order and this Contract, the Contract shall control 
in accordance with Section H.1, Order of Precedence.  The parties shall not use Task Orders to modify 
the Contract.  
 
C.2.3 Task orders will be issued electronically by the SAA to the Contractor.  
 
C.2.4 All Contractor price proposals must use the rates in Section B.7.1 and be submitted to the SAA 
for distribution to the Senate Office(s) for proposal evaluation.  Each  Task Order Proposal Request 
(TOPR) will specify the contract type (Firm Fixed Price or Labor-Hour) for the specific Task Order.  
 
C.2.5 The SAA shall provide a method to initiate the creation or modification of Purchase Order(s), and 
the associated Task Order(s), to fund payments for ordered contract line items.   
 

Page 6 of 57  
  
C.3 STATEMENT OF WORK   
 
The Contractor shall assist the Senate Offices with the website design and development, training, 
content migration, and, if requested, ongoing website maintenance and end-user support of their public 
websites within the senate.gov domain.  
 
Senate Offices’ website content shall be stored and managed using an SAA-provided base build.  This 
base Content Management System (CMS) build is configured with tools, features, content types, and 
plugins for use by Senate Office websites.  For additional information and technical requirements, refer 
to Attachment J-001-A Senate Web Development Guide.  
 
General requirements are stated below:  
 
a. All of the website content shall reside on and downloaded from senate.gov, or from other 
Senate-sanctioned public social media sites. 
b. The site download shall be at no cost to the site user. 
c. There shall be no passive personal data collection, except for Senate-provided analytics tools 
and security logging (external IP capture) explicitly required by Attachment J-001-A Senate 
Web Development Guide and Attachment J-001-B Senate Web Design Guide.   
d. Personal data/smartphone resources can only be activated/used with the express permission of 
the site user.  
 
C.3.1 Pre-Award Procedures  
 
Unless otherwise provided in the Task Order Proposal Request (TOPR), the SAA will follow the below 
procedures for gathering Senate Offices’ website requirements and awarding Task Orders:

RFI Training Management System 
Solicitation Number: 26-FLETC-TMS-RFI 
Agency: Department of Homeland Security 
Office: Federal Law Enforcement Training Centers (FLETC)

Special
