Services 


Synopsis: 
THE PURPOSE OF THIS NOTICE IS TO CONDUCT MARKET RESEARCH: The Department of Homeland 
Security (DHS), Federal Law Enforcement Training Centers (FLETC) is conducting market research to 
determine the capabilities of industry and sources available to provide a Training Management System (TMS), 
to develop and support such a system or provide operational, maintenance, and developmental support for the 
current system in place. FLETC requires a program that can manage, schedule, report and to conduct financial 
transactions for student training. The following is a description of key functions and mission critical processes 
that FLETC’s current TMS provides: 
O
ur current TMS is an enterprise wide IT system that encompasses the majority of the FLETC’s mission–
critical processes. Our TMS is designed around and interconnected with approximately 32 functional areas. The 
TMS allows for automated alignment of the training needs and available training resources to develop a 
schedule based on a series of business rules to ensure proper sequencing of classes. The system primary 
functions include a scheduling system to automate the FLETC’s process of scheduling training programs; a 
student-registration and management system; a testing and evaluation function that provides tools to develop 
and manage tests and other evaluation instruments; a tuition component allowing for detailed monthly and semi-
annual tuitions to be developed; and a student billing component to allow for various daily and one-time charges 
to be billed on a per student basis. The current TMS is the result of combining commercial-off-the-shelf 
software with a government-developed interface to meet the needs of FLETC (Enterprise).  
Th
e TMS Portal is a secure web-based application. Access to the system is controlled by FLETC. The portal 
provides real-time access to FLETC data, applications, and processes. This allows for improved 
communications and collaboration between FLETC directorates and between FLETC and our PO’s. 
T
he key functions of FLETC’s TMS include the following: 
•A
dministration
•C
ontacts
•C
urriculum Design and Management
•F
inancial Management
•H
ousing
•I
nstruction Management
•P
lanning and Forecasting
•R
eporting
•S
cheduling
•S
tudent Administration
•S
tudent Registration
•T
esting & Evaluation
•V
isitor Registration
•Electronic Medical Records
•Ammo inventory management
F
LETC currently has two operating instances of our TMS: a Production environment located in Glynco, GA and 
a Pre-production/disaster recovery site (DR) located in Artesia, NM. If the FLETC decides to move to a cloud 
based option to house our TMS operating environments, in the future the FLETC will desire support for the 
migration from current operating environments to new environments in the cloud.  FLETC wants to understand 
the feasibility of either acquiring a new system for the management of scheduling training and the management 
of all student-required services or the management of the current system in place. We want your honest and 
candid feedback, as it will play a significant role in our procurement planning decisions for Fiscal Year 2027. 

 
 
FLETC welcomes all businesses-large and small-to provide their thoughts on the following questions: 
 
1. Do you have a Software-As-A-Service (SaaS) platform that can perform some or any of the following? 
(Check all that apply, or add additional features provided by your company or software) 
CAPABILITY      RESPONSE 
SCHEDULING YES NO COMMENTS 
Dynamic class scheduling       
Specifiable sequences for blocks of instruction 
(prerequisites) 
      
Customizable business rules for instructional blocks (e.g. 
specified venue required) 
      
Training venue schedule conflict de-confliction 
(automatic by system preferred) 
      
Dynamic instructor scheduling (based on specific 
instructional blocks of variable length) 
      
CURRICULUM DESIGN AND MANAGEMENT YES NO COMMENTS 
Curriculum design - system incorporates class models 
(time of instructional block, varied student group sizes 
based on venue capacity, lesson completion ) 
      
Lesson plan Objective management       
Incorporation of lesson plan scenarios       
Schedule training facilities needed for a lesson plan       
Schedule training at one site to continue on that site       
Ability to categorize facility types (e.g. computer lab, 
standard classroom, firing range, driving range, court 
room, etc) 
      
PLANNING AND FORECASTING YES NO COMMENTS 
Supports forecasting and projections for upcoming FY to 
determine resources (e.g. venues, housing, supplies, 
instructional staff) 
      
Supports allocation (reserved seats for specific agencies)       
INSTRUCTOR MANAGEMENT YES NO COMMENTS 
Manages and maintains assignment pool of instructors       
Manages qualifications and certifications for instructors 
to teach a lesson plan 
      
Tracks instructor availability (incl. vacations and standby 
for call outs) 
      
LODGING YES NO COMMENTS 
Ability to schedule students to housing assignments       
Manages lodging assignment variables to include number 
of students per room, shared bathrooms/gender 
assignments, class integrity in lodging assignments 
      
FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT YES NO COMMENTS 
Ability to account for class item costs, including pens, 
books, % of equipment life cycle, ammunition, guest 
lecturers, contract instructors, etc. 
      

Ability to cost class item costs per student, per specified 
group of students, and per class size 
Resources management - ability to create a pick list of 
resources required for a class 
Ability to generate agency-specific bills on course costs 
based on agencies' specific number of students in the 
class (classes may be comprised of multiple agencies) 
Ability to bill various daily and one-time rates on a per 
student basis (e.g. meals, lodging, tuition, instructor, 
overhead) 
ADMINISTRATION YES NO COMMENTS 
Support student registration, both bulk registration by 
agency and individual self-registration 
Support emergency notifications to various groups (e.g. 
all students, specific classes, instructors) 
Create transcripts 
Support dynamic examinations based on lesson plans 
completed since last exam 
Create random test banks for each lesson plan Objective 
Manage visitor registrations 
Ability to track visitors to a training delivery point 
(campus) 
Ability to assign visitor lodging 
Ability to conduct visitor billing 
REPORTING – such as the following: 
Note: You may provide a list of any other reports that 
may be applicable. 
YES NO COMMENTS 
Budget reports (e.g. cost comparison, tuition, resources, 
inter-entity costing) 
Various lodging reports 
Various lodging projections 
Class schedules 
Master schedules 
Instructor availability reports 
Ad-hoc reporting feature 
STUDENT ADMINISTRATION MANAGEMENT YES NO COMMENTS 
Student surveys management 
Student certificates 
Books 
Student conduct 
Student injuries 
Early departures 
Evacuations 
Low fill class visibility 
Examinations and tests management 
Practical exercise management 

Student rosters       
Ad hoc rosters       
Statistical analysis       
Transcripts       
 
2. Can you manage a system, or do you have a system, that can produce a bill, by month, per student, for 
90+ different organizations?  On a per student basis, this bill may include one-time tuition and instructor 
charges, as well as daily meals, lodging, and overhead rate.  Tuition costs are inclusive of all the resources 
required to complete the training class the student attended.  Students may be billed all five billable items or a 
subset depending upon variables.  There could be as many as 300-400 different classes in a one month billing 
cycle. 
 
3. Can your company manage a system, or do you have a system that can produce up to 600 different class 
schedules that include the following: 
• Lesson plan session and time 
• Facility assignments ofPur over 1,000 unique training venues across four training delivery points. 
• Instructor assignments 
• Resource requirements per session (for example role player requirements, vehicles, training props, etc.) 
 
4. As part of accreditation requirements, would your company be able to manage or provide a holistic 
system of records and versioning for vital areas, such as, lesson plans, schedules, and resource costs?  If not, 
would your company be able to provide independent systems of records that could interface allowing for data 
input and output for data analysis? 
 
5. Can your company develop a wide range of data reporting mechanisms for ad hoc reporting? Unique 
data calls with short deadlines are common.  
 
6. Would your strategy to provide the required services be accomplished in one commercial-off-the-shelf 
(COTS) system or would you use a collection of integrated COTS products to meet the requirements? 
 
7. Does your company have experience providing complex training management services as outlined 
above? If so, provide some examples of past performance and contracts within the past five years. 
 
8. Does your platform have an existing Federal or FEDRamp Authority To Operate (ATO)? 
 
9. Does your company have experience implementing federal IT system security controls? This includes: 
• Creating and maintaining detailed design drawings. 
• Creating and maintaining a detailed Security Plan (SP) that has full descriptions of how all applicable 
security controls are implemented. 
• Creating and maintaining a detailed Contingency Plan (CP) 
• Creating and maintaining a detailed SOP on how to switch the system from the primary location to the 
alternate geographic location. 
• Creating and maintaining highly detailed SOP’s regarding how all aspects of the system are managed 
and how activities are performed. 
• Securing enough personnel to create separation of duties between conflicting roles. 
• The ability to successfully participate in a continuous, highly scrutinized A123 financial audit with 
limited findings, the ability to correct any findings that are cited, and the ability to complete any action 
or provide any documentation requested by the A123 assessors in a timely manner. 
• The ability to work with any Security Operations Center necessary to assist in the event of a security 
incident. 
 
10.  Can your system support both two-factor authentication for user access and a public facing portal for 
non-users?  
 

For this RFI we would like to hear from you by 4:00 pm ET, on Friday April 10th, 2026. When submitting 
your answers to these questions, please feel free to tell us a little bit about your company by including a 
capability statement, or a single electronic pamphlet, of less than 10 pages. Please be sure to tell us if you 
are a large business or a small business under NAICS code 541511 or another applicable NAICS code; and 
please include your company's DUNS number. If you are a small business with any additional small 
business classifications (i.e. women-owned, veteran-owned, 8(a), etc.), then please share those with us too.  
This notice is a request for information only. FLETC is not obligated to release any subsequent solicitation 
for these services nor is it obligated to follow any of the hypothetical solicitation procedures laid out in this 
notice. Should FLETC release a solicitation for any of the services mentioned then it will likely be through 
the government point-of-entry at www.fbo.gov. 
Contracting Office Address: 
1131 Chapel Crossing Road 
Glynco Ga 31524 
United States 
Place of Performance: 
1131 Chapel Crossing Road 
Glynco Ga. 31524 
United States 
Primary Point of Contact.: 
Michael J. Haynes 
Contracting Officer 
michael.j.haynes@fletc.dhs.gov 
Phone: 912-261-4186

REQUEST FOR QUOTATION: 
This is a REQUEST FOR QUOTATION (Not a Purchase Order). Offerors may use another format for 
submission of a Request for Quote (RFQ). The Government reserves the right to make award to the responsible 
vendor whose offer conforming to this RFQ will be most advantageous to the Government, when price 
considered. Incomplete quotes will be considered nonresponsive. Please respond to this RFQ by providing the 
following information, not later than 10:00 JST 4 May 2026 
 
*Quotations submitted in response to this RFQ must remain valid through 10 August 26. Please ensure 
quotations are marked with an acceptable expiration date. 
POC: Katie Kershaw/ Contract Specialist Alt. POC: Michiko Yoshizawa / Contracting Officer 
Phone: 315-226-8035 Phone: 0176-77-3895 
E-mail: katie.kershaw@us.af.mil E-mail: michiko.yoshizawa.jp@us.af.mil 
 
 
Quoter is required to be registered in SAM when submitting an offer or quotation, and shall continue to be 
registered until time of award, during performance, and throughout final payment of any contract, basic 
agreement, basic ordering agreement, or blank purchasing agreement resulting from this solicitation (ref. FAR 
52.204-7 System for Award Management). 
 
Contractors must be registered in Wide Area WorkFlow (WAWF). The WAWF system is the method to 
electronically process vendor payment requests and receiving reports, at http://wawf.eb.mil/. (ref. DFARS 
252.232-7003, Electronic Submission of Payment Requests and Receiving Reports) 
 
Offerors may use another format for submission of a response to this RFQ. All responses must contain the 
minimum information required as listed below. 
 
Please provide the following information: 
 
Company Name:  
 
Name of Offeror:  
 
Offeror Email: Offeror Phone:

A.2 COMBINED SYNOPSIS/SOLICITATION 
 
This is a combined synopsis/solicitation for commercial products or commercial services 
prepared in accordance with part 12. This announcement constitutes the only 
solicitation. Offers are being requested and a separate written solicitation will not be 
issued.  
 
i. Solicitation number 36C10B26R0006 is issued as a Request for Proposal 
(RFP) for Ambient Scribe.  
 
ii. This acquisition is not set-aside for small business concerns. This solicitation 
incorporates provisions and clauses by reference. The full text of provisions 
and clauses may be accessed electronically at www.acquisition.gov.  
 
iii. See Section B.2 below for the Price Schedule.  
 
iv. The purpose of this Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract is to 
provide a Commercial-Off-the-Shelf (COTS), cloud-based, ambient scribe 
solution to transcribe clinical encounters, generate notes, and other Artificial 
Intelligence (AI) advanced functionalities to improve the efficiency of medical 
care utilizing a Software as a Service (SaaS) delivery model with AI 
techniques and technologies. This solution shall integrate with VA’s Electronic 
Health Record (EHR) systems and workflows so providers can initiate 
encounter recordings. This mitigates the need for manual entry of patient 
identifying information and enables automatic insertion of draft notes into 
EHR. Consistent with this SaaS delivery model, standard license-based 
access controls, including expiration of access upon the end of a subscription 
term, are permitted and shall not be considered “disabling software code” 
under Section A.4(4), provided Government access to its data is not impaired. 
 
v. This is an IDIQ Multiple Award Task Order (MATO) contract. Individual Task 
Orders (TO) shall be issued on a Firm-Fixed-Price basis. The ordering period 
for the basic contract shall be 5-years following the date of award.  
 
vi. See Section E for the incorporated provisions.  
 
vii. See Section C for the incorporated clauses. 
 
viii. Any questions/concerns shall be submitted by March 26, 2026 by 3:00 p.m. 
EST with the subject line “Questions – 36C10B26R0006 – Ambient Scribe” to: 
Brandon Caltabilota, Contract Specialist at Brandon.Caltabilota@va.gov and 
Meghan McCloskey, Contracting Officer at Meghan.McCloskey@va.gov.

1.0  BACKGROUND 
The mission of the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) Veterans Health Administration 
(VHA) is to provide superior healthcare services to Veterans of the United States. As 
the largest integrated healthcare system in the country, VA is uniquely positioned to 
develop and provide advanced healthcare capabilities to over 9 million Veterans. One 
significant challenge facing the VA and its ability to deliver high quality and reliable 
patient care to our Nation’s Veterans is the increase in demand for veteran patients to 
be seen within the VA healthcare system. In the short window of time allocated for each 
patient appointment, providers have a multitude of clinical and administrative tasks to be 
completed. This increases risk of important information being omitted due to rushed 
interviews and contributes to provider burn out. The myriad of tasks expected of 
providers beyond caring for patients puts significant cognitive burden and increased 
strain on them professionally. This strain has the potential to negatively impact patient 
care. It can also contribute to provider attrition, causing additional impact to Veterans 
and clinical staff.  
 
One method to reduce the risk of missing vital clinical information that impacts patient 
care planning and reducing cognitive burden for our providers is the use of ambient 
scribe technology. Artificial Intelligence (AI) ambient scribes utilize a microphone on a 
secure device. The ambient AI scribe transcribes patient encounters. It then uses 
machine learning and natural-language processing to summarize the conversation's 
clinical content and produce a transcribed note documenting the visit. Such transcribed 
conversations between patients and providers automatically leverage AI technologies to 
create notes in the appropriate format (generate clinical documentation) and summarize 
a patient’s visit. Automating these tasks increases accuracy of information, saves the 
providers time, and reduces their mental strain. Visit notes that include a patient’s care 
plan is a critical component of treatment and care delivery. The software functions as a 
medical and clinical system, equipment, device, or software (MCSEDS) as defined in 
VA Directive 6008. 
 
Ambient scribe technology also enables other labor-saving capabilities to include but 
not limited to: pre and post visit charting to summarize important information prior to visit 
by either completing the task with AI or integrating with provider pre-charted note, 
coding support, order capture, after-visit summaries, drafting care plans and other 
capabilities.  
 
The ambient scribing solution required is unique to the operational environment and 
specific challenges faced by the Veterans Health Administration (VHA). In January 
2024, VHA undertook an AI Technical Sprint challenge to ensure that potential solutions 
were rigorously tested through a competitive and impartial evaluation process under 
H.R. 4521-Bioeconomy Research and Development Act Of 2021 [America COMPETES 
Act of 2022]. The goal of the competition was to identify the top AI solutions identified 
through the VHA AI Tech Sprint Track 1: Ambient Dictation of Clinical Encounters.  
 
Throughout the Technical Sprint, many solutions demonstrated superior technical 
maturity and clinical performance. Their development was closely aligned with the 
urgent needs of the VHA, specifically targeting enhancements in provider burnout and 
clinical documentation efficiencies. IT professionals and healthcare providers assessed 

36C10B26R0006 A00001 
 
Page 7 of 61 
these solutions for their maturity and practical applicability in real-world clinical settings, 
ensuring that they met the highest standards for operational readiness.  
 
Moreover, the effectiveness and safety of these solutions were evaluated through a 
series of blind scoring assessments. Physicians compared the AI-generated outputs 
against those produced by human clinicians, establishing their accuracy and reliability 
without bias. Additionally, their usability and potential for seamless integration into the 
VHA’s existing EHR systems were critically assessed to determine their capability to 
enhance operational continuity and data consistency within the VA health care system. 
This approach ensured a thorough evaluation of potential Contractors in a manner that 
fostered broad competition and verified that the selected Contractors met legal, 
technical, and operational requirements. Evaluations concluded in May 2024. 
 
2.0  APPLICABLE DOCUMENTS 
In the performance of the tasks associated with this Performance Work Statement, the 
Contractor shall comply with the following: 
1. “Federal Information Security Modernization Act of 2014”

Page 1 of 41 
MAR 2025 
PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) 
Test Scheduling and Administration Services 
FY26-31 
Part 1 
General Information 
1. General:  This is a non-personal service(s) contract under which the personnel rendering 
the service(s) are not subject, either by the contract’s terms or by the manner of its 
administration, to the supervision and control usually prevailing in relationships between the 
Government and its employees.
1.1  Description of Services/Introduction:  This is a non-personal service(s) contract to 
provide support of The Defense Language Institute Foreign Language Center (DLIFLC) 
student and DoD linguist testing programs. The Government shall not exercise any supervision 
or control over the contract service providers performing the services herein.  Such contract 
service providers shall be accountable solely to the Contractor who, in turn is responsible to 
the Government. Contractor will be required to provide Test Administration/Proctoring, Oral 
Proficiency Interview (OPI) and Web-Based Test Scheduling and Program Management and 
OPI Facilitation units as stipulated in 5.0 during the period of performance specified below.  
1.2  Background: 
The Department of Defense (DoD) launched the Defense Language Transformation Initiative 
in 2004 with the objective of improving the Armed Forces’ capacity to work effectively with 
international partners. In January of 2005, the Defense Language Transformation Roadmap 
was published requiring that the Department of Defense and its components train and maintain 
personnel with language skills capable of responding for operations with the correct levels of 
proficiency. In August 2005, the Undersecretary of Defense for Personnel and Readiness 
(OUSD) (P&R)) issued a memorandum tasking the Defense Language Institute Foreign 
Language Center (DLIFLC) to program funds for test development and field testing. DoD 
Directive (DoDD) 5160.41E and TRADOC Regulation 350-70 charged DLIFLC with overseeing 
the standardization, testing, research, development and evaluation of all foreign language 
training and education.  
1.3  Scope: 
1.3.1  On-site Program Manager 
1.3.1.1  It is mandatory that the contractor provides at a minimum one (1) full-time on-site 
Program Manager (PM) to oversee and direct the contractor employees. 
1.3.1.2  The PM will be responsible for learning the basic responsibilities of the three positions: 
Test Examiner, OPI/Web-based Test Scheduler and OPI Facilitator. 

Page 2 of 41 
  MAR 2025 
1.3.1.3  The PM will coordinate daily with both the section supervisors and the COR. 
 
1.3.1.4  The PM must complete all orientation for their position and all orientations and 
certifications required for the Test Examiner position. 
 
1.3.1.5  The PM will be present during the initial orientation of all employees. 
 
1.3.1.6  The PM will also fill the position of OPI Facilitator when required.  
 
1.3.2  Test Examiner: 
 
1.3.2.1  The Test Examiner will administer, or proctor tests as directed by the PM.  Each test 
session is defined as one AM or PM test session.  The government will determine the number 
of sessions required each week. The number of sessions per week will not exceed 50.  The 
COR will notify the contractor with the intended schedule no later than 14 days prior to the 
designated work unit.  The maximum number of sessions in a year would be 2500 and the 
minimum 1250. 
 
1.3.2.2  Authorized examinees who may be tested include active-duty military,       reservist, 
national guard and DoD civilians.  In addition, each session will include testing and applicable 
administrative time. 
 
1.3.2.3  Across the 5 testing labs the maximum number of sessions in one week would be five 
morning sessions, either 0700-1100 and/or 0745-1145, and five afternoon sessions either 
1200-1600 and/or 1245-1645. 
 
1.3.2.4  Contractor will provide the required support to meet the requirement of one (1) 
examiner for every ten (10) examinees, or two (2) examiners for eleven (11) or more 
examinees for tests using audio equipment. 
 
1.3.2.5  Contractor will provide the required support to meet the requirement of one (1) 
examiner for every fifteen (15) examinees, or two (2) examiners for sixteen (16) or more 
examinees for tests not using audio equipment. 
 
1.3.2.6  The Contractor will provide equal coverage for the AM sessions of 0700-1100 and/or 
0745-1145, and equal coverage for the 1200-1600 and/or 1245-1645 sessions. 
 
1.3.2.7  Test Examiners must complete all orientation including the on-line Army Personnel 
Training (APT) Security Awareness Briefing, taking and passing the Test Control Officer Basic 
Test, the Army Personnel Testing Defense Foreign Language Proficiency Test, and the Army 
Personnel Testing Defense Manpower Data Center Test.  All tests will be provided by the 
Government at no cost. 
 
1.3.2.8  Test Examiners may serve as OPI Test Facilitator when assigned by the PM. 
  
1.3.3  OPI/Web-Based Test Schedulers: 

Page 3 of 41 
  MAR 2025 
 
1.3.3.1  No less than two (2) OPI/Web-Based Test Schedulers will schedule OPI or Web-
Based tests as directed by the PM. 
   
1.3.3.2  Authorized examinees who may be scheduled include active-duty military, reservist 
and national guard, military recruit candidates, DLIFLC faculty job applicants and DoD 
civilians. 
   
1.3.3.3  The total number of units for this position is 100, with each unit equally to one 40-hour 
week.  There are 50 units per scheduler. 
 
1.3.3.4  Schedulers also may be required to assist in OPI Facilitation as needed. 
  
1.3.3.5  Hours of operation for OPI/Web-based Scheduling are Monday through Friday, 0700-
1600 
 
1.3.3.6  Schedulers certified as test examiners may assist with Test Administration at the 
request of the Government and as directed by the PM.  Time spent assisting in the test labs 
will be done during their normal 8-hour workday. 
 
 
1.3.4  OPI Test Facilitator: 
 
1.3.4.1  A minimum of one (1) OPI Test Facilitator will facilitate OPI tests as directed by the 
PM.  Each test session is defined as one AM or PM test session. The government will 
determine the number of sessions required each week. The number of sessions will not 
exceed 10 per week total.   The COR or government will notify the contractor with the intended 
testing requirement or schedule no later than 14 days prior to the designated work unit. 
 
1.3.4.2  OPI Test Facilitator will process and monitor all assigned OPI tests. 
 
1.3.4.2.1  OPI Facilitator will prepare equipment and test site for testing. 
 
1.3.4.2.3  When assigned, OPI Facilitator will monitor all tests onsite. 
 
1.3.4.2.4  OPI Facilitator will process all documentation pertinent to each test session. 
 
1.3.4.3  Contractor shall not provide an OPI facilitator when not required by the COR or 
government. 
  
1.3.4.4   Authorized examinees who may be testing include active-duty military, reservist and 
national guard, military recruit candidates, DLIFLC faculty job applicants and DoD civilians. 
   
1.3.4.5  The maximum number of units for this position is 50 when the PM is serving as an OPI 
Facilitator, with each unit equal to one 40-hour week. 
 

Page 4 of 41 
  MAR 2025 
1.3.4.6  Hours of operation for OPI Facilitation are Monday through Friday, 0700-1600 
 
1.3.4.7  The PM shall serve as an OPI facilitor when required or designate a subordinate 
employee in coordination with the Government. 
 
1.3.5  All Contractor employees will complete AT Level 1 Training, OPSEC Level 1 Training 
and IA Assurance training. 
 
 
 
1.4  Objectives: 
The Contractor shall provide non-personal service(s). 
 
1.4.1  No less than six (6) Defense Foreign Language Program (DFLP) Test Examiners. 
 
1.4.1.1  Contractor shall administer approximately 10,000-20,000 language tests throughout a 
one-year period. 
 
1.4.1.2  Contractor shall ensure the operation of testing equipment, ensure test site is arranged 
and ready for testing, follows Department of Army (DA) and DLIFLC regulations, and provides 
clear instructions to all examinees. 
 
1.4.2  No less than Two (2) Oral Proficiency Interview (OPI)/Web-based Test Schedulers. 
 
1.4.2.1  Contractor shall schedule 2,000-8,000 examinees through a one-year period. 
 
1.4.2.2  Contractor shall ensure adherence to schedules, maintain scheduling records, notify 
examinees and testers, and follow DA and DLIFLC regulations. 
 
1.4.3  One (1) OPI Test Facilitator/Test Examiner 
 
1.4.3.1  Contractor shall facilitate 2,000-8,000 examinees through a one-year period. 
 
1.4.3.2  Contractor shall ensure adherence to schedules, maintain sign-in records, notify OPI 
schedulers of no-shows or issues, and follow DA and DLIFLC regulations. 
 
1.4.4   One (1) Program Manager 
 
 
1.5  General Information: 
 
1.5.1  Quality Control Plan (QCP):  
 
The Contractor shall develop and maintain a QCP to ensure services are performed in 
accordance with (IAW) this PWS. The Contractor shall develop and implement procedures to 
identify, prevent, and ensure non-recurrence of defective services. The Contractor's QCP is 

Page 5 of 41 
  MAR 2025 
the means by which it assures that the work provided complies with the requirements of the 
contract. 
 
The Contractor’s Proposed QCP shall be submitted to the Contracting Officer (KO) through the 
Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) for review within ten (10) after date of contract 
award. 
 
The Government will review and either notify the Contractor in writing of acceptance of the 
plan or return their comments to the Contractor within 10 days. If the Government has provided 
comments, the Contractor shall then have 10 days to submit a Final QCP. After receipt of the 
Final QCP, the Contractor may receive the Contracting Officer’s acceptance in writing. Any 
proposed changes to the accepted QCP are required to be resubmitted for acceptance by the 
Contracting Officer no later than 30 days prior to the anticipated change and before 
implementation by the Contractor. The timeline noted above will apply for review and 
acceptance of proposed changes. At a minimum, the QCP must include and answer the 
following to be acceptable (Deliverables B.1). 
 
(a) A chart showing the organizational structure and lines of authority, the names, 
qualifications, duties, responsibilities, and classification of each member of the Contractor's 
Quality Control Team 
 
(b) How the Contractor will monitor work to ensure performance complies with all 
deliverables (etc. timelines, deadlines, and goals) 
 
(c) How the Contractor will monitor work to ensure performance complies with all 
specifications and requirements of the contract, including the contract’s clauses 
 
(d) How the Contractor will monitor and ensure staff qualifications remain current and 
valid including Department of Defense (DoD) Contractor Personnel Office (DOCPER) 
processes/approvals throughout contract performance 
 
(e) How the Contractor will ensure all keys issued will remain controlled items 
(Paragraph 1.7 Key Control) 
 
(f) How the Contractor will inventory and track maintenance of all Government Provided 
Equipment / Materials 
 
(g) How the Contractor will identify, investigate, and correct any non-conforming 
performance and prevent similar deficiencies in the future 
 
(h) How the Contractor will file and save all Quality Control related documents for the 
life of the contract plus 5 years. 
 
1.5.1.1  An Analysis of Quality Control and Timeliness of Submittals (i.e. deliverables) shall be 
provided to the COR within 30 days upon completion of each option period or Task Order 
(Deliverables B.2). 

Page 6 of 41 
  MAR 2025 
 
1.5.1.2  The Contractor shall inform the COR of issues or potential issues that might affect the 
performance within 48 hours of identifying those issues. Verbal reports shall be followed up by 
written reports within 72 hours (Deliverables B.3).  
 
1.5.2  Quality Assurance:  The Government will evaluate the Contractor’s performance under 
this contract in accordance with the Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP). This plan is 
primarily focused on what the Government must do to ensure that the Contractor has 
performed in accordance with the performance standards. It defines how the performance 
standards will be applied, the frequency of surveillance, and the acceptable quality levels 
(performance thresholds). 
 
1.5.3  Recognized Holidays:  The following provides information on recognized holidays for 
the purpose of the PWS. If submittal of any documentation (e.g. deliverables, submittals, etc.) 
deadlines fall on a holiday, the closest work day prior to the holiday will apply as the deadline 
for submittal.  
 
1.5.3.1  U.S. Holidays: Work shall not be performed on U.S. federally recognized holidays 
occurring during the normal workweek unless otherwise directed by the Contracting Officer. 
When a U.S. holiday occurs on a Saturday or a Sunday, the holiday is observed on the 
preceding Friday or following Monday, respectively.  Contractors will not provide services 
during the DLIFLC Holiday Block Leave and/or designated training holidays.  
New Year’s Day January 1st   M. L. King Memorial Day 3rd Monday in Jan 
Presidents Day 3rd Monday in February  Memorial Day last Monday in May 
Juneteenth June 19th                                          Independence Day July 4th     
Labor Day 1st Monday in September                  Columbus Day 2nd Monday in October 
Veteran’s Day November 11th                            Thanksgiving Day 4th Thursday in November 
Christmas Day December 25th  
 
 
1.5.4  Operating Hours:  The Government facility office hours, facility operating hours, and 
the Contractor support hour requirements often coincide, however, they may differ. Please 
refer to the following.  
 
1.5.4.1  Government Facility Office Hours:  Government facility office hours are from 0700 
to 1645, Monday thru Friday, except Federal holidays or when the Government facility is 
closed due to local or national emergencies, administrative closings, or similar Government 
directed facility closings. 
 
1.5.4.2  Government Facility Operating Hours:  The Government facility operating hours 
occur between the hours of 0700-1645, Monday thru Friday except Federal holidays or when 
the Government facility is closed due to local or national emergencies, administrative closings, 
or similar Government directed facility closings. 
 
1.5.4.3  Contractor Support Hours:  The Contractor shall provide support from 0700 to 1645, 
Monday thru Friday, except Federal holidays or when the Government facility is closed due to 

Page 7 of 41 
  MAR 2025 
local or national emergencies, administrative closings, or similar Government directed facility 
closings.  Exceptions can be made for Contract employees to start as early as 0630 and stay 
as late as 1700 and that both the Government and the Contractor’s PM agree to the revised 
schedule.  The Contractor shall maintain an adequate

GENERAL INFORMATION

PLACE OF PERFORMANCE

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

DESCRIPTION

Department of Veterans Affairs

Request for Information (RFI)

Healthcare Provider Credentialing and Privileging Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) and Credentialing-as-a-Service (CaaS)

This is a Request for Information (RFI) only.  This RFI is for planning purposes only and shall not be considered a Request for Quotation.  Additionally, there is no obligation on the part of the Government to acquire any products or services described in this RFI.  Your response to this RFI will be treated only as information for the Government to consider.  You will not be entitled to payment for direct or indirect costs that you incur in responding to this RFI.  This request does not constitute a solicitation for quotes or the authority to enter into negotiations to award a task order. No funds have been authorized, appropriated or received for this effort.  The information provided may be used by the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) in developing its acquisition strategy.  Interested parties are responsible for adequately marking proprietary, restricted or competition sensitive information contained in their response.  The Government does not intend to pay for the information submitted in response to this RFI.

This RFI will be conducted in two parts, Part 1 will cover a potential strategy for a Healthcare Provider Credentialing and Privileging as a SaaS solution, and Part 2 will cover another potential strategy for Credentialing as a Service in which the respondents will address the covering the entire scope of the credentialing process.

The VA seeks industry feedback to inform on these strategies for development of potential future acquisition strategies that supports timely onboarding of healthcare providers, ensures continuous credential monitoring, and maintains full compliance with VA, federal, and accreditation standards. Responses may inform potential set-aside determinations; therefore, submissions should be complete, detailed, and accurate. Interested Parties may respond to one or both Parts as noted in the Submission Instructions. 

PART 1: HEALTHCARE PROVIDER CREDENTIALING AND PRIVILEGING SOFTWARE-AS-A-SERVICE (SAAS) 

Introduction / Background: The VA is conducting market research for a potential strategy involving implementation and deployment of a fully electronic, enterprise-wide SaaS platform that supports all aspects of the credentialing and privileging lifecycle across the VA healthcare system. The goal would be to streamline workflows, reduce provider onboarding time, and transition to a modern, cloud-based system that improves operational efficiency and timeliness of care for Veterans via said solution that will meet all security (e.g., FedRAMP), functional, technical, and privacy requirements.

The VA would potentially seek a fully formed, commercially available SaaS system that integrates technology, automation, and compliance-driven workflows with minimal customization required to meet Government requirements.

As part of this market research, the VA seeks solutions that combine advanced technology platforms with validated processes, strong compliance controls, and demonstrable real-world performance.

Purpose of this Part of the RFI: This Part of the RFI seeks industry feedback and solution concepts from qualified vendors. The Government intends to use responses to:

Validate technical feasibility and market availability

Refine its potential acquisition strategy

Determine industry capacity to meet Healthcare Provider Credentialing and Privileging requirements

Identify best practices and potential innovations

Scope of the Credentialing and Privileging Solution: The solution must be delivered as a total turnkey implementation, meaning the contractor shall be responsible for all components and phases of delivery without reliance on VA-led development or integration efforts, and is customizable to meet VA workflows and processes. The solution shall have the ability to be able to connect to both VistA and the Federal Health Record. The VA seeks a SaaS solution that performs end-to-end credentialing, verification, continuous monitoring, privileging, peer review activities and committee workflows with decisions. The solution must integrate seamlessly with VA medical staff processes and support the full lifecycle of healthcare provider credentialing and privileging.

Salient Characteristics Respondents should confirm their solution supports or addresses the requirements laid forth in the attached excel file within the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) to include the below characteristics. If requirements cannot be met, depict in your response rationale or steps to ensure that the solution could meet the requirements set forth.

Provide a secure, fully electronic credentialing system that supports complete lifecycle credentialing and privileging activities.

Automatically display a complete, accurate list of each provider’s active credentials and all required primary and secondary source verification entities.

Process new-provider credentialing files within five (5) days for providers with no red flags, or no later than 120 days prior to expiration for existing providers.

Maintain provider records in full compliance with: 

100% Joint Commission standards

Minimum 99% primary source verification accuracy

Minimum 99% secondary source verification accuracy

Continuous monitoring of all time-limited credentials for all provider categories (including pending hires, without-compensation, contract, fee-basis, and full/part-time employees).

Support migration of all current VA credentialing data into the SaaS platform.

Provide system access for authorized users at national, regional, and facility levels with role-based permissions.

Generate data reports at national, regional, and facility levels.

Support concurrent end users across the VA enterprise without impeding functionality.

Accurately display Occupational Category and Occupation for all providers.

Collect, verify, evaluate, and maintain credentials in compliance with VHA policy, federal law, and accrediting body standards.

Comply with VHA Directives 1100.20 and 1100.21.

Protect all PHI, PII, and sensitive data in accordance with federal privacy and cybersecurity requirements.

Provide continuous monitoring, automated alerts, and reporting for all time-limited credentials.

Required Information from Respondents for Part 1

Vendors are required to provide the following:

A description of its proposed solution, including people processes and technologies

A response matrix mapping their solution to the RTM

Details of past performance with similar size and scale SaaS deployments, within both Federal and Commercial environments

Vendors must identify in a response matrix what exists in the current solution today and are fully deployable: 

• Credentialing Lifecycle Management
• Primary Source Verification
• Secondary Source Verification
• Privileging Management
• Focused Professional Practice Evaluation (FPPE)
• Ongoing Professional Practice Evaluation (OPPE)
• Continuous Credential Monitoring
• Provider Profile & Data Management
• Workflow Automation & Task Management
• Quality and Compliance Dashboarding
• Reporting & Analytics
• Role-Based Access Control
• Document and Evidence Repository
• Data Migration & Onboarding Tools
• Provider Self-Service Portal

Describe your ability to support the VA requirements to obtain/maintain an Authority to Operate (ATO) and/or current status of FedRAMP status annotating any risks, challenges, dependencies that will impact the operational status of the solution.

Provide a listing and detail about any integrations that you feel would also be necessary to meet requirements with both internal VA systems or external systems, as applicable.

A Rough Order of Magnitude for the proposed solution and listing of any potential dependencies or assumptions that VA should consider.

Additional requirements noted under Submission In

PART 2: CREDENTIALING-AS-A-SERVICE 

Introduction / Background: The VA is exploring a potential strategy to partner with a credentialing service organization to conduct full-spectrum credentialing operations for healthcare providers across the VA healthcare system. The intent is to streamline credentialing workflows, reduce onboarding timelines, and transition to a fully electronic, modernized process that enhances operational efficiency and supports optimal provider readiness.

Credentialing includes obtaining, verifying, and assessing a provider’s qualifications—such as education, licensure, training, experience, and certifications—to determine their eligibility to deliver care within VA facilities. The VA is committed to accelerating credentialing timelines to enhance provider availability and deliver timely care to Veterans.

As part of this market research, VA seeks feedback from organizations that combine advanced technology platforms with robust administrative, verification, and compliance capabilities, demonstrating effective implementation and measurable value in real-world settings.

Purpose of this Part of the RFI: The VA requires information related to a proposed strategy of working with a credentialing service partner that can provide an efficient, easy-to-use, and fully electronic credentialing solution—supported by qualified personnel—to perform end-to-end credentialing activities and coordinate seamlessly with existing VA medical staff processes for eventual privileging.

Requirements: The credentialing service organization shall provide a comprehensive, fully electronic credentialing solution—supported by qualified personnel and compliant processes—to perform end-to-end credentialing functions on behalf of the VA. The solution must meet the following requirements:

Provide a secure, fully electronic credentialing system that supports complete lifecycle credentialing activities.

Ensure the software automatically displays a complete, accurate listing of each provider’s active credentials and all required primary and secondary source verification entities.

Process each provider’s credentialing file within five (5) calendar days of notification of a new provider with no red flags, or no later than 120 days prior to the expiration of an existing appointment.

Maintain all provider files in full compliance with the following standards:
a. 100 percent compliance with Joint Commission standards
b. Minimum 99 percent accuracy rate for primary source verification
c. Minimum 99 percent accuracy rate for secondary source verification
d. 100 percent compliance with VHA Credentialing Policy requirements
e. Continuous monitoring of all time-limited credentials for all VA healthcare providers, including pending hires, full- or part-time employees, without-compensation (WOC) providers, contracted providers, and fee-based providers (currently over 250,000 providers, including nearly 30,000 new providers annually)

Migrate all existing credentialing data and provider records from current VA systems into the contractor’s electronic platform.

Provide routine system access to authorized VA personnel at national, regional, and facility levels, in accordance with approved role-based access controls.

Generate data reports on all provider credentials, with the ability to stratify findings by national, regional, and facility levels.

Support concurrent VA end users while maintaining strict, enforceable role-based access permissions.

Ensure the system accurately identifies and displays both Occupational Category and Occupation for all enrolled providers.

Collect, verify, evaluate, and maintain all provider credentials in accordance with all applicable VHA policies, federal laws, and healthcare accreditation standards.

Maintain strict compliance with all VHA credentialing requirements, including VHA Directives 1100.20 and 1100.21.

Protect and safeguard all Protected Health Information (PHI), Personally Identifiable Information (PII), and VA-sensitive data in accordance with federal privacy and security standards.

Ensure continuous monitoring and verification of all time-limited credentials, with reporting and alerts as specified by VA policies.

Required Information from Respondents for Part 2: 

Respondents must clearly describe how their credentialing service organization meets the requirements listed in this document, including technology capabilities, staffing model, verification processes, compliance standards, and operational capacity. For any unmet requirements, respondents should provide an implementation plan, cost considerations, and timeline to achieve compliance.

Additionally, respondents must detail :
• Demonstrate reductions in credentialing timelines based on previous implementations.
• Provide evidence of validated processes, transparent verification methods, and quality controls.
• Confirm current or planned ability to meet VA Information Technology (IT), security, and data protection requirements.
• Explain how their service model integrates technology with credentialing personnel to improve workflow efficiency.
• Detail software and operational capacity to support high-volume processing, rapid risk flagging, and continuous monitoring.
• Confirm their ability to begin service by October 30, 2026.

Details pertaining to your proposed commercial licensing structures and offering.

Identify if your proposed software was created and developed by your company or the teaming arrangement proposed to meet this requirement. 
• Identify and propose additional capabilities or innovations to further reduce credentialing timelines based on its corporate expertise.
• Describe potential deployment strategies across the VA healthcare system, including staffing approach, dependencies such as required VA support, and a Rough Order of Magnitude for these strategies. 

SUBMITTAL INSTRUCTIONS FOR THE RFI 

This is a request for information (RFI) only.  It is requested that all companies interested in participating in these efforts note their interest and provide indication of their respective capabilities to perform the efforts described above. SUBMISSIONS SHOULD BE IN ONE FILE BUT CLEARLY IDENTIFY ITS RESPONSE TO PART 1 AND/OR PART 2 OF THE RFI. It is not mandatory to respond to both parts of the RFI, but each response should clearly identify which part is being addressed.  Marketing material will not be accepted as an indication of capability.  Only responses related to the Government’s requirements as listed above will be considered.

All responsible sources may submit a response in accordance with the following:

RFI Submissions are limited to 15 Pages for Part 1 and 15 Pages for Part 2. Cumulatively, the RFI Page limitation should not exceed 30 Pages (if responding to both Parts). 

Mark your response as “Proprietary Information” if the information is considered business sensitive. 

NO MARKETING MATERIALS ARE AL

search_text:
P
erformance Work Statement  
Dynamic Frequency Comb Applications Support 
Indefinite Delivery, Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) 
Re
questing Laboratory / Division / Group: 
C
ommunications Technology Laboratory 
Spectrum Research and Technology Division 
Fiber Sources and Applications, (675.02) 
I.
BACKGROUND:
T
he Fiber Sources and Applications Group (675.02), at the National Institute of Standards and Technology
(NIST) Boulder Colorado campus, develops novel applications of frequency combs for optical time transfer,
spectroscopy and ranging.  Of particular relevance here, this group has developed frequency comb-based
optical two-way time-frequency transfer (O-TWTFT) that can allow frequency comparisons and time
synchronization across a distributed network of clocks.  They have also developed dual-comb spectroscopy
for open-path atmospheric sensing. This work requires tight collaboration between laser science and digital
control communities.
II.
PURPOSE:
F
requency comb-based applications of time-transfer and dual-comb spectroscopy require advanced control
algorithms and real-time processing. The objective of this Performance Work Statement (PWS) is to continue
advancement of both systems by exploring the use of real-time phase/timing control of the frequency comb
pulse train output. The work will develop the necessary digital-control of the phase/timing of the frequency
comb output, contribute to the algorithm modifications and system modifications for both time-transfer and
dual-comb spectroscopy to exploit this new capability, and then implement the new algorithms by modifying
the existing NIST real-time digital signal processing systems accordingly.
T
his work will require the tight integration of FPGA based digital control systems with a number of laser
frequency comb platforms. The goal is to build upon existing infrastructure in comb control including phase
locking algorithms and FPGA platforms. Algorithm development by the contract will be performed with high
level guidance only and the contractor will need to provide technical solutions to a range of scientific
problems. Strong understanding of femtosecond laser operation, free-space optical time transfer and phase
locking will be required.
T
he current state-of-the-art for this work can be found in the following publications
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-022-05225-8 and
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06032-5
Draft

 
 
 
Figure 1:  Existing real-time digital signal processor hardware for the optical time transfer.   
The digital signal processor for the dual-comb spectroscopy is similar to this design but uses a 
different FPGA. The digital control system for the frequency combs is located at 
https://www.nist.gov/services-resources/software/fpga-based-digital-control-box-phase-
stabilization-frequency-comb.   
 
Place of Performance:  
 
The development work shall be completed at the Contractor’s facility. Any required onsite testing and 
meetings will be conducted at the NIST, Boulder, CO campus, specifically Bldg. 1 (i.e., the Radio bldg.) and 
Bldg. 81 (i.e., the Precision Measurement’s Lab). Specific travel requirements based on required tasks will 
be identified under the respective task order. The Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) and/or Subject 
Matter Expert (SME) may direct the flow of technical matters while on the Boulder campus. 
 
Regular Business Hours: 
 
Regular business hours are Monday through Friday, 8:00 am to 5:00 pm Local Time, excluding Federal 
holidays and NIST closures. 
 
Period of Performance/Delivery Date: 
 
Base Period 1 Year 
Option Period 1 1 Year  
Option Period 2 1 Year 
Draft

 
 
Option Period 3 1 Year 
Option Period 4 1 Year 
 
III. SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS: 
 
1. The following are the types of services that may be procured via Task Orders: 
 
a. Task 1 - Algorithms and digital implementation for dynamic, arbitrary real-time control of the comb 
phase/time output 
 
NIST frequency combs are controlled by a field-programmable gate array (FPGA) controller. 
Through real-time analysis and feedback to the comb, the controller is able to adjust the absolute 
timing of the comb pulses in an arbitrary fashion, limited only by the comb actuator. While the basic 
comb control has been shown, the Contractor shall build on existing technology to develop advanced 
algorithms to generate the maximum dynamic flexibility in the comb phase/timing control. The 
Contractor shall develop and implement algorithms necessary in VHDL/Verilog code for the FPGA. 
The Contractor shall modify this comb control to implement the applications. 
 
Task 1.1 - Develop next generation firmware for maximum dynamic control of the frequency 
comb pulse train phase/timing to accommodate platform motion 
 
The contractor will refine existing control firmware and algorithms to support new frequency 
comb platforms and actuator designs as well as scan patterns that will be required to 
accommodate platform motion and atmospheric induced timing noise in optical time transfer 
applications as well as novel scan petters for spectroscopy. The firmware on the existing NIST 
FPGA comb controller shall be updated, tested and optimized for maximum dynamic control 
allowing the slewing of control signals to compensate doppler shifts. 
 
Task 1.2 - Dynamic and coherent CW laser scanning 
 
The contractor will propose and develop algorithms to maintain tight phase lock between a 
frequency comb and a CW laser while that laser is swept to match the expected Doppler shift 
profile associated with a reflection off of a LEO satellite. Sweep will match both the expected 
frequency shift range and slew rate while maintaining less than 0.1 rad of residual phase noise on 
the CW laser even in the presence of comb crossings.  
 
Task 1.3 - Variable logging and system start up 
 
Contractor will facilitate software and firmware development to ensure that critical measurement 
variables such a repetition rate are implemented in the control software. Additionally, the 
contractor will contribute to the development of algorithms to simplify cold start operations. 
Contractor should propose a list of logged frequency comb parameters that will be necessary to 
recover all required time shifts. Logging and cold start algorithms will be updated with each 
iteration of the systems design on one year cadence. 
 
b. Task 2 - Long-range Optical Time Transfer through phase-controlled combs 
 
Research has shown that Optical Time Transfer could operate at much lower received powers, and 
therefore longer range, if it were possible to dynamically control the phase of the frequency combs 
(see Task 1). The Contractor shall work with NIST to advance current algorithms to fully develop this 
Draft

 
 
phase-control approach of the combs for a long-range mode of operation in the presence of platform 
motion. This task shall include contributions to overall system design, algorithm development, 
implementation in the existing NIST real-time digital signal processor through firmware 
modifications to both the FPGA and digital signal processor, field tests, and data analysis, as well as 
porting existing code bases to new FPGA platforms as old equipment becomes unavailable. This 
process shall involve the Contractor participating in at least two field tests of the system for a total 
duration of two months. 
 
Task 2.1 - System design and algorithm development for platform motion 
 
The Contractor shall develop tracking oscillator algorithms that can deal with the substantial 
platform motion expected when transferring femtosecond timing from ground to a LEO satellite. 
Work will mix algorithms for steering the frequency combs to ensure the pulses reflected off the 
satellite are always within 10 fs of the pulse center of the pulses from the local tracking oscillator 
comb. Additionally, the contractor should develop algorithms to ensure that the heterodyne beat 
between the Doppler-shifted return frequency comb and the tracking oscillator comb is well 
controlled enough to allow narrow filtering of return signals for improved signal in the presence 
of high link loss. Contractor should propose viable solutions for measuring and compensating for 
these Doppler shifts.   
 
Task 2.2 - Implementation of Long-range Optical Time Transfer 
 
The Contractor shall implement the algorithms by making modifications to the VHDL and C++ 
code in the existing real-time digital signal processor hardware for the optical time transfer. The 
Contractor shall also develop the Python code for the associated modifications to the GUI 
interfaces. The Contractor will also provide for the porting of existing capabilities to newer 
hardware platforms. 
 
Task 2.3 - SLR station field tests and data analysis  
 
The Contractor shall participate in three rounds of field tests of the optical time transfer system at 
an SLR station. Field tests are still being scheduled but will occur on a roughly 18-month 
cadence starting in September 2026. The Contractor shall perform data analysis and 
documentation of the results of the field tests and will work with the team to iterate on the 
control and Doppler correction algorithms to maintain femtosecond level time-transfer 
performance. The data analysis shall generate level 2 processed data directly from the raw data 
files saved by the real-time digital signal processor. The data analysis shall be completed in 
Matlab and the Matlab code library supplied to NIST with sufficient documentation to reproduce 
the analysis in another language such as Python.   
 
Task 2.4 - SLR Optical Communication channel  
 
The Contractor shall specify hardware, presumably an FPGA or RFSoC, and build a digital 
backbone for an optical communication channel on this platform. In the first iteration the digital 
backbone will generate a data stream at least 10 MB/s that will be written onto a cw laser with an 
EOM or used to directly amplitude modulate a diode laser. The second iteration will incorporate 
PRBS streams (or similar) that can be used for course ganging and timing measurements 
between nodes. A third iteration will push data rates up to 1 GB/s 
 
Task 2.5 - Optical Terminal Tracking Control  
Draft

 
 
 
The Contractor shall develop robust software solutions to interface with existing optical terminal 
hardware and will enable active tip tilt correction for the free space link at >100 Hz feedback 
bandwidths. Error readout in this is done with a SUI 320CSX camera and which will record the 
center of mass of the incoming beam and will feed a +/-2V control signal for X and Y degrees of 
freedom to either a fast-steering mirror or a galvo mirror pair. Camera readout and center of 
mass processing as well as the generation of the control signal should be done on a 
microcontroller or the contractor’s choosing. 
 
c.  Task 3 - Phase-stepped dual-comb  
 
Dual-comb and related measurement techniques can operate with a much higher signal-to-noise ratio 
by dynamically controlling the phase of the frequency combs (see Task 1). The Contractor shall work 
with NIST to exploit this new capability by consulting on advanced algorithms and system design. 
The algorithms shall be capable of real-time operation on existing NIST digital processors. The 
system design shall be documented to the component level with a preference for standard components 
already in use by NIST. The Contractor shall modify the NIST FPGA comb controller system to 
interface with the dual-comb system both for control and data acquisition. The Contractor shall 
facilitate the testing of the system and testing with a focal plane array through algorithm development 
and firmware/software modifications. This process shall involve the Contractor participating in at 
least one lab experiment. 
 
 Task 3.1 Algorithms and system design for phase- stepped dual-comb spectroscopy 
 
The Contractor shall develop the system design and algorithms for phase-stepped dual-comb 
spectroscopy based on the results of Task 1. 
  
 Task 3.2 Implementation and testing 
 
The Contractor shall assist with the implementation of the necessary software/firmware changes 
in the existing NIST FPGA comb controller and dual-comb acquisition system for phase-stepped 
dual-comb spectroscopy. 
   
For code developed under Tasks 1 through 3, the following specifications shall be met:  
 
1. All code shall be covered with unit tests to at least 95% to verify functionality and to guard against 
regressions from future refactoring, extension, and/or bug fixes. 
 
2. All code shall be supplemented by software test benches that enable simulation and unit testing 
without the need for hardware. Testing code may be written in Matlab or Python. 
 
3. All code shall be documented by appropriate comments and naming conventions (in addition to Task 2 
above.) 
 
4. Proprietary or closed-source software, including FPGA IP cores, shall not be used unless the 
Contractor has previously verified NIST already owns such IP cores.   
 
5. Code shall be organized to be reviewed by the Government on a regular basis, as specified in the 
deliverable table (Section IV).  The results of the reviews are to be integrated into successive 
development steps. 
Draft

 
 
 
6. This code shall be compatible with the existing firmware and custom NIST real-time processor for 
optical two-way time transfer. 
 
7. All project software being developed shall be maintained in a suitable repository (e.g., GitHub) to 
allow Government to monitor development progress and provide rapid feedback when changes are 
needed. 
 
8. The documentation shall include a description of the basic algorithms and their implementation in the 
DSP or FPGA. The documentation shall also include a basic mathematical description of the algorithm 
appropriate for publication in a scientific journal. 
 
The code shall be implemented on the custom NIST real-time processor shown in Figure 1.  
 
III. GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
 
1. Government Responsibilities: 
 
Government will provide access to the time transfer systems during development cycles. Because of the 
cost of this access, access time will be limited to 2 weeks per year. Early-stage development should be 
done on a vendor owned test system and implemented on the government system only after significant 
testing has been done. 
 
2. Government Furnished Property  
 
All property, data and information provided by the Government in the performance of this task remains 
the property of the Government and shall be surrendered to the government upon completion or 
termination of this requirement. Likewise, all deliverables generated under this requirement remain the 
property of the Government. 
  
IV. REQUIREMENTS FOR CONTRACTOR PERSONNEL 
 
1. Homeland Security Presidential Directive-12 (HSPD-12) 
  
The Contractor (and/or any subcontractor) and its employees who require physical access to NIST 
facilities or logical access to NIST IT networks or

title: 7E--DOI TABLET COMMAND
search_text:
Justification for Other than Full and Open Competition Bureau of Land Management

FAR 13.106-1(b)(1) Simplified Acquisitions

PR # 0044040825

Identification of the agency and the contracting office. 

Bureau of Land Management – Department of the Interior -US Wildland Fire Service 4955 Canyon Crest Dr.

Riverside CA, 92507

Description of supply or service required to meet the agency’s needs. 

Tablet Command is an approved app that can be downloaded on our mobile devices through the DOI company portal. In order to use the app we need to purchase 25 licenses for users. We also need them to establish a portal for our organization that has maps of our specific Direct Protection Areas, Mutual Threat Zones, as well as the real-time satellite location data for all of our fire resources. We also need “view only” access to our cooperator’s Computer Aided Dispatch logs for fires, and vehicle location data for those same cooperators who we have data sharing agreements with. We also need the functionality to use tablet command for our Incident Commander’s to manage our Incidents.

The rationale or circumstances for limiting competition. We need to be able to receive and share data with our cooperators in real time, in the field, on mobile devices. This is important so we can have a common operating picture and make timely decisions on wildland fires that don’t conflict with our cooperator’s decisions. There is no other platform that can share the types of information that we need to share. We need to see the real time GIS locations of our cooperator’s fire equipment, and they need to be able to see our equipment locations in GIS. We need to be able to view Computer Aided Dispatch notes for our cooperators with data sharing agreements.

A description of the Market Research (MR) conducted and the results or a statement of the reason MR was not conducted. 

The Government has conducted research for a platform that could have the features and integration of tablet command. The data sharing ability with cooperators is the most important aspect. So many of our cooperators already use Tablet Command including San Bernardino County, Cal Fire SDU, Cal Fire RRU, Cal Fire BDU, San Diego County, Riverside County, Kern County, Imperial County, Los Angeles County, Redlands City, Highland City, Apple Valley City, Victorville City, and Palm Spring City. Since these cooperators are already using this platform, it is really easy to share locations, intelligence, and other data to maintain a common operating picture. There are many times on incidents that we are in unified command with 3 or 4 of these cooperators and the timely information sharing is critical. Tablet Command will remove several barriers to much of the information.

The Government was unable to locate a competitor with the ability to view interagency shared AVL (automatic vehicle locations or view shared interagency CAD (computer aided dispatch) incident data across

jurisdictional boundaries. These two items are the most critical components that we need to carry out our mission.
