1 
 
 
DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION 
FEDERAL HIGHWAY ADMINISTRATION 
JUSTIFICATION FOR OTHER THAN FULL AND OPEN COMPETITION  
 
 
1. Identification of the agency and the contracting activity  
 
Federal Highway Administration Headquarters, Office of Acquisition and Grants 
Management, 1200 New Jersey Avenue, S.E., Washington, DC 20590. 
 
- for     - 
 
The Build America Bureau, U.S. DOT Office of the Secretary (the Bureau) 
1200 New Jersey Avenue, S.E., Washington, DC 20590 
 
 
2. Nature and/or description of the action being approved 
 
The Bureau has a requirement for specialized financial analysis and credit risk modeling 
support services to support components of its Credit Programs. Specifically, this 
requirement would be for unique and critical services that will support and maintain the 
Railroad Credit Assessment and Portfolio Management System (RCAPM) that supports 
the Bureau credit Office’s (OST-Z20) Railroad Rehabilitation and Improvement 
Financing (RRIF) Program, and assists OST-Z20 in generating loan level subsidy 
calculations, modification cost analyses, and annual re-estimates, as well as the annual 
budget formulation calculations in accordance with Federal Credit Reform Act (CRA) 
requirements. 
 
3. Description of the supplies or services required to meet the agency’s needs 
(including the estimated value) 
 
Task 1: Maintenance of Railroad Credit Assessment and Portfolio Management System 
x Maintain, test, and deploy two separate RCAPM production instances (external with 
2-factor auth; internal with PIV/OWIN/iCAM), plus two staging environments for each; 
follow Configuration Control Board (CCB) procedures for all code/data changes and 
facilitate internal/external testing prior to production rollout. 
x Ensure error-free operation of all RCAPM modules (CRA, Re-estimate, Budget 
Formulation, Modification, Loan Management), provide technical support and coding 
fixes, and manage security remediation per severity timelines (Critical: immediate; High: 
30 days; Medium: 60 days; Low: 90 days), including participation in monthly security 
briefings and ongoing control assessments. 
x Maintain comprehensive continuity and recovery procedures to minimize downtime, 
participate in tabletop and full contingency exercises (failovers, backups, restorations), 

2 
 
and ensure compliance of contractor processes with required security assessment 
controls. 
Task 2: Updates to the Railroad Credit Assessment and Portfolio Management System 
x Ensure RCAPM credit-subsidy modules (CRA, Re-estimate, Budget Formulation) stay 
consistent with OMB’s Credit Subsidy Calculator and update CRA inputs (discount rates, 
default/recovery rates, collateral haircuts, borrower rates) as required. 
x Support Loan Management System data import, management, and reporting—guarantee 
error-free data transfer to dependent modules, implement changes for new 
reporting/data/import requirements and external system interfaces (Delphi, iCAM, 
OMB), and manage users/user groups and access. 
x 3HUIRUP KLVWRULFDO GHIDXOW DQDO\VLV  UDWHG FRPSDQLHV  WRXSGDWH ORJLVWLF UHJUHVVLRQ
coefficients for the internal credit-rating model and RRIF portfolio adjustments, apply 
results to RCAPM, and update all supporting documentation. 
Task 3: Develop and Conduct Training for RCAPM system to Bureau Personnel 
x The Contractor shall develop and provide in-person training for RCAPM system to the 
Bureau staff on an as needed basis during the life of the contract. Training shall be 
provided at the Bureau site. 
Task 4: Provide Modeling Support 
x Consult with the Bureau on complex subsidy calculations, provide recommendations, and 
assist with audit responses related to the RCAPM system. 
x Run subsidy scenarios (including altering technical assumptions), perform modification 
analyses with supporting spreadsheets, and build Excel-based models for analyses outside 
the RCAPM framework when needed. 
x Participate in third-party meetings as requested (e.g., OMB, DOT leadership, OST, 
Treasury, ESC) to represent analyses and coordinate with stakeholders. 
Task 5: Provide Budget Formulation, FAIC / Reestimate, and Loan Management Support 
x Provide budget formulation and execution support: determine Budgetary Resources for 
Delphi’s Budget Execution Module, produce supporting spreadsheets and an annual 
methodology summary, prepare Credit Supplement input for the President’s Budget, and 
compute Financing Account Interest (FAIC) and year-end borrowings. 
x Perform interest and technical re-estimates and reconciliations: run Re-estimate Module 
calculations with memos for the Bureau/OMB, update R-Summary for OST/OMB 
approval, recalculate interest/principal and revise repayment schedules on prepayments, 
and reconcile loans monthly between ESC and LMS. 
x Support audits and portfolio actions: respond to borrower audit requests with LMS/ESC 
reconciliations, review requests to sell collateral for financing-agreement compliance, 
and implement required LMS updates. 

3 
 
Task 6: Ongoing RRIF Program Support 
x Provide information for the preparation of the apportionments (SF-132s) to OST for 
OMB signature. 
x Review the budget execution reports (SF-133s) on a monthly basis and reconcile against 
the RCAPM system. 
x Work with ESC to identify unobligated balances to be transferred to the Treasury’s 
general fund. 
x Support the preparation of MAX Data based on OMB Circular A-11. 
x Support of financial inquiries and payment/disbursement processing requests for the 
RRIF program from financial reporting team. 
The total estimated amount of this anticipated contract is  to be expended 
over an 23-month period of performance. 
 
 
4. Statutory authority permitting other than full and open competition 
 
The authority to enter into a contract for this effort without full and open competition is 
DOT Deviation No.2025-010 dated July 24,2025, which implements the Revolutionary 
FAR Overhaul (RFO) Part 6 competition requi rements per 6.103-1. Only one responsible 
source and no other supplies or services will satisfy agency requirements.  This justification 
is prepared under the authority of 41 U.S.C. 1901. BancLab designed the subject portfolio 
management tool, inclusive of coding, calcu lations, and updates to align with Bureau 
practices. Searches for contractors/vendors  with the requisite knowledge of the RRIF 
program in concert with an understanding of federal credit subsidy program reporting and 
having a readily available portfolio management tool for a seamless transition to the DOT 
and Bureau’s future state (Google) operating environment revealed that BancLab is the 
only knowledgeable contractor that can provi de this service as required and without 
substantive delay. 
 
5.  Demonstration that the proposed contractor’s unique qualifications or the nature of 
the acquisition requires use of the authority cited 
 
BancLab LLC built the RCAPM Application for the Bureau’s RRIF credit program.  This 
includes coding, subsidy calculations/inter polation, program modules, and reporting 
functionality that comports with the requirements of the Credit Reform Act. The Bureau 
relies on the functionality and unconstrained availability of RCAPM to manage its RRIF 
program.  Only Banclab has the required knowledge and experience to support and 
maintain this system in the short term while the Department of Transportation transitions 
to Google and aligns its requi rement with the OneGov initiative. This sole source award 
will allow time for the Government to transition this requirement to a competitive 
requirement in the future.   
 
Market Research performed resulted in no other commercially available solution or vendor 
that provides an application with this f unctionality readily available and without a 


4 
 
requirement for extensive customization to meet the needs of the RRIF program. The time 
and coordination efforts required to develop and customize a comparable application will 
hinder the Bureau’s ability to facilitate the close of RRIF projects currently in the credit 
review process that require financial information/parameters produced by RCAPM and/or 
unintentionally cause delays and inaccurate reporting of end of year calculations and audits.  
Due to the timeframe required and the unique qualifications of Banclab, only one source 
will satisfy the Government’s requirement.  
 
 
 
7. Determination by the Contracting Officer that the anticipated cost to the 
Government will be fair and reasonable 
 
The FHWA Contracting Officer will conduct a price analysis of the contractor’s proposal 
to  ensure that all costs associated with the proposed contract are fair and reasonable.   
8. Description of the market research conducted and the results or a statement of the 
reason market research was not conducted 
 
The GSAeLibrary Contractor Listing was searched for the presence of qualified contractors 
using NAICS Code 541511 (Custom Computer Programming Services) and key words 
indicative of the knowledge and experience requi red to perform the services required for 
successful performance under this contract.  No contractors were identified.  Additionally, 
online searched for vendors providing such services supporting federal programs did not 
identify any sources that could meet the Bureau’s immediate needs.  
 
9. Other facts supporting the use of other than full and open competition 
 
The Build America Bureau intends to recompete this requirementvia full and open 
competition in the future. To allow for full and open competition, the Government intends 
to conduct an Industry Day and a request for information (RFI) notice in spring of 2026 to 
gain private sector perspective on required services and work elements and to help develop 
a competitive solicitation  
 
10. Listing of the sources, if any, that expressed, in writing, an interest in the 
acquisition. 
 
The Government posted a notice on SAM.gov of its intent to solicit this requirement sole 
source and accepted capability statements from interested vendors. One source responded 
with a capability statement, DreamCatchers Consulting LLC.  The Government reviewed 
the capability statement and determined that this vendor was not capable of meeting the 
requirement in the timeframe required.  
 
11. Statement of the actions, if any, the agency may take to remove or overcome any 
barriers to competition before any subsequent acquisition for the supplies or 
services required. 

5 
The Government will recompete this requirement using full and open competition.  
12. Certification
I certify that the information provided in this justification is complete and accurate to the
best of my knowledge and belief.
_______________________________________________  _________________ 
Melvin Waldrop Date 
Acting Head of Portfolio Management 
Build America Bureau (OST-Z20)

BACKGROUND

The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), Office of Information & Technology (OIT), Office of Information Security (OIS) strives to provide high quality, effective, and efficient Information Technology (IT) services to those responsible for providing care to the Veterans at the point-of-care as well as throughout all the points of the Veterans’ health care in an effective, timely and compassionate manner. VA depends on Information Management (IM)/IT systems to meet mission goals.

OIS is one of six divisions within OIT. OIS’s mission, in collaboration with its business partners, is to provide an enterprise information security program that strengthens security strategy, risk management, engineering excellence, policy, budgeting, and operations services to over 25 million Veterans, families, and their caregivers.

Within OIS, the Information Security Programs and Logistics (ISPL) service line is responsible for coordinating activities across OIS to ensure operations and the use of resources are integrated, transparent, and efficient. ISPL develops the program’s strategic goals, objectives, and recommends necessary adjustments to improve the organization’s ability to meet those goals. Additionally, ISPL evaluates, develops, recommends, and implements management and administrative practices for efficiencies and to meet organizational goals and objectives. ISPL functions include the following: Cybersecurity Program Planning; Programming; Budgeting and Execution; Acquisition Management; Cybersecurity Program Integration; and OIS logistics functions, such as, Chief of Staff and Business/Program Administration.

A primary task within the lifecycle management of OIT Audit processes is ISPL’s support of multiple audit activities that involve Veterans Health Administration (VHA), Veterans Benefits Administration (VBA) and National Cemetery Administration component agencies and their respective applications. These activities include interviews of management personnel, inspection of information security policies and procedures, observation of operational controls, and technical control testing of VA general support systems and major applications. Additionally, corrective actions identified in prior audits are often evaluated for implementation.

ISPL supported audits currently include, but are not limited to, the following:

A.	Federal Information Security Management Act (FISMA) of 2002 (“FISMA”, 44 U.S.C. § 3541) is a United States Federal Law enacted in 2002 as Title III of the E-Government Act of 2002. The act requires each federal agency to develop, document, and implement an agency-wide information security program to ensure the adequacy and effectiveness of VA’s information security programs. FISMA requires agency program officials, Chief Information Officers, and Inspector Generals to conduct annual reviews of information security programs.

B.	Federal Information System Controls Audit Manual (FISCAM) provides auditors with specific guidance in order to evaluate the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information systems. FISCAM is consistent with the National Institute of Standards and Technology’s (NIST) guidelines for complying with FISMA. NIST Special Publication 800-53 provides recommended security controls for federal information systems and organizations. Appendix 3 of FISCAM provides a crosswalk to those controls. On a recurring basis, the VA conducts audits of IT systems, programs, and procedures to identify material weaknesses within VA IT systems.

C.	Internal Revenue Service (IRS) Publication 1075 requires the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to ensure that personal and financial information furnished to the Internal Revenue Service is protected against unauthorized use, access, or disclosure. The VA receives Federal Tax Information directly from the IRS and from secondary sources such as the Social Security Administration. Pursuant to Internal Revenue Code IRC 6103 or through an IRS-approved exchange agreement, the VA must have adequate programs in place to protect any data received.

The IRS conducts cyclic audits to evaluate the VA’s compliance with IRS data security requirements. The ISPL Division’s functions include identifying broad trends of non-compliance among all audit activities (FISMA/FISCAM, Government Accountability Office (GAO), IRS, etc.); identifying and involving stakeholders at all levels during all audit activities, facilitating root cause analysis on audit findings: coordinating remediation activities based on applicable laws and regulations and validating enterprise compliance implementation activities.

1 
 
Program Executive Office - Defense Healthcare Management Systems 1 
(PEO DHMS)  2 
Deployment Solutions  3 
Performance Work Statement (PWS) 4 
1. Background 5 
The Program Executive Office - Defense Healthcare Management Systems (PEO DHMS) 6 
is responsible for delivering advanced health IT solutions to the Department of War 7 
(DoW), the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), the United States Coast Guard 8 
(USCG) and other federal partners including efforts coordinated through the Federal 9 
Electronic Health Record Modernization (FEHRM) office.  Within its portfolio, PEO 10 
DHMS oversees three critical programs: Defense Healthcare Management System 11 
Modernization (DHMSM), Joint Operational Medicine Information System (JOMIS) and 12 
Enterprise Intelligence and Data Solutions (EIDS). These programs collectively enhance 13 
healthcare delivery, operational medicine capabilities, and medical readiness across 14 
diverse environments. 15 
DHMSM was established to acquire and field a configurable and scalable modernized 16 
Electronic Health Record (EHR) system to replace DoW legacy healthcare systems. In 17 
support of this mission, DHMSM acquired an integrated inpatient/outpatient Commercial 18 
EHR solution known as MHS GENESIS. The DHMSM portfolio extends beyond MHS 19 
GENESIS, incorporating a broader set of solutions to create a single, unified EHR for the 20 
federal health community. Through a suite of clinical applications and products, 21 
DHMSM provides comprehensive health management, advanced data applications, and 22 
improved usability, supporting priorities such as telehealth, population health analytics, 23 
and a user-facing patient portal.  The DHMSM Program Management Office (PMO) 24 
manages the integration of these business processes and clinical workflows to ensure user 25 
adoption and a seamless experience throughout the full continuum of care.  26 
JOMIS provides warfighters with technologies to make life-saving and critical mission 27 
decisions in operational medicine (OpMed) environments.  In support of this mission, 28 
JOMIS develops, deploys, and manages OpMed products that allow for documentation of 29 
patient care, transmission of patient care data through all roles of care, medical readiness 30 
tracking and aggregation of medical Command and Control Situation Awareness (C2SA) 31 
data.  The JOMIS products are meant to be used as an integrated suite of products that 32 
work together with DHMSM and EIDS products to inform the longitudinal health record 33 
and conduct aggregated medical reporting and simulations to support C2SA. The suite 34 
includes but is not limited to OpMed Care Delivery Platform (CDP), Battlefield Assisted 35 
Trauma Distributed Observation Kit - Joint (BATDOK-J), Medical Hub (MedHUB), and 36 
Theater Blood-Mobile (TBLD-M).  As new capabilities are identified, new products or 37 
capabilities may need to be deployed, once developed.  JOMIS users are deployed forces 38 
across the globe supporting all roles of care in connected and disconnected operational, 39 
austere, and forward-deployed locations. To deploy and train OpMed products to the 40 
field, JOMIS collaborates with their Service partners to provide a variety of different 41 

 
2 
 
deployment and training methods based on each individual site or unit, to include low-to-42 
no touch deployment and training. 43 
Due to the growing demand for its product portfolio, PEO DHMS has expanded its focus 44 
to include the deployment to operational locations, and additional federal partners, as 45 
well as specialized environments where standard network connectivity is limited or 46 
unavailable. 47 
2. Purpose & Scope 48 
This is a non-personal services contract to provide all services and materials necessary to 49 
support and execute the on-demand deployment of DHMS products, and related or 50 
supporting components. This includes program management, deployment planning and 51 
execution, hardware delivery and connectivity, product connectivity and interface 52 
validation, user enablement services, and data management and migration 53 
Services are applicable to Continental United States (CONUS) and Outside Continental 54 
United States (OCONUS) locations, covering various roles of care and facility types as 55 
specified in individual task orders1.  56 
3. Desired Outcomes/Tasks/Objectives 57 
This section outlines the desired outcomes, high-level tasks, and core objectives for the 58 
successful deployment of products and integrated systems to hospital (fixed facility) and 59 
field unit (expeditionary) environments under this Indefinite Delivery, Indefinite Quantity 60 
(IDIQ) contract. The contractor shall be responsible for all activities necessary to ensure a 61 
seamless transition from project initiation to full operational capability at each 62 
deployment site with minimal disruption to ongoing operations.   The overarching goal is 63 
to deliver a fully functional and adopted solution that ensures the seamless and accurate 64 
transmission of data through all roles of care and meets the mission needs of each 65 
location. This is critical for updating patient health records and aggregating data for 66 
C2SA reporting. 67 
Each task order will specify the exact scope, schedule, and performance criteria for a 68 
given deployment.  The objectives listed herein describe the full range of capabilities that 69 
may be required across the full range of military operations. 70 
 
1 The Department anticipates that the user base, supported organizations, and operational 
footprint may expand over the period of performance.  While the scope and timing of any 
such expansion cannot be precisely defined at this time, offerors should be aware that 
future requirements may include support for periods of increased operational tempo and 
additional participating entities consistent with evolving mission needs. 

 
3 
 
3.1 Program Management 71 
As part of the Deployment Approach (A003) and the Implementation Plan (A010), the 72 
contractor shall provide a program management framework to ensure deployment efforts 73 
are governed by consistent standards of quality, risk management, and fiscal discipline.  74 
This framework shall provide the Government with full transparency into project 75 
performance and ensure the contractor is accountable for all outcomes.  The contractor 76 
shall successfully execute all work under this contract, including the necessary processes 77 
and tools to effectively manage cost, schedule, and performance/resources.  Specific 78 
requirements, deliverables, and reporting frequencies for each activity will be defined 79 
within individual task orders. 80 
The contractor shall designate a Program Manager (PM) who will serve as the 81 
Government's primary point of contact. The PM must be authorized to act on behalf of 82 
the contractor for all matters related to this contract and its subsequent task orders. The 83 
PM is responsible for the overall management of contract performance, including 84 
resource allocation, risk mitigation, and ensuring clear communication with the 85 
Government's Contracting Officer's Representative (COR). 86 
Core Management Functions: 87 
• Support for Governance and Stakeholder Engagement: The contractor shall 88 
provide dedicated support for all Government-led governance bodies (e.g., 89 
Informatics Steering Committee or OpMed IT Steering Committee). The 90 
contractor's role is to facilitate informed decision-making by: 91 
o Preparing and Facilitating Materials: Developing meeting agendas, 92 
presentation materials (D001), data-driven analyses, and expert 93 
recommendations on deployment strategies for Government review and 94 
consideration. 95 
o Documenting and Tracking Outcomes: Recording meeting minutes 96 
(D002) and tracking action items and key decisions made by the 97 
Government-led committees. 98 
o Executing a Stakeholder Communications Plan: Developing and 99 
executing a comprehensive communications plan that identifies all key 100 
stakeholder groups and defines the contractor's strategy for disseminating 101 
information about project status, risks, and changes as directed by 102 
Government leadership. 103 
• Risk and Issue Management: The contractor shall develop and actively maintain 104 
a risk register. This includes creating a comprehensive Contingency Plan to 105 
mitigate identified risks, such as data loss, network failures, or schedule delays, 106 
ensuring mission-critical operations are protected for the task order. 107 
• Quality and Change Management: The contractor shall implement a robust 108 
Quality Management process to ensure all deliverables meet or exceed contract 109 

 
4 
 
standards.  The contractor shall develop and maintain an effective Quality Control 110 
Plan (QCP) (CDRL A001), which outlines quality procedures and includes a self-111 
inspection and internal coordination plan. The QCP shall establish a formal 112 
Change Control process to manage, document, and validate any modifications to 113 
the system or its configuration post-deployment. Required revisions must be made 114 
within 5 working days. 115 
• Staffing Plan: The contractor shall develop, maintain, and deliver a Staffing Plan 116 
(D003) that details the roles and labor categories required to successfully execute 117 
all tasks specified in the task order.  118 
• Contract Reporting and Administration: The contractor shall manage the 119 
timely development and submission of all contract deliverables as delineated in 120 
the PWS and Sections 33-36.  121 
• Financial and Contract Reporting: The contractor shall provide a Monthly 122 
Progress Report (D00) detailing performance against schedule, budget, and 123 
objectives.  124 
• Kick-Off Meeting: The contractor shall conduct a kick-off meeting with the 125 
Government for each awarded task order. The purpose of this meeting is to ensure 126 
a shared understanding of the task order requirements, establish lines of 127 
communication, and review the contractor's planned approach.  128 
3.2 Deployment Planning and Execution 129 
The contractor shall employ an adaptable framework for planning and executing all 130 
deployments. This framework ensures that each project, regardless of scale or location, 131 
moves from concept to sustained operation in a deliberate and controlled manner. 132 
3.2.1 Initiation and Planning 133 
During initiation and planning, the focus shall be on discovery and creating the blueprint 134 
for successful deployment. 135 
• The Deployment Approach (CDRL A003) is the foundation for the 136 
Implementation Plan (CDRL A010). The contractor shall provide the overall 137 
approach and methodology for deployment, training, user adoption, change 138 
management, and communications across multiple environments/networks, 139 
fixed/expeditionary facilities for multiple products, and with different federal 140 
partners (DoW and non-DoW).  The contractor shall include any potential 141 
opportunities, risks, issues, and dependencies for each area, and mitigation plans. 142 
• Deployment Readiness Assessment (D008): The contractor shall provide a 143 
Deployment Readiness Assessment, which is a formal and systematic evaluation 144 
used to determine if an organization, its users, and the technology itself are fully 145 
prepared for a successful "go-live" event. This assessment is a comprehensive 146 
review that measures preparedness against a predefined set of criteria, moving 147 

 
5 
 
beyond just technical functionality to include people, processes, and support 148 
systems. The contractor shall identify risks, gaps, and potential points of failure 149 
that could jeopardize the deployment's success, disrupt operations, or hinder user 150 
adoption.   151 
• Implementation Plan (CDRL A010): Based on the Readiness Assessment, the 152 
contractor shall develop, maintain, and actively manage an implementation plan, 153 
to include a detailed Integrated Master Schedule (IMS) (CDRL A011). The 154 
Implementation Plan is the detailed tactical execution plan that operationalizes the 155 
approach outlined in the Deployment Approach. 156 
3.2.2 Configuration, Validation, and Implementation 157 
As specified in the task order, the contractor shall perform configuration, validation, and 158 
implementation activities required for deployment, culminating in the formal cutover 159 
event. 160 
• Data Collection: As specified by the task order, the contractor shall facilitate the 161 
timely and accurate collection of all required product configuration data (Product 162 
Configuration Data (D009)).  163 
• Product(s) Configuration: The contractor shall execute the configuration 164 
activities as specified in the task order.  DHMS products may include hardware 165 
and/or component installation and configuration across various networks. This 166 
may include user role assignment, security permissions, and system-level 167 
parameters. 168 
• Cutover: The contractor shall manage the cutover to the new product(s).  Specific 169 
activities will be specified in individual task orders.   170 
 171 
3.2.3 Stabilization and Transition 172 
The contractor shall stabilize the new environment and ensure a seamless hand-off to 173 
sustainment teams. 174 
• Post-Deployment Stabilization Support: The contractor shall implement and 175

1 
 
1 REQUEST FOR INFORMATION (RFI) PURPOSE AND GOVERNMENTS 
DISCLAIMERS  
1.1 PURPOSE 
The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) is issuing this Request for Information (RFI) to request 
detailed data, innovative approaches, and strategic insights from industry partners regarding their 
capabilities and readiness to (1) support the transition of the National Airspace System (NAS) and its 
supporting Air Traffic Control (ATC) infrastructure to Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) and (2) support 
the transition of FAA business systems to PQC. 
The NAS is undergoing a generational transformation to realize a modern, resilient, and globally leading 
ATC system capable of integrating new entrants ( e.g., drones, Advanced Air Mobility (AAM), and 
commercial space operations). Achieving this vision requires a secure foundation capable of withstanding 
emerging threats, including those posed by quantum computing. W ithout quantum‑resistant, crypto‑agile 
security, the NAS cannot achieve the reliability, performance, or international leadership required in th e 
decades ahead. FAA therefore views PQC not as a compliance exercise, but as a foundational enabler of 
modernization—one that must be embedded into every vendor solution, every system upgrade, and every 
step of the Brand New Air Traffic Control System (“BNATCS”)1. 
Specifically, th is RFI seeks industry input on the challenging and mandatory transition of both FAA 
enterprise IT and FAA NAS safety-critical ATC systems to quantum-resistant algorithms to meet federal 
mandates, while managing the high cost, complex recertification, and potential operational disruption of 
integrating new cryptography into FAA operations.  The Government is also seeking information regarding 
the most effective architecture for the enterprise-wide transition to PQC. As National Institute of Standard 
(NIS) standards continue to finalize, the Government is evaluating the feasibility of a "One -Stop Shop" 
(Integrated Suite) approach versus a "Best-of-Breed" (Modular Component) approach. The goal is to ensure 
FAA NAS and FAA non-NAS systems remain "crypto-agile" while minimizing implementation risks and 
interoperability gaps.  FAA recognizes that the PQC market is still maturing and that potential vendors may 
not currently possess the internal capability to execute a fully  integrated solution. Specifically, the FAA 
acknowledges that: 
• A single provider may not have validated all necessary cryptographic primitives across all hardware 
and software layers.   
 
1 https://www.faa.gov/new-atcs 

 
 
2 
 
• A provider’s offerings may not support both FAA Information Technology (IT) and FAA 
Operational Technology (OT) systems. 
• Integration of PQC into legacy National Airspace System (NAS) infrastructure or legacy FAA 
business infrastructure may require specialized expertise that exceeds the scope of a single product 
suite. 
1.2 REQUEST FOR INFORMATION (RFI) ONLY. 
This RFI is issued solely for information and planning purposes and does not constitute a Request for 
Proposal (RFP) or a promise to issue an RFP in the future. Your response will be treated as information 
only, and it shall not be used as a proposal. This RFI does not commit the Government to contract for any 
supply or service whatsoever. 
The Government is not, at this time, seeking proposals and will not accept unsolicited proposals. 
Respondents are advised that the Government will not reimburse any costs associated with preparing or 
submitting a response to this RFI. Submission of a response does not guarantee participation in any future 
solicitation or resulting contract award. Information obtained through this RFI may be used to inform the 
FAA’s acquisition strategy for PQC modernization. The FAA, at its sole discretion, may contact one, some, 
all, or none of the respondents to the RFI and ask for additional information. 
 
1.3 GOVERNMENT POINTS OF CONTACT  
Jackson LeMay 
Contract Specialist 
Jackson.T.LeMay@faa.gov  
 
Kristin Frantz 
Contracting Officer 
Kristin.T.Frantz@faa.gov  
1.4 RESPONSE INSTRUCTION  
Vendors are requested to respond to the questions in Section 3 in a clear, concise, and thorough manner. 
Responses to this RFI must be received electronically by email to the government points of contact in 
Section 1.3 above no later than 4:00 PM Eastern Time on April 24, 2026. Responses received after this date 
and time may not be considered.  The email subject line MUST adhere to the following format: RFI 
Response: [697DCK-26-RFI-PQC– Company Name]. 

 
 
3 
 
ZIP files, executable files (.exe), or links to external cloud storage drives will NOT be accepted for security 
reasons. 
Responses must use a standard 11-point font (e.g., Times New Roman, Arial, or Aptos) with at least 1-inch 
margins on all sides. All pages must be sequentially numbered.  The total response (excluding the cover 
page, table of contents, and proprietary legends) should NOT exceed 30 pages in length.  The Government 
reserves the right to disregard any content beyond the 30-page  limit.  All content responding to RFI 
questions, diagrams, charts, graphs, and executive summaries ARE included in the page count.   The 
following are excluded from the total page count: Cover Page, Table of Contents, Corporate 
Background/PQC Expertise, and Glossary/Acronym List. 
 
1.5 PROPRIETARY INFORMATION  
Respondents are strongly advised not to include in their responses any (1) information that they consider to 
be a trade secret (as defined under the Trade Secret Act (18 U.S.C. § 1905)) and (2) commercial or financial 
information that is  privileged or confidential  (“Proprietary Information”) , unless such information is 
essential for the Government to evaluate the response.  Respondents grant to the Government unlimited 
rights to use, modify, reproduce, release, perform, display, or disclose the information contained in the 
responses, except for Proprietary Information. Respondents grant to the Government limited rights to use, 
modify, reproduce, release, perform, display, or disclose Proprietary Information for government purposes, 
including competitive procurement. Government purposes do not include public disclosure. 
Proprietary Information  submitted in  response to this RFI must be clearly and conspicuously marked . 
Proper marking is necessary for the Government to identify Proprietary Information as such , including for 
exemption from the public disclosure requirements under the Freedom of Information Act (5 U.S.C. § 552) 
(FOIA), specifically Exemption 4 (which protects against public disclosure of trade secrets and confidential 
commercial or financial information). The marking should be included in the transmittal page and on each 
page of the response clearly and with specificity identifying therein the Proprietary Information. Responses 
without appropriate markings will be treated as having been submitted with unlimited rights . The 
Government will not be liable for any use, disclosure, release, reproduction, modification, display and/or 
performance of any Proprietary Information not properly marked by the vendor.  
If the submission contains any proprietary information, a second, redacted version suitable for public 
release MUST also be provided as a separate PDF file. The file name for the redacted version must be 
clearly marked: RFI_ 697DCK-26-RFI-PQC_[Company Name] _PublicRelease.pdf. 

 
 
4 
 
2 POST-QUANTUM CRYPTOGRAPHY (PQC) MIGRATION FOR FAA 
INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND NAS AIR TRAFFIC CONTROL 
(ATC) SYSTEMS 
The migration of Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) systems, encompassing both enterprise 
Information Technology (IT) and the safety-critical National Air Space (NAS) infrastructure, to Post -
Quantum Cryptography (PQC) represents an immediate and foundat ional security imperative.  The 
transition to enhanced cryptography is paramount due to the nature of FAA operations and the focus on 
safety, security, data integrity, confidentiality, and system authentication. This transition must be initiated 
now, given the inherent conflict between the rapid progression toward cryptographically relevant quantum 
computers (CRQC), which some projections estimate may appear in less than 10 years, and the 
characteristically slow, multi-year integration timelines required for the deployment of new cryptographic 
algorithms across large, complex information systems 
  
The industry responses to this RFI will help to inform FAA of the development of cost estimates required 
by the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) for strategic planning and resource allocation.  
Introducing PQC, which may necessitate deep structural modifications to both hardware and software 
across the NAS, could profoundly affect the projected benefits, costs, and timelines of ongoing Air Traffic 
Control (ATC) System improvements.  This RFI explicitly requests vendor analysis on the potential 
disruption or delay caused by PQC insertion into both NAS ATC as well as FAA Information Technology 
systems.     
 
Since FAA is currently in the fact-finding phase of its transition to PQC, it is not committed to any specific 
procurement model and is evaluating the feasibility of both a "One -Stop Shop" (Integrated) approach and 
an "Individual Component" (Modular) approach. 
 
• Model A: Integrated Enterprise Solution (One-Stop Shop)  
In this model, a single lead integrator or vendor provides a comprehensive, pre -validated PQC suite 
including algorithm implementation, key management, and hardware integration. The Government is 
interested in understanding the trade -offs regarding vendor lock -in versus the speed of deployment and 
simplified compliance auditing. 
 
• Model B: Modular Component/Subsystem Approach  
In this model, the Government would source individual subsystems —such as NIST -standardized Key 
Encapsulation Mechanisms (KEMs), Digital Signature algorithms, and PQC -ready Hardware Security 
Modules (HSMs) from specialized providers. The Government seeks information on how "crypto-agility" 
is best maintained in this model and the potential challenges regarding multi-vendor intero

Call for Solutions for

Airspace Common Operating Picture

Note: Please advise the cognizant Procurement Contracting Officer and Contract Specialist as soon as possible via the email above if your organization intends to submit a Solution Brief in response to this Call for Solution.

Any submissions that are received by the Procurement Contracting Officer (PCO) after the suspense date/time listed above will be deemed late and not evaluated. It is the sole responsibility of the company to ensure that its submission is received in the inbox listed above before the deadline specified.

Description:

The U.S. Army Contracting Command - Orlando (ACC-Orlando) is issuing this Call for Solutions under the authority of 10 U.S.C. § 3458 and Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS) Subpart 212.70. This Call for Solutions is intended to competitively acquire innovative commercial products or technologies to support the development and deployment of the: 

Airspace Common Operating Picture (A-COP) 

The U.S. Army requires an advanced airspace management capability to ensure safe, efficient, and compliant operations within Special Use Airspace (SUA) at Army installations. Current systems lack the ability to provide real-time surveillance, monitoring, and deconfliction of manned aircraft and Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) Groups 1-5, as defined by AR 95-2, AR 350-52, AR 385-63, and TC 3-04.15. This capability gap poses significant risks to safety, operational effectiveness, and compliance with Army regulations.

The envisioned solution must integrate real-time data from manned and unmanned platforms, including Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast (ADS-B) equipped military and civilian aircraft, and provide situational awareness to Range Management Authorities (RMA). The system must operate continuously, with minimal latency, and deliver precise positional data to support safe operations in high-density environments. Additionally, the solution must align with the Modular Open Systems Approach (MOSA) to ensure compatibility with current and future tactical networks, such as the Tactical Awareness Kit (TAK) and Range Facility Management Support System (RFMSS). The solution must also be scalable, customizable, and capable of supporting operations across multiple installations listed in the Basis of Issue Plan. It must provide flexible data relay options, customizable alerts, and ensure secure operations within a limited-access network. 

1.1	The U.S. Army seeks innovative commercial solutions that address the following attributes: 

Real-Time Airspace Surveillance and Monitoring:

Systems capable of integrating data from multiple sources, including ADS-B, radar, and other sensors, to provide a comprehensive airspace picture.

Solutions that support continuous, low-latency operations in high-density environments.

Deconfliction of Manned and Unmanned Aircraft:

Tools for real-time deconfliction of UAS Groups 1-5 and manned aircraft operating within SUA.

Capabilities to provide automated alerts and recommendations to Range Management Authorities (RMA).

Integration with Tactical Networks:

Solutions that align with the Modular Open Systems Approach (MOSA) and are compatible with existing Army systems, such as the Tactical Awareness Kit (TAK) and Range Facility Management Support System (RFMSS).

Systems that support future scalability and interoperability with emerging technologies.

Scalability and Customizability:

Solutions that can be deployed across multiple Army installations and adapted to varying operational requirements.

Customizable alerting mechanisms, data relay options, and user interfaces to meet mission-specific needs.

Secure Operations:

Systems that ensure secure data transmission and operations within limited-access networks.

Compliance with Army cybersecurity standards and protocols.

Advanced Data Analytics and Visualization:

Tools for advanced data processing, visualization, and reporting to enhance situational awareness and decision-making.

Capabilities to provide predictive analytics for airspace management.

Awarding Instrument

This Call for Solution solicitation will be awarded in accordance with the Army Open Solicitation procedures detailed within Solicitation W9128Z-25-S-A002, posted to SAM.gov on 01 May 2025. This Call for Solution will utilize CSO procedures. Use of a CSO in accordance with DFARS Subpart 212.70 in conjunction with FAR 35 and DFARS 235 is considered to be a competitive procedure for the purposes of 10 U.S.C. chapter 221 and FAR 6.102.

Solicitation Notes

In addition to the general guidelines included in the Open Solicitation, the following guidelines apply to this Call for Solution.

Offerors are responsible for monitoring SAM.gov for any amendments to this solicitation that may have updated information.

The Government will give preference to submissions that demonstrate an innovative, existing, and reference-verifiable commercial ability to accomplish the desired solution attributes listed above.

Resellers and academic research proposals are not desired.

All solution briefs will be valid for 180 calendar days after the submission date.

The Solution Brief must identify any intellectual property involved in the effort and associated restrictions on the Government’s use of that intellectual property.

The Government reserves the right to go directly from Solution Brief to award.

General Information and Questions

Questions are to be sent only to the Procurement Contracting Officer and Contract Specialist (POCs) listed in this Call for Solution. The following shall be utilized in the email subject line: Call for Solution – CPE-STRI-ACOP (reference the section area). Material questions and answers will be published and updated on www.sam.gov.

Phase 0: Solicitation for Solution Briefs

Interested offerors must submit a concise solution brief (maximum 10-page white paper or 20 slides) addressing the following:

Executive Summary: Overview of the proposed solution and its relevance to the desired solution under this Call for Solutions.

Technology Concept: Description of the innovative aspects of the solution, including any required development or adaptation. Show and/or describe how your proposed solution or potential new capabilities represent an application of innovative commercial products, innovative commercial technologies or adaptations/modifications of existing commercial product technologies to the A-COP.

Company Viability: Overview of the company including relevant experience.

Intellectual Property (IP): Description of any proprietary technologies or data rights.

Submission Guidelines:

Interested offerors shall include Company Name, Title, Date, Point of Contact Name, E-Mail Address, Phone, and Address.

The title page does not count against page limit, however, the executive summary does.

The period of performance of the contract resulting from this Call for Solutions should generally be no greater than five (5) years.

Technical data with military application may require approval, authorization, or license for lawful exportation.

All solution briefs, presentations, and proposals must be Unclassified. Do NOT mark any documents as “CONFIDENTIAL”. Solution briefs, presentations, and proposals containing data that is not to be disclosed to the public for any purpose or use by the government except for evaluation purposes shall include the following general disclaimer on the cover page:

“This [select one: solution brief, Presentation, or Proposal] includes data that shall not be disclosed outside the government, except to non-government personnel for evaluation purposes, and shall not be duplicated, used, or disclosed - in whole or in part - for any purpose other than to evaluate this submission. If, however, an agreement is awarded to this company as a result of - or in connection with - the submission of this data, the government shall have the right to duplicate, use, or disclose the data to the extent agreed upon by both parties in the resulting agreement. This restriction does not limit the government's right to use information contained in this data if it is obtained from another source without restriction. The data subject to this restriction are contained in sheets [insert numbers or other identification of sheets]” 

Submissions must be submitted electronically.

Offerors that are not chosen for the presentation phase will be notified in writing as soon as practicable. Further verbal feedback may be provided, upon request, at the Contracting/Agreements Officer’s discretion.

Phase 1: Solution Brief Evaluation

The Government will conduct an initial down select based on solution briefs submitted in Phase 0. Upon review of a solution brief, the Government may elect to invite “all, some or none” of the proposed solutions into Phase 2, the presentation/pitch based on the merits of the solution brief. In Phase 2, companies are invited to pitch and further discuss their proposed concept/technology/solution in person or virtually. During Phase 2, the Government may request and/or provide additional information to the company.

Solution Briefs will be evaluated on the following criteria:

Technical merit of the proposed solution, including the extent to which the proposed solution is unique, and/or innovative to fulfill the requirement for the A-COP. Technical Merit is defined as having direct relevance to the stated requirement, addressing identified capability gaps, and providing innovative technological advancements. 

The government may elect to use external market research in the evaluation of a company’s viability.

Phase 2: Solution Presentation/Pitch

Down selected offerors will be invited to present their solutions to the Government and to participate in a live demonstration. The presentation shall include:

Detailed technical and business viability of the solution

Proposed schedule and milestones

Anticipated data rights assertions

Explanation of Data Rights

Rough Order of Magnitude (ROM) pricing: Estimated cost and payment terms.

7.2.		Participation in the live demonstration in a relevant environment will be coordinated by the Government.

Phase 2: Presentation/Pitch Evaluation

Individual presentations/pitches will be evaluated against the evaluation criteria below and not against any other presentations/pitches. Presentation/pitch submissions will be valid for 120 days after pitch evaluation. The government will aim to complete evaluation of presentation/pitches within 30 calendar days of the presentation/pitch. After completing the evaluation of presentation/pitch submissions, the government will notify the company that: they may submit a Full Proposal for evaluation in Phase 3; or their proposed concept/technology/solution is not of continued interest to the Government, and they will not be required to submit a Full Proposal and advance to Phase 3. 

Presentation/Pitch evaluation criteria are listed in order of importance from top to bottom. Presentation/pitches shall be evaluated/scored on the following factors:

Level of relevancy of the solution in addressing the A-COP with a commercially available product and/or process;

The technical merit of the proposed solution adequately addressed the A-COP and demonstrated feasibility for the Government to pursue the proposed solution;

Level of uniqueness, and/or innovative approach to solve the Government’s need;

Level of technical risk/maturity;

Level of risk placed with the proposed ROM;

Level of risk in the proposed milestone schedule and its ability to meet the A-COP requirement within a relevant time period;

Level of risk in the company’s viability and business solution; and

Level of potential risk in anticipated IP and data rights assertions

 Phase 3: Full Proposal

Based upon the results of market research, Phase 1 and/or Phase 2 evaluation(s) above, the government may request a proposal. If that occurs, a company, or companies will be invited to develop and submit a written proposal as well as negotiate appropriate terms and conditions, as applicable, governing the requirement. At this stage, the selected companies may discuss proposal development details during the proposal writing process with their assigned government expert(s) or government teaming partner(s).

Additionally, companies are invited to provide their own internal terms and conditions that may be consolidated with those of the government. This includes, but is not limited to, Service License Agreements (SLA) and/or User License Agreements (ULA).

Companies should note that there are certain terms and conditions the government may be unable to accept. However, projects awarded through the CSO may provide flexibility to adopt customary industry standards where it is otherwise legal and meets the government’s general public responsibility.

Offerors advancing to this phase will submit a detailed proposal, including:

Technical Volume: The technical volume shall include a detailed work plan indicating how each aspect of the objectives is to be accomplished. The technical volume should be in as much detail as the offeror considers necessary to fully explain its proposed technical approach or method, including all assumptions utilized to develop its proposed solution and associated costs. The technical volume should reflect a clear understanding of the nature of the work being undertaken. The technical volume must include information on how the project will be organized, staffed, and managed. Information should be provided that demonstrates Offeror’s understanding and management of important events or tasks. The technical volume must include a list of suggested deliverables and deliverable dates.

NOTE: The government reserves the right to further define the required technical proposal content based upon the specific contract requirements for the A-COP.

Price Volume: Detailed cost breakdown and justification. The government reserves the right to request any other price data or supporting cost information the government deems necessary to determine the total overall price is fair and reasonable. This can include but is not limited to commercial price catalog(s), previous commercial sales receipts, and/or other proprietary information to help the government determine reasonableness and future budgetary cost estimates. The Government reserves the right to further define the required price proposal content based upon the relevant requirements of the proposed solution.

Payment Milestones: In-Depth timeline/schedule to include parts lead time, build phase, and fielding. 

Additional Proposal Submission Content:

Small Business Subcontracting Plan: Large business Offerors shall submit either an individual subcontracting plan and/or master subcontracting plan (if available) in accordance with FAR 52.219-9 Small Business Subcontracting Plan (Sep 2023), DFARS 252.219-7003 Small Business Subcontracting Plan (DoD

search_text:
U.S. DEPARTMENT of  STATE 
 
Embajada de los Estados Unidos de América 
Carrera 45 # 24B- 27 - (+57601) 275.2000 -  Bogotá D.C. – Colombia 
co.usembassy.gov 
 
NOTICE OF INTENT – BRAND-NAME 
Solicitation Number: PR15893854 
 
 
This is not a solicitation; this is a NOTICE OF INTENT to award a brand-name procurement action as 
follows: 
 
The Bureau of International Narcotics and Law Enforcement (INL) at the U.S. Embassy in Bogotá -
Colombia intends to award a firm fixed-price purchase order for the supply of Financial Investigation 
Software Licenses. For this requirement, ALTIA SOFTWARE LICENSES is considered the only 
brand, and no other supplies or services will accomplish Embassy Bogota requirements. 
 
A brand name restriction is required for this software procurement due to integration and compatibility 
issues with the software currently in use by the Colombian National Police (CNP)  and to meet 
operational requirements. CNP investigators are trained exclusively with Altia software, and it is the 
only brand authorized for use by the CNP as part of their financial crime investigations. 
 
Product Service Code: 7A21, IT and Telecom - Business Application Software. 
NAICS code: 541511, Custom Computer Programming Services. 
 
This procurement action is being conducted in accordance with the statutory authority of FAR Part 
6.103-1, which states that only one brand-name and no other will satisfy Embassy Bogota requirements.  
 
Any interested party that believes it can meet the requirements described herein may identify 
themselves and must provide evidence of their capability to fulfill the requirement.  
 
Responses must be submitted in writing to contact Javier F. Lopez at LopezJF@state.gov and must 
be received no later than April 4, 2026, at 16:00 hours (Bogota, Colombia) time. 
 
 
Sincerely, 
 
Chantale Breton 
Contracting Officer, INL Bogota

U.S. DEPARTMENT of  STATE 
 
Embajada de los Estados Unidos de América 
Carrera 45 # 24B- 27 - (+57601) 275.2000 - Bogotá D.C. – Colombia 
co.usembassy.gov 
Bogota - March 20, 2026 
 
COMBINED SYNOPSIS/SOLICITATION 
RFQ Number: PR15893854 
 
The Bureau of International Narcotics and Law Enforcement Affairs (INL) invites you to submit a quotation for 
FINANCIAL INVESTIGATION SOFTWARE LICENSES. 
 
Place of performance: Electronic delivery – Bogota, Colombia 
Contract managed: Local Contracting 
Required: forty-five days (45) after receipt of order. 
 
Quote must be submitted via email no later than April 29, 2026, 4:00 PM local (Bogotá, Colombia) time.  
Refer to SF-1449 Continuation Pages, Section 3 (Solicitation Provisions), Subsection III (Addendum to FAR 
12.202), Item 2.0 for complete submission instructions.  
 
Questions pertaining to this solicitation, if any, must be received by the Government no later than April 
1, 2026, 4:00 PM local (Bogotá, Colombia) time. 
 
This is a combined synopsis/solicitation prepared in accordance with the format in FAR Subpart 12.202, as 
supplemented with additional information included in this notice. This announcement constitutes the only 
solicitation; quotations are being requested by this notice alone, and a written solicitation will not be issued. The 
resultant purchase order will be awarded using Simplified Acquisition Procedures in accordance with FAR 12. 
 
The U.S. Government intends to award a purchase order if the responsive quote is technically acceptable and 
reasonably priced. We intend to award a contract based on initial quote, without holding discussions, although 
we may hold discussions if it is in the best interest of the Government to do so. 
 
Unless an exception in FAR 4.1102 applies, your company must be registered in the System for Award 
Management (SAM) (www.SAM.gov) in order to be eligible for award.  You may contact Javier F. Lopez at 
LopezJF@state.gov should you have any questions relating to SAM registration. 
 
Sincerely, 
 
Chantale Breton 
Contracting Officer, INL Bogota

DESCRIPTION

Purpose: 

The Network Contracting Office (NCO) 16 located at 5075 Westheimer Road, 

Galleria Financial Center, Suite 750, Houston, Texas, hereby issues the following Sources Sought/ Request for Information (RFI) with the intent of determining the contractor interest, capabilities, qualifications of potential businesses, nonmanufacturer rule compliance, Buy American Act compliance, Trade Agreement compliance and estimated industry pricing information for market research and estimated budgetary purpose for Contractor to assist in Remote Automated Laboratory Systems (RALS) for Hemochron Signature Elites for Central Arkansas Veterans Healthcare System (CAVHS), AR, 72205.  

Notice of Intent to Sole Source.  If there are no responses to this notice, a determination by the Government not to compete based upon responses to this notice is solely within the discretion of the Government to sole source the requirement. No response is a response.

This Sources Sought is Brand Name or Equal.

The Government request that interested offerors complete all questions below, provide required documentation, descriptive literature and authorization letter as described below. The Government is in no way obligated to do business with or to enter into any form of contract with any person, firm or other entity that receives or responds to this announcement. 

Objective: To find qualified and certified contractors with the capability to provide the following: 

The contractor shall provide all labor, tools, materials, equipment, transportation and supervision necessary to complete all work associated with providing regulated medical waste and reusable sharps container management & disposal services. All work shall be complete and strict in accordance with the Performance Work Statement.

Delivery Location: 

The services will be conducted at Central Arkansas Veterans Healthcare System facilities at the Point of Care Department and Cardiac Cath Lab:

Contractor shall possess the capability to provide all requirements and objectives. 

 

Instructions and Response Guidelines: 

Sources Sought responses are due by 03/31/2026 at 3:00 PM (CST) via email to t.miller@va.gov. Telephone requests or inquires will not be accepted. Public Information will not be provided and shall be utilized to the full extent possible. This a New Requirement.

Information provided will be used solely by CAVHS as "market research" and will not be released outside of the CAVHS Purchasing and Contract Team. B.1  CONTRACT ADMINISTRATION DATA

  1.  Contract Administration:  All contract administration matters will be handled by the following individuals:

    a.  CONTRACTOR:  	Les Systemes Medicaux Inc. 

		Intelerad Medical Systems, Inc. 

		800 Boul De Maisonneuve E 12th Floor 

		Montreal, Quebec H2L 4L8, Canada

		

			

    b.  GOVERNMENT:  	Contracting Officer 36C10B, 

Kathryn Pantages/Kathryn.pantages@va.gov

		Department of Veterans Affairs

				Technology Acquisition Center

				23 Christopher Way

				Eatontown NJ 07724

  2.  CONTRACTOR REMITTANCE ADDRESS:  All payments by the Government to the contractor will be made in accordance with:

  3.  INVOICES:  Invoices shall be submitted in arrears:

     a.  Quarterly	[]

     b.  Semi-Annually	[]

     c.  Other	[X] Upon receipt and acceptance of deliverables IAW Section B Price Schedule.

  4.  GOVERNMENT INVOICE ADDRESS:  All Invoices from the contractor shall be submitted electronically in accordance with VAAR Clause 852.232-72 Electronic Submission of Payment Requests.

  5. ACKNOWLEDGMENT OF AMENDMENTS: The offeror acknowledges receipt of amendments to the Solicitation numbered and dated as follows:

B.2  GOVERNING LAW CLAUSE

Federal law and regulations, including the Federal Acquisition Regulations (FAR), shall govern this Contract/Order. Commercial license agreements may be made a part of this Contract/Order but only if both parties expressly make them an addendum hereto. If the commercial license agreement is not made an addendum, it shall not apply, govern, be a part of or have any effect whatsoever on the Contract/Order; this includes, but is not limited to, any agreement embedded in the computer software (clickwrap), any agreement that is otherwise delivered with or provided to the Government with the commercial computer software or documentation (shrinkwrap), or any other license agreement otherwise referred to in any document. If a commercial license agreement is made an addendum, only those provisions addressing data rights regarding the Government’s use, duplication and disclosure of data (e.g., restricted computer software) are included and made a part of this Contract/Order, and only to the extent that those provisions are not duplicative or inconsistent with Federal law, Federal regulation, the incorporated FAR clauses and the provisions of this Contract/Order; those provisions in the commercial license agreement that do not address data rights regarding the Government’s use, duplication and disclosure of data shall not be included or made a part of the Contract/Order. Federal law and regulation including, without limitation, the Contract Disputes Act (41 U.S.C. § 7101 et seq.), the Anti-Deficiency Act (31 U.S.C. § 1341 et seq.), the Competition in Contracting Act (41 U.S.C. § 3301 et seq.), the Prompt Payment Act (31 U.S.C. § 3901 et seq.), Contracts for Data Processing or Maintenance (38 USC § 5725), Source Code Harmonization and Reuse in Information Technology Act “SHARE IT Act” (P.L. 118-187), and FAR clauses 52.212-4 and 52.227-14 shall supersede, control, and render ineffective any inconsistent, conflicting, or duplicative provision in any commercial license agreement. In the event of conflict between this clause and any provision in the Contract/Order or the commercial license agreement or elsewhere, the terms of this clause shall prevail. The Contractor shall deliver to the Government all data first produced under this Contract/Order with unlimited rights as defined by FAR 52.227-14, this includes any custom code developed for the purpose of performing under this contract. Claims of patent or copyright infringement brought against the Government as a party shall be defended by the U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) in accordance with 28 U.S.C. § 516; at the discretion of DOJ, the Contractor may be allowed reasonable participation in the defense of the litigation. Any additional changes to the Contract/Order must be made by modification (Standard Form 30) and shall only be made by a warranted Contracting Officer. Nothing in this Contract/Order or any commercial license agreement shall be construed as a waiver of sovereign immunity.

B.3  SOFTWARE LICENSE, MAINTENANCE, AND TECHNICAL SUPPORT

(1). Definitions. 

a) Licensee. The term “licensee” shall mean the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (“VA”) and is synonymous with “Government.” 

b) Licensor. The term “licensor” shall mean the Contractor having the necessary license or ownership rights to deliver license, software maintenance and support of the computer software being acquired. The term “Contractor” is the party identified in Block 17a on the SF1449. If the Contractor is a reseller and not the Licensor, the Contractor remains responsible for performance under this Contract/Order. 

c) Software. The term “software” shall mean the licensed computer software product(s) cited in the Schedule of Supplies/Services. 

d) Maintenance. The term “maintenance” is the process of enhancing and optimizing software, as well as remedying defects. It shall include all new fixes, patches, releases, updates, versions and upgrades, as further defined below. 

e) Technical Support. The term “technical support” refers to the range of services providing assistance for the software via the telephone, email, a website or otherwise. 

f) Release or Update. The term “release” or “update” are terms that refer to a revision of software that contains defect corrections, minor enhancements, or improvements of the software’s functionality. This is usually designated by a change in the number to the right of the decimal point (e.g., from Version 5.3 to 5.4). An example of an update is the addition of new hardware. 

g) Version or Upgrade. The term “version” or “upgrade” are terms that refer to a revision of software that contains new or improved functionality. This is usually designated by a change in the number to the left of the decimal point (e.g., from Version 5.4 to 6). 

(2). Software License. 

a) Unless otherwise stated in the Schedule of Supplies/Services, the Performance Work Statement or Product Description, the software license provided to the Government is a perpetual, nonexclusive license to use the software. 

b) The Government may use the software in a networked environment. 

c) Any dispute regarding the license grant or usage limitations shall be resolved in accordance with the Disputes Clause incorporated in FAR 52.212-4(d). 

d) All limitations of software usage are expressly stated in the Schedule of Supplies/Services and the Performance Work Statement/Product Description. 

(3). Software Maintenance and/or Technical Support. 

a) If the Government desires to continue software maintenance and support beyond the period of performance identified in this Contract/Order, the Government will issue a separate contract or order for maintenance and support. Conversely, if a contract or order for continuing software maintenance and technical support is not received, the Contractor is neither authorized nor permitted to renew any of the previously furnished services. 

b) The Contractor shall provide software support services, which includes periodic updates, enhancements and corrections to the software, and reasonable technical support, all of which are customarily provided by the Contractor to its commercial customers to cause the software to perform according to its specifications, documentation or demonstrated claims. 

c) Any telephone support provided by Contractor shall be at no additional cost. 

d) The Contractor shall provide all maintenance services in a timely manner in accordance with the Contractor’s customary practice or as defined in the Performance Work Statement or Product Description. However, prolonged delay (exceeding two business days) in resolving software problems will be noted in the Government’s various past performance records on the Contractor (e.g., www.cpars.gov). 

e) If the Government allows the maintenance and support to lapse and subsequently wishes to reinstate it, any reinstatement fee charged shall not exceed the amounts that would have been charged if the Government had not allowed the subscription to lapse. 

(4). Disabling Software Code. 

The Government requires delivery of computer software that does not contain any code that will, upon the occurrence or the nonoccurrence of any event, disable the software. Such code includes but is not limited to a computer virus, restrictive key, node lock, time-out, or other function, whether implemented by electronic, mechanical, or other means, which limits or hinders the use or access to any computer software based on residency on a specific hardware configuration, frequency of duration of use, or other limiting criteria. If any such disabling code is present, the Contractor agrees to indemnify the Government for all damages suffered as a result of a disabling caused by such code, and the contractor agrees to remove such code upon the Government’s request at no extra cost to the Government. Inability of the Contractor to remove the disabling software code will be considered an inexcusable delay and a material breach of contract, and the Government may exercise its right to terminate for cause. In addition, the Government is permitted to remove the code as 

it deems appropriate and charge the Contractor for consideration for the time and effort expended in removing the code. 

(5). Manuals and Publications.

 	

Upon Government request, the Contractor shall furnish the most current version of the user manual and publications for all products/services provided under this Contract/Order at no cost.

B.4  PRICE SCHEDULE

All days referenced below shall be considered calendar days, unless otherwise noted. All items are Inspection/Acceptance/FOB Destination. 

Any resulting contract will be awarded on a firm-fixed-price basis as defined by Federal Acquisition Regulation Subpart 16.202. Accordingly, the Contractor shall ensure that any and all costs associated with the Contractor’s proposed application(s), software products, software solution, and/or system, shall be included in the Contractor’s proposed firm-fixed price, and shall serve as the Contractor’s firm-fixed price for the life of any resulting contract. No additional costs or fees relative to the Contractor’s proposed application(s), software products, software solution, and/or system including, but not limited to, licensing costs and any associated licensing maintenance required for the development, delivery, integration, operation, and/or maintenance of the Contractor’s proposed solution will be allowed, accepted, and/or paid by the Government.

B.5  PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT

	

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS)

DEPARTMENT OF VETERANS AFFAIRS

Veterans Health Administration (VHA)

National Teleradiology Program (NTP) 

IntelePACS Upgrade 2026

Date: 02/26/2026

VA-26-00042676

	PWS Version Number:  2.0	

BACKGROUND

The Veteran Health Administration (VHA) National Teleradiology Program (NTP) is the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) in-house Teleradiology service and has been providing 24x7 Teleradiology service to VA facilities since 2010.  NTP is currently supporting over 120 sites across all Veterans Integrated Service Networks

(VISNs).  Yearly workload is approximately 1.4 million exams of which 80% are STAT interpretations.  Currently NTP has approximately 230 Picture Archive and Communication System (PACS) workstations distributed across remote users and in-house reading centers providing remote Radiology interpretations.  

NTP’s current Teleradiology PACS archive (IntelePACS) and core application servers are located in the Sacramento Data Center, and the software and hardware are well past their end of life.  Additional servers are located in regional data centers and NTP reading centers and those system are similarly past their useful life.  NTP requires an upgrade of the existing IntelePACS software to the latest commercially available release while providing ongoing service/support to the legacy system until the upgrade is approved for clinical use. 

In November 2018, VA issued the Cloud First Policy mandating that all new and existing

Information Technology (IT) solutions be assessed to determine suitability to be offered as an enterprise cloud computing service. The VA Enterprise Cloud (VAEC) was established by the Enterprise Cloud Solutions Office (ECSO) in 2017, to host cloud computing solutions. The upgraded system shall be deployed in the VA Enterprise Cloud (VAEC) and the VA shall be responsible for VAEC hosting, while the Contractor shall provide all software and licenses beyond items within the VAEC catalog.  The Contractor shall be responsible for managing all VAEC resources associated with the PACS as part of initial deployment and ongoing service and support.

BACKGROUND

The mission of the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) Veterans Health Administration (VHA) is to provide superior healthcare services to Veterans of the United States. As the largest integrated healthcare system in the country, VA is uniquely positioned to develop and provide advanced healthcare capabilities to over 9 million Veterans. One significant challenge facing the VA and its ability to deliver high quality and reliable patient care to our Nation’s Veterans is the increase in demand for veteran patients to be seen within the VA healthcare system. In the short window of time allocated for each patient appointment, providers have a multitude of clinical and administrative tasks to be completed. This increases risk of important information being omitted due to rushed interviews and contributes to provider burn out. The myriad of tasks expected of providers beyond caring for patients puts significant cognitive burden and increased strain on them professionally. This strain has the potential to negatively impact patient care. It can also contribute to provider attrition, causing additional impact to Veterans and clinical staff. 

One method to reduce the risk of missing vital clinical information that impacts patient care planning and reducing cognitive burden for our providers is the use of ambient scribe technology Artificial Intelligence (AI) ambient scribes utilize a microphone on a secure device. The ambient AI scribe transcribes patient encounters. We then use machine learning and natural-language processing to summarize the conversation's clinical content and produce a transcribed note documenting the visit. Such transcribed conversations between patients and providers automatically leverage AI technologies to create notes in the appropriate format (generate clinical documentation) and summarize a patient’s visit. Automating these tasks increases accuracy of information, saves the providers time, and reduces their mental strain. Visit notes that include a patient’s care plan is a critical component of treatment and care delivery. The software functions as a medical and clinical system, equipment, device, or software (MCSEDS) as defined in VA Directive 6008.

Ambient scribe technology also enables other labor-saving capabilities to include but not limited to: pre and post visit charting to summarize important information prior to visit by either completing the task with AI or integrating with provider pre-charted note, coding support, order capture, after-visit summaries, drafting care plans and other capabilities. 

The ambient scribing solution required is unique to the operational environment and specific challenges faced by the Veterans Health Administration (VHA). In January 2024, VHA undertook an AI Technical Sprint challenge to ensure that potential solutions were rigorously tested through a competitive and impartial evaluation process under H.R. 4521-Bioeconomy Research and Development Act Of 2021 [America COMPETES Act of 2022]. The goal of the competition was to identify the top AI solutions identified through the VHA AI Tech Sprint Track 1: Ambient Dictation of Clinical Encounters. 

The advanced AI-enabled tools for ambient scribing focus on enhancing clinical documentation across primary care, mental health, and specialty care settings. The solution shall meet the following requirements:

Transcription and Note Generation: Automatically extract transcripts and key details from ambient recordings of patient encounters, creating traceable, high-fidelity medical records to include capabilities to populate diversified note templates.

Interoperability and Integration: Seamlessly integrate with existing VA health system information technologies, particularly the Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems, to ensure smooth operational continuity and data consistency.

Security Compliance: Adhere to stringent VA privacy and security standards for the protection of patient data.

Advanced Functionalities: Provide continuous improvements to include record query, recommend diagnoses and referrals, inpatient discharge summaries, nursing patient management, pre-charting capabilities and maintain high performance even in challenging clinical settings.

Patient Communications: Generate patient-facing summaries (After Visit Summaries and Discharge Summaries) that include educational materials, reminders, and follow-up recommendations which adhere to clinical documentation compliance standards.

Throughout the Technical Sprint, many solutions demonstrated superior technical maturity and clinical performance. Their development was closely aligned with the urgent needs of the VHA, specifically targeting enhancements in provider burnout and clinical documentation efficiencies. IT professionals and healthcare providers assessed these solutions for their maturity and practical applicability in real-world clinical settings, ensuring that they met the highest standards for operational readiness. 

Moreover, the effectiveness and safety of these solutions were evaluated through a series of blind scoring assessments. Physicians compared the AI-generated outputs against those produced by human clinicians, establishing their accuracy and reliability without bias. Additionally, their usability and potential for seamless integration into the VHA’s existing EHR systems were critically assessed to determine their capability to enhance operational continuity and data consistency within the VA health care system. This approach ensured a thorough evaluation of potential Contractors in a manner that fostered broad competition and verified that the selected Contractors met legal, technical, and operational requirements. Evaluations concluded in May 2024.

VA personnel assigned to the Digital Health Office will coordinate with SimLEARN personnel to access the system and run a real-time test to verify outputs. The test will occur at VA’s SimLEARN facility and will be conducted by VA staff. The test will require access to the system to simulate an encounter.
